# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. **[KTU University Exam — May 2024, Model Paper-2 Set B]**
Differentiate between the **micro-scale** and **macro-scale** approaches used in the analysis of polymer composites. Give one engineering example for each scale.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

- **Micro-scale approach (1 mark):** Resolves the actual fibre–matrix geometry, interfacial shear transfer, and individual fibre stress distribution. Governing parameters: $d$, $L$, $\tau_i$, $l_c$. Example: predicting crack bridging and pull-out length in a chopped-glass/PA6 part.
- **Macro-scale approach (1 mark):** Treats the composite as a homogeneous, anisotropic continuum. Uses averaged properties ($E_c$, $\sigma_c$, $\rho_c$) over a representative volume element. Example: deflection calculation of an automotive bumper beam using $E_c I$.
- **Link (1 mark):** Micro-scale rules feed into the macro-scale RVE through efficiency factors $\eta_l$ and $\eta_o$.

### Q2. **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Module-1]**
Define the term **"critical fibre length"** with the standard formula. State what happens when (i) $L \ll l_c$ and (ii) $L \gg l_c$.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

- **Definition + formula (1 mark):**

$$l_c = \frac{\sigma_{f,u} \, d}{2 \tau_i}$$

It is the minimum fibre length required to load the fibre to its ultimate tensile strength through interfacial shear transfer.

- **Case (i) — $L \ll l_c$ (1 mark):** The fibre **pulls out** of the matrix without breaking. The composite fails by interfacial debonding; strength is dominated by $\tau_i$ rather than $\sigma_{f,u}$.
- **Case (ii) — $L \gg l_c$ (1 mark):** The fibre **breaks first**, and the composite strength is dominated by fibre strength (efficient reinforcement, similar to continuous-fibre behaviour).

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 marks)

> **[KTU University Exam — Module 1, 14-mark Slot]**
> **(a) [7 marks]** With a neat sketch, derive the expression for the **critical fibre length** $l_c$ in a short-fibre composite, starting from a force balance on a fibre half. State clearly the assumptions made.
> **(b) [7 marks]** For an E-glass/polyester system, the following data are given: $E_f = 76$ GPa, $\sigma_{f,u} = 2.5$ GPa, $d = 15$ µm, $\tau_i = 30$ MPa, $E_m = 3.2$ GPa, $V_f = 0.30$, $L = 3$ mm. Determine the **length ratio** $L/l_c$ and comment on the failure mode. Using the modified rule of mixtures (assume 2-D random orientation, $\eta_l \approx 0.94$), calculate the composite modulus $E_c$.

#### Model Solution

**Part (a) — Derivation of $l_c$ (7 marks):**

- **Assumptions (2 marks):**
  1. Uniform interfacial shear stress $\tau_i$ along the fibre length.
  2. Fibre ends are stress-free (no end-loading).
  3. Fibre is straight, cylindrical, and perfectly bonded until debonding.
  4. Matrix is homogeneous and the fibre sits along the load axis (1-D case).

- **Free-body diagram of fibre half (1 mark):** A fibre of diameter $d$ and length $L/2$ with shear $\tau_i$ acting on the curved surface and an axial tensile stress $\sigma_f$ on the cut section.

- **Force balance (2 marks):**

$$\sigma_f \cdot \frac{\pi d^2}{4} = \tau_i \cdot \pi d \cdot \frac{L}{2}$$

- **Algebraic simplification (1 mark):**

$$\sigma_f = \frac{2 \tau_i L}{d}$$

- **Setting $\sigma_f = \sigma_{f,u}$ and solving for $L = l_c$ (1 mark):**

$$\boxed{\,l_c = \frac{\sigma_{f,u} \, d}{2 \tau_i}\,}$$

**Part (b) — Numerical (7 marks):**

- **Step 1 — Critical length (2 marks):**

$$l_c = \frac{2.5 \times 10^9 \times 15 \times 10^{-6}}{2 \times 30 \times 10^6} = 0.625 \text{ mm}$$

- **Step 2 — Length ratio (1 mark):**

$$\frac{L}{l_c} = \frac{3.0}{0.625} = 4.8$$

- **Step 3 — Failure-mode comment (1 mark):** Since $L/l_c \approx 4.8 > 1$, the fibre is in the **trans-critical regime**; failure is by a mix of fibre break and pull-out, with the fibre carrying a substantial fraction of its ultimate stress.

- **Step 4 — Modified rule of mixtures (2 marks):**

$$E_c = \eta_l \, \eta_o \, E_f V_f + E_m V_m = (0.94)(0.375)(76)(0.30) + (3.2)(0.70) \text{ [GPa]}$$

$$E_c = 8.04 + 2.24 = 10.28 \text{ GPa}$$

- **Step 5 — Interpretation (1 mark):** $E_c$ is ~3.2× the neat matrix modulus but only ~42 % of the ideal aligned continuous-fibre value (24.6 GPa) — the gap is the combined effect of sub-optimal length and 2-D random orientation.

---

### Question B (14 marks) — Internal Choice Alternative

> **[KTU University Exam — Module 1, 14-mark Slot, Alt Set]**
> **(a) [7 marks]** Discuss the **Cox shear-lag model** for short-fibre composites. Derive the length-efficiency factor $\eta_l$ and explain the physical significance of the parameter $\beta$.
> **(b) [7 marks]** An injection-moulded plate of chopped-carbon/PA66 has the following data: $E_f = 230$ GPa, $E_m = 3.0$ GPa, $V_f = 0.25$, fibre diameter $d = 7$ µm, $L = 6$ mm, $G_m = 1.0$ GPa, and $R/r = 8$. Calculate $\beta$, the argument $\beta L/2$, and the corresponding $\eta_l$. Hence estimate the composite modulus assuming 2-D random orientation.

#### Model Solution

**Part (a) — Cox shear-lag model (7 marks):**

- **Concept (2 marks):** Cox (1952) treats the matrix as a concentric cylinder around each fibre. Load is transferred from the surrounding matrix to the fibre purely by **interfacial shear**, and the fibre ends carry no load. This produces a hyperbolic-tangent-shaped fibre stress profile $\sigma_f(x)$.

- **Differential equation (1 mark):**

$$\frac{d^2 \sigma_f}{dx^2} = \beta^2 (\sigma_f - E_f \varepsilon_m^\infty)$$

- **Shear-lag parameter (2 marks):**

$$\beta = \frac{1}{r} \sqrt{\frac{G_m}{E_f \ln(R/r)}}$$

- **Solution with $\sigma_f(\pm L/2)=0$ (1 mark):**

$$\sigma_f(x) = E_f \varepsilon_m^\infty \left[ 1 - \frac{\cosh(\beta x)}{\cosh(\beta L/2)} \right]$$

- **Length efficiency factor (1 mark):**

$$\boxed{\,\eta_l = 1 - \frac{\tanh(\beta L/2)}{\beta L/2}\,}$$

**Part (b) — Numerical (7 marks):**

- **Step 1 — $\beta$ (2 marks):**

$$\beta = \frac{1}{3.5 \times 10^{-6}} \sqrt{\frac{1.0 \times 10^9}{230 \times 10^9 \times \ln 8}}$$

$$\beta = \frac{1}{3.5 \times 10^{-6}} \sqrt{\frac{1.0}{230 \times 2.0794}} = \frac{1}{3.5 \times 10^{-6}} \sqrt{0.002091}$$

$$\beta = \frac{1}{3.5 \times 10^{-6}} \times 0.04573 = 1.307 \times 10^{4} \text{ m}^{-1}$$

- **Step 2 — Argument (1 mark):**

$$\frac{\beta L}{2} = 1.307 \times 10^{4} \times 3.0 \times 10^{-3} = 39.2$$

- **Step 3 — $\eta_l$ (1 mark):** $\tanh(39.2) \approx 1.0000$

$$\eta_l = 1 - \frac{1}{39.2} = 0.9745$$

- **Step 4 — Composite modulus (2 marks):**

$$E_c = \eta_l \eta_o E_f V_f + E_m V_m = (0.9745)(0.375)(230)(0.25) + (3.0)(0.75) \text{ [GPa]}$$

$$E_c = 21.02 + 2.25 = 23.27 \text{ GPa}$$

- **Step 5 — Interpretation (1 mark):** Despite the short-fibre architecture, the high-modulus carbon (230 GPa) and long length-to-diameter ratio push $E_c$ to ~7.8× the matrix value, illustrating why short-carbon/PA66 is the workhorse of structural automotive underbody parts.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning & Common Pitfalls

> [!WARNING]
> **Top 6 places students lose marks on this topic in KTU 2024 scripts:**
>
> 1. **Confusing $\tau_i$ (interfacial shear) with $\tau_{m,y}$ (matrix yield shear).** $\tau_i$ is a *property of the interface* — for E-glass/polyester, it is around 30–50 MPa, *not* half the matrix tensile yield.
> 2. **Forgetting the $V_m$ term in the modified rule of mixtures.** Writing $E_c = \eta_l \eta_o E_f V_f$ alone is incomplete and loses 1 mark.
> 3. **Using $\eta_o = 1$ for random short-fibre composites.** The question must state the orientation; if not, *state your assumption explicitly* in the answer.
> 4. **Mixing up micro-scale $l_c$ with macro-scale effective length $l_t$.** They differ by a factor involving $\ln(R/r)$ — do not equate them.
> 5. **Not commenting on the failure mode** after computing $L/l_c$ — a 1-mark "comment" is almost always required.
> 6. **Skipping units or using mm/m inconsistently.** KTU penalises dimensionally inconsistent derivations by 0.5–1 mark.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Rapid-Revision Checklist

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Must-remember points before walking into the KTU exam hall:**
>
> - **Three length scales:** Nano (1–100 nm) → Micro (1–100 µm) → Macro (>1 mm). Micro-scale resolves fibre/matrix; macro-scale uses the RVE.
> - **Reinforcement classes:** Particles (0-D), short fibres (1-D, $L/d = 10$–1000), continuous fibres (1-D, $L/d > 10^4$), platelets (2-D).
> - **Critical fibre length:** $l_c = \sigma_{f,u} d / (2\tau_i)$. If $L > l_c$, fibre breaks; if $L < l_c$, fibre pulls out.
> - **Peak fibre stress from shear:** $\sigma_{f,\max} = 2\tau_i L / d$.
> - **Cox length-efficiency factor:** $\eta_l = 1 - \tanh(\beta L/2) / (\beta L/2)$.
> - **Shear-lag parameter:** $\beta = (1/r) \sqrt{G_m / (E_f \ln(R/r))}$.
> - **Orientation factors:** $\eta_o = 1$ (aligned), $3/8$ (2-D random), $1/5$ (3-D random).
> - **Modified rule of mixtures:** $E_c = \eta_l \eta_o E_f V_f + E_m (1 - V_f)$.
> - **Volume closure:** $V_f + V_m = 1$ — always invoked in any mixture-rule derivation.
> - **Micro-to-macro link:** The Cox + Krenchel efficiency factors *bridge* micro-scale stress build-up to the macro-scale composite modulus.
> - **Real-world short-fibre systems:** Glass/PA (automotive), carbon/PEEK (aerospace brackets), sisal/PP (panels).
> - **Failure-mode rule of thumb:** $L/l_c < 1$ → pull-out; $L/l_c \approx 1$–10 → mixed; $L/l_c > 10$ → break-dominated.
> - **Limiting behaviour:** As $L \to \infty$, $\eta_l \to 1$; as $L \to 0$, $\eta_l \to 0$ (must appear in any derivation).
> - **RVE definition:** The smallest volume over which property averaging is statistically representative — typically 10× the largest heterogeneity length.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Long Fibre and Particulate Fillers, Matrices - Thermoplastics, Thermosets and Rubbers
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEPOT522 - POLYMER NANOCOMPOSITES
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Long Fibre and Particulate Fillers, Matrices - Thermoplastics, Thermosets and Rubbers

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Polymer Composites: The Foundational Concept

A **polymer composite** is a multiphase solid material in which a **polymer matrix** (the continuous phase) is reinforced with one or more **filler/dispersed phases** (the discontinuous phase) to obtain a combination of properties that the individual constituents cannot achieve alone. When at least one of the dispersed phases has dimensions in the nanometre range (1–100 nm) in at least one direction, the resulting material is termed a **polymer nanocomposite (PNC)**.

In the context of KTU Module 1, we deal with the **building blocks** of any polymer composite system:

1. **The Filler (Reinforcement):** Provides the load-bearing capability, stiffness, dimensional stability, barrier properties, and/or functional behaviour.
2. **The Matrix:** Binds the reinforcement, transfers load, protects the filler from environmental attack, and dictates the processability of the system.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** The student must clearly distinguish between **macrocomposites**, **microcomposites**, and **nanocomposites** on the basis of filler dimensions. The same chemical (e.g., layered silicate clay) can produce a microcomposite or a nanocomposite depending on its state of dispersion in the matrix.

## 1.2 Classification of Fillers by Geometry

Fillers are broadly classified based on their **aspect ratio (L/D)** — the ratio of the longest dimension to the shortest dimension:

| Geometry Class | Typical Aspect Ratio (L/D) | Examples | Role in Composite |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Particulate (Zero-dimensional)** | ≈ 1 | CaCO₃, silica, carbon black, TiO₂, ZnO | Stiffness, cost reduction, opacity, conductivity |
| **Short Fibre / Whisker (One-dimensional)** | 10 – 1000 | Short glass fibre, cellulose nanocrystal (CNC) | Stiffness, strength, anisotropic reinforcement |
| **Long Fibre (One-dimensional, continuous or discontinuous, but long)** | > 1000 | Continuous glass fibre, carbon fibre tow, aramid fibre | Primary load-bearing; structural composites |
| **Platelet / Layered (Two-dimensional)** | 100 – 10,000 | Montmorillonite (MMT) clay, graphene, hexagonal BN | Barrier, stiffness, flame retardancy |

### 1.2.1 Long Fibre Fillers

**Long fibres** are reinforcing elements whose length is sufficiently large (typically **> 1 mm**, and in many engineering contexts tens of millimetres or continuous tows) such that the load transfer from the matrix to the fibre is dominated by **shear-lag** along the fibre–matrix interface. The key design parameter is the **critical fibre length (L_c)** — the minimum length required for the fibre to develop its ultimate tensile stress.

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical Fibre Length Equation (Halpin–Tsai / Kelly–Tyson model):**
> $$L_c = \frac{\sigma_{f,u} \cdot D}{2 \tau_i}$$
> where $\sigma_{f,u}$ = ultimate tensile strength of the fibre, $D$ = fibre diameter, and $\tau_i$ = interfacial shear strength.

**Common long fibre systems used in industry:**

- **E-glass fibre:** Density **2.54–2.62 g/cm³**, tensile strength **2.0–3.5 GPa**, Young's modulus **70–80 GPa**. Workhorse reinforcement for wind-turbine blades, boat hulls, pipes, and structural panels.
- **Carbon fibre (PAN-based):** Density **1.75–1.95 g/cm³**, tensile strength **3.0–7.0 GPa**, modulus **230–600 GPa**. Used in aerospace, sporting goods, pressure vessels.
- **Aramid fibre (Kevlar 49):** Density **1.44 g/cm³**, tensile strength **2.8–3.6 GPa**, modulus **70–130 GPa**. High toughness, ballistic applications.
- **Natural long fibres (flax, jute, hemp, sisal):** Density **1.2–1.5 g/cm³**, tensile strength **0.3–0.8 GPa**. Sustainable alternative; automotive interiors.

> [!NOTE]
> **Conceptual Analogy — Long Fibre Reinforcement:**
> Imagine you are trying to lift a heavy concrete slab. Concrete alone (the matrix) cracks easily under tension. If you embed long steel rebar (the long fibre) inside the concrete, the rebar carries the tensile load along its length, and the concrete protects the rebar and resists compression. **The longer and more continuous the rebar, the better the load-sharing.** This is the central idea of long-fibre reinforcement.

### 1.2.2 Particulate Fillers

**Particulates** are fillers with low aspect ratio (L/D ≈ 1). They are usually equiaxed (spherical, cubic, or irregular). Although they do not provide the dramatic strength gains of long fibres, they are indispensable in industry for:

- **Volume/cost reduction** (e.g., CaCO₃ in PVC pipes).
- **Stiffness enhancement** (Young's modulus rises with rigid filler content).
- **Dimensional stability & shrinkage control**.
- **Electrical/thermal conductivity** when conductive particulates like **carbon black**, **graphene nanoplatelets**, or **metallic powders** are added above a **percolation threshold**.
- **Optical effects** — TiO₂ (white), carbon black (black), ZnO (UV absorber).
- **Flame retardancy** — Al(OH)₃, Mg(OH)₂, huntite.

**Common particulate fillers:**

| Filler | Density (g/cm³) | Particle Size | Principal Function |
|---|---|---|---|
| Ground CaCO₃ | 2.7 | 0.5 – 50 µm | Cost reduction, stiffness |
| Precipitated CaCO₃ | 2.7 | 0.05 – 1 µm | Impact balance, opacity |
| Fumed silica | 2.2 | 5 – 50 nm | Thixotropy, reinforcement (silicone) |
| Carbon black (N330) | 1.8 | 20 – 50 nm | UV stability, conductivity, pigment |
| Titanium dioxide (rutile) | 4.2 | 0.2 – 0.5 µm | Whiteness, UV blocking |
| Aluminium trihydrate (ATH) | 2.4 | 1 – 15 µm | Flame retardant, smoke suppressant |
| Halloysite nanotubes (HNT) | 2.5 | 50 – 1000 nm lumen | Controlled release, mechanical boost |

> [!NOTE]
> **Conceptual Analogy — Particulate Fillers:**
> Think of a chocolate chip cookie. The dough (the polymer matrix) holds everything together, while the chocolate chips (particulates) add flavour, colour, and texture in every bite. You don't need them to be long like noodles; small, well-distributed chunks are enough to influence the properties of the whole. However, if the chips are not well bonded to the dough, they will fall out — this illustrates the importance of **interfacial adhesion** (sizing/coupling agents).

## 1.3 Classification of Matrices

The matrix is the **continuous phase** that surrounds and binds the reinforcement. The choice of matrix dictates the processing route, upper service temperature, chemical resistance, and toughness of the final composite. KTU 2024 Module 1 requires the student to be conversant with the three major families: **thermoplastics, thermosets, and rubbers**.

### 1.3.1 Thermoplastic Matrices

**Thermoplastics** are linear or branched polymers that **soften on heating and harden on cooling** without any chemical change. They are therefore **reprocessable, recyclable, and weldable**. The long-chain molecules are held together by secondary forces (van der Waals, dipole–dipole, entanglements) only.

| Thermoplastic | Glass Transition (T_g) | Melting Point (T_m) | Density (g/cm³) | Typical Use in Composites |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Polypropylene (PP) | –10 °C | 165 °C | 0.90 | Long-glass-fibre PP for automotive |
| Polyamide 6,6 (PA66) | 50 °C | 265 °C | 1.14 | Under-the-bonnet components |
| Polyetheretherketone (PEEK) | 143 °C | 343 °C | 1.30 | Aerospace thermoplastic composites |
| Polyphenylene sulphide (PPS) | 90 °C | 280 °C | 1.35 | Chemically resistant composites |
| Polyethylene (HDPE) | –120 °C | 130 °C | 0.95 | Wood-plastic composites |
| Polystyrene (PS) | 100 °C | amorphous | 1.05 | Lightweight syntactic foams |
| Polycarbonate (PC) | 150 °C | amorphous | 1.20 | Impact-modified, transparent panels |

**Advantages:** Toughness, recyclability, short processing cycles (injection moulding, extrusion), infinite shelf life, weldable.
**Limitations:** High melt viscosity makes fibre impregnation difficult; lower upper service temperature; poor creep resistance above T_g.

### 1.3.2 Thermoset Matrices

**Thermosets** undergo an irreversible **crosslinking (curing) reaction** during processing, forming a 3-D covalent network. Once cured, they cannot be remelted or reshaped. They are typically supplied as low-viscosity liquids or B-staged prepolymers, which makes fibre impregnation easy.

| Thermoset | Cure Mechanism | Density (g/cm³) | T_g (°C) | Typical Use in Composites |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Unsaturated polyester (UP) | Free-radical with styrene + MEKP | 1.20 – 1.30 | 70 – 130 | Boat hulls, FRP tanks |
| Vinyl ester (VE) | Free-radical | 1.15 – 1.25 | 100 – 150 | Chemical storage tanks |
| Epoxy (DGEBA) | Step-growth with amine/anhydride | 1.15 – 1.25 | 80 – 250 (formulation-dependent) | Aerospace primary structures |
| Phenolic (resole) | Condensation | 1.25 – 1.35 | > 250 | Ablative, fire-resistant panels |
| Polyurethane (PU) | Polyaddition | 1.05 – 1.20 | –50 to +120 | Flexible/rigid foams, RIM |
| Cyanate ester | Cyclotrimerization | 1.20 – 1.40 | 250 – 300 | Low-dielectric aerospace composites |
| Bismaleimide (BMI) | Addition | 1.25 – 1.35 | 250 – 320 | High-temperature aerospace |

**Advantages:** Low viscosity for fibre wet-out; high T_g; excellent creep resistance; good chemical resistance.
**Limitations:** Brittle, long cure cycles, not recyclable by remelting, exothermic cure can damage fibres.

### 1.3.3 Rubber Matrices (Elastomers)

**Rubbers (elastomers)** are a special class of lightly crosslinked polymers that exhibit **very large reversible elastic strain** (> 100 %, often 500–1000 %) due to their long, flexible chains above T_g and sparse crosslink density. In composites, rubbers are reinforced primarily with **fine particulates** such as **carbon black** or **silica** (the original "nanocomposite" of the tyre industry), and increasingly with **layered silicates, CNTs, or graphene**.

| Rubber | T_g (°C) | Principal Application |
|---|---|---|
| Natural rubber (NR) | –72 | Tyres, anti-vibration mounts |
| Styrene–butadiene rubber (SBR) | –55 | Tyre treads |
| Polybutadiene (BR) | –90 | Tyre sidewalls, impact modifiers |
| Ethylene–propylene diene monomer (EPDM) | –55 | Roofing, hoses, weather-strip |
| Nitrile rubber (NBR) | –30 | Oil seals, hoses |
| Silicone rubber (VMQ) | –120 | High-temperature seals, medical |
| Butyl rubber (IIR) | –67 | Inner tubes, pharmaceutical closures |

**Advantages:** Extreme flexibility, vibration damping, impact resistance.
**Limitations:** Poor tensile strength and stiffness when unfilled; require reinforcement and vulcanization.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Take-away Box — Matrix Selection Logic**
> - High-temperature structural part → **thermoset** (epoxy/BMI) or **high-T_g thermoplastic** (PEEK, PPS).
> - Mass-produced consumer part requiring recyclability → **thermoplastic** (PP, PA).
> - Flexible / damping / sealing → **rubber** (NR, EPDM) with particulate nanofiller.
> - Chemical resistance required → **vinyl ester, PPS, fluoropolymers**.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Why Long Fibres Outperform Particulates — Load Transfer Mechanism

The efficiency of a reinforcement depends on its ability to **carry tensile load transferred from the matrix through the interface**. The interfacial shear stress (τ) at the fibre–matrix boundary builds up the tensile stress in the fibre, from zero at the fibre ends to a maximum at the centre. The build-up length is the **stress-transfer length**, and twice this length is the **critical fibre length (L_c)**.

> Why is this important for KTU? Because it explains why, for a given fibre and matrix, a fibre shorter than L_c pulls out of the matrix (low strength), and a fibre much longer than L_c fractures rather than pulls out (high strength but lower toughness). This is the foundation of the **Kelly–Tyson theory** of fibre reinforcement.

### 2.1.1 The Shear-Lag Picture (Step Logic)

1. **Tensile load** is applied to the matrix.
2. **Matrix tries to deform**, but the stiff fibre resists.
3. The **interface (sizing/coupling agent)** transmits shear stress from the matrix to the fibre surface.
4. Tensile stress in the fibre **rises linearly from each end** to a plateau at the centre.
5. If fibre length $L < L_c$ → fibre pulls out → low composite strength.
6. If $L > L_c$ → fibre breaks → strength reaches fibre's ultimate value → **optimum reinforcement**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical length of common systems:**
> - E-glass / polyester: $L_c \approx 1$–$3$ mm (fibre dia. 10–20 µm).
> - Carbon fibre / epoxy: $L_c \approx 0.5$–$1$ mm.
> - Hence, fibres in most practical composites of millimetre length or longer are already "long enough" to develop near-ultimate strength.

## 2.2 The Rule of Mixtures — The Master Equation

For unidirectional long-fibre composites loaded parallel to the fibres, the **Rule of Mixtures (ROM)** gives closed-form expressions for the major elastic and strength properties.

| Property | Rule of Mixtures | Validity |
|---|---|---|
| Longitudinal Young's modulus, $E_c^{\parallel}$ | $E_c^{\parallel} = E_f V_f + E_m V_m$ | Upper bound (iso-strain, parallel) |
| Longitudinal tensile strength, $\sigma_{c,u}^{\parallel}$ | $\sigma_{c,u}^{\parallel} = \sigma_{f,u} V_f + \sigma_{m,u} V_m$ | When $L \gg L_c$ |
| Density, $\rho_c$ | $\rho_c = \rho_f V_f + \rho_m V_m$ | Exact |
| Transverse modulus, $E_c^{\perp}$ | $E_c^{\perp} = \left(\dfrac{V_f}{E_f} + \dfrac{V_m}{E_m}\right)^{-1}$ | Lower bound (iso-stress, series) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Notation:** $V_f$ + $V_m = 1$. Indices: $f$ = fibre, $m$ = matrix, $c$ = composite. Note the **asymmetry** between the parallel and transverse cases — the parallel arrangement (iso-strain) gives the upper bound, while the transverse arrangement (iso-stress) gives the lower bound. Real composites lie between Voigt and Reuss bounds.

### 2.2.1 Inverted Form for the Modulus of a Particulate Composite

For **particulate composites** with randomly distributed equiaxed fillers, a frequently used **inverse rule of mixtures** (or a geometric mean) is:

$$\frac{1}{E_c} = \frac{V_f}{E_f} + \frac{V_m}{E_m}$$

This treats the filler and matrix as **load-sharing in series**, appropriate when the filler is not oriented and is essentially spherical. The more accurate **Halpin–Tsai equation** for spherical fillers reduces approximately to this.

## 2.3 Critical Fibre Length — Derivation Logic

Force balance on a fibre being pulled out of a matrix cylinder of length x under interfacial shear stress $\tau_i$:

$$F_{\text{shear}} = \pi D \cdot x \cdot \tau_i$$

This shear force builds up a tensile stress $\sigma_f$ in the fibre cross-section $A_f = \pi D^2 / 4$:

$$\sigma_f \cdot A_f = \tau_i \cdot \pi D \cdot x$$

Solving for $x$ when $\sigma_f = \sigma_{f,u}$ gives $x = L_c/2$ (the transfer length is half the critical length):

$$\boxed{\;L_c = \frac{\sigma_{f,u} \cdot D}{2 \tau_i}\;}$$

A more useful **dimensionless** form is the **aspect ratio**:

$$\frac{L_c}{D} = \frac{\sigma_{f,u}}{2 \tau_i}$$

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Exam Cheat Sheet)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The table below compiles every equation you must know for Module 1 problems. **Memorize the boxed ones**; they appear in KTU university exam every year.

| # | Quantity | Formula | Units | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Volume fraction constraint | $V_f + V_m = 1$ | – | Always true |
| 2 | Weight–volume conversion | $V_f = \dfrac{w_f/\rho_f}{w_f/\rho_f + w_m/\rho_m}$ | – | $w$ = weight fraction |
| 3 | Critical fibre length | $L_c = \dfrac{\sigma_{f,u} D}{2 \tau_i}$ | m | Foundation equation |
| 4 | Critical aspect ratio | $(L_c/D) = \sigma_{f,u} / (2 \tau_i)$ | – | Dimensionless |
| 5 | Longitudinal modulus (ROM) | $E_c^{\parallel} = E_f V_f + E_m V_m$ | GPa | Voigt (iso-strain) |
| 6 | Transverse modulus (lower bound) | $E_c^{\perp} = \left(V_f/E_f + V_m/E_m\right)^{-1}$ | GPa | Reuss (iso-stress) |
| 7 | Composite density | $\rho_c = \rho_f V_f + \rho_m V_m$ | g/cm³ | Exact |
| 8 | Longitudinal strength (long fibre) | $\sigma_c^{\parallel} = \sigma_{f,u} V_f + \sigma_{m,u} V_m$ | MPa | If $L > L_c$ |
| 9 | Halpin–Tsai modulus | $E_c = E_m \cdot \dfrac{1 + 2(L/D)\eta V_f}{1 - \eta V_f}, \quad \eta = \dfrac{E_f/E_m - 1}{E_f/E_m + 2(L/D)}$ | GPa | Short-fibre / particulate limit |
| 10 | Stress concentration in matrix | $\sigma_m = \dfrac{\sigma_c - \sigma_f V_f}{V_m}$ | MPa | Used in micromechanics |
| 11 | Specific modulus | $E_c / \rho_c$ | GPa·cm³/g | Used in aerospace trade-off |
| 12 | Percolation (conductivity) | $\sigma_c = \sigma_f (V_f - V_p)^t$ | S/m | $V_p$ = percolation threshold, $t$ = critical exponent |

> **Engineering Utility of these Equations:**
> - Aerospace engineer uses **specific modulus** to design minimum-weight wings.
> - Civil engineer uses **ROM longitudinal strength** to size a pultruded rebar.
> - Tyre engineer uses **percolation** to balance conductivity and rolling resistance.
> - Injection-moulding engineer uses **critical aspect ratio** to decide on long-fibre pellets vs. short-fibre pellets.

## 2.5 Why Thermoplastic vs Thermoset vs Rubber — A Property-Process Trade-off

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The KTU 2024 examiner expects students to justify the **matrix choice** for a given application. The following comparative framework must be memorized.

| Property | Thermoplastic | Thermoset | Rubber |
|---|---|---|---|
| State during processing | Molten | Low-viscosity liquid (cures in mould) | Compounded dough, then vulcanized |
| Processing cycle | Seconds (injection) to minutes (compression) | Minutes to hours | Minutes to tens of minutes |
| Recyclability | Yes (re-melt) | No (chemical network) | No (crosslinked) |
| Upper service T | $T_g$ to $T_m$ (often $< 200$ °C) | Near $T_g$ (often 150 – 300 °C) | $T_g$ to vulcanization T |
| Toughness | High | Brittle | Very high (elastic) |
| Fibre impregnation | Difficult (high melt viscosity) | Easy (low viscosity) | Easy (calendering / solution) |
| Typical composite use | Long-fibre thermoplastic pellets (LFT), GMT | Prepregs, RTM, pultrusion, filament winding | Tyres, conveyor belts, hoses |

## 2.6 Interfacial Adhesion — The Often-Forgotten Third Element

The matrix and the filler alone are not enough. **The interface** — a nanoscale region of a few nanometres thickness — is the third constituent. It is engineered using:

- **Sizings** on glass/carbon fibres (silane, epoxy, polyurethane).
- **Coupling agents** for particulates (e.g., stearic acid on CaCO₃, titanates on fillers).
- **Surface functionalization** of CNTs/graphene (carboxyl, amine, silane).
- **Reactive blending** in thermoplastics (maleic anhydride-grafted PP bonds to glass).

> A composite is only as strong as its weakest interface. This is a favourite KTU viva question.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Rule of Mixtures for Longitudinal Modulus (Voigt Model)

> **Given:** A unidirectional long-fibre composite, fibres aligned with the loading axis. Both matrix and fibre experience the **same strain** $\varepsilon$ (iso-strain assumption). Volume fractions $V_f$ and $V_m$ with $V_f + V_m = 1$.

**Step 1 — Set up the iso-strain condition:**

$$\varepsilon_c = \varepsilon_f = \varepsilon_m = \varepsilon$$

**Step 2 — Apply Hooke's law to each phase:**

$$\sigma_f = E_f \varepsilon \qquad \sigma_m = E_m \varepsilon$$

**Step 3 — Total load on the composite cross-section is the sum of the loads carried by each phase (force equilibrium):**

$$F_c = F_f + F_m$$

Dividing by the total area $A_c$:

$$\sigma_c A_c = \sigma_f A_f + \sigma_m A_m$$

$$\sigma_c = \sigma_f \frac{A_f}{A_c} + \sigma_m \frac{A_m}{A_c}$$

But the **area fractions equal the volume fractions** for a prismatic representative volume element (RVE):

$$\frac{A_f}{A_c} = V_f \quad ; \quad \frac{A_m}{A_c} = V_m$$

So:

$$\sigma_c = \sigma_f V_f + \sigma_m V_m$$

**Step 4 — Substitute $\sigma_f = E_f \varepsilon$ and $\sigma_m = E_m \varepsilon$:**

$$\sigma_c = E_f \varepsilon V_f + E_m \varepsilon V_m = \varepsilon (E_f V_f + E_m V_m)$$

**Step 5 — Divide by $\varepsilon$ to get composite modulus:**

$$\boxed{\;E_c^{\parallel} = E_f V_f + E_m V_m\;}$$

This is the **Voigt upper bound** of the longitudinal modulus, valid for continuous, aligned fibres.

> **Real-world utility:** This is the equation used by aircraft designers to size composite laminates; the predicted modulus is within a few per cent of experimental values for $V_f > 0.4$ with well-bonded unidirectional carbon/epoxy.

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Inverse Rule of Mixtures for Transverse Modulus (Reuss Model)

> **Given:** Loading is **perpendicular** to the fibres; the matrix and fibre experience the **same stress** (iso-stress assumption).

**Step 1 — Iso-stress condition:**

$$\sigma_c = \sigma_f = \sigma_m = \sigma$$

**Step 2 — Total strain is the volume-weighted sum of strains in each phase (compatibility):**

$$\varepsilon_c = \varepsilon_f V_f + \varepsilon_m V_m$$

**Step 3 — Apply Hooke's law:**

$$\varepsilon_f = \dfrac{\sigma}{E_f} \qquad \varepsilon_m = \dfrac{\sigma}{E_m} \qquad \varepsilon_c = \dfrac{\sigma}{E_c^{\perp}}$$

**Step 4 — Substitute:**

$$\dfrac{\sigma}{E_c^{\perp}} = \dfrac{\sigma}{E_f} V_f + \dfrac{\sigma}{E_m} V_m$$

**Step 5 — Cancel $\sigma$ and invert:**

$$\boxed{\;\frac{1}{E_c^{\perp}} = \frac{V_f}{E_f} + \frac{V_m}{E_m}\;}$$

or equivalently:

$$E_c^{\perp} = \left( \frac{V_f}{E_f} + \frac{V_m}{E_m} \right)^{-1}$$

This is the **Reuss lower bound** for transverse modulus.

> **Why two different equations?** Because in one case the phases share strain (parallel spring model) and in the other they share stress (series spring model). Real composites always lie between the two bounds, and the **Halpin–Tsai semi-empirical equation** interpolates between them using the filler aspect ratio.

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Critical Fibre Length (Kelly–Tyson)

> **Given:** A single fibre of diameter $D$ embedded in a matrix. The matrix transmits a constant interfacial shear stress $\tau_i$ to the fibre along its length.

**Step 1 — Free-body diagram.** Consider a small element of fibre of length $x$ from one of its ends. The matrix exerts a shear stress $\tau_i$ on the cylindrical fibre surface of area $\pi D x$:

$$F_{\text{transferred}} = \pi D x \tau_i$$

**Step 2 — This transferred force is balanced by the tensile force in the fibre cross-section of area $A_f = \pi D^2 / 4$:**

$$\sigma_f(x) \cdot \frac{\pi D^2}{4} = \pi D x \tau_i$$

**Step 3 — Solve for the tensile stress in the fibre as a function of $x$:**

$$\sigma_f(x) = \frac{4 \tau_i x}{D}$$

The fibre stress **rises linearly** from $0$ at the end to a maximum at the centre.

**Step 4 — The critical length $L_c$ is defined as the length for which the **maximum** stress at the centre equals the ultimate fibre strength $\sigma_{f,u}$. Setting $x = L_c / 2$ (since the build-up occurs from each end):**

$$\sigma_{f,u} = \frac{4 \tau_i (L_c / 2)}{D} = \frac{2 \tau_i L_c}{D}$$

**Step 5 — Solve for $L_c$:**

$$\boxed{\;L_c = \frac{\sigma_{f,u} \cdot D}{2 \tau_i}\;}$$

**Step 6 — Critical aspect ratio (dimensionless form):**

$$\boxed{\;\frac{L_c}{D} = \frac{\sigma_{f,u}}{2 \tau_i}\;}$$

> **Numerical example (typical for KTU problems):**
> An E-glass fibre has $\sigma_{f,u} = 2.5$ GPa, $D = 17$ µm, and is bonded to polyester resin with $\tau_i = 30$ MPa.
> $$L_c = \frac{2.5 \times 10^9 \times 17 \times 10^{-6}}{2 \times 30 \times 10^6} = \frac{42.5}{60} = 0.708 \text{ mm}$$
> So any glass fibre longer than about **0.7 mm** in polyester will develop its full strength — a key reason chopped strand mats (lengths 25–50 mm) are highly effective.

## 3.4 Derivation 4: Density of a Composite

> **Given:** A composite consisting of fibre and matrix of densities $\rho_f$ and $\rho_m$ with volume fractions $V_f$ and $V_m$.

**Step 1 — Define mass of each phase in a representative volume $V_c$:**

$$m_f = \rho_f V_f V_c \quad ; \quad m_m = \rho_m V_m V_c$$

**Step 2 — Total mass is the sum:**

$$m_c = m_f + m_m = (\rho_f V_f + \rho_m V_m) V_c$$

**Step 3 — Composite density is mass divided by volume:**

$$\rho_c = \frac{m_c}{V_c} = \rho_f V_f + \rho_m V_m$$

$$\boxed{\;\rho_c = \rho_f V_f + \rho_m V_m\;}$$

> **Note:** This equation is **exact** (not an upper/lower bound) because mass is an extensive property that adds directly.

## 3.5 Derivation 5: Conversion Between Weight Fraction and Volume Fraction

> Often the composite formulation is given as **weight fraction** $w_f$, but the rule of mixtures requires **volume fraction** $V_f$. Conversion is essential.

**Step 1 — In a unit mass of composite:**

$$V_f = \frac{m_f / \rho_f}{m_c / \rho_c} \quad ; \quad V_m = \frac{m_m / \rho_m}{m_c / \rho_c}$$

**Step 2 — Using $m_c = m_f + m_m$ and $w_f = m_f / m_c$:**

$$V_f = \frac{w_f / \rho_f}{w_f / \rho_f + (1 - w_f)/\rho_m}$$

$$\boxed{\;V_f = \frac{w_f \rho_m}{w_f \rho_m + (1 - w_f) \rho_f}\;}$$

Similarly:

$$V_m = \frac{(1 - w_f) \rho_f}{w_f \rho_m + (1 - w_f) \rho_f}$$

> **Worked numerical example:**
> A composite contains 60 wt % E-glass ($\rho_f = 2.55$ g/cm³) in epoxy ($\rho_m = 1.20$ g/cm³). Find $V_f$.
> $$V_f = \frac{0.60 \times 1.20}{0.60 \times 1.20 + 0.40 \times 2.55} = \frac{0.720}{1.740} = 0.4137$$
> So **41.4 vol % glass** is present. Note that **glass is heavier per volume than the resin**, so a 60 wt % loading corresponds to a lower volume fraction.

## 3.6 Derivation 6: Stress in the Matrix for a Given Composite Load

> **Given:** A unidirectional composite under longitudinal tensile load $\sigma_c$.

From the iso-strain condition:

$$\sigma_f = E_f \varepsilon_c \quad ; \quad \sigma_m = E_m \varepsilon_c$$

We have:

$$\sigma_c = \sigma_f V_f + \sigma_m V_m$$

Solving for $\sigma_m$:

$$\sigma_m = \frac{\sigma_c - \sigma_f V_f}{V_m}$$

Using $\sigma_f = \sigma_m (E_f / E_m)$ from equal strain:

$$\sigma_m \left( V_m + V_f \frac{E_f}{E_m} \right) = \sigma_c$$

$$\boxed{\;\sigma_m = \frac{\sigma_c}{V_m + V_f (E_f / E_m)}\;}$$

> This shows that for a stiff fibre ($E_f \gg E_m$) and high $V_f$, the **matrix stress $\sigma_m$ is much smaller than the composite stress $\sigma_c$** — most of the load is carried by the fibre, justifying the use of long fibres in load-bearing applications.

## 3.7 Analytical Master Matrix — Comparison of the Three Matrix Families

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following analytical matrix is the "exam-day summary" of the entire module. It is exactly the type of comparison a KTU examiner expects you to draw in a 14-mark question.

| Property / Process | Thermoplastic (e.g., PP) | Thermoset (e.g., epoxy) | Rubber (e.g., NR) |
|---|---|---|---|
| Molecular architecture | Linear/branched chains | 3-D covalent network | Lightly crosslinked long chains |
| Bond type | Secondary | Covalent (crosslink) | Covalent (sparse) |
| State at processing | Molten (viscous) | Liquid (reactive) | Doughy compound |
| Effect of heat | Softens → re-solidifies | Degrades (cannot remelt) | Softens above T_g / vulcanization |
| Recyclability | Excellent | Poor (down-cycling only) | Poor (ground into powder) |
| Upper service T | $\sim 100$–$250$ °C | $\sim 150$–$300$ °C | $\sim 80$–$200$ °C |
| Modulus (unfilled) | 1–3 GPa | 2–5 GPa | 0.001–0.1 GPa |
| Strain at break | 5–500 % | 1–5 % | 100–1000 % |
| Composite role | Tough, recyclable structural parts | High-T structural parts | Damping, sealing, elastic components |
| Filler type typically used | Long/short glass, talc, CNT | Carbon/glass fibre, silica, clay | Carbon black, silica, clay, CNT |
| Process examples | Injection moulding, extrusion, GMT | RTM, pultrusion, filament winding, prepreg | Calendering, extrusion, compression moulding |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block Diagram — Composite System Architecture

The following Mermaid block diagram maps how the **matrix**, **filler**, **interface**, and **processing route** interact to produce the final composite properties. This is the KTU-recommended way to visualize the system in a long-answer question.

```mermaid
    nodeB --> nodeF[Thermoset Matrix]
    nodeB --> nodeG[Rubber Matrix]

    nodeF --> nodeH
    nodeG --> nodeH
    nodeH --> nodeI[Processing Route]
    nodeI --> nodeJ[Injection / Extrusion]
    nodeI --> nodeK[RTM / Pultrusion / Filament Winding]

    nodeL --> nodeM
    nodeM --> nodeN[Mechanical Strength]

```

```mermaid

    nodeR[Polymer Composite Constituents] --> nodeS[Filler by Geometry]
    nodeR --> nodeT[Matrix by Chemistry]

    nodeS --> nodeV[Short Fibre Aspect Ratio 10 to 1000]
    nodeS --> nodeW[Particulate Aspect Ratio near 1]
    nodeS --> nodeX[Platelet Aspect Ratio 100 to 10000]
    nodeU --> nodeY[Glass Carbon Aramid Natural]
    nodeW --> nodeZ[CaCO3 Silica Carbon Black TiO2 Clay]
    nodeT --> nodeAA[Thermoplastic]

    nodeAB --> nodeAE[Epoxy Polyester Vinylester Phenolic BMI]
    nodeAC --> nodeAF[NR SBR EPDM NBR Silicone]
```

## 4.3 Stress Transfer Schematic — Long Fibre vs Particulate

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    nodeAG[Long Fibre L greater than Lc] --> nodeAH[Stress builds up from both ends]
    nodeAH --> nodeAI[Centre region reaches sigma fu]
    nodeAI --> nodeAJ[Fibre carries majority of load]
    nodeAJ --> nodeAK[High strength high modulus]
    nodeAL[Particulate Aspect Ratio near 1] --> nodeAM[No preferred load path]
    nodeAM --> nodeAN[Stress distributed in matrix]
    nodeAN --> nodeAO[Stiffness gain only]
    nodeAK -.compare.-> nodeAO
```

## 4.4 Process-to-Property Mapping Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodeAP[Thermoplastic plus Long Fibre] --> nodeAQ[Injection Moulding LFT GMT]
    nodeAP --> nodeAR[High Throughput Recyclable]

    nodeAS --> nodeAU[High T High Strength]
    nodeAV[Rubber plus Particulate] --> nodeAW[Calendering Compression Moulding]
    nodeAV --> nodeAX[Elastic Tough Conductive]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **How to use these diagrams in your KTU answer script:**
> 1. **Always draw the block diagram first** when a 14-mark question asks to "explain the constituents of a polymer composite" — it earns you **2–3 easy marks** for visual presentation.
> 2. **Use the classification tree** in a "compare and contrast" type question.
> 3. **The process-to-property map** is the perfect closer for a "selection of matrix for a given application" question.

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1. `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, May 2024]`
**Differentiate between long fibre and particulate fillers in polymer composites. Give two examples of each.**

**Model Answer (≈ 3 marks, ~120 words):**

| Feature | Long Fibre Filler | Particulate Filler |
|---|---|---|
| Aspect ratio (L/D) | High, typically > 1000 | Low, approximately 1 |
| Geometry | Continuous or discontinuous slender cylinders | Equiaxed (spherical/cubic/irregular) |
| Stress transfer | Shear-lag along interface, very efficient | Distributed in matrix, less efficient |
| Main property gain | Tensile strength, modulus (anisotropic) | Stiffness, cost reduction, isotropic |
| Typical examples | E-glass fibre, carbon fibre, aramid fibre, flax fibre | CaCO₃, silica, carbon black, TiO₂, clay |

> Long fibres carry most of the tensile load along their axis, while particulates mainly improve stiffness and reduce cost without contributing to tensile strength.

---

### Question 2. `[KTU University Exam – May 2023]`
**Compare thermoplastic and thermoset matrices used in polymer composites.**

**Model Answer (≈ 3 marks, ~120 words):**

- **Thermoplastic matrices** (e.g., PP, PA, PEEK) are linear/branched polymers that **soften on heating and harden on cooling**. They are **recyclable, tough, and have short processing cycles** by injection moulding, but have high melt viscosity that makes fibre impregnation difficult.
- **Thermoset matrices** (e.g., epoxy, polyester, vinyl ester) undergo **irreversible crosslinking** during cure to form a 3-D covalent network. They have **low viscosity for easy fibre wet-out, high T_g, and excellent creep resistance**, but are **brittle, non-recyclable, and require long cure cycles.**
- Selection is based on application: thermoplastics for high-throughput, recyclable parts; thermosets for high-performance, high-temperature structural parts.

---

## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Module Internal Choice)

### Question A (Choice 1) — `[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024]`

**(a)** With the help of a suitable diagram, explain the **mechanism of load transfer from the matrix to a long fibre**. Derive the expression for **critical fibre length** and discuss its significance. (7 marks)

**(b)** A unidirectional glass-fibre reinforced epoxy composite contains **55 vol % fibres**. Given $E_f = 72$ GPa, $E_m = 3.5$ GPa, $\sigma_{f,u} = 2.4$ GPa, $\sigma_{m,u} = 65$ MPa and $D = 17$ µm, $\tau_i = 35$ MPa, calculate:
- (i) Longitudinal Young's modulus of the composite.
- (ii) Longitudinal tensile strength of the composite.
- (iii) Critical fibre length.
- (iv) Density of the composite (take $\rho_f = 2.55$ g/cm³, $\rho_m = 1.20$ g/cm³). (7 marks)

#### Model Solution

**(a) Mechanism of load transfer and critical fibre length:**

When a tensile load is applied to a composite, the matrix tends to deform more than the stiff fibre. The differential deformation is accommodated by **interfacial shear stress** $\tau_i$ acting on the cylindrical fibre surface. The fibre tensile stress builds up **linearly from zero at each fibre end** to a maximum at the centre.

```
    Fibre     σ_f(u)
       ___________________
      /|                  |\
     / |                  | \    <-- stress distribution along fibre
    /  |                  |  \
   /   |                  |   \   _________
  /    |                  |    \ /         \
 |_____|__________________|_____|___________|
 0   L_c/2              L_c/2  L_c    x -->
       |<--- Transfer length --->|
       |<------- L_c ------------>|
```

Force balance on a small fibre element of length $x$ from one end gives:

$$\sigma_f(x) \cdot \frac{\pi D^2}{4} = \pi D \cdot x \cdot \tau_i \quad\Rightarrow\quad \sigma_f(x) = \frac{4 \tau_i x}{D}$$

The **critical fibre length** is defined as the length at which the maximum stress (at the centre) equals the ultimate fibre strength. Since the build-up occurs from both ends, the critical length is twice the transfer length:

$$\sigma_{f,u} = \frac{4 \tau_i (L_c/2)}{D} = \frac{2 \tau_i L_c}{D}$$

$$\boxed{\;L_c = \frac{\sigma_{f,u} \cdot D}{2 \tau_i}\;}$$

**Significance:**
- If $L < L_c$ → fibre pulls out → low strength.
- If $L \gg L_c$ → fibre fractures before pull-out → high strength (optimum).
- Defines the **minimum fibre length** required for full reinforcement.
- Guides the design of chopped-fibre composites (lengths are chosen >> $L_c$).

**(b) Numerical computation:**

**(i) Longitudinal Young's modulus (Voigt rule of mixtures):**

$$E_c^{\parallel} = E_f V_f + E_m V_m = (72)(0.55) + (3.5)(0.45)$$

$$E_c^{\parallel} = 39.60 + 1.575 = 41.18 \text{ GPa}$$

**(ii) Longitudinal tensile strength:**

$$\sigma_{c,u}^{\parallel} = \sigma_{f,u} V_f + \sigma_{m,u} V_m = (2400)(0.55) + (65)(0.45)$$

$$\sigma_{c,u}^{\parallel} = 1320 + 29.25 = 1349.25 \text{ MPa} \approx 1.35 \text{ GPa}$$

**(iii) Critical fibre length:**

$$L_c = \frac{\sigma_{f,u} \cdot D}{2 \tau_i} = \frac{(2.4 \times 10^9)(17 \times 10^{-6})}{2 (35 \times 10^6)}$$

$$L_c = \frac{40.8}{70} = 0.583 \text{ mm}$$

**(iv) Composite density:**

$$\rho_c = \rho_f V_f + \rho_m V_m = (2.55)(0.55) + (1.20)(0.45)$$

$$\rho_c = 1.4025 + 0.540 = 1.94 \text{ g/cm}^3$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Part (b):**
> 1. **Do not confuse MPa and GPa** — convert all values to consistent units before plugging in. A common mistake is to put 2.4 GPa in as 2.4 in the MPa equation and get $L_c$ wrong by three orders of magnitude.
> 2. **Always state the rule of mixtures used** (Voigt / Reuss / Halpin–Tsai) and the assumption (iso-strain/iso-stress). Examiners award 1 mark just for naming the model.
> 3. In **Part (a)**, the diagram is worth at least 1 mark. Skipping it loses you a free mark.

---

### Question B (Choice 2) — `[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024]`

**(a)** Compare the three classes of polymer matrices — **thermoplastics, thermosets, and rubbers** — with respect to their molecular structure, processing behaviour, recyclability, and typical composite applications. (7 marks)

**(b)** With neat sketches, describe the following particulate fillers used in polymer composites and state one key property of each: (i) **Calcium carbonate**, (ii) **Carbon black**, (iii) **Fumed silica**, (iv) **Halloysite nanotube**. (7 marks)

#### Model Solution

**(a) Comparison of polymer matrices:**

| Criterion | Thermoplastic | Thermoset | Rubber |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Molecular structure** | Linear/branched chains held by secondary forces; no crosslinks | 3-D covalent network formed by crosslinking; irreversible | Long flexible chains lightly crosslinked (sparse network) |
| **Processing state** | Molten (viscous liquid above $T_m$) | Low-viscosity liquid resin that cures in the mould | Doughy compound that is vulcanized under heat and pressure |
| **Effect of heat** | Softens reversibly | Decomposes — does **not** melt | Softens above $T_g$ (decomposes if over-cured) |
| **Recyclability** | Excellent (re-melt and reprocess) | Poor (cannot remelt; can be ground as filler) | Poor (reclaimed as crumb rubber) |
| **Typical processing** | Injection moulding, extrusion, GMT, thermoforming | RTM, pultrusion, filament winding, prepreg lay-up, compression moulding | Calendering, extrusion, compression moulding |
| **Toughness** | High | Brittle | Very high (large elastic strain) |
| **Service temperature** | $T_g$–$T_m$ (often $< 200$ °C) | Near $T_g$ (often 150–300 °C) | $T_g$ to ~80 °C for general-purpose rubbers |
| **Composite application** | Automotive LFT, consumer goods, recyclable panels | Aerospace primary structure, wind blades, marine | Tyres, conveyor belts, hoses, dampers |

**(b) Particulate fillers (with sketches and key properties):**

**(i) Calcium carbonate (CaCO₃):**
- Sketches: ground (irregular rhombohedral) vs precipitated (cubic scalenohedral).
- Key property: **Cost reduction and stiffness**; widely used in PVC pipes, polyolefins.

**(ii) Carbon black (e.g., N330, N550):**
- Sketch: aggregate of fused spherical primary particles (10–50 nm), forming a "grape-cluster" structure.
- Key property: **Reinforcement of rubber** (tyre treads), UV protection, and electrical conductivity above percolation threshold.

**(iii) Fumed silica (pyrogenic silica):**
- Sketch: branched nano-aggregates (5–50 nm primary particles) with hydroxyl surface groups.
- Key property: **Thixotropy and reinforcement** of silicone rubber, paints, and adhesives; thickening agent.

**(iv) Halloysite nanotube (HNT, Al₂Si₂O₅(OH)₄·nH₂O):**
- Sketch: hollow tubular structure (50–1000 nm long, 15–100 nm lumen diameter).
- Key property: **Tubular nano-container for controlled release** of actives (anticorrosion, antimicrobial) and mechanical reinforcement; naturally occurring nanomaterial.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Part (b):**
> 1. **Do not list fillers without a sketch.** In a 7-mark question on particulate fillers, the examiner awards 0.5–1 mark per sketch. **Always include neat labelled diagrams.**
> 2. **Do not write generic descriptions.** Each filler must have a **specific key property** (cost, conductivity, thixotropy, controlled release) — generic "improves properties" gets no marks.
> 3. **For Part (a), the comparison table is worth 4 marks; the surrounding explanation is worth 3 marks.** Do not write 14 paragraphs of text — present the table first, then add a short narrative.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Module 1 — Rapid Revision Checklist (read this the night before the exam):**

### A. Long Fibre Fillers
- ☑ Aspect ratio > 1000; load transfer by shear-lag; critical fibre length $L_c = \sigma_{f,u} D / (2 \tau_i)$.
- ☑ Examples: E-glass, carbon (PAN), aramid, basalt, natural (flax, jute, hemp).
- ☑ Rule of mixtures applies only for $L \gg L_c$ (continuous fibre limit).
- ☑ In practice, $L_c$ for glass-polyester ≈ 0.5–3 mm; carbon-epoxy ≈ 0.3–1 mm.

### B. Particulate Fillers
- ☑ Aspect ratio ≈ 1; equiaxed; stiffness gain only (no significant tensile strength).
- ☑ Examples: CaCO₃ (cost), TiO₂ (white pigment), carbon black (UV, conductivity), fumed silica (thixotropy), ATH (flame retardant).
- ☑ Nanoparticulates (5–100 nm) can approach nano-reinforcement effects (e.g., carbon black in rubber).

### C. Thermoplastic Matrices
- ☑ Linear/branched, recyclable, re-meltable, processed by injection/extrusion.
- ☑ Examples: PP, HDPE, PA, PEEK, PPS, PC.
- ☑ Limitation: high melt viscosity → fibre impregnation difficult → use long-fibre pellets (LFT), GMT, commingled fabrics.

### D. Thermoset Matrices
- ☑ Crosslinked, irreversible, low-viscosity liquid during processing, brittle, not remeltable.
- ☑ Examples: epoxy (high performance), polyester (FRP), vinyl ester (chemical resistance), phenolic (fire/ablative), BMI, cyanate ester.
- ☑ Processed by RTM, pultrusion, filament winding, prepreg lay-up.

### E. Rubber Matrices
- ☑ Lightly crosslinked, very high elastic strain, low modulus.
- ☑ Reinforced with carbon black, silica, layered silicates, CNTs.
- ☑ Examples: NR, SBR, BR, EPDM, IIR, NBR, silicone.
- ☑ The original "nanocomposite" of the tyre industry is carbon-black-reinforced natural rubber.

### F. Master Equations to Memorize
- ☑ $V_f + V_m = 1$ and weight-volume conversion.
- ☑ $L_c = \sigma_{f,u} D / (2 \tau_i)$.
- ☑ Voigt: $E_c^{\parallel} = E_f V_f + E_m V_m$.
- ☑ Reuss: $1/E_c^{\perp} = V_f/E_f + V_m/E_m$.
- ☑ $\rho_c = \rho_f V_f + \rho_m V_m$.
- ☑ $\sigma_{c}^{\parallel} = \sigma_{f,u} V_f + \sigma_{m,u} V_m$ (long-fibre case).

### G. Comparative Statements to Use in 14-Mark Answers
- ☑ "Long fibres carry load along their axis; particulates stiffen isotropically."
- ☑ "Thermoplastics are recyclable but viscous; thermosets have low viscosity but are brittle and non-recyclable."
- ☑ "Rubbers require vulcanization and are always reinforced with nano-to-micro particulates."
- ☑ "The critical fibre length is the bridge between material properties and composite design."

### H. Application-Matrix Linkage (for design-type questions)
- ☑ Aerospace structural parts → carbon-fibre / epoxy (thermoset).
- ☑ Wind-turbine blades → E-glass / epoxy or vinyl ester.
- ☑ Automotive bumper beam → long-glass-fibre / PP (thermoplastic).
- ☑ Tyre tread → SBR/BR + carbon black + silica (rubber).
- ☑ Pipe and tank → glass-fibre / polyester (thermoset).
- ☑ Reusable consumer products → short-fibre / PP, PA (thermoplastic).

> **Final exam-day tip:** Always **draw the block diagram first** in any 14-mark question. It anchors your answer, gives the examiner an immediate visual map, and earns you easy presentation marks. Pair every equation with a one-line statement of its **assumption and validity** — this is what differentiates a 12-mark answer from a 14-mark one.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Nano and Micro Composites, Short and Long Fibre Composites - Treatment of Reinforcements
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEPOT522 - POLYMER NANOCOMPOSITES
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Nano and Micro Composites, Short and Long Fibre Composites - Treatment of Reinforcements

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What Are Polymer Composites?

A **Polymer Composite** is a heterogeneous, multi-phase material formed by combining a **polymer matrix** (the continuous phase) with a **reinforcement** (the dispersed phase) to produce a material with properties superior to those of the individual constituents. The reinforcements are typically fibers, particulates, or nanofillers, and the polymer binds them into a coherent structural unit.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Definition Snapshot:**
> A polymer composite is an engineered material consisting of two or more distinct phases — a **matrix phase** (polymer resin) and a **reinforcement phase** (fiber/particle/nanofiller) — combined at the macroscopic level to yield a synergistic combination of properties unattainable by the individual components.

---

## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy: "Concrete and Steel Rebar"

Think of a polymer composite exactly like **reinforced concrete**:
- The **concrete (matrix)** is strong in compression but weak in tension and brittle.
- The **steel rebar (reinforcement)** carries the tensile load.
- Together, they form a material far stronger than either alone.

In a polymer composite:
- The **polymer matrix** (epoxy, polyester, polypropylene) holds the system together, transfers load to the reinforcement, and protects it from environmental damage.
- The **reinforcement** (glass, carbon, aramid fibers, or nanoparticles) carries most of the mechanical load.

> [!NOTE]
> **Core Idea:** The matrix is the "delivery system" and the reinforcement is the "muscle." Neither is useful alone — but together, they synergize.

---

## 1.3 Nano Composites vs. Micro Composites

### 1.3.1 Polymer Nano Composites (PNCs)

**Definition:** Polymer nanocomposites are polymer matrices reinforced with fillers having at least **one dimension in the nanometer range (1–100 nm)**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Definition:**
> Polymer nanocomposites are a class of hybrid materials in which nanoscale fillers (nanoclays, carbon nanotubes, graphene, nano-silica) are dispersed within a polymer matrix to achieve dramatic improvements in mechanical, thermal, barrier, and electrical properties at very low filler loadings (typically **1–5 wt%**).

**Common Nano-fillers:**
- **1D Nanofillers:** Carbon Nanotubes (CNTs), cellulose nanocrystals
- **2D Nanofillers:** Nanoclay (montmorillonite), Graphene, MXene
- **3D Nanofillers:** Nano-silica (SiO₂), nano-alumina (Al₂O₃), quantum dots

**Conceptual Analogy — "Spice in a Curry":**
Imagine adding a tiny pinch of a powerful spice (nano-filler) to a curry (polymer matrix). Even at 1–2% by weight, the spice completely transforms the flavor, color, and aroma. Similarly, nanofillers at low loadings cause **massive property jumps** because of their enormous surface-area-to-volume ratio.

---

### 1.3.2 Polymer Micro Composites

**Definition:** Polymer micro composites use traditional **microscale fillers** (typically 1–1000 µm in size) such as glass microspheres, talc, calcium carbonate, and short glass/carbon fibers.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Distinction from Nanocomposites:**
> Micro composites typically require **20–40 wt%** filler loading to achieve property enhancement, whereas nanocomposites achieve equivalent or superior properties at **1–5 wt%** loading. The reason is the **interfacial area** — nanofillers expose orders of magnitude more surface to the matrix for stress transfer.

| Property | Micro Composite | Nano Composite |
|---|---|---|
| Filler Size | 1 – 1000 µm | 1 – 100 nm |
| Loading Required | 20 – 40 wt% | 1 – 5 wt% |
| Interfacial Area | Low | Extremely High |
| Property Boost | Moderate | Dramatic |
| Processability | Easy | Challenging (dispersion) |

---

## 1.4 Short Fibre vs. Long Fibre Composites

### 1.4.1 Short Fibre Composites (SFC)

**Definition:** Short fibre composites are reinforced with discontinuous fibers having an **aspect ratio (L/D) typically between 10 and 1000**. The fiber length is finite and may be less than, equal to, or greater than a critical length (l_c).

> [!NOTE]
> Short fibre composites are the most commercially significant form of fiber-reinforced polymers (FRP) because they can be processed using **conventional injection molding, extrusion, and compression molding** techniques.

**Common Examples:**
- Short glass fiber-reinforced polypropylene (GF-PP)
- Short carbon fiber-reinforced polyamide (CF-PA66)

### 1.4.2 Long Fibre Composites (LFC)

**Definition:** Long (or continuous) fibre composites are reinforced with fibers that are **continuous throughout the composite structure**, providing near-maximum translation of fiber properties to the composite. The aspect ratio is essentially infinite (L/D → ∞).

**Forms of Long Fibre Composites:**
- **Unidirectional (UD) tapes:** All fibers aligned in one direction.
- **Woven fabrics:** Fibers interlaced in 0°/90° or multi-axial patterns.
- **Multiaxial non-crimp fabrics (NCF):** Multiple fiber layers stitched together.
- **Roving-based laminates:** Used in filament winding and pultrusion.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Highlight — Critical Comparison:**
> - **Short fibers** → Isotropic behavior (properties similar in all directions); easier processing; lower cost.
> - **Long fibers** → Anisotropic behavior (properties highest along fiber direction); superior mechanical performance; higher cost and processing complexity.

---

## 1.5 Treatment of Reinforcements

### 1.5.1 Why Treat Reinforcements?

Inorganic fibers (glass, carbon) and nanoparticles are chemically **incompatible** with most organic polymer matrices. The reinforcement-matrix interface is therefore a **weak link** unless treated. Surface treatment improves:
- **Wettability** of the reinforcement by the matrix
- **Adhesion** at the interface
- **Stress transfer efficiency** from matrix to reinforcement
- **Dispersion** (especially critical for nanofillers)

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistake:** Students often treat surface treatment as "optional." In reality, untreated fibers yield composites that fail at the interface — not in the matrix or fiber — meaning the composite is only as strong as its weakest link.

### 1.5.2 Common Treatment Methods

| Treatment Type | Application | Mechanism |
|---|---|---|
| **Silane Coupling Agents** | Glass fibers, silica nanofillers | Forms chemical bridge between inorganic –OH and organic polymer |
| **Sizing** | Glass/carbon fiber bundles | Film-former + lubricant + coupling agent coating |
| **Plasma Treatment** | Carbon fibers, CNTs | Activates surface, introduces functional groups |
| **Acid/Oxidation** | CNTs, carbon fibers | Introduces –COOH, –OH groups for further functionalization |
| **Grafting/Functionalization** | Polymer-grafted nanofillers | Covalent attachment of polymer chains to filler surface |
| **Surfactant Treatment** | Nanoclays, CNTs in polymer melt | Reduces surface energy, prevents agglomeration |

---

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Mechanics of Fibre Reinforcement — The Foundation

The strength and stiffness of a fiber-reinforced composite depend fundamentally on three factors:
1. **The rule of mixtures** (how properties combine by volume fraction).
2. **The critical fiber length** (how long a fiber must be to develop its full stress).
3. **The interfacial shear strength** (how well load transfers from matrix to fiber).

---

## 2.2 Step-by-Step Theoretical Framework

### 2.2.1 Rule of Mixtures (ROM) — The Master Equation

The **Rule of Mixtures** assumes:
- Fibers are **continuous, uniformly distributed, and perfectly aligned** with the load.
- The matrix and fiber experience the **same strain** (iso-strain, Voigt model).
- Perfect bonding exists at the interface.

The general form for any property P is:

$$P_c = P_f \cdot V_f + P_m \cdot V_m$$

where $P_c$, $P_f$, and $P_m$ are the composite, fiber, and matrix properties respectively; $V_f$ and $V_m$ are the volume fractions of fiber and matrix, with the constraint:

$$V_f + V_m = 1$$

---

### 2.2.2 Longitudinal Modulus (E∥) — Iso-strain Condition

When the load is applied **parallel to the fibers**, both fiber and matrix experience identical strain (ε). The composite modulus is:

$$E_{c,\parallel} = E_f \cdot V_f + E_m \cdot V_m$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuition:** When you pull a rope made of two parallel strands, both stretch by the same amount. The stiffer strand carries more load. This is the **iso-strain** assumption.

### 2.2.3 Transverse Modulus (E⊥) — Iso-stress Condition

When the load is applied **perpendicular to the fibers**, both phases experience identical stress (σ). This is the **Reuss model** (inverse rule of mixtures):

$$\frac{1}{E_{c,\perp}} = \frac{V_f}{E_f} + \frac{V_m}{E_m}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuition:** When the load is perpendicular, both matrix and fiber are stressed equally, but the more compliant phase stretches more. The composite stiffness is dominated by the weaker, more compliant phase.

### 2.2.4 Strength of Composite (Longitudinal)

$$S_{c,\parallel} = S_f \cdot V_f + S_m \cdot V_m$$

(Valid only for **continuous fibers** with strong interfaces.)

---

### 2.2.5 Critical Fiber Length (l_c) — The Most Important Concept for Short Fibers

The **critical fiber length** is the minimum length required for a fiber to develop its **maximum tensile stress** (σ_fu) when embedded in a matrix of interfacial shear strength (τ_i).

**Derivation (qualitative):** Consider a single fiber of diameter d embedded in a matrix. Load is transferred into the fiber through interfacial shear stress τ_i along the fiber's cylindrical surface. The maximum tensile stress the fiber can carry is σ_fu.

Setting the tensile force in the fiber equal to the shear force transferred through the interface:

$$\sigma_{fu} \cdot \frac{\pi d^2}{4} = \tau_i \cdot \pi d \cdot \frac{l_c}{2}$$

(The factor of 2 in the denominator arises because shear stress builds up from both fiber ends toward the center, so the maximum tensile stress occurs at the midpoint.)

Solving for **l_c**:

$$l_c = \frac{\sigma_{fu} \cdot d}{2 \tau_i}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Exam Favorite:** This is the single most important formula in Module 1. Students must derive it and explain the meaning of every term.

### 2.2.6 Critical Aspect Ratio

Dividing l_c by d:

$$\left(\frac{l}{d}\right)_c = \frac{\sigma_{fu}}{2 \tau_i}$$

- If **l/d < (l/d)_c** → Fiber is **sub-critical**; breaks are interface-dominated.
- If **l/d ≥ (l/d)_c** → Fiber is **super-critical**; fiber fracture occurs.

---

### 2.2.7 Kelly-Tyson Theory for Short Fibre Strength

For short fibers below critical length, the average fiber stress is **half the maximum** (because shear builds linearly from both ends):

$$\bar{\sigma}_f = \frac{\tau_i \cdot l}{d} \quad \text{(for } l \le l_c\text{)}$$

$$\bar{\sigma}_f = \sigma_{fu} \left(1 - \frac{l_c}{2l}\right) \quad \text{(for } l > l_c\text{)}$$

The composite strength then becomes:

$$S_c = \bar{\sigma}_f \cdot V_f + S_m \cdot V_m$$

---

### 2.2.8 Strength of Nanocomposites

For nanocomposites, the simple ROM is **inadequate** because:
1. Nanoparticles rarely form continuous load-bearing paths.
2. The huge interfacial area (often > 100 m²/g) dominates the physics.
3. Percolation, exfoliation, and dispersion quality control the actual reinforcement.

**Modified Halpin-Tsai Equation (for randomly dispersed nanoparticles):**

$$\frac{E_c}{E_m} = \frac{1 + 2 \xi \eta V_f}{1 - \eta V_f}$$

where:

$$\eta = \frac{\left(\frac{E_f}{E_m}\right) - 1}{\left(\frac{E_f}{E_m}\right) + 2\xi}$$

and $\xi$ is the **aspect ratio-dependent shape factor** of the filler.

---

### 2.2.9 Why Surface Treatment Improves Performance

Surface treatment increases the **interfacial shear strength (τ_i)**, which:
- **Reduces the critical fiber length** (l_c ∝ 1/τ_i) → short fibers can develop more stress.
- **Increases the load transfer efficiency** → composite stiffness and strength rise.
- **Prevents fiber pull-out** as a failure mode → failure shifts to fiber fracture (a stronger mechanism).

> [!WARNING]
> **Pitfall:** Many students treat τ_i as a constant. It is highly dependent on the surface chemistry of the fiber, the choice of coupling agent, and the processing conditions.

---

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

| # | Equation | Application | Key Variables | Units |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $V_f + V_m = 1$ | Volume fraction balance | $V_f$, $V_m$ | — |
| 2 | $E_{c,\parallel} = E_f V_f + E_m V_m$ | Longitudinal modulus (Voigt) | Moduli, $V_f$ | **GPa** |
| 3 | $\frac{1}{E_{c,\perp}} = \frac{V_f}{E_f} + \frac{V_m}{E_m}$ | Transverse modulus (Reuss) | Moduli, $V_f$ | **GPa** |
| 4 | $S_{c,\parallel} = S_f V_f + S_m V_m$ | Composite longitudinal strength | Strengths, $V_f$ | **MPa** |
| 5 | $l_c = \dfrac{\sigma_{fu} \cdot d}{2 \tau_i}$ | Critical fiber length | σ_fu, d, τ_i | mm / µm |
| 6 | $(l/d)_c = \sigma_{fu} / (2 \tau_i)$ | Critical aspect ratio | σ_fu, τ_i | — |
| 7 | $\bar{\sigma}_f = \sigma_{fu} \left(1 - \frac{l_c}{2l}\right)$ | Average fiber stress (l > l_c) | σ_fu, l_c, l | **MPa** |
| 8 | $\eta = \frac{(E_f/E_m) - 1}{(E_f/E_m) + 2\xi}$ | Halpin-Tsai η parameter | E_f, E_m, ξ | — |
| 9 | $E_c/E_m = \dfrac{1 + 2\xi \eta V_f}{1 - \eta V_f}$ | Halpin-Tsai modulus | E_f, E_m, ξ, V_f | — |
| 10 | $w_f = \dfrac{\rho_f V_f}{\rho_f V_f + \rho_m V_m}$ | Weight-to-volume fraction conversion | Densities, V_f | — |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Pattern Note:** KTU ESE frequently asks students to derive equation 5 (critical fiber length) and apply equations 2, 3, and 7 in numerical problems. Memorize the table above for guaranteed marks.

---

## 2.4 Engineering Utility — Real-World Applications

- **Aerospace:** Carbon fiber/epoxy long fibre composites in Boeing 787 (50% composite by weight) — uses continuous UD tapes and woven fabrics.
- **Automotive:** Short glass fiber/PA66 in engine covers, intake manifolds — uses injection molding.
- **Packaging:** Nylon-6/nanoclay (PA6/MMT) for high-barrier food packaging.
- **Sports:** Graphene/epoxy nanocomposite tennis rackets, CNT-reinforced bicycle frames.
- **Electronics:** Silica nanoparticle-filled epoxy for low-CTE (coefficient of thermal expansion) semiconductor encapsulants.

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-world insight:** A Boeing 787 uses **continuous carbon fiber composites** with surface-treated fibers (silane + proprietary sizing) to achieve a 20% fuel saving compared to aluminum. The silane treatment increases τ_i by 5–10×, allowing thinner, lighter laminates to carry the same load.

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Critical Fiber Length (l_c)

**Starting Physical Setup:**
Consider a single, cylindrical, brittle fiber of diameter **d** and length **l** embedded in a polymer matrix. Load is applied to the matrix and is transferred to the fiber through the interfacial shear stress **τ_i** acting on the cylindrical surface of the fiber.

**Step 1 — Force Balance on the Fiber:**
The maximum tensile force the fiber can sustain is:

$$F_{fiber} = \sigma_{fu} \cdot A_f = \sigma_{fu} \cdot \frac{\pi d^2}{4}$$

where $\sigma_{fu}$ is the ultimate tensile strength of the fiber.

**Step 2 — Shear Force Transferred Through the Interface:**
The shear stress acts over the entire lateral surface of the fiber (cylindrical area). However, the load is transferred from **both ends of the fiber toward the center**, so the effective length over which shear acts is half the total fiber length (l/2):

$$F_{shear} = \tau_i \cdot A_{interface} = \tau_i \cdot (\pi d) \cdot \frac{l}{2}$$

**Step 3 — Apply the Critical Condition:**
At the critical fiber length, the fiber just reaches its maximum stress σ_fu at its midpoint. Equating the two forces:

$$\sigma_{fu} \cdot \frac{\pi d^2}{4} = \tau_i \cdot \pi d \cdot \frac{l_c}{2}$$

**Step 4 — Simplify by Cancelling Common Factors:**

$$\sigma_{fu} \cdot d = 2 \cdot \tau_i \cdot l_c$$

**Step 5 — Solve for l_c:**

$$l_c = \frac{\sigma_{fu} \cdot d}{2 \tau_i}$$

This is the **critical fiber length**.

**Step 6 — Critical Aspect Ratio:**

$$\left(\frac{l}{d}\right)_c = \frac{\sigma_{fu}}{2 \tau_i}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Implication:** For glass fiber in epoxy (σ_fu ≈ 3500 MPa, d = 10 µm, τ_i ≈ 50 MPa), the critical length is l_c = (3500 × 10)/(2 × 50) = **350 µm**. Thus, fibers shorter than 350 µm do not develop full strength.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Rule of Mixtures (Longitudinal Modulus)

**Starting Physical Setup:**
A unidirectional composite with volume fraction $V_f$ of fibers and $V_m = 1 - V_f$ of matrix, loaded along the fiber direction.

**Step 1 — Apply Iso-strain Assumption:**
Under axial loading, fiber and matrix experience the same strain:

$$\varepsilon_c = \varepsilon_f = \varepsilon_m = \varepsilon$$

**Step 2 — Express Stresses in Each Phase (Hooke's Law):**

$$\sigma_f = E_f \cdot \varepsilon \qquad \sigma_m = E_m \cdot \varepsilon$$

**Step 3 — Total Load Carried by the Composite:**
By force equilibrium, the total load on the composite cross-section equals the sum of loads carried by fiber and matrix:

$$F_c = F_f + F_m$$

**Step 4 — Express in Terms of Stress and Cross-sectional Area:**

$$\sigma_c \cdot A_c = \sigma_f \cdot A_f + \sigma_m \cdot A_m$$

**Step 5 — Divide Through by A_c and Use Volume Fractions:**
Since $V_i = A_i / A_c$ (for uniform cross-section):

$$\sigma_c = \sigma_f \cdot V_f + \sigma_m \cdot V_m$$

**Step 6 — Substitute Stresses Using Hooke's Law:**

$$\sigma_c = E_f \cdot \varepsilon \cdot V_f + E_m \cdot \varepsilon \cdot V_m = \varepsilon \left( E_f V_f + E_m V_m \right)$$

**Step 7 — Define Composite Modulus:**

$$E_{c,\parallel} = \frac{\sigma_c}{\varepsilon} = E_f V_f + E_m V_m$$

This is the **Rule of Mixtures for longitudinal modulus**.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Reuss Model (Transverse Modulus)

**Starting Physical Setup:**
Same composite, but load is now applied **perpendicular** to the fibers. Both fiber and matrix experience the same stress (iso-stress assumption).

**Step 1 — Apply Iso-stress Assumption:**

$$\sigma_c = \sigma_f = \sigma_m = \sigma$$

**Step 2 — Express Strains in Each Phase:**

$$\varepsilon_f = \frac{\sigma}{E_f} \qquad \varepsilon_m = \frac{\sigma}{E_m}$$

**Step 3 — Total Elongation Equals Sum of Phase Elongations:**

$$\delta_c = \delta_f + \delta_m$$

**Step 4 — Express in Terms of Strain and Length:**

$$\varepsilon_c \cdot L = \varepsilon_f \cdot L_f + \varepsilon_m \cdot L_m$$

where L_f and L_m are the total lengths traversed through fiber and matrix regions.

**Step 5 — Convert to Volume Fractions:**
For a transverse cross-section, $V_f = L_f / L_c$ and $V_m = L_m / L_c$:

$$\varepsilon_c = \varepsilon_f \cdot V_f + \varepsilon_m \cdot V_m$$

**Step 6 — Substitute Stresses:**

$$\varepsilon_c = \frac{\sigma}{E_f} \cdot V_f + \frac{\sigma}{E_m} \cdot V_m = \sigma \left( \frac{V_f}{E_f} + \frac{V_m}{E_m} \right)$$

**Step 7 — Define Composite Modulus:**

$$E_{c,\perp} = \frac{\sigma}{\varepsilon_c} = \frac{1}{\frac{V_f}{E_f} + \frac{V_m}{E_m}}$$

Equivalently:

$$\frac{1}{E_{c,\perp}} = \frac{V_f}{E_f} + \frac{V_m}{E_m}$$

---

## 3.4 Worked Numerical Example — KTU Style

> **Problem [KTU University Exam – May 2024]:**
> A unidirectional glass fiber-reinforced epoxy composite contains 60% volume fraction of fibers. Given:
> - $E_f = 72 \text{ GPa}$, $E_m = 3.5 \text{ GPa}$
> - $\sigma_{fu} = 2400 \text{ MPa}$, $\tau_i = 45 \text{ MPa}$
> - Fiber diameter $d = 15 \text{ µm}$
>
> Calculate:
> (a) The longitudinal modulus $E_{c,\parallel}$
> (b) The transverse modulus $E_{c,\perp}$
> (c) The critical fiber length $l_c$

### Solution:

**Part (a) — Longitudinal Modulus:**

$$E_{c,\parallel} = E_f \cdot V_f + E_m \cdot V_m$$

$$E_{c,\parallel} = 72 \times 0.60 + 3.5 \times 0.40$$

$$E_{c,\parallel} = 43.2 + 1.4 = 44.6 \text{ GPa}$$

**Part (b) — Transverse Modulus:**

$$\frac{1}{E_{c,\perp}} = \frac{V_f}{E_f} + \frac{V_m}{E_m}$$

$$\frac{1}{E_{c,\perp}} = \frac{0.60}{72} + \frac{0.40}{3.5}$$

$$\frac{1}{E_{c,\perp}} = 0.00833 + 0.11429 = 0.12262 \text{ GPa}^{-1}$$

$$E_{c,\perp} = \frac{1}{0.12262} = 8.155 \text{ GPa}$$

**Part (c) — Critical Fiber Length:**

$$l_c = \frac{\sigma_{fu} \cdot d}{2 \tau_i} = \frac{2400 \times 15 \times 10^{-6}}{2 \times 45 \times 10^{6}}$$

Wait — careful with units. Let's express in MPa and µm:

$$l_c = \frac{2400 \text{ MPa} \times 15 \text{ µm}}{2 \times 45 \text{ MPa}} = \frac{36000}{90} = 400 \text{ µm}$$

$$l_c = 400 \text{ µm} = 0.4 \text{ mm}$$

**Result Summary:**
- $E_{c,\parallel} = 44.6 \text{ GPa}$
- $E_{c,\perp} = 8.16 \text{ GPa}$
- $l_c = 0.4 \text{ mm}$

> [!NOTE]
> **Notice the anisotropy:** $E_{c,\parallel}$ is **5.5×** higher than $E_{c,\perp}$. This dramatic difference is why continuous fiber composites are designed for unidirectional loads.
