# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. [KTU University Exam - June 2024]
**"Distinguish between long fibre composites and short fibre composites with suitable examples."**

**Model Answer:**

| Aspect | Long Fibre Composite (LFC) | Short Fibre Composite (SFC) |
|--------|----------------------------|------------------------------|
| Fibre length | Continuous; L >> $L_c$ | Discontinuous; L ≈ $L_c$ |
| Aspect ratio | Very high | Moderate (10–1000) |
| Strength | Maximum possible | Reduced by ~20–30% |
| Processing | Hand layup, pultrusion, filament winding | Injection moulding, extrusion |
| Example | Carbon fibre/epoxy aircraft parts | Glass fibre/PA6 automotive parts |
| Failure | Fibre fracture | Fibre pull-out + matrix yielding |
| Property model | Rule of Mixtures directly | Halpin–Tsai with length factor |

> **Mark distribution:** Definition (1) + comparison points (1) + example (1) = **3 marks**

---

### Q2. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]
**"What is the significance of the aspect ratio of layered silicates in polymer nanocomposites?"**

**Model Answer:**

The aspect ratio $\alpha$ of layered silicates (typically 100–2000 for MMT) is the single most important geometric parameter in polymer nanocomposites because:

1. **Property amplification:** Higher $\alpha$ means fewer particles are needed to achieve the same property enhancement. Per Nielsen's model, a 5 vol% loading of an $\alpha = 500$ platelet reduces gas permeability by ~85%.

2. **Tortuous path formation:** A diffusing molecule (O₂, H₂O vapour) must travel a longer zig-zag path around the impermeable platelet. The path length scales as $1 + \alpha V_f/2$.

3. **Percolation threshold:** Mechanical, electrical, and barrier percolation thresholds all scale as $\approx 1/\alpha$, so high-$\alpha$ fillers like graphene ($\alpha$ ≈ 1000) percolate at < 0.5 vol%.

4. **Melt viscosity:** Higher $\alpha$ increases viscosity more strongly, affecting processability.

> **Mark distribution:** Definition of $\alpha$ (1) + its three roles (1) + example (1) = **3 marks**

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam - June 2024, Modified]**

**(a)** Derive the expression for the **critical fibre length** in fibre-reinforced polymer composites. A unidirectional glass-fibre reinforced epoxy composite contains 40% volume fraction of fibres. The fibre diameter is 15 µm, fibre tensile strength is 2.0 GPa, and the interfacial shear strength is 40 MPa. Determine whether 0.5 mm-long fibres will provide effective reinforcement. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Discuss in detail the **various methods of preparation of long fibre composites** with emphasis on hand lay-up, filament winding, and pultrusion. Compare their relative advantages and limitations. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution for Q(a):

**Step 1 — Define the critical fibre length:**

The critical fibre length $L_c$ is the minimum fibre length at which the fibre can be loaded up to its ultimate tensile strength $\sigma_{fu}$ before the matrix fails. The formula is:

$$\frac{L_c}{d} = \frac{\sigma_{fu}}{2 \tau_i}$$

**Step 2 — Substituting values:**

$$L_c = \frac{\sigma_{fu} \, d}{2 \tau_i} = \frac{(2.0 \times 10^9 \, \text{Pa})(15 \times 10^{-6} \, \text{m})}{2 \times (40 \times 10^6 \, \text{Pa})}$$

Numerator:

$$2.0 \times 10^9 \times 15 \times 10^{-6} = 30 \times 10^3 = 3.0 \times 10^4 \, \text{N/m}$$

Denominator:

$$2 \times 40 \times 10^6 = 8.0 \times 10^7 \, \text{Pa}$$

So:

$$L_c = \frac{3.0 \times 10^4}{8.0 \times 10^7} = 3.75 \times 10^{-4} \, \text{m} = 0.375 \, \text{mm}$$

**Step 3 — Comparison with given fibre length:**

Given fibre length $L = 0.5$ mm. Since $L > L_c$ (0.5 > 0.375), the fibre can be loaded close to its ultimate tensile strength before matrix failure. Therefore, the fibres **will provide effective reinforcement**, though the efficiency factor is:

$$\eta = 1 - \frac{L_c}{2L} = 1 - \frac{0.375}{2 \times 0.5} = 1 - 0.375 = 0.625$$

So 62.5% of the fibre strength is utilized.

> **Mark allocation:** Derivation (3) + Substitution (2) + Comparison (2) = **7 marks**

#### Model Solution for Q(b):

**Methods of Preparation of Long Fibre Composites:**

**(i) Hand Lay-Up:**
- **Process:** Fibre mats (woven roving or cloth) are manually placed in a mould, and liquid resin is poured and brushed/rolled in. Cure at room temperature or mild heat.
- **Steps:** Mould prep → release agent → gel coat → fibre placement → resin wetting → rolling for compaction → cure → demould.
- **Advantages:** Low capital cost; suitable for very large parts (boat hulls); simple tooling; flexibility in design.
- **Limitations:** Low fibre volume fraction (max ~30–35%); high void content; labour-intensive; poor reproducibility; only polyester and vinyl ester resins usable at room temperature; health hazards from styrene emission.

**(ii) Filament Winding:**
- **Process:** Continuous fibre rovings are passed through a resin bath and wound onto a rotating mandrel under controlled tension and winding angle.
- **Winding patterns:** Hoop (90°), helical (45°), polar (variable).
- **Advantages:** High fibre volume fraction (60–70%); excellent for axisymmetric parts (pipes, pressure vessels, rocket motor casings); automated, low labour cost; high strength-to-weight ratio.
- **Limitations:** Restricted to convex shapes; mandrel cost high; limited to thermoset matrices; difficult to place fibres along the axis of the part.

**(iii) Pultrusion:**
- **Process:** Continuous fibre rovings and mats are pulled through a resin bath and then through a heated die where curing occurs. The cured profile is cut to length.
- **Steps:** Fibre creel → resin bath → preformer → heated die (cure) → puller → cut-off saw.
- **Advantages:** Continuous production of constant cross-section profiles; high fibre volume fraction (up to 70%); excellent mechanical properties along the pull direction; low labour cost; excellent for structural shapes (I-beams, channels, rods, ladder rails).
- **Limitations:** Only constant cross-section parts; resin selection limited; high capital cost of die and puller; difficulty in placing fibres transversely.

> **Mark allocation:** Three methods × (process 1.5 + advantages 0.5 + limitations 0.5) = **7 marks**

---

### Question B (14 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Modified]**

**(a)** Explain the **different methods of preparation of polymer nanocomposites** based on layered silicates. Compare the three principal routes with their merits and demerits. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Describe the **mechanism of film formation from polymer latex** and the various stages of **drying of a polymer film**, including the role of capillary forces, polymer diffusion, and the constant-rate and falling-rate periods. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution for Q(a):

**Methods of Preparation of Layered Silicate Polymer Nanocomposites:**

**(i) In-Situ Polymerisation (Intercalation Polymerisation):**
- **Process:** The monomer is first intercalated into the organo-modified clay galleries, and polymerisation is then initiated thermally or by a catalyst inside the galleries.
- **Example:** ε-caprolactam with organo-MMT to form Nylon-6/MMT (UBE Nylon-6).
- **Advantages:** High degree of exfoliation achievable; the polymer is formed in-situ and can drive the layers apart; best thermodynamic driving force; suitable for condensation polymerisations.
- **Demerits:** Restricted to polymerisations that can be carried out in the presence of clay; long reaction times; organo-modifier may degrade at high temperature.

**(ii) Solution Intercalation (Solvent Casting):**
- **Process:** Polymer and organo-modified clay are dissolved/dispersed in a polar organic solvent. The solvent enables polymer chains to intercalate; the solvent is then evaporated.
- **Example:** PEO/MMT, PS/MMT in toluene.
- **Advantages:** Uses conventional polymer dissolution; suitable for polymers that cannot be melt-processed; good for thin film preparation.
- **Demerits:** Large volumes of solvent required; environmental concerns; complete exfoliation difficult; cost of solvent removal.

**(iii) Melt Intercalation:**
- **Process:** Polymer and organo-modified clay are mechanically mixed in the molten state using extruders or internal mixers. Polymer chains diffuse from the bulk melt into the clay galleries.
- **Example:** PP/MMT, PET/MMT, EVA/MMT.
- **Advantages:** Environmentally friendly (no solvent); industrially scalable; compatible with conventional polymer processing (extrusion, injection moulding); flexible.
- **Demerits:** Requires careful organo-modification of clay; high shear needed; may not achieve full exfoliation; residence time and temperature must be optimised.

> **Mark allocation:** Three methods × (process 1 + advantages 0.7 + demerits 0.6) ≈ **7 marks**

#### Model Solution for Q(b):

**Mechanism of Film Formation from Polymer Latex (Bradford–Dillon–Dobler Model):**

Film formation from latex occurs in three to four overlapping stages:

**Stage 1 — Water evaporation (concentration stage):**
- The latex dispersion loses water by evaporation; the volume fraction of polymer particles increases from ~50% to ~74% (random close packing).
- Particles approach each other but retain their identity; no deformation occurs yet.

**Stage 2 — Particle deformation (close packing):**
- As water continues to evaporate from the interstices, capillary forces develop. The pressure difference across the curved water meniscus is given by Laplace's equation:

$$\Delta P = \frac{2 \gamma_{LV} \cos \theta}{r}$$

where $\gamma_{LV}$ is the surface tension, $\theta$ is the contact angle, and $r$ is the meniscus radius.

- These capillary pressures (often several MPa) deform the soft polymer particles from spheres into polyhedra, closing the voids. This is the **first-order film formation** driven by water–air interfacial energy.

**Stage 3 — Coalescence:**
- Particle boundaries begin to disappear; polymer chains from adjacent particles inter-diffuse across the interface. The driving force is the reduction in total interfacial energy of the system (analogous to sintering).
- Autohesion occurs when the work of adhesion between two particles exceeds the elastic recovery forces.

**Stage 4 — Chain inter-diffusion (autohesion):**
- Polymer chains from adjacent particles interpenetrate across the original particle boundary, governed by reptation (de Gennes) and characterised by the **inter-diffusion coefficient** $D$.
- The characteristic interpenetration depth $\Delta x$ scales as:

$$\Delta x \approx \sqrt{2 D t}$$

where $t$ is the annealing time. After sufficient time, the boundaries vanish and a continuous, mechanically strong film is obtained.

**Drying of Polymer Films:**

Drying of cast films occurs in two principal periods:

**(A) Constant Rate Period (CRP):**
- The film surface remains saturated with solvent. Evaporation rate is constant and governed only by external conditions (temperature, humidity, air velocity).
- Heat transfer into the film balances the latent heat of vaporisation; the film surface temperature equals the wet-bulb temperature.
- The evaporation rate is approximately:

$$\frac{dW}{dt} = \text{constant} = h (p_s - p_a)$$

where $h$ is the mass transfer coefficient, $p_s$ is vapour pressure at surface, $p_a$ is ambient vapour pressure.

**(B) Falling Rate Period (FRP):**
- The surface dries out; the "drying plane" recedes into the film. Diffusion of solvent through the polymer matrix becomes rate-limiting.
- The drying rate falls continuously with time. Two sub-periods exist:
  - **First falling rate period:** The drying front is still within the film; surface is dry.
  - **Second falling rate period:** The temperature of the film rises to the dry-bulb temperature; internal diffusion of residual solvent controls the process.
- Fick's second law governs:

$$\frac{\partial C}{\partial t} = D \frac{\partial^2 C}{\partial x^2}$$

- Defects like **skinning** (surface dries faster than interior, trapping solvent), **blistering**, and **cracking** occur in this period.

> **Mark allocation:** Stagewise mechanism (3.5) + drying periods with equations (3.5) = **7 marks**

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls in this Module:**
>
> 1. **Confusing $L_c$ and $L_c/d$:** Many students write $L_c = \sigma_{fu}/(2\tau_i)$ without the $d$ factor. Always state the **aspect ratio form** $L_c/d$ explicitly. Examiner will deduct 1 mark.
>
> 2. **Rule of Mixtures direction error:** ROM ($E_c = E_f V_f + E_m V_m$) applies to **longitudinal/parallel** loading only. For transverse loading, the **inverse ROM** must be used. Mixing them up loses 2 marks.
>
> 3. **Halpin–Tsai geometry factor $\xi$:** Students forget to set $\xi = 2(L/d)$ for short fibres. Forgetting this gives the wrong modulus. Examiner awards 0 for the modulus value.
>
> 4. **Nielsen's tortuosity formula:** The formula is for **exfoliated, randomly oriented** platelets. If the question specifies aligned platelets, the form changes. Always re-read.
>
> 5. **Film formation vs drying:** These are two distinct concepts. Film formation is the **coalescence of polymer particles**; drying is the **physical removal of solvent/water**. Do not interchange.
>
> 6. **Stages of film formation:** Many students omit the **chain inter-diffusion (Stage 4)** and stop at coalescence. Examiner expects all four stages for full marks.
>
> 7. **Units:** Always express $L_c$ in mm or µm; $\sigma_{fu}$ in MPa or GPa; $\tau_i$ in MPa. Unit mismatch is a 0.5 mark penalty per KTU rubrics.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist for Module 3 Topics**

- **Layered particles** (MMT, GO, LDH, h-BN) provide reinforcement primarily through **exfoliation** and the **tortuous path mechanism**.
- **Nonlayered particles** (SiO₂, TiO₂, CNT, HNT, POSS) reinforce via **stress transfer** and (for conductive ones) **percolation**.
- **Aspect ratio $\alpha$** is the single most important geometric parameter for property enhancement.
- **Critical fibre length** $L_c/d = \sigma_{fu}/(2\tau_i)$ governs whether a fibre is effectively utilised.
- **Rule of Mixtures (longitudinal):** $E_c = E_f V_f + E_m(1 - V_f)$.
- **Inverse Rule of Mixtures (transverse):** $1/E_{c\perp} = V_f/E_f + V_m/E_m$.
- **Halpin–Tsai equation** is the universal model: $E_c = E_m [1 + \xi\eta V_f]/[1 - \eta V_f]$ with $\xi = 2(L/d)$ for SFC.
- **Minimum fibre volume fraction** for composite to be stronger than matrix: $V_{f,min} = (\sigma_{mu} - \sigma_m')/(\sigma_{fu} - \sigma_m')$.
- **Nielsen barrier model:** $P_c/P_m = (1 - V_f)/(1 + \alpha V_f/2)$.
- **Long fibre composite methods:** Hand lay-up, filament winding, pultrusion, RTM.
- **Short fibre composite methods:** Extrusion compounding, injection moulding, SMC, BMC.
- **Nanocomposite methods:** In-situ polymerisation, solution intercalation, melt intercalation, sol–gel.
- **Film formation stages:** (1) water evaporation, (2) particle deformation by capillary forces, (3) coalescence, (4) chain inter-diffusion.
- **Capillary pressure in film formation:** $\Delta P = 2\gamma_{LV}\cos\theta/r$.
- **Chain inter-diffusion depth:** $\Delta x = \sqrt{2Dt}$ (Fickian).
- **Drying periods:** Constant Rate Period (surface evaporation-controlled) → Falling Rate Period (diffusion-controlled).
- **Fick's law for drying:** $\partial C/\partial t = D \partial^2 C/\partial x^2$.
- **Defects to mention:** skinning, blistering, cracking, pinholes.
- **Example systems (remember for 3-mark questions):** PA6/MMT, PP/MMT, PEO/MMT, CNT/epoxy, glass fibre/polyester.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Factors Affecting Coating Properties
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEPOT522 - POLYMER NANOCOMPOSITES
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Factors Affecting Coating Properties

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Coating Properties** refer to the collective set of physical, chemical, mechanical, optical, and barrier characteristics exhibited by a dried/cured polymeric film deposited on a substrate. In the context of **Polymer Nanocomposites**, the incorporation of nanoscale fillers (typically 1–100 nm in at least one dimension) into a polymeric binder matrix fundamentally alters the coating's properties through **interfacial interactions**, **nanoconfinement effects**, and **percolation phenomena**.

The principal categories of coating properties that are most affected by nanocomposite formulation include:

| Property Class | Specific Properties |
|---|---|
| **Mechanical** | Hardness, scratch resistance, flexibility, impact resistance, abrasion resistance |
| **Barrier** | Oxygen transmission rate (OTR), water vapor transmission rate (WVTR), permeability |
| **Optical** | Gloss, haze, transparency, color, refractive index |
| **Surface** | Surface energy, wetting, contact angle, smoothness |
| **Adhesion** | Pull-off strength, cross-hatch adhesion, tape adhesion |
| **Thermal** | Glass transition temperature (T_g), thermal stability, flame retardancy |
| **Rheological** | Viscosity, thixotropy, sagging, leveling |
| **Durability** | UV resistance, weathering, chemical resistance, corrosion protection |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** The focus of this module is on understanding **how nanocomposite architecture (filler type, size, shape, dispersion state, and interfacial chemistry) governs the final coating performance** — not merely the bulk mechanical properties of a generic polymer composite.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are **painting a wall with white cement paint**. The plain paint (just polymer + solvent) is like **plain cooked rice** — functional, but with limitations. Now, if you mix in **nano-silica particles**, the paint becomes like **rice mixed with tiny, evenly-distributed sesame seeds**:

- The **sesame seeds (nanoparticles)** fill microscopic gaps in the rice (polymer matrix).
- They create **tortuous paths** that water vapor must navigate, slowing down moisture penetration.
- They make the surface **harder** (like a tougher, more abrasion-resistant film).
- They may, however, **cluster together** if not mixed properly, creating rough spots and reducing gloss.

The "factors affecting coating properties" are essentially **every controllable variable** that determines whether your "sesame seeds" stay evenly dispersed, bond well with the rice, and deliver the desired final texture. A change in any one of these factors — particle size, surface chemistry, mixing speed, resin type, curing temperature — can dramatically alter the final coating's performance.

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard Metrics Used in KTU Board Examinations:**
> - **PVC** (Pigment Volume Concentration) — dimensionless ratio
> - **CPVC** (Critical Pigment Volume Concentration) — dimensionless
> - **Contact angle (θ)** — measured in **degrees (°)**
> - **Surface energy (γ)** — measured in **mN/m** or **dyne/cm**
> - **Interfacial tension (γ_ij)** — measured in **mN/m**
> - **Glass transition temperature (T_g)** — measured in **K** or **°C**

### 1.3 The Multi-Factor Nature of Coating Performance

Coating properties are **never determined by a single factor**. They emerge from the **synergistic or antagonistic interplay** between at least five major factor groups:

1. **Binder (Polymer Matrix) Characteristics** — chemistry, molecular weight, T_g, functionality
2. **Nanofiller Characteristics** — type, size, aspect ratio, surface modification, concentration
3. **Formulation Additives** — dispersants, plasticizers, coalescing agents, rheology modifiers
4. **Application & Processing Parameters** — substrate preparation, application method, film thickness
5. **Curing / Film Formation Conditions** — temperature, humidity, time, drying mechanism

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Detailed Factor-by-Factor Analysis

#### 2.1.1 Binder (Polymer Matrix) Characteristics

The polymer binder is the **continuous phase** that holds the nanofiller and substrate together. Key binder properties affecting coating performance:

- **Glass Transition Temperature (T_g):** Determines the coating's flexibility and hardness at service temperature. A high T_g yields a hard, scratch-resistant but brittle film; a low T_g gives a flexible, soft film.
- **Molecular Weight (M_w):** Higher M_w → higher viscosity, better mechanical strength, better chemical resistance, but reduced flow/leveling.
- **Crosslink Density (ν):** Governs solvent resistance, hardness, and T_g. A higher crosslink density typically increases hardness and chemical resistance while reducing flexibility.
- **Functionality & Chemistry:** Epoxy, polyurethane, acrylic, polyester, and silicone binders each impart unique property profiles (e.g., polyurethanes → abrasion resistance; epoxies → chemical resistance; silicones → thermal/UV stability).
- **Hydroxyl Value & Acid Number:** Determines the crosslinking reactivity and compatibility with curing agents.

**Why it matters for nanocomposites:** The polymer matrix must **wet** the nanofiller surface completely. Poor wetting leads to **agglomerates**, **voids**, and **stress concentration points** that degrade coating performance.

#### 2.1.2 Nanofiller Characteristics

This is the **central focus** of polymer nanocomposite coatings. The key variables are:

- **Nanofiller Type:**
  - **Layered silicates** (e.g., montmorillonite, MMT) → barrier, flame retardancy
  - **Nano-silica (SiO₂)** → scratch resistance, hardness
  - **Nano-alumina (Al₂O₃)** → abrasion resistance
  - **Carbon nanotubes (CNTs)** → conductivity, mechanical reinforcement
  - **Graphene / Graphene oxide (GO)** → barrier, conductivity
  - **Nano-TiO₂, Nano-ZnO** → UV blocking, photocatalytic self-cleaning
  - **POSS (Polyhedral Oligomeric Silsesquioxane)** → hybrid reinforcement

- **Particle Size (d):** As particle size decreases, **specific surface area (A_sp)** increases according to:
  $$A_{sp} = \frac{6}{\rho \cdot d}$$
  where ρ is the particle density. This means a smaller particle has exponentially more surface available for interaction with the polymer, dramatically increasing **interfacial adhesion** and **matrix-filler coupling**.

- **Aspect Ratio (α = L/d):** The ratio of length to thickness. Higher aspect ratio → more effective reinforcement at lower loadings. A platelet with α = 100 is far more effective than a sphere at creating **tortuous diffusion paths** for permeants.

- **Surface Modification:** Nanoparticles have a natural tendency to agglomerate due to high surface energy. **Surface treatment** with coupling agents (e.g., silanes, titanates, alkylammonium surfactants for clays) is essential to:
  - Reduce agglomeration
  - Improve compatibility with the polymer matrix
  - Promote **exfoliation** in layered silicates
  - Tune the hydrophilic/hydrophobic balance

- **Concentration (Loading, wt% or vol%):** Below a critical threshold, properties scale approximately linearly; above it, percolation, agglomeration, or saturation effects take over.

#### 2.1.3 Dispersion State — The Heart of Nanocomposite Performance

A critical and uniquely "nano" factor is the **state of dispersion**, which falls into three categories for layered silicates (applies analogously to other fillers):

| State | Description | Coating Impact |
|---|---|---|
| **Phase-Separated (Microcomposite)** | Stacks of clay tactoids remain intact; no polymer intercalation | Minimal property improvement |
| **Intercalated** | Polymer chains insert between clay layers; ordered multilayer structure | Moderate barrier and mechanical improvement |
| **Exfoliated** | Individual clay platelets are completely separated and dispersed in matrix | **Maximum property enhancement** |

> [!NOTE]
> The **intergallery spacing (d-spacing)** is measured by **X-Ray Diffraction (XRD)** using Bragg's Law: **nλ = 2d sin θ**. Exfoliation is confirmed when the characteristic clay peak disappears or shifts to very low angles.

#### 2.1.4 Formulation Additives

- **Dispersants / Wetting Agents:** Reduce surface/interfacial tension to break up agglomerates (e.g., BYK, Disperbyk series).
- **Plasticizers:** Lower T_g and increase flexibility; reduce brittleness.
- **Coalescing Agents:** Aid film formation in latex coatings by softening particles during drying.
- **Defoamers / Anti-foaming Agents:** Eliminate bubbles introduced during mixing.
- **UV Stabilizers (HALS, UVAs):** Absorb or quench UV radiation to prevent polymer degradation.
- **Rheology Modifiers:** Control viscosity, sagging, and leveling (e.g., associative thickeners).

#### 2.1.5 Application & Processing Parameters

- **Substrate Preparation:** Cleaning, degreasing, priming, and surface roughening (profiling) dramatically affect **adhesion**.
- **Application Method:** Spray, brush, roller, dip, spin, and electrodeposition each produce different film thickness, leveling, and defect profiles.
- **Film Thickness (Wet and Dry):** Specified in **micrometers (µm)** or **mils**. Thicker films generally improve barrier and durability but increase cost, drying time, and the risk of sagging.
- **Number of Coats:** Multi-coat systems (primer + basecoat + topcoat) optimize different property sets in each layer.

#### 2.1.6 Curing / Film Formation Conditions

- **Temperature:** Higher curing temperature accelerates crosslinking but can cause solvent entrapment, pinholes, and yellowing.
- **Humidity:** Affects water-based coatings; high humidity can cause blushing (moisture-induced haze) in polyurethanes.
- **Air Flow / Ventilation:** Controls solvent evaporation rate; poor ventilation causes solvent retention and soft films.
- **Time:** Under-cured films lack chemical resistance; over-cured films become brittle.

### 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Yield Formula Sheet** — These equations are most likely to appear in KTU 2024 ESE derivations and numerical problems.

| # | Formula | Description | Variables & Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $A_{sp} = \dfrac{6}{\rho \cdot d}$ | Specific surface area of a spherical particle | $A_{sp}$ in m²/g, $\rho$ in g/cm³, $d$ in nm |
| 2 | $n\lambda = 2d\sin\theta$ | Bragg's Law (XRD) for d-spacing | $n$ = integer, $\lambda$ in nm, $d$ in nm, $\theta$ in ° |
| 3 | $\gamma_{SV} = \gamma_{SL} + \gamma_{LV}\cos\theta$ | Young's equation (contact angle) | $\gamma$ in mN/m, $\theta$ in ° |
| 4 | $W_a = \gamma_{SV} + \gamma_{LV} - \gamma_{SL}$ | Work of adhesion (Young-Dupré) | $W_a$ in mN/m |
| 5 | $W_a = \gamma_{LV}(1 + \cos\theta)$ | Young-Dupré simplified form | $W_a$ in mN/m |
| 6 | $\eta = A \exp\left(\dfrac{E_a}{RT}\right)$ | Arrhenius viscosity-temperature relation | $\eta$ in Pa·s, $E_a$ in J/mol, $T$ in K |
| 7 | $t_{dry} = t_{wet} \times \dfrac{\% \text{ solids by volume}}{100}$ | Wet-to-dry film thickness conversion | $t$ in µm |
| 8 | $\phi_c = \dfrac{0.7}{f}$ | Percolation threshold for aspect ratio $f$ (random 3D rods) | $\phi_c$ = volume fraction, $f$ = aspect ratio |
| 9 | $\nu = \dfrac{E'}{3RT}$ | Crosslink density from rubber elasticity | $\nu$ in mol/m³, $E'$ in Pa, $T$ in K |
| 10 | $\tau = \tau_0 + \dfrac{G}{\lambda}$ | Tortuosity factor in nanocomposite barrier | $\tau$ dimensionless, $G$ geometry, $\lambda$ spacing |
| 11 | $PVC = \dfrac{V_p}{V_p + V_b}$ | Pigment Volume Concentration | $V_p, V_b$ in cm³ |
| 12 | $\dfrac{P_c}{P_0} = \dfrac{1 - \phi}{1 + \dfrac{\alpha \cdot \phi}{2}}$ | Nielsen permeability model for platelets | $P_c, P_0$ in cm³·cm/cm²·s·Pa, $\phi$ vol. fraction, $\alpha$ aspect ratio |
| 13 | $\Delta T_g = k \cdot M_w^{-1}$ | Fox-Flory relation (molecular weight effect) | $M_w$ in g/mol, $k$ constant |
| 14 | $S = \dfrac{\gamma_{SV} - \gamma_{SL}}{\gamma_{LV}}$ | Spreading coefficient | dimensionless |

### 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

Understanding factors affecting coating properties is critical in industries such as:

- **Automotive OEM & Refinish Coatings:** Achieving the "diamond-hard" scratch resistance of clearcoats using nano-silica; reducing orange peel via rheology control.
- **Marine Anti-Corrosive Coatings:** Epoxy coatings reinforced with nano-clay for offshore oil rigs — extending service life from 5 to 20+ years.
- **Food Packaging:** PLA/nano-clay films replacing aluminum foil — improving oxygen barrier by 50–80% with only 3–5 wt% clay.
- **Aerospace:** CNT-reinforced epoxy primers for lightning-strike protection (electrical conductivity).
- **Photovoltaic Encapsulation:** UV-blocking nano-ZnO/EVA composites to extend solar panel life.
- **Biomedical Implants:** Hydroxyapatite nanocomposite coatings for titanium implants — promoting osseointegration.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1: Wet-to-Dry Film Thickness Conversion

**Starting Point:** When a wet coating is applied, it contains both **non-volatile solids** and **volatile solvents**. After drying, only the solids remain, producing a thinner film. The relationship between wet and dry film thickness is critical for quality control.

**Given:**
- $t_{wet}$ = wet film thickness (µm)
- $V_{solids}$ = volume of solids (cm³)
- $V_{total}$ = total volume of wet coating (cm³)
- $S_v$ = volume fraction of solids (decimal form of % solids by volume)

**Assumption:** Volume is conserved during solvent evaporation (no shrinkage of solids, no entrapped voids).

**Step 1:** Define the solids volume fraction.

$$S_v = \frac{V_{solids}}{V_{total}}$$

**Step 2:** Express the dry film volume per unit area.

For a unit area (A = 1 cm²), the wet film has volume $t_{wet}$ (in cm³ if $t_{wet}$ is in cm; but we will keep consistent units).

$$V_{total} = t_{wet} \cdot A = t_{wet}$$

**Step 3:** The dry film contains only the solids, so its volume is:

$$V_{solids} = S_v \cdot V_{total} = S_v \cdot t_{wet}$$

**Step 4:** Therefore, the dry film thickness is:

$$t_{dry} = \frac{V_{solids}}{A} = S_v \cdot t_{wet}$$

**Step 5:** Substituting $S_v = \frac{\% \text{ solids by volume}}{100}$:

$$\boxed{t_{dry} = t_{wet} \times \frac{\% \text{ solids by volume}}{100}}$$

**Numerical Example (KTU-style):**
A coating has 45% solids by volume. If applied at a wet film thickness of 200 µm, what is the dry film thickness?
$t_{dry} = 200 \times 0.45 = 90 \text{ µm}$.

### 3.2 Derivation 2: Specific Surface Area and Its Coating Implications

**Starting Point:** Spherical nanoparticles. Surface area per unit mass is the key parameter governing **interfacial interaction** with the polymer.

**Given:**
- $d$ = particle diameter (assume spherical, in cm for CGS, or m for SI)
- $\rho$ = particle density (g/cm³ or kg/m³)
- Surface area of one sphere: $A_1 = \pi d^2$
- Volume of one sphere: $V_1 = \frac{\pi d^3}{6}$
- Mass of one sphere: $m_1 = \rho \cdot V_1 = \rho \cdot \frac{\pi d^3}{6}$

**Step 1:** Surface area per unit mass for one sphere:

$$A_{sp} = \frac{A_1}{m_1} = \frac{\pi d^2}{\rho \cdot \frac{\pi d^3}{6}} = \frac{6}{\rho \cdot d}$$

**Step 2:** To obtain units of m²/g, ensure $\rho$ is in g/cm³ and $d$ is in cm.

**Step 3:** Numerical comparison: A particle of $d = 10 \text{ nm} = 10^{-6} \text{ cm}$ and $\rho = 2.5 \text{ g/cm}^3$ (typical for SiO₂):

$$A_{sp} = \frac{6}{2.5 \times 10^{-6}} = 2.4 \times 10^{6} \text{ cm}^2/\text{g} = 240 \text{ m}^2/\text{g}$$

**Step 4:** Compare with a 10 µm particle (same density):

$$A_{sp} = \frac{6}{2.5 \times 10^{-4}} = 2.4 \times 10^{4} \text{ cm}^2/\text{g} = 2.4 \text{ m}^2/\text{g}$$

**Step 5:** The 10 nm particle has **100× more surface area per unit mass** than the 10 µm particle. This is the fundamental reason why **nanoparticles are vastly more effective** than microparticles at modifying coating properties — every additional square meter of surface is a site for polymer-filler bonding, stress transfer, and barrier tortuosity.

$$\boxed{\text{Therefore, } A_{sp} \propto \frac{1}{d}}$$

### 3.3 Derivation 3: Nielsen Model for Permeability Reduction in Nanocomposites

**Starting Point:** Polymer nanocomposites with platelet fillers dramatically reduce gas permeability. Nielsen (1967) proposed a model relating permeability to filler geometry.

**Given:**
- $P_0$ = permeability of pure polymer
- $P_c$ = permeability of composite
- $\phi$ = volume fraction of filler
- $\alpha$ = aspect ratio of platelet (L/t)

**Step 1:** A diffusing molecule must travel a longer path through a composite due to the impermeable platelets that force lateral diffusion.

The **tortuosity factor τ** is defined as the ratio of actual path length to direct path length.

**Step 2:** For randomly oriented platelets, Nielsen derived:

$$\frac{P_c}{P_0} = \frac{1 - \phi}{1 + \frac{\alpha \cdot \phi}{2}}$$

**Step 3:** **Derivation of the tortuosity contribution:**

Assume the platelets are oriented perpendicular to the diffusion direction (worst case for barrier). The minimum distance a molecule must travel is approximately:

$$L_{min} = L + \frac{d}{\sin\theta_{avg}}$$

where $\theta_{avg}$ is the average tilt angle. For a random distribution, $\theta_{avg} \approx 45°$.

**Step 4:** Defining $\tau = L_{min}/L$:

$$\tau \approx 1 + \frac{\alpha \cdot \phi}{2(1 - \phi)}$$

**Step 5:** Combining with the volume reduction (solvent is replaced by impermeable filler):

$$\frac{P_c}{P_0} = \frac{(1 - \phi)}{\tau} \approx \frac{1 - \phi}{1 + \frac{\alpha \cdot \phi}{2}}$$

**Step 6:** **Numerical Example:**

Consider a 5 vol% (φ = 0.05) nano-clay with aspect ratio α = 100 in a polymer:

$$\frac{P_c}{P_0} = \frac{1 - 0.05}{1 + \frac{100 \times 0.05}{2}} = \frac{

topics.title: Film Thickness
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEPOT522 - POLYMER NANOCOMPOSITES
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Film Thickness

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In the context of **Polymer Nanocomposites (PEPOT522)**, **Film Thickness** is defined as the perpendicular distance measured between the top surface (air–film interface) and the bottom surface (film–substrate interface) of a uniformly cast or deposited polymer nanocomposite layer. It is a critical processing–structure–property (PSP) parameter that governs the dispersion, orientation, exfoliation, optical transparency, barrier, and mechanical response of the resulting nanoscale composite membrane or coating.

Mathematically, for a uniform film of area $A$ and mass $m$ containing nanofiller of mass fraction $w_f$ and polymer matrix of mass fraction $(1 - w_f)$, the average film thickness $t$ is expressed as:

$$t = \frac{m}{\rho_{c} \cdot A}$$

where $\rho_{c}$ is the density of the nanocomposite film given by the rule of mixtures:

$$\rho_{c} = \rho_{m}(1 - w_f) + \rho_{f} w_f$$

Here, $\rho_{m}$ and $\rho_{f}$ denote the densities of the polymer matrix and the nanofiller, respectively. **Standard units:** micrometers ($\mu m$) for polymer films and **nanometers (nm)** for ultrathin nanocomposite films.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** For Module 3 of PEPOT522, the term *film thickness* is not merely a geometric attribute; it directly controls the **degree of nanofiller intercalation/exfoliation**, **clay/polymer tactoid size**, and **optical/transport anisotropy** of the cast film. Examiners frequently test this inter-relationship.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine spreading **honey** on a flat glass plate. The faster you drag a straight rod (the **doctor blade**) across it, the **thinner** the honey film becomes; the slower you drag, the **thicker** it remains. This everyday phenomenon captures the essence of three classical film-forming processes used in nanocomposites:

- **Solution Casting** → Think of pouring melted butter onto a flat pan: thickness is set by **gravity, viscosity, and evaporation rate**.
- **Spin Coating** → Imagine a spinning merry-go-round where excess water is flung outward; the film gets **thinner at higher spin speeds**.
- **Dip Coating** → Like slowly lifting a wet glass out of water; a thin liquid film clings to the glass, with thickness depending on the **withdrawal speed**.

In **Polymer Nanocomposite (PNC)** systems, nanofillers such as **organically modified montmorillonite (OMMT)**, **layered double hydroxides (LDH)**, **graphene nanoplatelets (GNP)**, or **cellulose nanocrystals (CNC)** are dispersed in the polymer solution or melt. The way these nanoplatelets *stack*, *orient*, or *exfoliate* inside the final film is critically dictated by the **final cast thickness** and the **drying kinetics**. A thinner film often yields better **in-plane orientation** of platelets (improved barrier), while a thicker film may retain stacked tactoids (lower performance).

### 1.3 Standard Metrics and Key Physical Constants

| Parameter | Symbol | Typical Value / Range | Unit |
|---|---|---|---|
| Ultrathin film thickness | $t$ | 10 – 500 | nm |
| Conventional cast film | $t$ | 1 – 500 | $\mu m$ |
| Polymer melt density | $\rho_m$ | 0.85 – 1.50 | g/cm³ |
| Nanoclay (OMMT) density | $\rho_f$ | 1.8 – 2.6 | g/cm³ |
| Surface tension (water) | $\gamma$ | 72.8 | mN/m |
| Gravitational acceleration | $g$ | **9.81** | m/s² |

> [!NOTE]
> **Why $g = 9.81 \text{ m/s}^2$ matters:** It appears in the classical Landau–Levich equation that predicts dip-coating film thickness. Examiners expect this value to be substituted numerically.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Factors Governing Film Thickness in PNC Systems

The final thickness of a polymer nanocomposite film is a **multi-parameter function** of:

- **Process parameters**: spin speed, withdrawal speed, doctor blade gap, evaporation temperature
- **Material parameters**: solution viscosity $\eta$, surface tension $\gamma$, polymer concentration $C_0$, nanofiller loading $w_f$
- **Substrate parameters**: surface energy, wettability, roughness
- **Drying kinetics**: solvent vapor pressure, ambient humidity

#### 2.1.1 Spin-Coating Model (Emslie–Bonner–Peck)

For a Newtonian fluid, the final spin-coated thickness is:

$$h_f = \left(1 - \frac{\rho_0}{\rho_s}\right) \cdot \frac{C_0 \, \nu^{1/4}}{k \, \omega^{1/2}}$$

where:
- $C_0$ = initial solution concentration
- $\nu$ = kinematic viscosity
- $\omega$ = angular spin speed (rad/s)
- $\rho_0, \rho_s$ = initial and final (solid) densities
- $k$ = empirical constant

The key insight: **doubling the spin speed reduces thickness by a factor of $\sqrt{2} \approx 1.414$**.

#### 2.1.2 Landau–Levich–Derjaguin (LLD) Model for Dip Coating

For dip coating at low capillary number, the entrained film thickness is:

$$h_0 = 0.94 \cdot \frac{(\eta \, U)^{2/3}}{\gamma^{1/6} \, (\rho \, g)^{1/2}}$$

where:
- $\eta$ = dynamic viscosity of solution
- $U$ = withdrawal speed
- $\gamma$ = surface tension
- $\rho$ = solution density
- $g$ = gravitational acceleration

#### 2.1.3 Doctor Blade / Tape Casting Model

For a blade moving with velocity $U_b$ over a wet layer of initial thickness $h_0$:

$$h_f = \frac{h_0}{2} \left(1 + \frac{\rho \, g \, h_0^2}{3 \, \eta \, U_b}\right)^{-1}$$

At high blade speeds (dominant viscous drag), $h_f \approx \frac{3 \eta U_b}{2 \rho g h_0}$.

#### 2.1.4 Beer–Lambert Relation (UV–Vis for thickness)

If the film absorbance $A_\lambda$ at wavelength $\lambda$ follows Beer–Lambert law with extinction coefficient $\epsilon$ and concentration $c$:

$$A_\lambda = \epsilon \, c \, t \quad \Rightarrow \quad t = \frac{A_\lambda}{\epsilon \, c}$$

### 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Process / Method | Governing Equation | Key Variables | Typical Use in PNC |
|---|---|---|---|
| Spin coating | $h_f = k \cdot \omega^{-1/2}$ | $\omega$ (spin speed) | Ultrathin PNC films |
| Dip coating (LLD) | $h_0 = 0.94 \, (\eta U)^{2/3} / \left[\gamma^{1/6} (\rho g)^{1/2}\right]$ | $\eta, U, \gamma, \rho$ | Free-standing PNC membranes |
| Doctor blade | $h_f = h_0/2 \left[1 + \rho g h_0^2/(3 \eta U_b)\right]^{-1}$ | $h_0, U_b, \eta$ | Thick PNC films / tapes |
| Gravimetric | $t = m / (\rho_c \cdot A)$ | $m, A, \rho_c$ | Bulk / large-area films |
| Beer–Lambert | $t = A_\lambda / (\epsilon \, c)$ | $A_\lambda, \epsilon, c$ | Transparent thin films |
| Ellipsometry | $\tan\Psi e^{i\Delta} = f(n, k, d, \lambda)$ | $\Psi, \Delta, n, k$ | Sub-100 nm PNC films |
| X-ray Reflectivity | $\theta_c = \sqrt{2\delta}$ (critical angle) | $\delta, \theta_c$ | Angstrom-level thickness |

> [!TIP]
> **Examiner's Hint:** For a 14-mark problem, you are expected to write the governing equation, identify the controlling parameter, and discuss how **nanofiller addition** modifies the **effective viscosity** $\eta_{eff}$ via the Einstein–Guth–Gold or Krieger–Dougherty equation:
> $$\eta_{eff} = \eta_0 \left(1 - \frac{\phi}{\phi_{max}}\right)^{-[\eta] \phi_{max}}$$
> This **increases film thickness** for a given spin speed or withdrawal speed.

### 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

Controlling PNC film thickness is central to:

1. **Food packaging** – thinner films (5–50 $\mu m$) with exfoliated nanoclay deliver O₂ permeability reduction of 50–80 %.
2. **Flexible electronics** – uniform sub-100 nm films enable organic thin-film transistors (OTFTs).
3. **Fuel cell membranes** – Nafion®/clay nanocomposite films of 25–50 $\mu m$ reduce methanol crossover.
4. **Anticorrosion coatings** – epoxy/GO films of 100–300 $\mu m$ with controlled thickness for barrier uniformity.
5. **Biomedical scaffolds** – PNC films of defined thickness regulate cell adhesion and drug release kinetics.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation: Spin-Coating Film Thickness (Emslie–Bonner–Peck Model)

**Starting point:** A Newtonian fluid of viscosity $\eta$ and density $\rho$ is spread on a rotating substrate. The centrifugal force drives radial flow, and the fluid thins until the solvent evaporates.

**Step 1 — Write the centrifugal body force per unit volume.**

For a fluid element at radius $r$ rotating at angular speed $\omega$, the centrifugal acceleration is $\omega^2 r$. The corresponding body force per unit volume is:

$$f_c = \rho \, \omega^2 \, r$$

**Step 2 — Apply the lubrication approximation.**

For a thin film ($h \ll r$), the Navier–Stokes equation in the radial direction reduces to:

$$\eta \, \frac{\partial^2 u}{\partial z^2} = -\rho \, \omega^2 \, r$$

**Step 3 — Integrate twice with respect to $z$.**

Applying the no-slip condition $u(r, 0) = 0$ at the substrate and zero shear stress $\partial u / \partial z = 0$ at the free surface ($z = h$):

$$u(r, z) = \frac{\rho \, \omega^2 \, r}{2 \eta} \left(h^2 - z^2\right)$$

**Step 4 — Compute the volumetric flow rate per unit circumference.**

$$Q = \int_0^h u \, dz = \frac{\rho \, \omega^2 \, r}{3 \eta} \, h^3$$

**Step 5 — Apply the continuity (mass balance) equation.**

For an incompressible fluid in a cylindrical geometry:

$$\frac{\partial h}{\partial t} = -\frac{1}{r} \frac{\partial (r Q)}{\partial r}$$

Substituting $Q$:

$$\frac{\partial h}{\partial t} = -\frac{2 \rho \, \omega^2}{3 \eta} \cdot \frac{1}{r} \frac{\partial}{\partial r}\left(r^2 h^3\right)$$

**Step 6 — Seek a self-similar solution of the form $h(r, t) = h_0(t) \cdot f(r)$.**

For a uniform initial layer, the solution is:

$$h_0(t) = \frac{h_0(0)}{\sqrt{1 + \frac{4 \rho \, \omega^2 h_0(0)^2}{3 \eta} \, t}}$$

**Step 7 — Account for solvent evaporation in parallel.**

Solvent evaporation at rate $e$ (mass per area per time) introduces an additional thinning term. Combining centrifugal thinning with evaporation:

$$h_f = \left(1 - \frac{\rho_0}{\rho_s}\right) \cdot \frac{C_0 \, \nu^{1/4}}{k \, \omega^{1/2}}$$

This is the **final KTU-result form** of the Emslie–Bonner–Peck equation, where:
- $C_0$ = initial concentration (mass fraction)
- $\nu$ = kinematic viscosity ($\eta / \rho$)
- $k$ = empirical constant (depends on solvent volatility)
- $\rho_0$ and $\rho_s$ are initial solution and dry film densities, respectively

**Step 8 — Interpret the scaling.**

The thickness scales as $h_f \propto \omega^{-1/2}$ and $h_f \propto C_0$ and $h_f \propto \nu^{1/4}$. For PNC systems, the nanofiller loading $w_f$ effectively raises $\nu$ via the Krieger–Dougherty relation, increasing $h_f$ for a given $\omega$.

---

### 3.2 Exhaustive Derivation: Landau–Levich–Derjaguin Equation

**Step 1 — Set up the coordinate system.**

A plate is withdrawn vertically from a Newtonian liquid bath at speed $U$. The film is entrained and thins by gravity before reaching steady thickness $h_0$.

**Step 2 — Apply the balance of viscous drag, gravity, and capillary pressure.**

In the dynamic meniscus region, the lubrication equation becomes:

$$\eta \, \frac{d^3 h}{dx^3} = \rho g - \frac{\gamma \, h''}{h^2}$$

**Step 3 — Non-dimensionalize with the capillary length.**

Let $Ca = \eta U / \gamma$ (capillary number), $h \rightarrow h_0 \tilde h$, $x \rightarrow (\gamma / \rho g)^{1/2} \tilde x$. The leading-order balance gives:

$$h_0 \, \sim \, Ca^{2/3} \left(\frac{\gamma}{\rho g}\right)^{1/2}$$

**Step 4 — Compute the numerical prefactor (Levich, 1942).**

A more detailed asymptotic matching yields:

$$h_0 = 0.944 \, \frac{(\eta U)^{2/3}}{\gamma^{1/6} \, (\rho g)^{1/2}}$$

This is the **standard LLD result**, with the prefactor $0.944$ (often approximated as $0.94$).

**Step 5 — Apply to a PNC dip-coating scenario.**

If the polymer solution has $\eta = 1.2 \text{ Pa}\cdot\text{s}$, $\gamma = 30 \text{ mN/m}$, $\rho = 980 \text{ kg/m}^3$, and $U = 0.01 \text{ m/s}$:

$$h_0 = 0.94 \cdot \frac{(1.2 \cdot 0.01)^{2/3}}{(0.030)^{1/6} (980 \cdot 9.81)^{1/2}}$$

Evaluating step by step:

$$(\eta U)^{2/3} = (0.012)^{2/3} = 0.0524$$

$$(\gamma)^{1/6} = (0.030)^{0.1667} = 0.5301$$

$$(\rho g)^{1/2} = (9603.8)^{1/2} = 98.0$$

Therefore:

$$h_0 = 0.94 \cdot \frac{0.0524}{0.5301 \cdot 98.0} = 0.94 \cdot \frac{0.0524}{51.95} \approx 0.94 \cdot 1.009 \times 10^{-3}$$

$$\boxed{h_0 \approx 9.48 \times 10^{-4} \text{ m} \approx 948 \, \mu m}$$

This is the dip-coated film thickness for the given PNC solution.

---

### 3.3 Numerical Worked Example — PNC Film Thickness via Gravimetric Method

A **polyamide-6 / OMMT (3 wt %)** nanocomposite film is cast over a glass substrate of dimensions $10 \text{ cm} \times 10 \text{ cm}$. The dry film mass is $0.42 \text{ g}$. The densities are: $\rho_{PA6} = 1.14 \text{ g/cm}^3$, $\rho_{OMMT} = 2.10 \text{ g/cm}^3$.

**Step 1 — Compute composite density via rule of mixtures.**

$$\rho_c = (1 - 0.03)(1.14) + (0.03)(2.10) = 0.97 \times 1.14 + 0.063 = 1.1058 + 0.063 = 1.1688 \, \text{g/cm}^3$$

**Step 2 — Compute area in cm².**

$$A = 10 \times 10 = 100 \, \text{cm}^2$$

**Step 3 — Apply $t = m / (\rho_c \cdot A)$.**

$$t = \frac{0.42}{1.1688 \times 100} = \frac{0.42}{116.88} = 3.59 \times 10^{-3} \text{ cm}$$

$$\boxed{t \approx 35.9 \, \mu m}$$

This is the **average film thickness** — a typical value for solvent-cast PNC films in barrier packaging studies.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

```mermaid

```

> [!NOTE]
> **Why Mermaid Block Diagrams Are Used:** True physical schematics of film cross-sections with nanofiller dispersion are extremely difficult in plain text. The above **functional architecture flows** map out the **process → thickness → property** chain that examiners commonly ask students to draw in PNC questions.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
Define the term *film thickness* in the context of polymer nanocomposites. Mention any **two** factors that control the final film thickness in a solution-cast PNC.

**Model Answer:**
Film thickness in polymer nanocomposites is the perpendicular distance between the top and bottom interfaces of a cast nanocomposite layer, typically expressed in $\mu m$ (or nm for ultrathin films). Two controlling factors are:
1. **Polymer solution concentration** $C_0$ — higher $C_0$ produces thicker films.
2. **Spin speed (in spin coating)** or **withdrawal speed (in dip coating)** — higher speeds produce thinner films via the $\omega^{-1/2}$ or $U^{2/3}$ scaling.

A third acceptable factor: **nanofiller loading** $w_f$ (raises effective viscosity, increases thickness).

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2023]**
List any **three** techniques used to measure the thickness of polymer nanocomposite films. State the principle of **one** method in brief.

**Model Answer:**
Three techniques:
1. **Stylus profilometry** – mechanical stylus traverses a film edge; the vertical step gives thickness.
2. **Ellipsometry** – measures change in polarization ($\Psi, \Delta$) of reflected light; thickness is extracted by fitting a Fresnel multilayer model.
3. **SEM cross-section** – film is fractured in liquid N₂ and imaged; the scale bar is used to measure thickness.

*(Gravimetric method and XRR also acceptable.)*

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — KTU ESE Module Internal Choice)

#### **Question A (14 Marks)**
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2022 Model]**

**(a)** With the help of a neat schematic, describe the **solution casting** and **spin coating** techniques for preparing polymer nanocomposite films. Compare their thickness control and uniformity. (7 marks)

**(b)** Derive the **Emslie–Bonner–Peck relation** for the final thickness of a spin-coated polymer film, clearly stating all assumptions. Discuss how the addition of **5 wt % nanoclay** affects the final film thickness quantitatively. (7 marks)

**Model Solution for (a):**
- **Solution casting**: A PNC solution (polymer + nanofiller in volatile solvent) is poured onto a flat substrate (glass, PET, or Teflon). The solvent evaporates under controlled temperature/humidity, leaving a solid PNC film. The thickness is controlled by:
  - Solution concentration $C_0$
  - Cast volume $V$
  - Substrate area $A$
  - Evaporation rate
  
  Uniformity is moderate and depends on drying uniformity; films of 10–500 $\mu m$ are typical.

- **Spin coating**: The substrate is mounted on a chuck and rotated at high speed (500–6000 rpm). The PNC solution is dispensed at the center, and centrifugal force spreads it into a thin film. Solvent evaporates simultaneously. Final thickness depends on:
  - Spin speed $\omega$
  - Acceleration ramp
  - Solution viscosity $\eta$
  - Solvent volatility
  
  Uniformity is excellent (sub-nm roughness); films of 20–500 nm are typical.

**Comparison Table (in exam — write as a paragraph or simple table):**

| Parameter | Solution Casting | Spin Coating |
|---|---|---|
| Thickness range | 10–500 $\mu m$ | 20–500 nm |
| Uniformity | Moderate | Excellent |
| Equipment cost | Low | High |
| Scalability | High (industrial) | Low (lab-scale) |

---

**Model Solution for (b):**
The derivation follows the 8-step procedure detailed in **Section 3.1** of these notes. Summarised key steps:

1. Body force: $f_c = \rho \omega^2 r$.
2. Lubrication approximation: $\eta \partial^2 u / \partial z^2 = -\rho \omega^2 r$.
3. Velocity profile: $u(r, z) = (\rho \omega^2 r / 2 \eta)(h^2 - z^2)$.
4. Flow rate: $Q = (\rho \omega^2 r / 3 \eta) h^3$.
5. Continuity: $\partial h / \partial t = -(2 \rho \omega^2 / 3 \eta) (1/r) \partial(r^2 h^3) / \partial r$.
6. Self-similar form: $h_0(t) = h_0(0) / \sqrt{1 + (4 \rho \omega^2 h_0(0)^2 / 3 \eta) t}$.
7. With evaporation: $h_f = (1 - \rho_0/\rho_s) \cdot C_0 \nu^{1/4} / (k \omega^{1/2})$.

**Assumptions:** Newtonian fluid, no-slip at substrate, free surface stress-free, axisymmetric flow, evaporation coupled to centrifugal thinning.

**Effect of 5 wt % nanoclay:**
The nanofiller raises the effective kinematic viscosity via the Krieger–Dougherty relation:

$$\nu_{eff} = \nu_0 \left(1 - \frac{\phi}{\phi_{max}}\right)^{-[\eta] \phi_{max}}$$

For $\phi_{max} = 0.6$, $[\eta] = 2.5$, and $\phi \approx 0.025$ (for 5 wt %, $\rho_{clay} = 2.0$ g/cm³, $\rho_{polymer} = 1.1$ g/cm³):

$$\nu_{eff} / \nu_0 = (1 - 0.025/0.6)^{-2.5 \times 0.6} = (0.9583)^{-1.5} \approx 1.069$$

So $\nu^{1/4}$ changes by a factor of $(1.069)^{1/4} \approx 1.017$, i.e., the film thickness increases by **~1.7 %** compared to the neat polymer for the same spin speed.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)**
**[KTU University Exam – June 2022 Model]**

**(a)** Explain the **Landau–Levich–Derjaguin (LLD)** theory for the entrained film thickness during dip coating. State the final equation and the meaning of each variable. (7 marks)

**(b)** A PNC solution with viscosity $\eta = 1.5$ Pa·s, surface tension $\gamma = 28$ mN/m, and density $\rho = 1050$ kg/m³ is dip-coated at $U = 5$ mm/s. Calculate the **entrained film thickness** $h_0$. Discuss how incorporating **3 wt % graphene nanoplatelets (GNP)** would alter this thickness. (7 marks)

**Model Solution for (a):**
The Landau–Levich–Derjaguin theory models the steady-state film thickness entrained on a plate withdrawn vertically from a Newtonian liquid. The balance of **viscous drag, gravity, and capillary pressure** in the dynamic meniscus region gives:

$$h_0 = 0.94 \cdot \frac{(\eta U)^{2/3}}{\gamma^{1/6} \, (\rho g)^{1/2}}$$

**Variables:**
- $\eta$ = dynamic viscosity of the liquid (Pa·s)
- $U$ = withdrawal speed (m/s)
- $\gamma$ = surface tension (N/m)
- $\rho$ = liquid density (kg/m³)
- $g$ = gravitational acceleration, **9.81 m/s²**
- $h_0$ = entrained film thickness (m)

**Validity:** Applicable in the **capillary number regime** $10^{-6} < Ca < 10^{-3}$, where $Ca = \eta U / \gamma$. Outside this range, evaporation or drainage dominate.

---

**Model Solution for (b):**
**Given:** $\eta = 1.5$ Pa·s, $\gamma = 28 \times 10^{-3}$ N/m, $\rho = 1050$ kg/m³, $U = 5 \times 10^{-3}$ m/s, $g = 9.81$ m/s².

**Step 1 — Compute $(\eta U)^{2/3}$:**

$$\eta U = 1.5 \times 0.005 = 0.0075 \text{ Pa·m}$$

$$(\eta U)^{2/3} = (0.0075)^{0.6667} = 0.01782$$

**Step 2 — Compute $\gamma^{1/6}$:**

$$\gamma^{1/6} = (0.028)^{0.1667} \approx 0.5157$$

**Step 3 — Compute $(\rho g)^{1/2}$:**

$$\rho g = 1050 \times 9.81 = 10300.5 \text{ N/m}^3$$

$$(\rho g)^{1/2} = \sqrt{10300.5} \approx 101.49$$

**Step 4 — Compute $h_0$:**

$$h_0 = 0.94 \cdot \frac{0.01782}{0.5157 \times 101.49} = 0.94 \cdot \frac{0.01782}{52.34}$$

$$h_0 = 0.94 \times 3.405 \times 10^{-4} \approx 3.20 \times 10^{-4} \text{ m}$$

$$\boxed{h_0 \approx 320 \, \mu m}$$

**Effect of 3 wt % GNP:**
GNP loading of 3 wt % (volume fraction $\phi \approx 0.018$, assuming $\rho_{GNP} = 2.2$ g/cm³) raises $\eta_{eff}$ by 15–25 % (GNP are high-aspect-ratio, so the Einstein coefficient $[\eta]$ is large, often 5–10). For a 20 % viscosity increase, $\eta_{eff} = 1.8$ Pa·s:

$$h_0^{PNC} = 0.94 \cdot \frac{(1.8 \times 0.005)^{2/3}}{0.5157 \times 101.49} \approx 0.94 \cdot \frac{(0.009)^{2/3}}{52.34}$$

$$(0.009)^{2/3} = 0.01999 \quad \Rightarrow \quad h_0^{PNC} \approx 0.94 \times 3.82 \times 10^{-4} \approx 3.59 \times 10^{-4} \text{ m}$$

$$\boxed{h_0^{PNC} \approx 359 \, \mu m}$$

The film thickness **increases by ~12 %** due to GNP-induced viscosity enhancement, which has practical implications for dip-coating throughput.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Unit Conversion Errors:** The most common mistake is mixing **cm** and **m** in the LLD equation. Always convert $\eta$ to Pa·s, $\gamma$ to N/m, and $\rho$ to kg/m³ **before** substitution.
> 2. **Forgetting $g = 9.81$:** Many students drop $g$ in the LLD equation. Examiners deduct 1–2 marks.
> 3. **Spin speed in Hz vs. rad/s:** The Emslie–Bonner–Peck equation uses $\omega$ in **rad/s**. If rpm is given, multiply by $2\pi/60$.
> 4. **Neglecting nanofiller effect on viscosity:** Examiners award bonus marks if you mention the **Krieger–Dougherty** or **Einstein** equation to quantify viscosity change with filler loading.
> 5. **Confusing mass density with areal density:** The gravimetric method requires the **volumetric** density $\rho_c$ (rule of mixtures), not the areal coverage.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- **Definition:** Film thickness = perpendicular distance between air–film and film–substrate interfaces.
- **Standard units:** $\mu m$ (conventional) and **nm** (ultrathin PNC).
- **Key process routes:** spin coating, dip coating, doctor blade, melt extrusion.
- **Emslie–Bonner–Peck spin-coating relation:** $h_f \propto \omega^{-1/2} \cdot C_0 \cdot \nu^{1/4}$.
- **Landau–Levich–Derjaguin dip-coating relation:** $h_0 = 0.94 (\eta U)^{2/3} / [\gamma^{1/6} (\rho g)^{1/2}]$.
- **Always use SI units** (Pa·s, N/m, kg/m³, m/s) and $g = 9.81$ m/s² in calculations.
- **Nanofiller effect:** Raises $\eta_{eff}$ (Krieger–Dougherty) → increases film thickness for a given set of process conditions.
- **Measurement techniques:** stylus profilometry, ellipsometry, SEM cross-section, XRR, gravimetric, AFM step height.
- **Ellipsometry parameters:** $\Psi$ and $\Delta$ are the two measured angles; thickness is fitted from Fresnel model.
- **Beer–Lambert for optical thickness:** $t = A_\lambda / (\epsilon c)$.
- **Beer–Lambert assumes** dilute, homogeneous, non-scattering films — often invalid for PNC due to nanofiller scattering.
- **Composite density:** Use rule of mixtures $\rho_c = \rho_m(1 - w_f) + \rho_f w_f$.
- **Process–property link:** Thinner films → better exfoliation + in-plane platelet orientation → improved barrier and transparency.
- **Examiner's favourite question type:** "Calculate film thickness for a given PNC system" with sub-parts on process description and nanofiller effect.
- **Avoid:** Mixing up withdrawal speed $U$ and blade speed $U_b$ — they appear in different equations.
- **Memorise the constants:** 0.94 (LLD prefactor), 9.81 m/s² (g), $2\pi/60$ (rpm to rad/s).
- **Cross-link to Module 3 outcomes:** Thickness directly influences **intercalation/exfoliation**, **clay tactoid size**, and **tortuosity** of the diffusion path in barrier applications.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Film Density
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEPOT522 - POLYMER NANOCOMPOSITES
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Film Density

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

**Film density** in polymer nanocomposites is defined as the **mass per unit volume** of a thin solid film cast or fabricated from a polymer matrix containing dispersed nanoscale fillers (nanoparticles, nanofibers, nanoclays, graphene derivatives, etc.), typically with film thicknesses ranging from a few **nanometers (nm)** to several **micrometers (µm)**.

Mathematically, it is expressed as:

$$\rho_{film} = \frac{m_{film}}{V_{film}} = \frac{m_{film}}{A_{film} \times t_{film}}$$

Where:
- $\rho_{film}$ = Film density (g/cm³ or kg/m³)
- $m_{film}$ = Mass of the film (g or kg)
- $V_{film}$ = Geometric volume of the film (cm³ or m³)
- $A_{film}$ = Film area (cm² or m²)
- $t_{film}$ = Film thickness (cm or m)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Definition Highlight:** Film density is a *bulk-averaged* property that reflects the **overall packing efficiency** of polymer chains and nanofillers, including the presence of free volume, voids, and interfacial regions in the thin-film geometry.

---

## 1.2 Intuitive Overview (Real-World Analogy)

> [!NOTE]
> **Conceptual Analogy: The "Suitcase Packing" Model**
> Imagine packing a suitcase (the film volume) with two types of items: rolled-up t-shirts (polymer chains) and small hard boxes (nanoparticles). The **film density** is essentially the *total weight of the suitcase divided by its external volume*.
> - If the t-shirts are loosely rolled, the suitcase weighs less (lower density → more free volume).
> - If you stuff the t-shirts tightly and squeeze in hard boxes into every gap, the suitcase becomes much heavier for the same volume (higher density → better packing).
> - Polymer nanocomposite film density tells you **how efficiently the polymer chains and nanofillers are packed together** in the thin film — a direct measure of microstructural quality.

---

## 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

| Parameter | Symbol | Typical Range/Value (Polymer Nanocomposite Films) | Unit |
|-----------|--------|--------------------------------------------------|------|
| Polymer film density | $\rho_p$ | 0.85 – 1.50 | g/cm³ |
| Nanofiller density | $\rho_f$ | 2.00 – 22.00 (e.g., Au, SiO₂, TiO₂, CNT) | g/cm³ |
| Film thickness | $t$ | 10 nm – 100 µm | nm, µm |
| Film area | $A$ | 1 – 100 | cm² |
| Free volume fraction | $f_v$ | 0.01 – 0.15 | dimensionless |
| Critical angle (XRR) | $\theta_c$ | 0.1 – 0.5 | degrees |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Standard SI Unit:** **kg/m³**; **CGS Unit:** **g/cm³**; **Conversion:** 1 g/cm³ = **1000 kg/m³**

---

## 1.4 Why Film Density is Critical in Polymer Nanocomposites

1. **Quality Control:** Detects voids, pinholes, and processing defects in cast/spun films.
2. **Filler Dispersion Quality:** Deviations from the rule-of-mixtures density indicate **agglomeration** or **interfacial voids**.
3. **Permeability Prediction:** Lower density → higher free volume → higher gas/vapor permeability.
4. **Optical & Dielectric Property Linkage:** Density affects refractive index via the **Lorentz–Lorenz equation**.
5. **Barrier Performance:** Denser films (well-packed nanocomposites) typically exhibit superior barrier properties against O₂, CO₂, and moisture.
</br>

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Principle — Why "Density" is Non-Trivial for Thin Films

Unlike bulk polymer specimens (where Archimedes' principle works well), thin films present unique challenges:

- **Sub-milligram masses** → gravimetric imprecision
- **Sub-micron thicknesses** → geometric volume errors dominate
- **Substrate contribution** → must be decoupled
- **Surface/interface effects** → polymer chains at the air interface and substrate interface pack differently than bulk

Therefore, multiple complementary techniques are used to obtain an *accurate* film density.

---

## 2.2 Step-by-Step Logic: How Film Density is Determined

### Step 1: Film Fabrication
Polymer nanocomposite films are typically prepared by:
- **Solution casting** (solvent evaporation)
- **Spin coating** (centrifugal spreading)
- **Doctor blade / tape casting**
- **Hot-melt extrusion + compression molding**
- **Layer-by-layer (LbL) assembly**

### Step 2: Mass Determination
The film is **delaminated** (peeled off) or **weighed with the substrate**, and the substrate mass is subtracted.
For ultra-thin films (< 100 nm), a **Quartz Crystal Microbalance (QCM)** or **X-ray Reflectivity (XRR)** is mandatory.

### Step 3: Geometry Determination
- **Thickness ($t$):** Profilometry, ellipsometry, XRR, or cross-sectional SEM
- **Area ($A$):** Calibrated image analysis or known substrate geometry

### Step 4: Density Calculation
$$\rho_{film} = \frac{m_{film}}{A \times t}$$

### Step 5: Comparison with Theoretical Density (Rule of Mixtures)

For an ideal, void-free polymer nanocomposite:

$$\rho_{theoretical} = \phi_p \cdot \rho_p + \phi_f \cdot \rho_f$$

Where $\phi_p$ and $\phi_f$ are the volume fractions of polymer and filler.

**Void fraction ($V_{void}$):**
$$V_{void} = 1 - \frac{\rho_{experimental}}{\rho_{theoretical}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight:** If $\rho_{experimental} < \rho_{theoretical}$, voids are present. If $\rho_{experimental} > \rho_{theoretical}$, strong polymer–filler interfacial interactions have **densified** the interfacial polymer layer (a positive nanocomposite effect).

---

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Description | Units | Validity / Notes |
|---|---------|-------------|-------|------------------|
| 1 | $\rho = m / V$ | Basic density | g/cm³ | Universal |
| 2 | $\rho_{film} = m_{film} / (A \cdot t)$ | Film density (gravimetric) | g/cm³ | Requires precise $t$ |
| 3 | $\rho_{ROM} = \phi_p \rho_p + \phi_f \rho_f$ | Rule of Mixtures (theoretical) | g/cm³ | Assumes no voids |
| 4 | $V_{void} = 1 - \rho_{exp} / \rho_{ROM}$ | Void volume fraction | dimensionless | $0 \le V_{void} < 1$ |
| 5 | $\theta_c = \sqrt{2\delta}$ (XRR) | Critical angle of total reflection | radians | $\delta$ = dispersion |
| 6 | $\rho_e = (r_0 \lambda^2 / 2\pi) \cdot \theta_c^2$ | Electron density → mass density | mol e⁻/cm³ | XRR-derived |
| 7 | $n = 1 - \delta + i\beta$ | Refractive index from XRR | dimensionless | $\delta$ from $\theta_c$ |
| 8 | $(\rho - \rho_l) / (\rho_h - \rho_l) = h_{film} / H_{column}$ | Flotation gradient position | dimensionless | Density gradient column |
| 9 | $\Delta m = -C_f \cdot \Delta f$ (QCM) | Sauerbrey equation for mass | g | $C_f$ = sensitivity constant |
| 10 | $A_{mix} = (\phi_f / \rho_f) + (\phi_p / \rho_p)$ | Specific volume of mixture | cm³/g | Composite formulation |

> [!WARNING]
> **Exam Tip:** KTU examiners *specifically test* the difference between **Rule of Mixtures** (theoretical, void-free) and **experimental film density** — the gap between them is the **void content** question.

---

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Food packaging industry:** Density-engineered PA6/MMT nanocomposite films show 40% lower O₂ permeability.
- **Flexible electronics:** PEDOT:PSS + graphene nanocomposite film density governs charge transport uniformity.
- **Membrane separation:** Film density correlates directly with selectivity in nanofiltration membranes.
- **Biomedical coatings:** PLA/nHA nanocomposite film density controls degradation kinetics and drug release rates.
- **Aerospace:** PEEK/CNT nanocomposite film density is critical for mass-constrained structural skins.
</br>

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation 1: Film Density from X-ray Reflectivity (XRR)

XRR is a non-destructive, surface-sensitive technique that yields film density, thickness, and surface/interface roughness simultaneously.

**Starting Point — X-ray Refractive Index:**
For X-rays, the refractive index of a material is slightly less than unity:

$$n = 1 - \delta + i\beta$$

Where:
- $\delta$ = dispersion term (real part correction) → related to **electron density** $\rho_e$
- $\beta$ = absorption term (imaginary part)

**Step 1:** The dispersion $\delta$ is proportional to the electron density:

$$\delta = \frac{r_0 \lambda^2}{2\pi} \cdot \rho_e$$

Where:
- $r_0$ = classical electron radius = **2.818 × 10⁻¹⁵ m**
- $\lambda$ = X-ray wavelength (typically 0.154 nm for Cu K$\alpha$)
- $\rho_e$ = electron number density (electrons/m³)

**Step 2:** Below the critical angle $\theta_c$, total external reflection occurs. At the critical angle:

$$\theta_c \approx \sqrt{2\delta}$$

(derived from Snell's law at the critical condition $n \cos\theta_c = \cos 90° = 0$)

**Step 3:** Substituting the expression for $\delta$ into $\theta_c$:

$$\theta_c^2 = 2\delta = 2 \cdot \frac{r_0 \lambda^2}{2\pi} \cdot \rho_e = \frac{r_0 \lambda^2 \rho_e}{\pi}$$

**Step 4:** Solve for electron density:

$$\rho_e = \frac{\pi \theta_c^2}{r_0 \lambda^2}$$

**Step 5:** Convert electron density to **mass density** using the molecular composition:

$$\rho_{mass} = \frac{\rho_e \cdot M}{N_A \cdot Z}$$

Where:
- $M$ = molar mass of the monomer/repeat unit (g/mol)
- $N_A$ = Avogadro's number = **6.022 × 10²³ mol⁻¹**
- $Z$ = total number of electrons per repeat unit (dimensionless)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> For a polymer nanocomposite, the average $M$ and $Z$ are weighted by the mass fractions of polymer and filler.

---

## 3.2 Exhaustive Derivation 2: Rule of Mixtures for Nanocomposite Film Density

**Starting Assumption:** Volume additivity (no interfacial excess volume).

**Step 1:** Define the total film volume as the sum of polymer and filler contributions:

$$V_{total} = V_p + V_f$$

**Step 2:** Express each volume in terms of mass and density:

$$V_p = \frac{m_p}{\rho_p}, \quad V_f = \frac{m_f}{\rho_f}$$

**Step 3:** Total mass is the sum:

$$m_{total} = m_p + m_f$$

**Step 4:** Substitute into the density expression:

$$\rho_{ROM} = \frac{m_{total}}{V_{total}} = \frac{m_p + m_f}{\frac{m_p}{\rho_p} + \frac{m_f}{\rho_f}}$$

**Step 5:** Define mass fractions $w_p = m_p / m_{total}$ and $w_f = m_f / m_{total}$:

$$\rho_{ROM} = \frac{1}{\frac{w_p}{\rho_p} + \frac{w_f}{\rho_f}}$$

**Step 6:** Convert mass fractions to volume fractions. Recall:

$$\phi_f = \frac{w_f / \rho_f}{w_p / \rho_p + w_f / \rho_f}$$

**Step 7:** Substituting back yields the *more familiar* volume-fraction form:

$$\rho_{ROM} = \phi_p \rho_p + \phi_f \rho_f$$

**Where:** $\phi_p + \phi_f = 1$ (no voids assumption)

---

## 3.3 Worked Numerical Example (KTU-Style)

> **Problem (KTU 2024 ESE Style):**
> A polymer nanocomposite film is fabricated with 5 wt% of silica (SiO₂) nanoparticles in a polystyrene (PS) matrix. Given: $\rho_{PS} = 1.05$ g/cm³, $\rho_{SiO_2} = 2.20$ g/cm³, experimental film density $\rho_{exp} = 1.08$ g/cm³. Calculate (a) the theoretical density, (b) the volume fraction of SiO₂, and (c) the void fraction.

### Solution

**Part (a): Theoretical density (Rule of Mixtures using mass fractions)**

Using $\rho_{ROM} = \dfrac{1}{\dfrac{w_p}{\rho_p} + \dfrac{w_f}{\rho_f}}$:

$$\rho_{ROM} = \frac{1}{\frac{0.95}{1.05} + \frac{0.05}{2.20}}$$

$$\rho_{ROM} = \frac{1}{0.9048 + 0.0227} = \frac{1}{0.9275}$$

$$\boxed{\rho_{ROM} = 1.0781 \ \text{g/cm}^3}$$

**Part (b): Volume fraction of SiO₂**

$$\phi_f = \frac{w_f / \rho_f}{w_p / \rho_p + w_f / \rho_f} = \frac{0.05/2.20}{0.95/1.05 + 0.05/2.20}$$

$$\phi_f = \frac{0.02273}{0.90476 + 0.02273} = \frac{0.02273}{0.92749}$$

$$\boxed{\phi_f = 0.0245 \ \text{ or } \ 2.45 \ \text{vol\%}}$$

**Part (c): Void fraction**

$$V_{void} = 1 - \frac{\rho_{exp}}{\rho_{ROM}} = 1 - \frac{1.08}{1.0781}$$

$$\boxed{V_{void} = 1 - 1.00176 = -0.00176 \approx -0.18\%}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Physical Interpretation:** A *negative* void fraction is physically impossible — it means the experimental density is **slightly higher** than the rule-of-mixtures prediction. This indicates **strong polymer–filler interfacial densification** (a signature of well-coupled nanocomposites, where polymer chains pack more tightly at the nanofiller interface than they would in the bulk).

> If $\rho_{exp} < \rho_{ROM}$, then $V_{void} > 0$ → processing defects or poor filler–matrix adhesion.
</br>

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
