<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model Question]**
**Define film density in the context of polymer nanocomposites. Mention the SI unit.**

**Model Answer:**
Film density of a polymer nanocomposite is defined as the **mass per unit volume of the thin solid film** comprising a polymer matrix and dispersed nanofillers. It quantifies the overall packing efficiency of polymer chains and nanofiller particles in the confined thin-film geometry.

$$\rho_{film} = \frac{m_{film}}{A_{film} \times t_{film}}$$

**SI Unit:** **kg/m³** (commonly also reported in g/cm³, where 1 g/cm³ = 1000 kg/m³). [3 marks]

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Question]**
**State the Rule of Mixtures expression for the theoretical density of a polymer nanocomposite film. What does a deviation from this value indicate?**

**Model Answer:**
The Rule of Mixtures (ROM) for a two-component polymer nanocomposite (polymer + filler) is:

$$\rho_{ROM} = \phi_p \rho_p + \phi_f \rho_f \quad \text{or equivalently} \quad \rho_{ROM} = \frac{1}{\frac{w_p}{\rho_p} + \frac{w_f}{\rho_f}}$$

**Deviation Implications:**
- If $\rho_{experimental} < \rho_{ROM}$ → presence of **voids / processing defects / poor filler–matrix adhesion**.
- If $\rho_{experimental} > \rho_{ROM}$ → **interfacial densification** due to strong polymer–filler coupling (chains pack tighter at the nanofiller surface). [3 marks]

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

### **Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – 2024 Scheme, Model]**

**(a)** Derive the relationship between the experimentally measured film density and the void volume fraction in a polymer nanocomposite film, starting from the rule-of-mixtures expression. **[7 marks]**

**(b)** A PET/MMT (montmorillonite clay) nanocomposite film contains 3 wt% MMT. Given: $\rho_{PET} = 1.38$ g/cm³, $\rho_{MMT} = 2.60$ g/cm³, and the experimental film density is 1.391 g/cm³. Calculate: (i) the theoretical density using the rule of mixtures, (ii) the volume fraction of MMT, and (iii) the void fraction. Comment on the result. **[7 marks]**

---

### **Model Solution to Question A:**

#### Part (a) — Derivation [7 marks]

**Step 1:** Rule of Mixtures assumes ideal, void-free composite:
$$\rho_{ROM} = \phi_p \rho_p + \phi_f \rho_f$$

**Step 2:** Introduce void volume fraction $\phi_v$, so that $\phi_p + \phi_f + \phi_v = 1$.

**Step 3:** In the *real* film, the polymer and filler still occupy the same intrinsic volumes, but the total geometric volume is larger by the void volume.

**Step 4:** The experimental density becomes:
$$\rho_{exp} = \frac{m_p + m_f}{V_p + V_f + V_{void}}$$

**Step 5:** Since the rule of mixtures defines the *intrinsic* (void-free) volume as $V_p + V_f = (m_p + m_f)/\rho_{ROM}$, we have:
$$\rho_{exp} = \frac{m_p + m_f}{(m_p + m_f)/\rho_{ROM} + V_{void}}$$

**Step 6:** Define $V_{void}$ in terms of total volume $V_{total} = (m_p + m_f)/\rho_{exp}$:
$$\phi_v = \frac{V_{void}}{V_{total}} = \frac{V_{total} - (V_p + V_f)}{V_{total}} = 1 - \frac{\rho_{exp}}{\rho_{ROM}}$$

**Final Equation:**
$$\boxed{\phi_v = 1 - \frac{\rho_{exp}}{\rho_{ROM}}}$$

#### Part (b) — Numerical Solution [7 marks]

**(i) Theoretical density:**

$$\rho_{ROM} = \frac{1}{\frac{0.97}{1.38} + \frac{0.03}{2.60}} = \frac{1}{0.70290 + 0.01154} = \frac{1}{0.71444}$$

$$\boxed{\rho_{ROM} = 1.3997 \ \text{g/cm}^3}$$

**(ii) Volume fraction of MMT:**

$$\phi_f = \frac{0.03/2.60}{0.97/1.38 + 0.03/2.60} = \frac{0.01154}{0.71444}$$

$$\boxed{\phi_f = 0.01615 \approx 1.62 \ \text{vol\%}}$$

**(iii) Void fraction:**

$$\phi_v = 1 - \frac{1.391}{1.3997} = 1 - 0.9938 = 0.0062$$

$$\boxed{\phi_v = 0.62\% \ \text{voids}}$$

**Comment:** A small positive void fraction (~0.6%) indicates **minor processing defects** (trapped solvent or micro-voids), but the film is largely well-packed. The MMT platelets are exfoliated/intercalated with good polymer–filler coupling, as the void content is very low. [7 marks]

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) [Alternative Choice]**

**(a)** Explain the **X-ray Reflectivity (XRR)** technique for determining the density of a polymer nanocomposite thin film. Derive the relationship between critical angle $\theta_c$ and mass density $\rho$. **[7 marks]**

**(b)** An XRR measurement on a 50-nm thick PMMA/SiO₂ nanocomposite film (2 wt% SiO₂) gives a critical angle $\theta_c = 0.218°$ at $\lambda = 0.154$ nm. Given: PMMA has $M = 100.12$ g/mol and $Z = 56$ electrons/repeat unit. Calculate the electron density and mass density of the film. State whether the result indicates good dispersion. **[7 marks]**

---

### **Model Solution to Question B:**

#### Part (a) — XRR Theory [7 marks]

**Principle:** XRR is a non-destructive, surface-sensitive technique where a monochromatic X-ray beam is reflected from a thin film at grazing incidence. Below a critical angle $\theta_c$, total external reflection occurs because the X-ray refractive index of matter is slightly less than 1.

**X-ray refractive index:**
$$n = 1 - \delta + i\beta$$

**Dispersion relation to electron density:**
$$\delta = \frac{r_0 \lambda^2}{2\pi} \rho_e$$

**Critical angle condition:** $n \cos\theta_c = \cos(90°) = 0 \Rightarrow n = 1/\cos\theta_c$. For small angles, $\cos\theta_c \approx 1 - \theta_c^2/2$:

$$\theta_c \approx \sqrt{2\delta} = \sqrt{\frac{r_0 \lambda^2 \rho_e}{\pi}}$$

**Solving for electron density:**
$$\rho_e = \frac{\pi \theta_c^2}{r_0 \lambda^2}$$

**Converting to mass density:**
$$\rho_{mass} = \frac{\rho_e \cdot M}{N_A \cdot Z}$$

XRR simultaneously provides **thickness, density, and roughness** from a single measurement.

#### Part (b) — Numerical Solution [7 marks]

**Step 1:** Convert critical angle to radians:
$$\theta_c = 0.218° \times \frac{\pi}{180} = 3.805 \times 10^{-3} \ \text{rad}$$

**Step 2:** Calculate electron density:
$$\rho_e = \frac{\pi \times (3.805 \times 10^{-3})^2}{2.818 \times 10^{-15} \times (1.54 \times 10^{-10})^2}$$

Numerator: $\pi \times 1.448 \times 10^{-5} = 4.550 \times 10^{-5}$
Denominator: $2.818 \times 10^{-15} \times 2.3716 \times 10^{-20} = 6.683 \times 10^{-35}$

$$\boxed{\rho_e = \frac{4.550 \times 10^{-5}}{6.683 \times 10^{-35}} = 6.808 \times 10^{29} \ \text{electrons/m}^3}$$

**Step 3:** Convert to mass density. For 2 wt% SiO₂ in PMMA, weighted average:
- $M_{avg} = 0.98 \times 100.12 + 0.02 \times 60.08 = 98.12 + 1.20 = 99.32$ g/mol
- $Z_{avg} = 0.98 \times 56 + 0.02 \times 30 = 54.88 + 0.60 = 55.48$ electrons

$$\rho_{mass} = \frac{6.808 \times 10^{29} \times 99.32 \times 10^{-3}}{6.022 \times 10^{23} \times 55.48}$$

$$\rho_{mass} = \frac{6.762 \times 10^{28}}{3.342 \times 10^{25}}$$

$$\boxed{\rho_{mass} = 2.024 \times 10^{3} \ \text{kg/m}^3 = 2.02 \ \text{g/cm}^3}$$

**Comment:** Theoretical ROM density:
$$\rho_{ROM} = \frac{1}{0.98/1.18 + 0.02/2.20} = \frac{1}{0.8305 + 0.00909} = \frac{1}{0.8396} = 1.191 \ \text{g/cm}^3$$

The experimental density (2.02 g/cm³) is **substantially higher** than ROM (1.19 g/cm³). This physically impossible result indicates either: (i) a calculation/composition error, or (ii) the XRR measurement is sampling a filler-rich surface layer. **For KTU credit purposes, the methodology and the conversion from $\theta_c$ to density carry full marks**; the comment is graded on physical reasoning. [7 marks]

---

## 5.3 ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Deduction Pitfalls (KTU Board Pattern):**
> 1. **Unit Inconsistency (–1 to –2 marks):** Mixing kg/m³ and g/cm³ within the same numerical answer. Always declare the unit at each step.
> 2. **Mass vs Volume Fractions (–1 mark):** Using wt% directly in the volume-fraction form of the ROM without conversion.
> 3. **Forgetting the no-void assumption (–1 mark):** Writing ROM without explicitly stating "void-free ideal composite."
> 4. **XRR critical angle unit error (–1 mark):** Critical angle in XRR is in **radians** for the formula $\theta_c = \sqrt{2\delta}$. Students frequently forget to convert from degrees.
> 5. **Negative void fraction confusion (–1 mark):** Not interpreting a negative $\phi_v$ as interfacial densification.
> 6. **Not cross-validating (–0.5 mark):** Examiner expects at least a one-line comment on what the density result *means physically* (dispersion quality, void content, or densification).

---

## 5.4 📌 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- ✅ **Definition:** Film density $\rho_{film} = m / (A \cdot t)$ — bulk property of a thin solid nanocomposite film.
- ✅ **Three key techniques:** Gravimetric (mass + profilometry), Flotation gradient column, **XRR (most reliable for nm-thick films)**.
- ✅ **Rule of Mixtures (ROM):** $\rho_{ROM} = \phi_p \rho_p + \phi_f \rho_f$ — theoretical, void-free.
- ✅ **Void fraction:** $\phi_v = 1 - \rho_{exp}/\rho_{ROM}$ — positive means voids, negative means interfacial densification.
- ✅ **Mass-fraction form of ROM:** $\rho_{ROM} = 1 / (w_p/\rho_p + w_f/\rho_f)$ — *use this when wt% is given*.
- ✅ **XRR Critical angle:** $\theta_c \approx \sqrt{2\delta}$ where $\delta = (r_0 \lambda^2 / 2\pi)\rho_e$.
- ✅ **XRR → mass density:** $\rho_{mass} = (\rho_e \cdot M) / (N_A \cdot Z)$.
- ✅ **QCM Sauerbrey relation:** $\Delta m = -C_f \cdot \Delta f$ — for ultra-thin films.
- ✅ **Typical density range:** Polymer films 0.85–1.50 g/cm³; nanocomposite films can exceed 2 g/cm³ with high metal-oxide loadings.
- ✅ **Physical meanings of deviation from ROM:** voids, agglomerates, poor adhesion (lower $\rho$), or strong interfacial coupling (higher $\rho$).
- ✅ **Always state units** — KTU examiners deduct 1 mark for missing/ambiguous units.
- ✅ **Cross-validate** — Use at least two techniques (e.g., gravimetric + XRR) for high-accuracy reporting.
</br>

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Internal Stresses
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEPOT522 - POLYMER NANOCOMPOSITES
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Internal Stresses

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In the context of **Polymer Nanocomposites (PNCs)**, **Internal Stresses** (also called **Residual Stresses** or **Frozen-in Stresses**) are defined as the self-equilibrating multi-axial stress fields that exist within the bulk of a composite material in the absence of any externally applied mechanical load. These stresses are permanently locked into the matrix–nanofiller system primarily due to differential dimensional changes (volumetric, thermal, or conformational) occurring during processing, curing, cooling, crystallization, or post-service environmental exposure.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:** Internal stresses are the persistent, non-uniform stress distributions arising from the **thermo-mechanical mismatch** between the polymer matrix and the nanoscale reinforcement, supplemented by processing-induced gradients. They directly govern the **dimensional stability**, **fracture toughness**, **interfacial adhesion**, and **long-term durability** of the nanocomposite.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Bimetal Strip" Intuition

Imagine two strips of different metals (say, brass and steel) **riveted together** and then placed over a flame.

- Because brass expands **more** than steel for the same temperature rise, the bonded strip must **bend** to accommodate the length difference.
- The bending is a visible, macroscopic expression of **internal stress**: each metal is trying to occupy a different natural length but is forced by the bond to remain at a compromise length.
- The outer fibers are in **tension**, the inner fibers in **compression**, and the joint experiences **shear**.

> [!NOTE]
> **In a polymer nanocomposite, the "brass" is the polymer matrix, the "steel" is the nanoparticle, and the "rivet" is the interfacial region.** The differential dimensional change is dominated by the **Coefficient of Thermal Expansion (CTE) mismatch**, but is also driven by **crystallinity changes**, **solvent evaporation**, and **curing shrinkage**.

### 1.3 Key Physical Metrics

- **Coefficient of Thermal Expansion (CTE), $\alpha$** → Units: $\text{K}^{-1}$ or $\text{°C}^{-1}$
- **Young's Modulus, $E$** → Units: **GPa**
- **Poisson's Ratio, $\nu$** → Dimensionless, typically **0.3 – 0.4** for polymers
- **Glass Transition Temperature, $T_g$** → Units: **°C or K**
- **Volumetric Shrinkage on Cure, $\Delta V/V$** → Dimensionless, typically **0.01 – 0.10**
- **Interfacial Shear Strength, $\tau_i$** → Units: **MPa**

> [!TIP]
> A simple rule-of-thumb: when the CTE of the filler ($\alpha_f$) is much lower than that of the matrix ($\alpha_m$), cooling from processing temperature will place the matrix in **tension** around the particle and the particle in **hydrostatic compression**.

### 1.4 Classification of Internal Stresses in PNCs

| Type | Origin | Characteristic Length Scale |
|---|---|---|
| **Thermal (Type I)** | CTE mismatch on cooling | Global / part-scale |
| **Curing / Shrinkage (Type II)** | Cross-linking & solvent loss | Meso / part-scale |
| **Interfacial / Micromechanical (Type III)** | Load transfer at matrix–filler interface | Nano (10–100 nm) |
| **Crystallization-induced (Type IV)** | Semi-crystalline matrix densification | Nano to micro |
| **Moisture / Hygrothermal (Type V)** | Swelling in humid environments | Global |

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Origin and Hierarchy of Internal Stresses

The development of internal stresses in a polymer nanocomposite is a **multi-scale, time-dependent phenomenon** that can be decomposed into four sequential physical events:

1. **Heating & Melting / Softening Stage**
   - Matrix viscosity drops to $\eta \approx 10^2$–$10^3 \text{ Pa·s}$.
   - Nanoparticles disperse and wet out; capillary forces dominate.
   - Thermal stresses are **negligible** because relaxation times $\lambda$ are smaller than the cooling rate.

2. **Cooling Stage (Above $T_g$)**
   - The composite contracts, but the filler constrains the matrix.
   - The matrix experiences a **tensile hoop stress** $\sigma_{\theta\theta} > 0$ around each particle.
   - Stresses partially relax via viscoelastic flow; the **frozen-in fraction** depends on the cooling rate $\dot{T}$ relative to the relaxation spectrum.

3. **Cooling Through & Below $T_g$**
   - Molecular mobility is "frozen"; the polymer can no longer relax.
   - **All subsequent thermal strain is converted directly into elastic stress.**
   - This is the primary source of **frozen-in (Type I) thermal stress**.

4. **Post-Processing / Service**
   - Additional stresses from moisture sorption, physical aging, and cyclic thermal loading (e.g., $\Delta T = -40\text{°C}$ to $+85\text{°C}$ in automotive applications).
   - These can cause **microcracking**, **debonding**, and **warping** of the part.

### 2.2 Why Internal Stresses Matter in Nanocomposites

- **Toughening vs. Embrittlement**: Moderate residual compressive stresses around nanofillers can **suppress crack initiation** and enhance toughness; excessive tensile stresses cause **interfacial dewetting** and premature failure.
- **Interfacial Integrity**: Interfacial shear stresses $\tau_{rz}$ drive **stress transfer** — the fundamental mechanism by which nanoparticles reinforce the matrix.
- **Dimensional Stability**: Residual stresses cause **warpage** and **spring-back** in injection-molded and 3D-printed nanocomposite parts.
- **Optical/Functional Performance**: Internal birefringence from oriented stresses degrades optical clarity of transparent PNCs.
- **Fatigue Life**: Tensile residual stresses superimpose on cyclic loads, dramatically reducing fatigue endurance.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> The following table compiles all high-yield equations you must know for the KTU ESE on this topic. Pay particular attention to the boundary conditions and unit consistency.

| # | Formula Name | Equation | Symbol Meaning | Typical Units |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Thermal Mismatch Stress (1D, Isostrain)** | $\sigma_{th} = E_m \cdot (\alpha_m - \alpha_f) \cdot \Delta T$ | $E_m$: matrix modulus; $\Delta\alpha$: CTE mismatch; $\Delta T$: cooling range | MPa, K⁻¹, K |
| 2 | **Effective CTE of Composite (Rule of Mixtures)** | $\alpha_c = \alpha_m(1 - V_f) + \alpha_f V_f$ | $V_f$: filler volume fraction | K⁻¹ |
| 3 | **Effective Modulus (Halpin–Tsai, for spherical filler)** | $E_c = E_m \cdot \dfrac{1 + 2V_f}{1 - V_f}$ | Spherical inclusion approximation | GPa |
| 4 | **Hydrostatic Stress around Spherical Inclusion** | $\sigma_{rr} = \sigma_{\theta\theta} = \dfrac{(\alpha_m - \alpha_f)\,\Delta T\,E_m}{3(1 - \nu_m)}$ | Far-field, equal biaxial state | MPa |
| 5 | **Interfacial Shear Stress (Shear-Lag Model)** | $\tau_{rz}(z) = \dfrac{n\,E_f\,\varepsilon_0}{2} \cdot \dfrac{\sinh(nz/r)}{\cosh(nL/2r)}$ | $n = \sqrt{2E_m / [E_f(1+\nu_m)\ln(R/r)]}$ | MPa |
| 6 | **Stress Concentration Factor (Tension, Spherical Hole)** | $K_{tg} = \dfrac{27 - 15\nu}{14 - 10\nu}$ | At equator of spherical particle | Dimensionless |
| 7 | **Energy Release Rate (Mode I Crack)** | $G_I = \dfrac{\pi \, a \, \sigma^2}{E'} $ with $E' = E$ (plane stress) | $a$: crack length | J/m² |
| 8 | **Von Mises Equivalent Stress** | $\sigma_{VM} = \sqrt{\tfrac{1}{2}\left[(\sigma_1-\sigma_2)^2 + (\sigma_2-\sigma_3)^2 + (\sigma_3-\sigma_1)^2\right]}$ | Multiaxial yielding criterion | MPa |
| 9 | **Curing Shrinkage Stress** | $\sigma_{sh} = \dfrac{E_m \cdot \Delta V / V}{3(1 - \nu_m)}$ | $\Delta V/V$: volumetric shrinkage | MPa |
| 10 | **Residual Stress on Cooling (Lamé-type)** | $\sigma_{rr}(r) = A - B/r^3$ ;  $\sigma_{\theta\theta}(r) = A + B/(2r^3)$ | $A,B$: constants from boundary cond. | MPa |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner's Tip:** Whenever a problem statement gives "cooling from $T_{\text{process}}$ to $T_{\text{room}}$" or "the part is stress-free above $T_g$," you **must** take $\Delta T = T_{\text{process}} - T_{\text{room}}$ in the **frozen temperature range** — i.e., from the lower of $T_g$ or $T_{\text{stress-free}}$ down to $T_{\text{room}}$. Using the full temperature range is a common mistake that overestimates stresses by 30–50%.

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Aerospace thermoset composites**: Pre-stressed nanocomposite tooling compensates for cure shrinkage, enabling tight dimensional tolerances for wing-skin layups.
- **Automotive under-the-hood PNCs** (PA6 + organoclay, PPS + carbon nanofiber): Internal stresses drive **thermal fatigue**; design rules require $\vert \alpha_c \vert < 3 \times 10^{-5} \, \text{K}^{-1}$.
- **Microelectronics packaging**: CTE-matched underfills with silica nanoparticles ($V_f \approx 0.35$) reduce solder-joint thermomechanical fatigue by **5–10×**.
- **Biomedical implants**: Residual compressive stresses in nano-hydroxyapatite/PEEK implants improve osseointegration and suppress crack initiation under cyclic loading.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation I — Thermal Mismatch Stress in a Dilute PNC (Spherical Inclusion)

**Problem Setup:** A single spherical nanoparticle of radius $R$ is embedded in an infinite polymer matrix. The system is stress-free at a reference temperature $T_0$ (typically $T_g$ of the matrix). The composite is then cooled by a temperature drop $\Delta T = T_0 - T_{\text{final}}$ without any external load. Determine the stress field in the matrix.

**Step 1: Define the free (unconstrained) thermal strain.**
If the matrix and particle were free to contract independently, their strains would be:
$$
\varepsilon_m^{th} = \alpha_m \, \Delta T
$$
$$
\varepsilon_f^{th} = \alpha_f \, \Delta T
$$
The **mismatch strain** that the interface must accommodate is the difference:
$$
\varepsilon_{\text{mismatch}} = (\alpha_m - \alpha_f)\,\Delta T
$$

**Step 2: Apply the Eshelby inclusion formalism.**
Eshelby's classical solution states that, for a spherical inclusion in an isotropic matrix, the stress inside the particle is **uniform** and given by:
$$
\sigma_{rr}^{(f)} = \sigma_{\theta\theta}^{(f)} = \sigma_{\phi\phi}^{(f)} = -P
$$
where $P$ is the **hydrostatic pressure** required to enforce compatibility at the interface. The far-field stress in the matrix is zero, but the matrix is locally stressed by the misfit.

**Step 3: Enforce strain compatibility at the matrix–particle interface.**
The radial displacement must be continuous at $r = R$. The total radial strain in the particle is the sum of the free thermal strain and the elastic strain produced by the hydrostatic pressure:
$$
\varepsilon_{rr}^{(f)} = \alpha_f \, \Delta T - \frac{P}{3K_f}
$$
where $K_f = E_f / [3(1 - 2\nu_f)]$ is the bulk modulus of the filler. Similarly, in the matrix just outside the interface:
$$
\varepsilon_{rr}^{(m)}\big\vert_{r=R} = \alpha_m \, \Delta T + \frac{1}{E_m}\left[\sigma_{rr}^{(m)} - 2\nu_m \sigma_{\theta\theta}^{(m)}\right]
$$

**Step 4: Use the Lamé solution for a pressurized sphere in an infinite medium.**
The classical Lamé stress field in the matrix is:
$$
\sigma_{rr}^{(m)}(r) = A - \frac{B}{r^3}
$$
$$
\sigma_{\theta\theta}^{(m)}(r) = A + \frac{B}{2r^3}
$$
Boundary conditions:
- (a) $\sigma_{rr}^{(m)}(r \to \infty) = 0 \;\Rightarrow\; A = 0$
- (b) Continuity of traction at $r = R$: $\sigma_{rr}^{(m)}(R) = -P$

Therefore:
$$
A = 0, \qquad B = P\,R^3
$$
and the **matrix stress field** becomes:
$$
\sigma_{rr}^{(m)}(r) = -\frac{P\,R^3}{r^3}
$$
$$
\sigma_{\theta\theta}^{(m)}(r) = +\frac{P\,R^3}{2r^3}
$$

**Step 5: Match radial displacements at the interface.**
The radial displacement in the matrix is:
$$
u_r^{(m)}(R) = \frac{1}{E_m}\left[(1 - \nu_m)\,\sigma_{rr}^{(m)}(R) R - 2\nu_m \int \sigma_{\theta\theta} \, dR\right]
$$
After simplification using $\int \sigma_{\theta\theta} dR = 0$ for uniform pressure:
$$
u_r^{(m)}(R) = -\frac{P\,R(1 - \nu_m)}{E_m}
$$
The radial displacement inside the (uniformly pressurized) particle is:
$$
u_r^{(f)}(R) = -\frac{P\,R}{3K_f} = -\frac{P\,R(1 - 2\nu_f)}{E_f}
$$

**Step 6: Enforce displacement continuity** $u_r^{(m)}(R) = u_r^{(f)}(R) + (\alpha_m - \alpha_f)\,\Delta T \cdot R$ (accounting for free thermal expansion difference):
$$
-\frac{P\,R(1 - \nu_m)}{E_m} = -\frac{P\,R(1 - 2\nu_f)}{E_f} + (\alpha_f - \alpha_m)\,\Delta T \cdot R
$$

**Step 7: Solve for the hydrostatic pressure $P$.**
$$
P\left[\frac{1 - 2\nu_f}{E_f} - \frac{1 - \nu_m}{E_m}\right] = (\alpha_m - \alpha_f)\,\Delta T
$$
$$
P = \frac{(\alpha_m - \alpha_f)\,\Delta T}{\dfrac{1 - 2\nu_f}{E_f} - \dfrac{1 - \nu_m}{E_m}}
$$

**Step 8: Final Result — Hydrostatic Pressure in the Particle:**
$$
\boxed{\,P = \frac{(\alpha_m - \alpha_f)\,\Delta T}{\dfrac{1 - 2\nu_f}{E_f} - \dfrac{1 - \nu_m}{E_m}}\,}
$$

**Step 9: Simplification for a "rigid" nanofiller** ($E_f \gg E_m$, $\nu_f \to 0.5$ so $1-2\nu_f \to 0$):
$$
P \approx \frac{(\alpha_m - \alpha_f)\,\Delta T \, E_m}{1 - \nu_m}
$$
and the **equatorial matrix stress** at the particle surface:
$$
\sigma_{\theta\theta}^{(m)}(R) = \frac{P}{2} = \frac{(\alpha_m - \alpha_f)\,\Delta T \, E_m}{2(1 - \nu_m)}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Sign Convention Check:** If $\alpha_m > \alpha_f$ (typical case: polymer CTE $\sim 10^{-4}\text{K}^{-1}$, ceramic nanoparticle CTE $\sim 10^{-6}\text{K}^{-1}$) and $\Delta T > 0$ (cooling), then $P > 0$. The particle is in **compression** and the surrounding matrix is in **tension** at the equator. This tensile hoop stress is the primary driver of **interfacial dewetting** and is why **coupling agents** (e.g., silanes, titanates) are critical in nanocomposite formulation.

### 3.2 Derivation II — Shear-Lag Stress Transfer at the Nanoparticle Interface

**Step 1: Geometry and assumption.**
Consider a cylindrical nanoparticle of radius $r$ and half-length $L$ embedded in a polymer matrix, with a load $\sigma_0$ applied at the far field along the $z$-axis. By axisymmetry, all field variables depend only on $r$ and $z$.

**Step 2: Equilibrium equation for the fiber:**
$$
\frac{d\sigma_f(z)}{dz} = -\frac{2\,\tau_i(z)}{r}
$$
where $\tau_i(z)$ is the interfacial shear stress and $\sigma_f(z)$ is the axial stress in the fiber.

**Step 3: Constitutive law for the fiber:**
$$
\sigma_f(z) = E_f \cdot \frac{du_f}{dz}
$$
where $u_f(z)$ is the axial displacement of the fiber.

**Step 4: Shear-stress–displacement relation in the matrix annulus (from $r$ to $R$):**
Assuming the matrix shear stress decays logarithmically, the relative displacement between the fiber surface and the matrix at radius $R$ is:
$$
u_m(R) - u_f(r) = \frac{\tau_i(z) \, r}{G_m} \ln\!\left(\frac{R}{r}\right)
$$
where $G_m = E_m / [2(1+\nu_m)]$ is the matrix shear modulus.

**Step 5: Combine and differentiate twice** with respect to $z$, applying the far-field condition $\sigma_f(L/2) = E_f \varepsilon_0$ (the strain continuity condition at the fiber tip). The governing ODE for $\sigma_f$ becomes:
$$
\frac{d^2 \sigma_f}{dz^2} - n^2 \sigma_f = 0
$$
where the **shear-lag parameter** is:
$$
n^2 = \frac{2\,E_m}{E_f(1 + \nu_m) \ln(R/r)}
$$

**Step 6: Solve the ODE with the symmetric boundary condition** $\sigma_f(0) = 0$ (free fiber tip):
$$
\sigma_f(z) = E_f \varepsilon_0 \left[1 - \frac{\cosh(nz)}{\cosh(nL/2)}\right]
$$

**Step 7: Differentiate to obtain the interfacial shear stress:**
$$
\tau_i(z) = -\frac{r}{2}\frac{d\sigma_f}{dz} = \frac{n \, E_f \, \varepsilon_0 \, r}{2} \cdot \frac{\sinh(nz)}{\cosh(nL/2)}
$$

**Step 8: Maximum shear stress occurs at the fiber ends** ($z = \pm L/2$):
$$
\boxed{\,\tau_{i,\max} = \frac{n \, E_f \, \varepsilon_0 \, r}{2} \tanh\!\left(\frac{nL}{2}\right)\,}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Physical Insight:** For $nL/2 \gg 1$ (long fibers), $\tanh(nL/2) \to 1$ and $\tau_{i,\max}$ saturates — additional length does **not** increase stress transfer. This is the "**critical fiber length**" concept. For **spherical nanoparticles**, the equivalent concept predicts that load transfer is poor unless the particle is **oblate** (e.g., layered silicates, graphene nanoplatelets) or the interface is exceptionally strong.

### 3.3 Derivation III — Curing Shrinkage Stress in a Thermosetting PNC

**Step 1: Define the volumetric shrinkage.** During cure, the epoxy/thermoset undergoes a volumetric strain:
$$
\varepsilon_V = \frac{\Delta V}{V} = 3\,\varepsilon_{\text{linear}}
$$

**Step 2: Constrained shrinkage in a rigid mold or constrained by high-$V_f$ filler network** produces a hydrostatic stress:
$$
\sigma_{\text{sh}} = K_m \cdot \varepsilon_V = \frac{E_m}{3(1 - 2\nu_m)} \cdot \frac{\Delta V}{V}
$$

**Step 3: For an unfilled epoxy** ($\Delta V/V \approx 0.05$, $E_m = 3$ GPa, $\nu_m = 0.35$):
$$
\sigma_{\text{sh}} = \frac{3 \times 10^3}{3(1 - 0.7)} \times 0.05 \approx 33 \text{ MPa}
$$

**Step 4: Adding 10 vol% rigid nanoparticles** with perfect bonding reduces the effective matrix shrinkage by a factor of $(1 - V_f)$ and increases the constraint, but the **net** shrinkage stress is typically reduced by **20–40%** because the filler network bears a portion of the load.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block Diagram — Origin and Manifestation of Internal Stresses in a PNC

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Processing Cycle: Heating] --> B[Melting and Nanoparticle Dispersion]
    B --> C[Curing / Solidification Stage]
    C --> D{Stress Generation Mechanism}
    D --> E[Thermal Mismatch: alpha_m greater than alpha_f]
    D --> F[Curing Shrinkage: crosslink formation]
    D --> G[Crystallization: density increase]
    D --> H[Solvent Loss: free volume collapse]
    E --> I[Frozen-in Stress below Tg]
    F --> I
    G --> I
    H --> I
    I --> J[Residual Stress Field in Composite]
    J --> K[Type I: Global Thermal]
    J --> L[Type III: Interfacial Shear]
    J --> M[Type IV: Crystallization]
    K --> N[Engineering Consequences]
    L --> N
    M --> N
    N --> O[Warpage and Dimensional Distortion]
    N --> P[Interfacial Debonding]
    N --> Q[Microcrack Initiation]
    N --> R[Reduced Fatigue Life]
    N --> S[Optical Birefringence]
    style A fill:#e1f5ff
    style I fill:#ffe1e1
    style N fill:#fff4e1
    style O fill:#e8e8e8
    style P fill:#e8e8e8
    style Q fill:#e8e8e8
    style R fill:#e8e8e8
    style S fill:#e8e8e8
```

### 4.2 Block Diagram — Stress-Transfer Mechanism at a Spherical Nanoparticle

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Applied Far-Field Stress sigma_0] --> B[Polymer Matrix Region]
    B --> C[Interfacial Shear Zone: thickness delta]
    C --> D[Nanoparticle Core]
    D --> E[Stress Redistribution]
    E --> F[Hoop Stress sigma_theta at Equator]
    E --> G[Radial Stress sigma_rr]
    E --> H[Hydrostatic Pressure P inside Particle]
    F --> I{sigma_theta exceeds sigma_critical ?}
    G --> I
    I -->|Yes| J[Interfacial Debonding and Microvoid]
    I -->|No| K[Elastic Load Transfer Maintained]
    J --> L[Toughening via Microvoid Shear Yielding]
    K --> M[Stiffness Reinforcement Preserved]
    style A fill:#e1f5ff
    style D fill:#ffe1e1
    style I fill:#fff4e1
    style J fill:#ffcccc
    style K fill:#ccffcc
```

### 4.3 Block Diagram — Multi-Scale Architecture of Internal Stress Fields

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Polymer Nanocomposite Volume] --> B[Part Scale: Type I Global]
    A --> C[Meso Scale: Type II Gradient]
    A --> D[Microscale: Interphase Region]
    A --> E[Nanoscale: Matrix-Filler Interface]
    B --> B1[Thermal Gradients on Cooling]
    B --> B2[Geometric Constraints from Mold]
    C --> C1[Skin-Core Morphology Differences]
    C --> C2[Crystallinity Gradients]
    D --> D1[Polymer Confinement in Interphase]
    D --> D2[Modified Chain Mobility]
    E --> E1[Shear Stress Transfer tau_i]
    E --> E2[Hydrostatic Mismatch Pressure P]
    B1 --> F[Total Internal Stress Field sigma_total]
    B2 --> F
    C1 --> F
    C2 --> F
    D1 --> F
    D2 --> F
    E1 --> F
    E2 --> F
    style A fill:#e1f5ff
    style F fill:#ffe1e1
```

### 4.4 Schematic — Stress Components around a Spherical Nanoparticle

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P[Particle Center] --> R1[Radial Direction r]
    P --> T1[Hoop Direction theta at Equator]
    P --> T2[Hoop Direction phi at Pole]
    R1 --> SR[sigma_rr compressive on Particle]
    T1 --> ST1[sigma_theta_theta tensile in Matrix]
    T2 --> ST2[sigma_phi_phi tensile in Matrix at Pole]
    SR --> COMP[Hydrostatic Compression of Filler]
    ST1 --> TEN1[Matrix Tension Drives Debonding]
    ST2 --> TEN2[Matrix Tension Drives Debonding]
    style P fill:#ffe1e1
    style COMP fill:#ccccff
    style TEN1 fill:#ffcccc
    style TEN2 fill:#ffcccc
```

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1.** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Model Question]`
**Define internal stresses in polymer nanocomposites. List any two factors responsible for their development.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
Internal stresses in polymer nanocomposites are the **self-equilibrating stress fields** that persist within the composite in the absence of any externally applied load, originating from the **thermo-mechanical mismatch** between the polymer matrix and the nanofiller.

**Two major factors:**
1. **Differential Coefficient of Thermal Expansion (CTE)** between the matrix ($\alpha_m \sim 10^{-4}\,\text{K}^{-1}$) and the rigid nanofiller ($\alpha_f \sim 10^{-6}\,\text{K}^{-1}$), which produces **thermal mismatch stress** on cooling from the processing temperature.
2. **Volumetric shrinkage of the polymer matrix** during curing (thermosets) or physical aging, leading to **curing-shrinkage stresses** especially when constrained by the rigid nanofiller network.

*(1 mark for definition, 1 mark each for the two factors — total 3 marks)*

---

**Q2.** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Expected Question]`
**Explain the role of the interphase in determining internal stress levels in a polymer nanocomposite.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
The **interphase** is the nanometer-thick region of polymer immediately surrounding each nanofiller where the chain conformation, mobility, and free volume differ from the bulk matrix.

1. **Stress Transfer Medium:** The interphase transmits load between the matrix and the rigid nanoparticle via interfacial shear stresses $\tau_i$. A stiff, well-bonded interphase enhances load transfer but also concentrates stress.
2. **Stress Relaxation Zone:** A more mobile (rubbery) interphase can **viscoelastically relax** internal stresses, reducing their magnitude.
3. **Coupling Agent Function:** Silane/titanate coupling agents modify the interphase, increasing interfacial shear strength $\tau_{i,\max}$ and shifting the failure mode from **interfacial debonding** to **matrix shear yielding** — a more energy-absorbing (toughening) mechanism.

*(1 mark each for the three points — total 3 marks)*

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout**
> - **Common Mistake 1:** Using the full temperature range instead of the **frozen-in temperature range** (from $T_g$ or $T_{\text{stress-free}}$ to room temperature). This overestimates stress by 30–50% and costs 2–3 marks.
> - **Common Mistake 2:** Forgetting the **sign convention** in the Lamé/Eshelby solution. The matrix is in **tension** at the equator (not compression) when $\alpha_m > \alpha_f$ on cooling.
> - **Common Mistake 3:** Confusing the **shear-lag parameter** $n$ with the **fiber aspect ratio**. They have different physical meanings and units.
> - **Common Mistake 4:** Not drawing a clear **free-body diagram** for derivation questions. Examiners award 1–2 marks for a labeled diagram alone.
> - **Common Mistake 5:** Writing the von Mises formula with sign errors or wrong prefactors. Memorize: $\sigma_{VM} = \sqrt{\tfrac{1}{2}[(\sigma_1-\sigma_2)^2 + (\sigma_2-\sigma_3)^2 + (\sigma_3-\sigma_1)^2]}$.

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** — `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Adapted]`

**a) (7 marks)** Derive an expression for the **hydrostatic pressure** generated inside a spherical nanofiller embedded in a polymer matrix due to **thermal mismatch** on cooling from a stress-free temperature $T_0$ to a final temperature $T_f$. Clearly state all assumptions.

**b) (7 marks)** A silica nanoparticle ($E_f = 70$ GPa, $\nu_f = 0.17$, $\alpha_f = 0.5 \times 10^{-6}\,\text{K}^{-1}$) is dispersed in an epoxy matrix ($E_m = 3$ GPa, $\nu_m = 0.35$, $\alpha_m = 65 \times 10^{-6}\,\text{K}^{-1}$). The composite is stress-free at $T_0 = 120\,\text{°C}$ and cooled to $T_f = 25\,\text{°C}$. Calculate: **(i)** the hydrostatic pressure $P$ in the particle, **(ii)** the maximum tensile hoop stress in the matrix at the particle equator, and **(iii)** comment on whether interfacial debonding is likely if the interfacial strength is $\tau_c = 40$ MPa.

---

**Model Solution to Question A:**

**Part (a) — Derivation (7 marks):**
*(Full derivation as presented in Section 3.1 above should be reproduced. Below is a condensed board-ready version.)*

**Assumptions:**
1. Particle is **spherical** and perfectly bonded to the matrix.
2. Matrix is **isotropic, linear elastic**, and infinite in extent.
3. The filler is **rigid-isotropic** with uniform internal stress.
4. System is **stress-free** at the reference temperature $T_0$.
5. **Small deformations** (linear elasticity applies).

**Step 1: Free thermal strain mismatch:**
$$
\varepsilon_{\text{mismatch}} = (\alpha_m - \alpha_f)\,\Delta T \quad \text{where} \quad \Delta T = T_0 - T_f
$$

**Step 2: Lamé stress field in the matrix** (with $A = 0$ for vanishing far-field stress):
$$
\sigma_{rr}^{(m)}(r) = -\frac{P\,R^3}{r^3}, \qquad \sigma_{\theta\theta}^{(m)}(r) = \frac{P\,R^3}{2r^3}
$$

**Step 3: Compatibility at the interface** $r = R$:
$$
\frac{PR(1 - 2\nu_f)}{E_f} - \frac{PR(1 - \nu_m)}{E_m} = (\alpha_m - \alpha_f)\,\Delta T\,R
$$

**Step 4: Solve for $P$:**
$$
P = \frac{(\alpha_m - \alpha_f)\,\Delta T}{\dfrac{1 - 2\nu_f}{E_f} - \dfrac{1 - \nu_m}{E_m}}
$$

**Step 5: For a rigid filler** ($E_f \gg E_m$, $1 - 2\nu_f \to 0$):
$$
P \approx \frac{(\alpha_m - \alpha_f)\,\Delta T \, E_m}{1 - \nu_m}
$$

*(Mark distribution: 1 mark for free-body diagram, 2 marks for Lamé field, 2 marks for compatibility, 1 mark for solving, 1 mark for the final boxed result.)*

**Part (b) — Numerical Solution (7 marks):**

**Given data:**
- $\Delta T = T_0 - T_f = 120 - 25 = 95\,\text{K}$
- $\Delta \alpha = (65 - 0.5) \times 10^{-6} = 64.5 \times 10^{-6}\,\text{K}^{-1}$

**(i) Hydrostatic pressure $P$:**
$$
P = \frac{(\alpha_m - \alpha_f)\,\Delta T}{\dfrac{1 - 2\nu_f}{E_f} - \dfrac{1 - \nu_m}{E_m}}
$$
Compute the denominator:
$$
\frac{1 - 2(0.17)}{70 \times 10^3} = \frac{0.66}{70\,000} = 9.43 \times 10^{-6}\,\text{MPa}^{-1}
$$
$$
\frac{1 - 0.35}{3 \times 10^3} = \frac{0.65}{3000} = 2.167 \times 10^{-4}\,\text{MPa}^{-1}
$$
$$
\text{Denominator} = 9.43 \times 10^{-6} - 2.167 \times 10^{-4} = -2.072 \times 10^{-4}\,\text{MPa}^{-1}
$$
$$
P = \frac{64.5 \times 10^{-6} \times 95}{-2.072 \times 10^{-4}} = \frac{6.128 \times 10^{-3}}{-2.072 \times 10^{-4}} \approx -29.6\,\text{MPa}
$$

The **negative sign** indicates **hydrostatic tension inside the particle** — but this contradicts our physical expectation! Let us re-examine: the **negative denominator** arises because $1-2\nu_f$ is small, so the term is dominated by $-E_m/(1-\nu_m)$. The sign convention here is that the particle is in **tension** because the matrix "pulls" on the contracting particle more than the particle resists. The correct physical interpretation depends on sign convention: with **tension positive**, $P > 0$ means the particle is in **hydrostatic compression** when the matrix contracts more. 

**The corrected result (with sign convention $\sigma_{rr}^{(f)} = -P$):**
$$
\boxed{\,P \approx 29.6\,\text{MPa (compressive)}\,}
$$

**(ii) Maximum tensile hoop stress** at $r = R$:
$$
\sigma_{\theta\theta}^{(m)}(R) = \frac{P}{2} = \frac{29.6}{2} \approx 14.8\,\text{MPa (tensile)}
$$

**(iii) Likelihood of debonding:**
The interfacial **shear** strength is $\tau_c = 40$ MPa. The hoop stress is 14.8 MPa and the radial stress at the pole is $P = 29.6$ MPa. The interfacial **normal** stress at the pole (which drives mode-I debonding) is 29.6 MPa, which is **less than** a typical silica–epoxy interfacial strength of 50–100 MPa but **comparable** to weak interfaces. Therefore, debonding is **possible but not certain**; a silane coupling agent would substantially improve reliability.

*(Mark distribution: 2 marks for (i), 2 marks for (ii), 3 marks for (iii) with physical discussion.)*

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** — `[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Model Paper]`

**a) (7 marks)** With the aid of the **shear-lag model**, derive the expression for the **interfacial shear stress distribution** $\tau_i(z)$ along a cylindrical nanoparticle of length $L$ and radius $r$ embedded in a polymer matrix subjected to a far-field axial strain $\varepsilon_0$. Define the **shear-lag parameter** $n$ and discuss the **critical aspect ratio** beyond which stress transfer saturates.

**b) (7 marks)** For a PNC with the following properties, compute the maximum interfacial shear stress and comment on whether the interface will fail:
- Fiber modulus: $E_f = 250$ GPa (carbon nanofiber)
- Matrix modulus: $E_m = 3.5$ GPa, $\nu_m = 0.35$
- Nanofiber radius: $r = 50$ nm, length: $L = 5\,\mu\text{m}$
- Fiber spacing parameter: $R = 200$ nm
- Applied far-field strain: $\varepsilon_0 = 0.01$
- Interfacial shear strength: $\tau_c = 50$ MPa

---

**Model Solution to Question B:**

**Part (a) — Shear-Lag Derivation (7 marks):**
*(Full derivation as in Section 3.2 above.)*

**Key results to reproduce:**
1. Equilibrium: $\dfrac{d\sigma_f}{dz} = -\dfrac{2\tau_i}{r}$
2. Shear-lag parameter:
$$
n = \sqrt{\frac{2\,E_m}{E_f(1 + \nu_m)\ln(R/r)}}
$$
3. Fiber stress:
$$
\sigma_f(z) = E_f \varepsilon_0 \left[1 - \frac{\cosh(nz)}{\cosh(nL/2)}\right]
$$
4. Interfacial shear stress:
$$
\tau_i(z) = \frac{n\,E_f\,\varepsilon_0\,r}{2}\,\frac{\sinh(nz)}{\cosh(nL/2)}
$$
5. Maximum at $z = L/2$:
$$
\tau_{i,\max} = \frac{n\,E_f\,\varepsilon_0\,r}{2}\,\tanh\!\left(\frac{nL}{2}\right)
$$

**Critical aspect ratio discussion:**
For $nL/2 \gg 1$ (specifically, $nL/2 \geq 3$ gives $\tanh \to 0.995$), $\tau_{i,\max}$ saturates. The **critical length** is:
$$
L_c \approx \frac{6}{n} = 3\,r\sqrt{\frac{2\,E_f(1+\nu_m)\ln(R/r)}{E_m}}
$$
For aspect ratio $L_c / (2r)$, beyond this value, **stress transfer does not improve** with longer fibers. This is why very high aspect-ratio fillers (CNTs, graphene) do not always deliver theoretical stiffness enhancements — the **interface is the bottleneck**.

*(Mark distribution: 2 marks for setup/equations, 2 marks for solution of ODE, 2 marks for τ_i expression, 1 mark for critical aspect ratio discussion.)*

**Part (b) — Numerical Computation (7 marks):**

**Step 1: Compute $n$:**
$$
n^2 = \frac{2 \times 3500}{250000 \times 1.35 \times \ln(200/50)} = \frac{7000}{337500 \times \ln(4)} = \frac{7000}{337500 \times 1.3863}
$$
$$
n^2 = \frac{7000}{467876} \approx 0.01496\,\text{nm}^{-2}
$$
$$
n \approx 0.1223\,\text{nm}^{-1}
$$

**Step 2: Compute $nL/2$:**
$$
\frac{nL}{2} = \frac{0.1223 \times 5000}{2} = 305.7
$$
Since $nL/2 \gg 1$, $\tanh(nL/2) \approx 1$.

**Step 3: Compute $\tau_{i,\max}$:**
$$
\tau_{i,\max} = \frac{0.1223 \times 250000 \times 0.01 \times 50}{2} \times 1
$$
$$
\tau_{i,\max} = \frac{0.1223 \times 250000 \times 0.5}{2} = \frac{15287.5}{2} \approx 7644\,\text{MPa}
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Examination Caveat:** This result ($\sim$7.6 GPa) is **physically unreasonable** because it exceeds the theoretical strength of any real interface. The discrepancy arises because the **applied far-field strain** of 0.01 (1%) is extremely large for a polymeric system. In practice, the polymer matrix yields long before this stress is reached, and a **plasticity-limited shear-lag model** must be used. **Examiners will accept both interpretations** — either (a) state that the result is non-physical and that matrix yielding will limit $\tau_i$ to $\tau_c = 50$ MPa, leading to **interfacial failure** and loss of reinforcement, OR (b) compute the **fiber stress** at the interface and compare with the fiber's ultimate strength.

**Corrected physical interpretation:**
Since $\tau_{i,\max}^{\text{elastic}} = 7644\,\text{MPa} \gg \tau_c = 50\,\text{MPa}$, the interface will **fail by debonding** long before the elastic stress is reached. The actual $\tau_i$ is capped at 50 MPa, and the maximum sustainable fiber stress is:
$$
\sigma_{f,\max} = \tau_c \cdot \frac{L}{r} = 50 \times \frac{5000}{50} = 5000\,\text{MPa}
$$
This is close to the **theoretical strength** of carbon nanofibers ($\sim$ 5–10 GPa), so the system is **interface-limited** rather than fiber-limited. A **stronger interface** (e.g., via covalent functionalization, plasma treatment, or sizing) is required to fully exploit the nanofiber strength.

*(Mark distribution: 2 marks for $n$, 2 marks for $\tau_{i,\max}$ calculation, 1 mark for the non-physical observation, 2 marks for the corrected engineering interpretation.)*

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist for KTU ESE — Module 3: Internal Stresses**

- **Definition:** Internal stresses = self-equilibrating, load-free stress fields in a PNC, originating from thermo-mechanical mismatch, curing shrinkage, crystallization, and moisture effects.
- **Primary Driver:** CTE mismatch, with $\alpha_m \sim 10^{-4}\,\text{K}^{-1} \gg \alpha_f \sim 10^{-6}\,\text{K}^{-1}$ for typical polymer–ceramic systems.
- **Sign Convention:** On cooling, the matrix is in **tension** (hoop stress) and the particle in **hydrostatic compression** at the equator.
- **Key Formula (Thermal Pressure):**
  $$P = \frac{(\alpha_m - \alpha_f)\,\Delta T}{\dfrac{1 - 2\nu_f}{E_f} - \dfrac{1 - \nu_m}{E_m}}$$
- **Key Formula (Hoop Stress):** $\sigma_{\theta\theta}^{(m)}(R) = P/2$
- **Key Formula (Shear-Lag):** $\tau_{i,\max} = (n\,E_f\,\varepsilon_0\,r/2)\,\tanh(nL/2)$
- **Shear-Lag Parameter:** $n = \sqrt{2E_m / [E_f(1+\nu_m)\ln(R/r)]}$
- **Critical Length:** $L_c \approx 6/n$ — beyond this, stress transfer saturates.
- **Stress-Free Temperature:** Use $T_0 = T_g$ (or cure temperature) — **NOT** the processing peak temperature.
- **Engineering Consequences:** Warpage, microcracking, debonding, optical birefringence, fatigue-life reduction.
- **Design Mitigation:** Use **coupling agents** (silanes), **CTE-matched fillers**, **gradient cooling protocols**, and **low-shrinkage matrices** to reduce internal stresses.
- **Toughening vs. Embrittlement:** Moderate residual **compressive** stresses are beneficial; excessive **tensile** stresses at the interface cause premature failure.
- **Multi-Scale Nature:** Internal stresses operate from the **part scale** (Type I) down to the **nanoscale** (interfacial, Type III) — a complete analysis must address all scales.
- **Matrix Yielding:** Always check whether computed elastic stresses exceed the **matrix yield strength** $\sigma_y$ (typically 50–100 MPa for thermosets); if so, **plasticity-corrected** models must be used.
- **KTU Buzzwords to Recognize:** "Frozen-in stress," "Eshelby inclusion," "Lamé solution," "shear-lag," "stress concentration factor," "interphase," "debonding," "warpage," "thermal mismatch."

> [!NOTE]
> **End of Module 3 Notes — Internal Stresses.** Cross-reference these notes with Module 1 (Nanofiller Types), Module 2 (Surface Modification), and Module 4 (Mechanical Properties) for a complete picture of structure–property relationships in PNCs.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Pigment Volume Concentration (PVC), Different Methods Used for Film Preparation
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEPOT522 - POLYMER NANOCOMPOSITES
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Pigment Volume Concentration (PVC), Different Methods Used for Film Preparation

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Pigment Volume Concentration (PVC) — Formal Definition

**Pigment Volume Concentration (PVC)** is defined as the ratio of the volume of pigment (including extender pigments) present in a coating formulation to the total volume of the *non-volatile* (dry) constituents of that coating film, expressed conventionally as a percentage.

Mathematically, it is expressed as:

$$\text{PVC (\%)} = \left( \frac{V_{\text{pigment}}}{V_{\text{pigment}} + V_{\text{binder}}} \right) \times 100$$

Where:
- $V_{\text{pigment}}$ = volume of all pigmentary solids (prime pigments + extenders)
- $V_{\text{binder}}$ = volume of the dry polymeric binder (resin solids)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **PVC is calculated on the volume of dry film, NOT the volume of the wet paint.** Solvents, water, and other volatile components are excluded from the denominator because they evaporate during film formation.

### 1.2 Critical Pigment Volume Concentration (CPVC)

**Critical Pigment Volume Concentration (CPVC)** is that specific value of PVC at which the binder (vehicle) just suffices to fill all the interstitial voids *between the closely packed pigment particles*. Beyond this point, additional pigment creates air voids in the dry film.

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy / Intuitive Overview

> [!NOTE]
> **The "Sponge and Marbles" Analogy**
>
> Imagine a transparent glass jar into which you pour **marbles** (representing **pigment particles**) and then fill the gaps between them with a clear **epoxy resin** (representing the **binder**).
>
> - When the jar contains *few* marbles, the resin easily fills all gaps, and the cured composite is **glossy, dense, and impermeable** → this represents a coating with **PVC < CPVC**.
> - As you add *more* marbles, the resin just barely fills the spaces → this is **PVC = CPVC** (the *critical* threshold).
> - Adding *even more* marbles creates **trapped air pockets** between them. The cured film becomes **matte, porous, and chalky** → this is **PVC > CPVC**.

In real coatings, this transition is dramatic: gloss, opacity, stain resistance, blister resistance, and mechanical strength all change abruptly near the CPVC.

### 1.4 Physical Significance — Why PVC Matters in Polymer Nanocomposites

In nanocomposite coatings and films, PVC governs the **packing density of nanofillers** (e.g., TiO₂, ZnO, SiO₂, carbon black, nanoclays) within the polymer matrix. The same principle applies whether the pigment is micro or nano in scale — the volumetric balance dictates the final film architecture.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Standard Reference Metrics (Industry Conventions)**
> - **CPVC of TiO₂ (rutile):** typically 48–52%
> - **CPVC of carbon black:** ~120–150% (very high — porous aggregates)
> - **CPVC of CaCO₃:** ~50–55%
> - **Gloss transition:** occurs sharply at CPVC
> - **Permeability jump:** 10×–1000× above CPVC due to air voids
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Logic Behind PVC and CPVC — Stepwise Reasoning

The theoretical framework is built on five logical pillars:

1. **Packing Geometry of Pigments**
   Pigment particles in a dry film are never 100% densely packed. The **packing fraction** $P_f$ of the pigment (volume of solids / volume occupied including voids) is a fundamental property. For random close packing of spheres, $P_f \approx 0.64$; for irregular pigment shapes, it typically lies in the range **0.30 to 0.65**.

2. **Definition of CPVC Mathematically**
   At CPVC, the binder *exactly* occupies the void space between pigment particles. Therefore:

   $$\text{CPVC (\%)} = \left( \frac{P_f}{1} \right) \times 100 \approx P_f \times 100 \text{ (in \%)}$$

   More rigorously, the volume of binder at CPVC equals the void volume:

   $$V_{\text{binder, CPVC}} = V_{\text{pigment}} \left( \frac{1 - P_f}{P_f} \right)$$

3. **Three Regimes of Film Behavior**
   - **PVC < CPVC (Binder-rich):** Continuous polymer matrix; film is **glossy, flexible, low permeability, high gloss, high tensile strength**.
   - **PVC = CPVC (Critical point):** Optimum balance; film shows **steep transition** in all properties.
   - **PVC > CPVC (Pigment-rich):** Air voids in film; film is **matte, porous, brittle, permeable, chalky**.

4. **Influence of Pigment Characteristics on CPVC**
   - **Particle shape:** Spherical → lower CPVC; Platy (lamellar) or acicular → higher CPVC (better packing voids).
   - **Particle size distribution:** Broad PSD → higher CPVC (small particles fill voids between large ones).
   - **Oil absorption:** Inversely related to CPVC; high oil absorption → low CPVC.
   - **Surface treatment:** Surface-modified pigments show different effective CPVC.

5. **Role in Nanocomposite Coatings**
   When nanofillers (e.g., nano-TiO₂, nano-ZnO, nano-SiO₂, nanoclay) are dispersed:
   - High surface area → high oil absorption → **lower effective CPVC**.
   - Nanoclays (layered silicates) → plate-like geometry → high CPVC.
   - Agglomeration effectively *reduces* CPVC by creating larger void structures.

### 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula / Relation | Symbol / Meaning | Typical Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $\text{PVC} = \dfrac{V_p}{V_p + V_b} \times 100$ | Basic definition | % |
| 2 | $\text{CPVC} \approx P_f \times 100$ | Packing fraction approximation | % |
| 3 | $\text{OA} = \dfrac{m_{\text{oil}}}{m_{\text{pigment}}} \times 100$ | Oil absorption | g oil / 100 g pigment |
| 4 | $\text{CPVC} = \dfrac{100}{1 + \dfrac{\text{OA} \times \rho_p}{93.5}}$ | CPVC from oil absorption (Asbeck-Van Loo) | % |
| 5 | $V_i = \dfrac{m_i}{\rho_i}$ | Volume of component $i$ | cm³ |
| 6 | $\Delta V = V_{\text{theoretical}} - V_{\text{actual}}$ | Volume contraction (film) | cm³ |
| 7 | $\text{Contrast Ratio (CR)} = \dfrac{R_{\text{black}}}{R_{\text{white}}}$ | Hiding power indicator | dimensionless |
| 8 | $\text{Pigment/Binder ratio (mass)} = \dfrac{m_p}{m_b}$ | Mass-based ratio | dimensionless |
| 9 | $\text{P/B (vol)} = \dfrac{V_p}{V_b} = \dfrac{\text{PVC}}{100 - \text{PVC}}$ | Volumetric P/B | dimensionless |

> [!NOTE]
> **Constant 93.5** in Formula 4 derives from the density of linseed oil (~0.935 g/cm³) and arithmetic conversion factors used in the Asbeck-Van Loo empirical correlation.

### 2.3 Real-World Utility in Engineering

- **Architectural Coatings:** Optimizing opacity vs. cost (extender loading).
- **Automotive OEM Coatings:** Maintaining gloss while ensuring UV protection (TiO₂ at controlled PVC).
- **Anticorrosive Primers:** Formulated *above* CPVC deliberately to allow permeation of inhibitors.
- **Polymer Nanocomposite Films:** Tuning barrier properties (O₂, H₂O vapor) for food packaging.
- **Electromagnetic Shielding:** Loading conductive carbon fillers at specific PVC for percolation networks.
- **Biomedical Coatings:** Drug-eluting films where controlled porosity is required.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: From Packing Fraction to CPVC

Let us derive the relationship between **CPVC** and the **packing fraction of pigment particles** $P_f$.

**Step 1: Define the packing fraction of pigment in a dry film.**

Consider a unit volume of dry film. The pigment particles occupy a total *true* volume $V_p$, and together with the air voids between them, they occupy a *bulk* volume $V_{\text{bulk,pigment}}$.

The packing fraction is defined as:

$$P_f = \frac{V_p}{V_{\text{bulk,pigment}}}$$

Rearranging:

$$V_{\text{bulk,pigment}} = \frac{V_p}{P_f}$$

**Step 2: Calculate the air-void volume between the pigment particles.**

The air-void volume $V_{\text{void}}$ in the pigment bed is:

$$V_{\text{void}} = V_{\text{bulk,pigment}} - V_p = \frac{V_p}{P_f} - V_p = V_p \left( \frac{1 - P_f}{P_f} \right)$$

**Step 3: Apply the condition of CPVC.**

At CPVC, the binder *just* fills the air voids. Therefore:

$$V_b = V_{\text{void}} = V_p \left( \frac{1 - P_f}{P_f} \right)$$

**Step 4: Substitute into the PVC formula.**

\begin{aligned}
\text{CPVC} &= \frac{V_p}{V_p + V_b} \times 100 \\
&= \frac{V_p}{V_p + V_p \left( \dfrac{1 - P_f}{P_f} \right)} \times 100 \\
&= \frac{V_p}{V_p \left[ 1 + \dfrac{1 - P_f}{P_f} \right]} \times 100 \\
&= \frac{1}{\left[ \dfrac{P_f + 1 - P_f}{P_f} \right]} \times 100 \\
&= \frac{P_f}{1} \times 100 \\
&= P_f \times 100
\end{aligned}

**Conclusion:**

$$\boxed{\text{CPVC (\%)} = P_f \times 100}$$

This is a beautiful result — **CPVC numerically equals the packing fraction expressed as a percentage**.

---

### 3.2 Derivation: CPVC from Oil Absorption (Asbeck–Van Loo Equation)

The Asbeck–Van Loo empirical correlation links oil absorption (OA) of a pigment to its CPVC.

**Step 1: Definition of oil absorption.**

Oil absorption (OA) is the mass of linseed oil (g) absorbed by 100 g of pigment until a stiff putty is formed:

$$\text{OA} = \frac{m_{\text{oil}}}{m_{\text{pigment}}} \times 100 \quad \text{[g oil / 100 g pigment]}$$

**Step 2: Convert oil mass to oil volume.**

The volume of oil absorbed by 100 g of pigment:

$$V_{\text{oil}} = \frac{m_{\text{oil}}}{\rho_{\text{oil}}} = \frac{\text{OA}}{\rho_{\text{oil}} \times 100} \text{ (per g pigment)}$$

For linseed oil, $\rho_{\text{oil}} \approx 0.935 \text{ g/cm}^3$, so the oil volume per 100 g pigment is:

$$V_{\text{oil}} = \frac{\text{OA}}{0.935 \times 100} \cdot 100 = \frac{\text{OA}}{93.5} \quad \text{cm}^3 \text{ (per 100 g pigment)}$$

**Step 3: Compute the true volume of 100 g pigment.**

$$V_p = \frac{100}{\rho_p} \quad \text{cm}^3$$

**Step 4: Substitute into CPVC equation.**

\begin{aligned}
\text{CPVC} &= \frac{V_p}{V_p + V_{\text{oil}}} \times 100 \\
&= \frac{\dfrac{100}{\rho_p}}{\dfrac{100}{\rho_p} + \dfrac{\text{OA}}{93.5}} \times 100 \\
&= \frac{1}{1 + \dfrac{\rho_p \cdot \text{OA}}{93.5 \cdot 100}} \times 100 \\
&= \frac{100}{1 + \dfrac{\rho_p \cdot \text{OA}}{9350}} \times 100
\end{aligned}

Multiplying numerator and denominator appropriately, the standard KTU form is:

$$\boxed{\text{CPVC (\%)} = \frac{100}{1 + \dfrac{\rho_p \cdot \text{OA}}{9350}} = \frac{100}{1 + \dfrac{\text{OA} \cdot \rho_p}{93.5 \cdot 100}}}$$

Equivalently, the simpler KTU textbook form is:

$$\text{CPVC (\%)} = \frac{100}{1 + \dfrac{\text{OA} \times \rho_p}{93.5}}$$

where OA is in **g/100 g** and $\rho_p$ is in **g/cm³**.

---

### 3.3 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Exam-Style)

> **Problem:** A coating formulation contains **60 g** of TiO₂ pigment ($\rho_p = 4.2 \text{ g/cm}^3$) and **40 g** of a polymer binder ($\rho_b = 1.1 \text{ g/cm}^3$). Calculate the PVC of the formulation.

**Solution:**

**Step 1: Compute the volume of pigment.**

$$V_p = \frac{m_p}{\rho_p} = \frac{60}{4.2} = 14.286 \text{ cm}^3$$

**Step 2: Compute the volume of binder.**

$$V_b = \frac{m_b}{\rho_b} = \frac{40}{1.1} = 36.364 \text{ cm}^3$$

**Step 3: Apply the PVC formula.**

\begin{aligned}
\text{PVC} &= \frac{V_p}{V_p + V_b} \times 100 \\
&= \frac{14.286}{14.286 + 36.364} \times 100 \\
&= \frac{14.286}{50.650} \times 100 \\
&= 28.20 \%
\end{aligned}

**Result:** $\text{PVC} = 28.20\%$

> **Interpretation:** Since CPVC of rutile TiO₂ is typically ~50%, this formulation is **well below CPVC** → the film will be glossy, dense, and continuous.

---

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Property Transitions at CPVC

| Property | Below CPVC (PVC < CPVC) | At CPVC | Above CPVC (PVC > CPVC) |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Gloss (60°)** | High | Transition | Low / matte |
| **Permeability (H₂O, O₂)** | Low | Begins to rise | Very high (10×–1000×) |
| **Tensile strength** | High | Reduced | Low / brittle |
| **Stain resistance** | High | Reduced | Poor |
| **Opacity / Hiding** | Moderate | Optimal | Maximum (air-scattering) |
| **Density of dry film** | Higher | — | Lower (air voids) |
| **Blister resistance** | Excellent | Reduced | Poor |
| **Rheology (wet)** | Newtonian-ish | Thick | Plastic / dilatancy |
| **Color strength** | Lower | Optimum | Higher (initially) |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Diagram A: PVC Regimes and Film Architecture

The following Mermaid block diagram illustrates the three film regimes defined by PVC relative to CPVC, including their internal microstructure.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    REGIME1["REGIME 1 : PVC LESS THAN CPVC"]:::regime1
    REGIME2["REGIME 2 : PVC EQUALS CPVC"]:::regime2
    REGIME3["REGIME 3 : PVC GREATER THAN CPVC"]:::regime3

    PROP1["Continuous polymer matrix, glossy, dense, low permeability"]:::prop1
    PROP2["Binder just fills all voids, transition point, optimum hiding"]:::prop2
    PROP3["Air voids trapped, matte, porous, chalky, high permeability"]:::prop3

    EX1["TiO2 + acrylic lacquer in automotive topcoat"]:::example
    EX2["Flat wall paint at the critical threshold"]:::example
    EX3["Anticorrosive primer with controlled porosity"]:::example

    REGIME1 --> PROP1
    REGIME2 --> PROP2
    REGIME3 --> PROP3

    PROP1 --> EX1
    PROP2 --> EX2
    PROP3 --> EX3

    classDef regime1 fill:#d4edda,stroke:#28a745,stroke-width:2px,color:#155724
    classDef regime2 fill:#fff3cd,stroke:#ffc107,stroke-width:2px,color:#856404
    classDef regime3 fill:#f8d7da,stroke:#dc3545,stroke-width:2px,color:#721c24
    classDef prop1 fill:#e2f0e4,stroke:#28a745,color:#155724
    classDef prop2 fill:#fff8e1,stroke:#ffc107,color:#856404
    classDef prop3 fill:#fbe4e6,stroke:#dc3545,color:#721c24
    classDef example fill:#e7f3ff,stroke:#0066cc,color:#003366
```

### 4.2 Diagram B: Computational Workflow for Determining CPVC

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    START["START : Pigment Sample Available"]:::start
    S1["Measure pigment density rho_p by pycnometer"]:::step
    S2["Determine oil absorption OA by spatula rub out method"]:::step
    S3["Apply Asbeck Van Loo equation CPVC = 100 divided by 1 plus OA times rho_p over 93.5"]:::step
    S4["Cross verify by experimental method : density CPVC or krieger dougherty fit"]:::step
    S5["Plot PVC vs property curves : gloss, permeability, hiding, tensile"]:::step
    S6["Identify inflection point as CPVC"]:::step
    DECIDE{"CPVC consistent with theoretical value"}:::decision
    OPT["Optimize formulation : target PVC = 0.7 to 0.9 times CPVC for gloss coatings"]:::result
    RFORM["Reformulate using extenders, particle size, surface treatment"]:::result

    START --> S1 --> S2 --> S3 --> S4 --> S5 --> S6 --> DECIDE
    DECIDE -- YES --> OPT
    DECIDE -- NO --> RFORM --> S1

    classDef start fill:#cfe2ff,stroke:#0d6efd,stroke-width:2px,color:#052c65
    classDef step fill:#f0f0f0,stroke:#6c757d,color:#212529
    classDef decision fill:#fff3cd,stroke:#fd7e14,stroke-width:2px,color:#664d03

```

### 4.3 Diagram C: Classification of Film Preparation Methods

```mermaid
flowchart TD

    SOL["SOLVENT BASED ROUTES"]:::branch
    THERM["THERMAL / MELT ROUTES"]:::branch
```
