```mermaid
    SPEC["SPECIALISED ROUTES"]:::branch

    SOL1["Solvent Casting"]:::method
    SOL2["Spin Coating"]:::method
    SOL3["Dip Coating"]:::method
    SOL4["Spray Coating"]:::method
    SOL5["Doctor Blade Film Applicator"]:::method
    SOL6["Drop Casting"]:::method

    THERM1["Hot Melt Extrusion + Calendaring"]:::method
    THERM2["Compression Molding"]:::method
    THERM3["Injection Molding (films)"]:::method
    THERM4["Melt Casting"]:::method
    THERM5["Blow Film Extrusion"]:::method

    UV1["UV Curing"]:::method
    UV2["Electron Beam Curing"]:::method

    SPEC1["In-situ Polymerization"]:::method
    SPEC2["Layer by Layer LBL Assembly"]:::method
    SPEC3["Electrospinning"]:::method
    SPEC4["Electrodeposition"]:::method
    SPEC5["Sol Gel Hybrid Film"]:::method

    ROOT --> SOL --> SOL1 & SOL2 & SOL3 & SOL4 & SOL5 & SOL6
    ROOT --> THERM --> THERM1 & THERM2 & THERM3 & THERM4 & THERM5
    ROOT --> UV --> UV1 & UV2
    ROOT --> SPEC --> SPEC1 & SPEC2 & SPEC3 & SPEC4 & SPEC5

    classDef root fill:#0d6efd,stroke:#052c65,stroke-width:3px,color:#ffffff
    classDef branch fill:#6610f2,stroke:#3d0a8c,stroke-width:2px,color:#ffffff
    classDef method fill:#f8f9fa,stroke:#6c757d,color:#212529
```

### 4.4 Brief Notes on Each Film Preparation Method

| Method | Principle | Typical Thickness | Best For Nanocomposites? |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Solvent Casting** | Polymer + nanofiller dissolved/dispersed in solvent, cast in mould, evaporated | 20–500 µm | Excellent for lab-scale uniform films |
| **Spin Coating** | Solution spun on rotating substrate; centrifugal force spreads film | 0.1–10 µm | Ideal for thin optical/electronic films |
| **Dip Coating** | Substrate withdrawn from solution reservoir at controlled speed | 1–50 µm | Uniform coatings on complex shapes |
| **Spray Coating** | Atomized dispersion sprayed by air/nozzle onto substrate | 5–200 µm | Industrial coatings |
| **Doctor Blade** | Film applicator with fixed gap spreads dispersion | 10–1000 µm | Reproducible lab films (e.g., TiO₂) |
| **Hot Melt Extrusion** | Polymer + filler melt-compounded, extruded through die | 10–200 µm | Industrial-scale films |
| **Compression Molding** | Melt pressed between heated platens | 50–500 µm | Bulk nanocomposite sheets |
| **UV Curing** | Liquid formulation polymerised by UV exposure | 5–100 µm | Fast-cure nanocomposite coatings |
| **In-situ Polymerisation** | Filler dispersed in monomer, then polymerised | Variable | Best nanofiller dispersion |
| **Layer-by-Layer** | Alternating polyelectrolyte/nanoparticle layers | nm-µm | Nanostructured films |
| **Electrospinning** | Charged jet of polymer solution forms nanofibres | nm scale | Nanofibrous mats |
| **Sol-Gel Hybrid** | Hydrolysis-condensation of precursors in polymer matrix | sub-µm | Hybrid organic-inorganic films |
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model Question]**
**Define Pigment Volume Concentration (PVC) and Critical Pigment Volume Concentration (CPVC). What is the significance of CPVC in coating formulation?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Definition (1 mark):** PVC is the ratio of the volume of pigment to the total volume of pigment plus dry binder, expressed as a percentage:

$$\text{PVC} = \frac{V_p}{V_p + V_b} \times 100$$

**Definition of CPVC (1 mark):** CPVC is the specific PVC value at which the binder is just sufficient to fill the interstices (voids) between the closely packed pigment particles. Beyond CPVC, voids are filled with air instead of binder.

**Significance (1 mark):** CPVC is a critical formulation parameter because at this point, film properties such as gloss, opacity, permeability, tensile strength, and stain resistance change abruptly. Coatings formulated above CPVC become matte and porous; below CPVC, they are glossy and continuous. CPVC therefore guides the formulator in choosing the right pigment/binder ratio for the desired end-use properties.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Question]**
**List any THREE methods used for the determination of CPVC of a pigment and explain any ONE in brief.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Three methods (1½ marks — ½ mark each):**
1. **Oil absorption method** (Asbeck–Van Loo equation).
2. **Dry film density method** (plotting dry film density vs PVC, finding discontinuity).
3. **Specific volume method** (extrapolation of pigment-binder specific volume curves).

**Explanation of Oil Absorption Method (1½ marks):**
The oil absorption (OA) of a pigment is determined by the spatula rub-out method — linseed oil is added dropwise to a known mass of pigment with continuous rubbing until a stiff, putty-like paste is formed. The mass of oil absorbed per 100 g of pigment is the OA value. The CPVC is then calculated using the Asbeck–Van Loo equation:

$$\text{CPVC} = \frac{100}{1 + \dfrac{\text{OA} \times \rho_p}{93.5}}$$

where $\rho_p$ is the density of the pigment. The factor 93.5 arises from the density of linseed oil and unit conversions.

---

### 5.2 PART B — Full 14-Mark Questions (Internal Choice Provided)

---

**QUESTION A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model Question]**

**(a)** Define PVC and CPVC. A coating formulation contains **50 g of ZnO pigment** ($\rho_p = 5.6 \text{ g/cm}^3$) and **30 g of an alkyd binder** ($\rho_b = 1.05 \text{ g/cm}^3$). The oil absorption of ZnO is **18 g/100 g pigment**. Calculate:
   (i) The PVC of the formulation.
   (ii) The CPVC from the Asbeck–Van Loo equation.
   (iii) Comment on the position of this formulation relative to CPVC. **[7 marks]**

**(b)** Describe the **solvent casting** and **spin coating** methods for the preparation of polymer nanocomposite films. Compare their advantages and limitations. Mention the typical film thickness range achievable by each method. **[7 marks]**

---

### Model Solution for Question A

#### Part (a) — 7 marks

**(i) PVC calculation (2 marks):**

Volume of pigment:

$$V_p = \frac{50}{5.6} = 8.929 \text{ cm}^3$$

Volume of binder:

$$V_b = \frac{30}{1.05} = 28.571 \text{ cm}^3$$

PVC:

\begin{aligned}
\text{PVC} &= \frac{V_p}{V_p + V_b} \times 100 \\
&= \frac{8.929}{8.929 + 28.571} \times 100 \\
&= \frac{8.929}{37.500} \times 100 \\
&= 23.81 \%
\end{aligned}

**(ii) CPVC from Asbeck–Van Loo (2 marks):**

\begin{aligned}
\text{CPVC} &= \frac{100}{1 + \dfrac{\text{OA} \times \rho_p}{93.5}} \\
&= \frac{100}{1 + \dfrac{18 \times 5.6}{93.5}} \\
&= \frac{100}{1 + \dfrac{100.8}{93.5}} \\
&= \frac{100}{1 + 1.0781} \\
&= \frac{100}{2.0781} \\
&= 48.12 \%
\end{aligned}

**(iii) Comment (1 mark):**

Since PVC (23.81%) < CPVC (48.12%), the formulation lies **well below CPVC**. The coating will be glossy, dense, continuous, with low permeability and good mechanical properties. There is scope to increase pigment loading if higher opacity or matte finish is desired.

**Margin work and unit consistency:** 2 marks (1 for correct V_p, V_b; 1 for correct substitution into formulas).

#### Part (b) — 7 marks

**Solvent Casting (3 marks):**

In solvent casting, the polymer is first dissolved in a suitable volatile solvent (e.g., chloroform, toluene, THF). The nanofiller (e.g., nanoclay, TiO₂, CNTs) is dispersed separately in the same solvent (often by ultrasonication or high-shear mixing). The two are then mixed, poured into a flat mould (Petri dish, glass plate), and the solvent is allowed to evaporate under controlled conditions (often in a fume hood or oven). After complete drying, the free-standing film is peeled off.

- **Thickness range:** typically **20–500 µm**.
- **Advantages:** Simple, low-cost, suitable for lab-scale, good for thermally sensitive polymers.
- **Limitations:** Solvent removal can cause bubbles or skin effects; not scalable industrially; solvent toxicity.

**Spin Coating (4 marks):**

In spin coating, a small volume of the polymer-nanofiller solution is dispensed onto the centre of a rotating substrate. The substrate is accelerated to a high rotational speed (typically 1000–6000 rpm); centrifugal force spreads the solution into a thin, uniform film, and excess is flung off. Final film thickness is controlled by spin speed, solution viscosity, and solvent volatility.

- **Thickness range:** typically **0.1–10 µm** (very thin films).
- **Advantages:** Extremely uniform films, excellent thickness control, ideal for optical, electronic, and sensor applications.
- **Limitations:** Wastes ~95% of the solution; only flat substrates; small-area films; difficult to scale up; nanofiller settling during spinning can cause non-uniformity.

**Comparison Summary (included in the 7 marks):**

| Aspect | Solvent Casting | Spin Coating |
|---|---|---|
| Film thickness | 20–500 µm | 0.1–10 µm |
| Uniformity | Moderate | Excellent |
| Scalability | Low–moderate | Very low |
| Material waste | Low | Very high |
| Best use | Lab films, packaging | Optical/electronic films |

---

**QUESTION B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Question]**

**(a)** With the help of a neat sketch, explain the **three regimes** of pigment volume concentration with respect to CPVC. Discuss how the following properties change across CPVC: (i) gloss, (ii) permeability, (iii) hiding power, and (iv) tensile strength. **[7 marks]**

**(b)** Explain the **in-situ polymerisation** method and the **doctor blade (film applicator)** method for preparing polymer nanocomposite films. Discuss the key advantages of in-situ polymerisation for achieving good nanofiller dispersion. Mention the typical film thickness range for each method. **[7 marks]**

---

### Model Solution for Question B

#### Part (a) — 7 marks

**Sketch description (1 mark):** A diagram showing pigment particles (as small circles) in a polymer matrix:
- *Left side (PVC < CPVC):* particles far apart, surrounded by continuous polymer matrix.
- *Centre (PVC = CPVC):* particles touching, polymer just filling all voids.
- *Right side (PVC > CPVC):* particles touching, **air gaps** visible between them.

**Three regimes described (2 marks):**

1. **Below CPVC (PVC < CPVC) — Binder-rich regime:** The polymer is the continuous phase; pigment particles are isolated and embedded in a thick binder layer. Film properties are dominated by the binder.
2. **At CPVC (PVC = CPVC) — Critical point:** The polymer just fills the voids. There is no air. All film properties show a sharp transition.
3. **Above CPVC (PVC > CPVC) — Pigment-rich regime:** Pigment particles form a continuous skeleton; binder is insufficient; air voids appear in the interstices. Film properties are dominated by the pigment-air composite.

**Property changes (4 marks — 1 mark each):**

(i) **Gloss:** High below CPVC (smooth binder-rich surface reflects light specularly). Drops sharply at CPVC. Becomes very low (matte) above CPVC because the air-pigment surface scatters light diffusely.

(ii) **Permeability:** Low below CPVC (dense polymer matrix resists diffusion). Increases **dramatically (10×–1000×)** above CPVC due to interconnected air voids that act as diffusion channels for water, oxygen, and ions.

(iii) **Hiding power / Opacity:** Below CPVC, opacity comes mainly from the pigment refractive index difference with binder. Near and above CPVC, the additional air-pigment interface enhances scattering → **maximum opacity** is reached above CPVC. This is why flat/matte wall paints are often formulated slightly above CPVC.

(iv) **Tensile strength:** High below CPVC (continuous binder phase bears load). Drops at CPVC. Becomes **brittle and weak** above CPVC because the air voids act as stress concentrators and crack initiation sites.

#### Part (b) — 7 marks

**In-Situ Polymerisation (3½ marks):**

In this method, the nanofiller is first dispersed in the *monomer* (or a solution of monomer) using ultrasonication, high-shear mixing, or magnetic stirring. The polymerization initiator is then added, and the reaction is carried out under controlled temperature. As the polymer chains grow, they form *around* the dispersed nanofiller, encapsulating individual nanoparticles or intercalating into layered structures (e.g., nanoclays).

- **Typical thickness range:** 50 µm – 5 mm (depends on mould).
- **Key advantages for nanofiller dispersion:**
  - Polymer chains grow *in situ* between nanofiller layers → excellent **exfoliation** of nanoclays.
  - Prevents re-aggregation of nanoparticles (chains "lock" them apart).
  - Covalent bonding can be introduced by functionalising the nanofiller with monomer-compatible groups.
  - Final nanocomposite shows superior mechanical, barrier, and thermal properties.

**Doctor Blade (Film Applicator) Method (3½ marks):**

The doctor blade is a precision film-casting device with an adjustable gap (typically 50–1000 µm). A pool of the polymer-nanofiller dispersion is placed in front of the blade, and the blade is drawn across a flat substrate (glass plate, PET film, steel) at a uniform speed. The gap setting controls wet film thickness; after solvent evaporation, the dry film thickness is obtained.

- **Typical dry film thickness range:** **10–500 µm** (depending on gap and solids content).
- **Advantages:** Highly reproducible film thickness; simple; suitable for routine quality control and research; minimal material waste compared to spin coating.
- **Limitations:** Limited to flat substrates; film uniformity depends on operator skill and viscosity control; not ideal for very thin films (< 5 µm).

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls in PVC/CPVC Questions**
>
> 1. **Using mass in the PVC formula instead of volume** — PVC is **volume-based**, not mass-based. Convert every mass to volume using $V = m/\rho$ first.
> 2. **Forgetting to express OA in g/100 g** — The Asbeck–Van Loo constant **93.5** assumes OA is in g/100 g of pigment. If the student reports OA in g/g, the formula fails silently.
> 3. **Including solvent in PVC calculation** — Solvents evaporate and must **NOT** be counted. Only the dry film constituents (pigment + binder solids) are used.
> 4. **Confusing CPVC with opacity** — CPVC is a *formulation parameter*, not a *property*. Opacity, gloss, etc. are *consequences* of being at/above/below CPVC.
> 5. **Mixing up binder and vehicle** — "Vehicle" usually means binder + solvent; "Binder" means the dry polymer. Always use the dry binder volume.
> 6. **Reporting PVC with no unit / wrong unit** — Always state PVC and CPVC in **% (v/v)**, not as a fraction.
> 7. **In film preparation questions**, students often describe the *principle* but forget the *thickness range* and *limitations* — both are mandatory for full marks in 7-mark sub-parts.

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> **RAPID REVISION CHECKLIST**
>
> - **PVC** = volume of pigment / (volume of pigment + volume of binder) × 100. Use **volumes**, not masses.
> - **CPVC** is the PVC at which binder *just* fills pigment interstices. Mathematically: **CPVC ≈ packing fraction × 100**.
> - **Three regimes:** below CPVC (glossy, dense), at CPVC (transition), above CPVC (matte, porous, air voids).
> - **Key properties that change abruptly at CPVC:** gloss ↓, permeability ↑↑, tensile strength ↓, opacity ↑, stain resistance ↓.
> - **Asbeck–Van Loo equation:** $\text{CPVC (\%)} = 100 / [1 + (\text{OA} \times \rho_p / 93.5)]$. OA in g/100 g; $\rho_p$ in g/cm³.
> - **Oil absorption** is determined by the spatula rub-out method using linseed oil.
> - **Methods to determine CPVC:** Oil absorption, dry film density vs PVC plot, specific volume extrapolation, Krieger–Dougherty fitting, gloss vs PVC plot, permeability vs PVC plot.
> - **Nanofillers generally have lower CPVC** than micro-pigments due to higher surface area and higher oil absorption.
> - **Nanoclay (layered silicates)** can be formulated near CPVC because their plate-like geometry creates structured voids.
> - **Film preparation methods classified into four groups:** Solvent-based, Thermal/Melt, Radiation-curing, Specialised.
> - **Solvent casting** → 20–500 µm; best lab uniformity for moderate thickness.
> - **Spin coating** → 0.1–10 µm; best uniformity, but high material waste.
> - **Doctor blade** → 10–500 µm; reproducible, ideal for routine coating studies.
> - **In-situ polymerisation** → best nanofiller dispersion because polymer chains grow *around* the filler, preventing re-agglomeration.
> - **Hot melt extrusion** → industrial-scale; suitable for thermoplastics + nanofillers.
> - **Layer-by-Layer (LBL)** → nanoscale precision; alternating polyanion/polycation or polymer/nanoparticle layers.
> - **For KTU answers always:** (i) state definitions, (ii) show all unit conversions, (iii) provide a labelled diagram, (iv) give a numerical example with worked steps, (v) conclude with a remark on the practical implication.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Mechanical and Optical Properties of Coatings
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEPOT522 - POLYMER NANOCOMPOSITES
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Mechanical and Optical Properties of Coatings

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# MODULE 3: MECHANICAL AND OPTICAL PROPERTIES OF COATINGS

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 What are "Mechanical Properties of Coatings"?

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal KTU Definition:** *Mechanical properties of coatings* are the measurable responses of a cured polymeric film (deposited on a substrate) to applied **mechanical forces, deformations, and surface contacts**. They quantify how the coating resists scratching, impacts, abrasion, bending, indentation, and adhesion failure under service conditions.

In simpler words — a coating is a **thin polymer skin** sitting on top of a substrate (metal, wood, plastic, concrete). Even though it is usually only **20–200 μm thick**, it is the **first line of defense** of any coated product. The mechanical properties decide whether this skin will:

- **Stay stuck** to the substrate (adhesion)
- **Resist a fingernail scratch** (hardness)
- **Bend without cracking** when the substrate flexes (flexibility)
- **Survive a falling object** (impact resistance)
- **Withstand thousands of rub cycles** (abrasion resistance)

> [!NOTE]
> **Conceptual Analogy:** Imagine applying a thin layer of nail polish on your nail. *Adhesion* = does it peel off in one piece? *Hardness* = can a coin scratch it? *Flexibility* = does it crack when you bend your finger? *Impact resistance* = does it survive when you tap your nail on a table? *Abrasion resistance* = does the colour stay even after rubbing keys against it for weeks? Every mechanical test on an industrial coating is essentially a controlled, instrumented version of one of these daily-life observations.

### 1.2 What are "Optical Properties of Coatings"?

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal KTU Definition:** *Optical properties of coatings* describe the interaction of the cured polymeric film with **visible and near-visible electromagnetic radiation (380–780 nm)**, encompassing reflection, transmission, scattering, absorption, and colour perception. They govern the **aesthetic appearance** and **functional visibility** of the coated surface.

A coating can be highly protective mechanically but visually unacceptable. Optical properties are therefore as critical as mechanical ones in **automotive, architectural, packaging, and display** applications.

> [!NOTE]
> **Conceptual Analogy:** A freshly polished car body is not just "shiny by chance." The clear-coat's **refractive index**, **surface smoothness**, and **pigment dispersion** together create a mirror-like **gloss**; its **thickness uniformity** and **pigment type** control **colour**; and its **transparency to UV/IR** decides whether the substrate yellows over time. The optical tests in industry are the same as what a paint chemist does with a handheld gloss meter in a body shop — just made quantitative, traceable, and reproducible.

### 1.3 Standard Metrics at a Glance

| Property | Standard Test | Reported Unit |
|---|---|---|
| Adhesion | ASTM D3359 (Cross-cut) | 0B – 5B rating |
| Pencil Hardness | ASTM D3363 | 6B (softest) → 6H (hardest) |
| Pendulum Hardness | ISO 1522 (König/Persoz) | Seconds (oscillation damping) |
| Impact Resistance | ASTM D2794 | kg·m (direct/reverse) |
| Flexibility | ASTM D522 (Mandrel) | % elongation or pass/fail |
| Abrasion | ASTM D4060 (Taber) | mg weight loss / 1000 cycles |
| Gloss | ASTM D523 | Gloss Units (GU) at 60° |
| Haze | ASTM D1003 | % haze |
| Colour | ASTM D2244 | L*, a*, b* (CIELAB) |

> [!TIP]
> Every property above is governed by a combination of **(a) polymer binder chemistry, (b) pigment/filler type & loading, (c) film thickness, (d) substrate, and (e) curing conditions.** This is the "5-knob" mental model examiners expect you to invoke whenever a question asks "How would you improve ___ property?"
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Mechanical Properties — Conceptual Framework

#### 2.1.1 Adhesion

**Why it matters:** A coating with poor adhesion fails *spontaneously* — peeling, flaking, or blistering. Adhesion is governed by four mechanisms:

1. **Mechanical interlocking** – coating flows into substrate pores/roughness (anchor effect)
2. **Chemical bonding** – covalent/ionic bonds across the interface (e.g., silane coupling agents)
3. **Adsorption (wetting) theory** – intimate molecular contact; governed by **Young's equation**
4. **Electrostatic / Diffusion theory** – inter-diffusion of polymer chains across the interface

The thermodynamic work of adhesion per unit area is given by the **Dupré equation**:

$$W_a = \gamma_S + \gamma_L - \gamma_{SL}$$

where $\gamma_S$ = substrate surface energy, $\gamma_L$ = liquid coating surface tension, $\gamma_{SL}$ = interfacial tension. For good adhesion, $\gamma_S > \gamma_L$ (wetting condition).

**Standard Tests:**
- **Cross-cut (tape) test (ASTM D3359):** A lattice is cut into the film, tape is applied and pulled off. Rated **5B (best) → 0B (worst)**.
- **Pull-off test (ASTM D4541):** A dolly is glued to the film and pulled perpendicular to the surface; failure load in **MPa** is the pull-off strength.

$$\sigma_{pull\text{-}off} = \frac{F}{\pi r^2}$$

where $F$ is the maximum tensile load and $r$ is the dolly radius.

#### 2.1.2 Hardness

Hardness in coatings is **not** a single property — it has at least three distinct meanings:

- **Indentation hardness** (resistance to a static indenter)
- **Scratch hardness** (resistance to a moving point)
- **Pendulum hardness** (damping of oscillation, related to viscoelastic dissipation)

**Pencil hardness (ASTM D3363):** Pencils of increasing hardness (6B → 6H) are pushed against the film at 45° under a 1 kg load. The hardest pencil that does **not** scratch the film is reported.

**Pendulum hardness (ISO 1522):** A pendulum rests on the coating; the time for the amplitude to decay from 6° → 3° is recorded. Two types:
- **König pendulum** (~150 oscillations on glass reference)
- **Persoz pendulum** (~430 oscillations on glass reference)

The number of oscillations is inversely related to the **viscoelastic energy dissipation** in the film — softer, more damping films give lower hardness.

#### 2.1.3 Flexibility and Impact Resistance

**Mandrel bend test (ASTM D522):** Coated panels are bent around cylindrical mandrels of decreasing diameter. Pass/fail is reported by visual or 10× magnification inspection for cracking.

**Falling-weight impact (ASTM D2794):** A 1–4 kg weight with a hemispherical indenter is dropped from a known height onto the coated (or reverse-coated) side. The energy at which cracking first appears is the impact resistance:

$$E_{impact} = m \cdot g \cdot h$$

where $m$ = mass (kg), $g = 9.81 \, m/s^2$, $h$ = drop height (m). Typical good coatings: $> 18 \, kg \cdot m$ (direct) and $> 9 \, kg \cdot m$ (reverse).

#### 2.1.4 Abrasion Resistance

**Taber Abraser (ASTM D4060):** Two abrasive wheels (e.g., CS-10, CS-17) rotate on the coating under a 500–1000 g load. **Weight loss after 1000 cycles** is reported — lower is better.

$$\text{Taber Wear Index} = \frac{(W_0 - W_f) \cdot 1000}{N}$$

where $W_0$ = initial mass, $W_f$ = final mass, $N$ = number of cycles.

#### 2.1.5 Tensile Properties of Free Films

When the coating is peeled off as a free film, classic stress–strain analysis applies:

$$\sigma = \frac{F}{A_0}, \quad \varepsilon = \frac{\Delta L}{L_0}$$

Young's modulus from the initial linear region: $E = \sigma / \varepsilon$. Elongation at break ($\varepsilon_b$) and tensile strength ($\sigma_b$) are read from the curve.

### 2.2 Optical Properties — Conceptual Framework

#### 2.2.1 Gloss

**Gloss** is the **specular reflectance** of light from a surface, perceived by the eye as "shininess." It is measured by a **gloss meter** that directs a beam of light at a defined angle and measures the reflected intensity relative to a black glass standard (refractive index 1.567, assigned **100 GU**).

**Standard geometries (ASTM D523):**
- **60°** — general purpose (universal angle)
- **20°** — high-gloss surfaces (>70 GU at 60°)
- **85°** — matte / low-gloss surfaces (<10 GU at 60°)

> [!NOTE]
> **Why three angles?** A single 60° reading cannot discriminate between a semi-gloss and a mirror — both could read 50 GU. Measuring at multiple angles creates a *fingerprint* of the surface.

#### 2.2.2 Haze

**Haze (ASTM D1003)** is the percentage of transmitted light that, passing through a transparent coating, deviates from the incident beam by more than **2.5°** due to forward scattering (from surface roughness, internal pigment particles, or micro-voids).

$$\% \text{Haze} = \frac{\text{Diffuse Transmittance}}{\text{Total Transmittance}} \times 100$$

Low-haze coatings (< 1%) are required for **display panels, optical lenses, and headlamp covers**.

#### 2.2.3 Colour and the CIELAB System

Human colour perception is tri-stimulus: cones in the retina respond to **L (red), M (green), S (blue)**. The **CIE 1931** system standardises this as **X, Y, Z** tristimulus values. To make colour differences perceptually uniform, the **CIELAB (L\*a\*b\*)** colour space was developed in 1976:

- **L\*** = Lightness, 0 (black) → 100 (white)
- **a\*** = red (+a*) ↔ green (−a*) axis
- **b\*** = yellow (+b*) ↔ blue (−b*) axis

The **total colour difference** between two specimens is:

$$\Delta E^* = \sqrt{(\Delta L^*)^2 + (\Delta a^*)^2 + (\Delta b^*)^2}$$

| $\Delta E^*$ | Perceived Difference |
|---|---|
| < 1 | Not perceptible to the human eye |
| 1 – 2 | Perceptible on close inspection only |
| 2 – 10 | Perceptible at a glance |
| > 10 | More similar to the opposite colour |

> [!TIP]
> KTU examiners love the question: *"What is $\Delta E^*$ and what does a value of 1.5 indicate?"* Memorise the table above.

#### 2.2.4 Hiding Power / Opacity

**Hiding power** is the ability of a coating to obliterate the colour of the substrate. Quantified by the **contrast ratio** (ASTM D2805):

$$\text{Contrast Ratio} = \frac{Y_{\text{black substrate}}}{Y_{\text{white substrate}}}$$

A contrast ratio of 1.0 = perfectly opaque; < 0.98 = visibly translucent. It depends strongly on **film thickness, pigment volume concentration (PVC), and refractive index contrast** between pigment and binder.

#### 2.2.5 Refractive Index

The refractive index $n$ of a polymer coating governs both gloss (Fresnel reflection) and hiding power (light scattering). For a clear film in air:

$$R = \left(\frac{n_{coating} - n_{air}}{n_{coating} + n_{air}}\right)^2$$

at normal incidence. Glossier coatings and hiding pigments (e.g., TiO$_2$, $n \approx 2.6$) are both strategies to maximise surface/sub-surface reflectivity.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Property | Formula | Units / Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Work of adhesion | $W_a = \gamma_S + \gamma_L - \gamma_{SL}$ | mJ/m² |
| 2 | Pull-off strength | $\sigma = F/(\pi r^2)$ | MPa |
| 3 | Impact energy | $E = m \cdot g \cdot h$ | J (or kg·m × 9.81) |
| 4 | Taber wear index | $(W_0 - W_f) \cdot 1000 / N$ | mg/1000 cycles |
| 5 | Engineering stress | $\sigma = F / A_0$ | MPa |
| 6 | Engineering strain | $\varepsilon = \Delta L / L_0$ | dimensionless (or %) |
| 7 | Young's modulus | $E = \sigma / \varepsilon$ (linear region) | MPa |
| 8 | % Haze | $100 \cdot T_{diffuse} / T_{total}$ | % |
| 9 | Contrast ratio | $Y_{black} / Y_{white}$ | 0 – 1 |
| 10 | Fresnel reflectance (normal) | $R = \left(\frac{n_2 - n_1}{n_2 + n_1}\right)^2$ | 0 – 1 |
| 11 | Total colour difference | $\Delta E^* = \sqrt{(\Delta L^*)^2 + (\Delta a^*)^2 + (\Delta b^*)^2}$ | dimensionless |
| 12 | Wetting condition | $\gamma_S > \gamma_L$ | condition for good adhesion |
| 13 | Young–Laplace capillary pressure | $\Delta P = 2\gamma_{LV} \cos\theta / r$ | Pa (pore penetration) |
| 14 | Hiding power (film thickness approx.) | $H \propto 1 / (n_{pigment} - n_{binder})^2$ | inverse-square with $\Delta n$ |

> [!WARNING]
> In KTU exam scripts, students frequently confuse **$\Delta E$** with **$\Delta H$** (haze) — they are completely different quantities! $\Delta E$ is colour difference; haze (%) is light scattering. Do not mix them up.

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Automotive clear-coats** require *high* gloss, *low* haze, *high* scratch hardness, and *excellent* UV durability.
- **Marine anti-fouling coatings** need *high* adhesion to steel, *high* impact resistance to wave strikes, and *controlled* optical transparency (for "see-through" variants).
- **Food packaging coatings** (e.g., PET films) need *high* transparency, *low* haze, and *FDA-compliant* polymer chemistry.
- **Aerospace coatings** must survive *thermal cycling* (-50 °C ↔ +150 °C) — flexibility at low temperature is critical.
- **Display panel hard-coats** combine *very high pencil hardness* (≥ 9H) with *near-zero haze* (< 0.5%) — a property combination only achievable with nanocomposite (e.g., silica nanoparticle-reinforced) coatings.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1: Pull-Off Strength from a Force–Diameter Reading

**Problem (typical KTU Part-B style):** A 20 mm-diameter aluminium dolly is glued to a polymer coating. A tensile tester pulls the dolly until the coating detaches, and the **peak force recorded is 2.80 kN**. Calculate the pull-off (adhesion) strength in MPa. State whether the adhesion is acceptable for an **automotive primer** (industry benchmark ≥ 7 MPa).

**Step 1 — Convert units consistently.**
Force: $F = 2.80 \, kN = 2.80 \times 10^3 \, N$
Diameter: $d = 20 \, mm \Rightarrow r = 10 \, mm = 10 \times 10^{-3} \, m = 0.010 \, m$

**Step 2 — Write the pull-off strength equation.**

$$\sigma_{po} = \frac{F}{A} = \frac{F}{\pi r^2}$$

**Step 3 — Substitute values.**

$$A = \pi \cdot (0.010)^2 = \pi \times 10^{-4} = 3.1416 \times 10^{-4} \, m^2$$

$$\sigma_{po} = \frac{2.80 \times 10^3}{3.1416 \times 10^{-4}} = 8.913 \times 10^{6} \, Pa = 8.91 \, MPa$$

**Step 4 — Compare to benchmark.**
$8.91 \, MPa \geq 7 \, MPa$ ⇒ **adhesion is acceptable** for an automotive primer. (Most industrially-rated pull-offs fall between 5 and 25 MPa.)

> **Insight:** Pull-off strength is **not** an intrinsic material property — it depends on the **dolly geometry, glue stiffness, loading rate, and substrate rigidity**. Always quote the standard (ASTM D4541) along with the value.

---

### 3.2 Derivation 2: Taber Wear Index from Mass Loss Data

**Problem:** A powder-coating sample weighs 4.8321 g initially. After 1000 Taber abrasion cycles with CS-17 wheels under 1000 g load, the mass is 4.8063 g. Calculate the Taber wear index and rate the abrasion resistance as *excellent* (< 20 mg), *good* (20–60 mg), *fair* (60–100 mg), or *poor* (> 100 mg) per 1000 cycles.

**Step 1 — Mass loss.**

$$\Delta m = W_0 - W_f = 4.8321 - 4.8063 = 0.0258 \, g = 25.8 \, mg$$

**Step 2 — Taber wear index** (over the given cycles).

$$\text{TWI} = \frac{\Delta m \times 1000}{N} = \frac{25.8 \times 1000}{1000} = 25.8 \, mg/1000 \text{ cycles}$$

**Step 3 — Rating.**
$25.8 \, mg \in [20, 60)$ ⇒ **Good** abrasion resistance.

**Step 4 — Engineering insight.**
The standard often uses **CS-10** (mild, rubber) for decorative coatings and **CS-17** (severe, vitrified) for industrial floors. Always report the wheel type along with the index.

---

### 3.3 Derivation 3: $\Delta E^*$ Colour Difference Between Two Batches

**Problem (KTU style 14-mark):** Two batches of the same red automotive topcoat give the following CIELAB coordinates under D65 illumination, 10° observer:

| Batch | L* | a* | b* |
|---|---|---|---|
| Reference | 45.20 | +52.80 | +28.50 |
| Production | 44.10 | +50.40 | +30.10 |

(a) Calculate $\Delta E^*$.
(b) Will a customer notice the difference?
(c) If the difference is due to 0.4 wt% excess of a yellow-shade pigment, what corrective action is recommended?

**Step 1 — Compute component differences.**

$$\Delta L^* = L^*_{prod} - L^*_{ref} = 44.10 - 45.20 = -1.10$$

$$\Delta a^* = 50.40 - 52.80 = -2.40$$

$$\Delta b^* = 30.10 - 28.50 = +1.60$$

**Step 2 — Apply the $\Delta E^*$ equation.**

$$\Delta E^* = \sqrt{(-1.10)^2 + (-2.40)^2 + (1.60)^2}$$

$$\Delta E^* = \sqrt{1.21 + 5.76 + 2.56} = \sqrt{9.53} = 3.087$$

**Step 3 — Perceptibility interpretation.**
$\Delta E^* = 3.09 \in [2, 10]$ ⇒ **"Perceptible at a glance"** — the customer *will* notice. The product is **out of colour-tolerance specification** (most automotive specs demand $\Delta E^* < 1.0$ between production and reference).

**Step 4 — Corrective action.**
The dominant shift is $\Delta a^* = -2.40$ (less red) and $\Delta b^* = +1.60$ (more yellow) — this is the classic signature of **excess yellow-shade pigment**. Recommended actions:

1. Reduce the **yellow-shade red pigment** (e.g., replace part of the red iron oxide with a blue-shade red, or reduce yellow pigment loading by ~0.3–0.5 wt%).
2. Re-check **pigment dispersion** — poor dispersion can shift b* positively (less chromatic, more yellow).
3. Verify **drying/curing conditions** — under-cure can darken and shift b* yellow.

---

### 3.4 Derivation 4: Impact Energy for a Falling-Weight Test

**Problem:** A 2 kg impactor with a 16 mm hemispherical indenter is dropped from 1.0 m onto the coated face of a steel panel. Calculate the impact energy in joules and in kg·m (the unit commonly quoted in ASTM D2794).

**Step 1 — Energy equation.**

$$E = m \cdot g \cdot h$$

**Step 2 — Substitute.**

$$E = 2 \, kg \times 9.81 \, m/s^2 \times 1.0 \, m = 19.62 \, J$$

**Step 3 — Convert to kg·m (the "weight × height" convention used in the standard).**

$$E = 2 \, kg \times 1.0 \, m = 2.0 \, kg \cdot m$$

(Since $1 \, kg \cdot m = 9.81 \, J$, the two numbers are equivalent: $19.62 \, J = 2.0 \, kg \cdot m$.)

**Step 4 — Interpretation.**
For a high-build industrial coating, a pass at $2.0 \, kg \cdot m$ **direct impact** is considered good. **Reverse impact** (impactor hits the steel, deforming it outward against the coating) is the harsher test — typical pass values are 0.5 – 1.0 kg·m.

---

### 3.5 Analytical Matrix — "Which Test, For What Failure Mode?"

| Failure mode observed in field | Likely coating property involved | Standard test | Key metric |
|---|---|---|---|
| Coating peels off in sheets after 6 months | Adhesion | ASTM D3359 / D4541 | Cross-cut rating / MPa |
| Scratches visible after light contact with keys | Surface hardness | ASTM D3363 | Pencil grade |
| Hairline cracks at bend radius | Flexibility | ASTM D522 | Pass at smallest mandrel |
| Coating chips at stone impact on highway | Impact resistance | ASTM D2794 | kg·m at first crack |
| Glossy coating looks dull after 1 year | Abrasion / UV degradation | ASTM D4060 + D523 | mg loss + GU retention |
| Two production batches look slightly different | Colour | ASTM D2244 | $\Delta E^*$ |
| Clear coating looks "foggy" | Haze | ASTM D1003 | % haze |
| Substrate visible through coloured paint | Hiding power | ASTM D2805 | Contrast ratio |
| Finger-print easily marks surface | Surface cure / hardness | Pencil + MEK double-rub | Pencil + rubs to mar |

> [!TIP]
> This matrix is your **diagnostic decision tree** in viva voce and design questions. Memorise the *failure → property → standard test* triplet.

---

### 3.6 Derivation 5: Gloss Reading as a Function of Refractive Index

**Conceptual derivation:** A clear polymeric coating ($n_2$) on a smooth substrate is in air ($n_1 = 1.00$). The specular reflectance at normal incidence is:

$$R = \left(\frac{n_2 - 1}{n_2 + 1}\right)^2$$

For a typical acrylic coating, $n_2 = 1.49$:

$$R = \left(\frac{1.49 - 1}{1.49 + 1}\right)^2 = \left(\frac{0.49}{2.49}\right)^2 = (0.1968)^2 = 0.0387 \approx 3.9\%$$

For a fluoropolymer coating with $n_2 = 1.34$:

$$R = \left(\frac{0.34}{2.34}\right)^2 = (0.1453)^2 = 0.0211 \approx 2.1\%$$

> **Conclusion:** *Lower* refractive index polymers reflect *less* light — they appear **less glossy** but also **less visible** (an advantage for anti-reflective lens coatings). Higher $n$ gives a **mirror-like** appearance.

> [!NOTE]
> A 60° gloss meter reading in GU is **not exactly the same as** the Fresnel $R$ — the gloss meter uses a **black glass standard of $n = 1.567$** as the 100 GU reference, so:

> $$GU = \frac{R_{sample}}{R_{standard}} \times 100, \quad R_{standard} = \left(\frac{1.567 - 1}{1.567 + 1}\right)^2 \approx 0.0499$$

> This is why maximum possible gloss reading for any coating rarely exceeds ~120 GU.

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2023]**
> *Define pencil hardness test. How is the hardness of a coating reported in this test?*

**Model Answer:**
The pencil hardness test (ASTM D3363) is a *scratch hardness* test in which pencils of progressively increasing hardness (graded **6B → B → HB → F → H → 6H**) are held at a **45° angle** to the coating surface and pushed forward under a **1 kg load**. The result is reported as the **hardest pencil grade that does NOT scratch or gouge the coating film**. A 6B rating means the coating is extremely soft (marks easily), while 6H means it is extremely hard (very scratch-resistant). For reference, an automotive clear-coat typically rates **2H – 4H**, and a display-panel hard-coat can reach **8H – 9H**.

> **Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2022]**
> *What is the CIELAB colour space? What does the term $\Delta E^* = 1.5$ signify?*

**Model Answer:**
The **CIELAB (L\*a\*b\*)** colour space, standardised by the **CIE in 1976**, is a three-dimensional, perceptually uniform model of human colour vision. **L\*** represents **lightness (0 = black, 100 = white)**, **a\*** represents the **red (+) ↔ green (−)** axis, and **b\*** represents the **yellow (+) ↔ blue (−)** axis. The total colour difference between two specimens is computed as $\Delta E^* = \sqrt{(\Delta L^*)^2 + (\Delta a^*)^2 + (\Delta b^*)^2}$. A value of $\Delta E^* = 1.5$ is **perceptible only on close, side-by-side inspection** but is generally acceptable for most architectural coatings (typically $\Delta E^* \le 2$); however, for **automotive topcoats, the specification usually requires $\Delta E^* \le 1.0$** between production batches and the master panel.

---

### 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Module Internal Choice)

> #### **QUESTION A — 14 Marks** `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`
>
> **(a)** *Discuss the various methods used to evaluate the **adhesion of polymer coatings** to substrates. Explain in detail the **cross-cut (tape) test (ASTM D3359)** and the **pull-off test (ASTM D4541)** with their rating systems. **(7 marks)**
>
> **(b)** *A 25 mm-diameter dolly is glued to a polymer coating on a mild-steel substrate. In a pull-off test, the coating detaches at a peak load of **3.50 kN**. Calculate the **pull-off strength in MPa**. Comment on whether this value is acceptable for a **marine coating** (typical industry benchmark ≥ 10 MPa). Suggest **two formulation strategies** to improve the adhesion if it is unacceptable. **(7 marks)*

#### Model Solution — Part (a)

**Adhesion evaluation methods (overview):**

1. **Qualitative / semi-quantitative tests**
   - **Cross-cut (tape) test – ASTM D3359**
   - **Scratch / scribe test – ASTM D2197**
   - **X-cut tape test (ASTM D3359, Method A)**
2. **Quantitative tests**
   - **Pull-off test – ASTM D4541** (uses tensile tester + dolly)
   - **Lap-shear test – ASTM D1002** (for adhesive joints, but adaptable)
3. **Specialised instruments**
   - **Stud-pull on thin films, AFM pull-off, scratch tester with acoustic emission**

**Cross-cut test in detail:**
A lattice pattern of **6 cuts in each direction** is made through the coating down to the substrate using a sharp blade and a **template/guide**. A **pressure-sensitive tape** (e.g., 3M 250) is firmly pressed over the lattice and then pulled off at a steady angle. The removed area is visually compared with a **standard rating chart** and assigned one of the following classifications:

| Rating | % Area Removed | Description |
|---|---|---|
| **5B** | 0% | None — the edges of the cuts are completely smooth |
| **4B** | < 5% | Tiny flakes detached at intersections |
| **3B** | 5 – 15% | Small flakes along edges and at intersections |
| **2B** | 15 – 35% | Flakes along edges in continuous strips |
| **1B** | 35 – 65% | Wide strips peeled off |
| **0B** | > 65% | Severe flaking; coating essentially detached |

**Pull-off test in detail:**
A **cylindrical aluminium or steel dolly** (commonly 10, 14, 20, or 25 mm diameter) is bonded to the coating surface using a high-strength cyanoacrylate or epoxy adhesive. After the adhesive cures (typically 24 h), a **portable hydraulic or pneumatic adhesion tester** applies a **tensile load perpendicular to the surface** at a controlled rate (≤ 1 MPa/s). The **peak load at detachment** is recorded, and the pull-off strength is calculated as $\sigma = F / (\pi r^2)$. Failure can be (a) **cohesive** (within coating — good adhesion), (b) **adhesive** (at coating–substrate interface — weak link), or (c) **glue failure** (test invalid).

#### Model Solution — Part (b)

**Step 1 — Unit conversion.**
$F = 3.50 \, kN = 3.50 \times 10^3 \, N$
$d = 25 \, mm \Rightarrow r = 12.5 \, mm = 0.0125 \, m$

**Step 2 — Cross-sectional area of the dolly.**
$$A = \pi r^2 = \pi \times (0.0125)^2 = \pi \times 1.5625 \times 10^{-4} = 4.909 \times 10^{-4} \, m^2$$

**Step 3 — Pull-off strength.**
$$\sigma_{po} = \frac{F}{A} = \frac{3.50 \times 10^3}{4.909 \times 10^{-4}} = 7.130 \times 10^6 \, Pa = 7.13 \, MPa$$

**Step 4 — Benchmark comparison.**
$7.13 \, MPa < 10 \, MPa$ ⇒ **Not acceptable** for a marine coating. Marine environments (saltwater, cyclic wet/dry, biological growth) impose severe adhesion demands — typical specifications are **≥ 10 MPa** and often **≥ 15 MPa** for offshore structures.

**Step 5 — Two formulation strategies to improve adhesion:**

1. **Surface pretreatment of the substrate** — Apply a chemical conversion coating (e.g., **zinc phosphate**, **chromate-free wash primer**, or **silane coupling agent** such as aminopropyltriethoxysilane) to introduce chemical bonding sites and increase surface energy, promoting wetting and chemical linkage to the polymer binder.
2. **Modify the binder chemistry** — Introduce **polar functional groups** (e.g., –OH, –COOH, epoxy) into the resin backbone, or use a **self-priming formulation** with adhesion-promoting additives (e.g., **2–5 wt% of an epoxy-phosphate adduct**). For epoxy/ polyurethane marine systems, **adhesion-promoting amines** in the hardener package can improve wet adhesion by 30–50%.

> [!WARNING]
> **Common student mistakes in this problem:**
> 1. Forgetting to convert **mm to m** before squaring the radius — gives an answer 1000× too large.
> 2. Using **diameter** instead of **radius** in the area formula — gives an answer 4× too small.
> 3. Quoting the answer in **kPa** instead of **MPa** — silent unit error that examiners penalise.
> 4. Stating that the test result is "good" without comparing to the given benchmark.

---

> #### **QUESTION B — 14 Marks** *(Alternative Choice)* `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`
>
> **(a)** *Explain the concept of **gloss** in polymer coatings. Describe the **gloss meter principle** and the three standard measurement geometries (20°, 60°, 85°) per **ASTM D523**. Why is gloss measured at multiple angles? **(7 marks)*
>
> **(b)** *A coated panel gives the following CIELAB coordinates under D65 / 10° observer — Reference: L\*=50.10, a\*=+45.20, b\*=+22.30; Production: L\*=49.80, a\*=+47.00, b\*=+21.50. Compute the **total colour difference $\Delta E^*$**. Will the deviation be **perceptible to the customer**? Identify the **direction of the dominant colour shift** and suggest a corrective formulation change. **(7 marks)*

#### Model Solution — Part (a)

**Gloss — concept:**
Gloss is the **specular (mirror-like) reflectance** of a surface, perceived by the eye as "shininess" or "sheen." Physically, it is governed by the **Fresnel reflection** of light from the air–coating interface, which depends on (i) the **refractive index difference** between coating and air, (ii) the **angle of incidence**, and (iii) the **micro-roughness** of the coating surface. A perfectly smooth surface behaves like a mirror; a rough surface scatters light diffusely and looks matte.

**Gloss meter principle:**
A gloss meter contains (1) a **light source** projecting a beam of defined intensity onto the coating at a fixed angle, and (2) a **photodetector** positioned to receive the specularly reflected beam at the **mirror angle** of incidence. The detector signal is **ratioed** to the signal obtained from a **calibration standard** — a highly polished **black glass plate of refractive index 1.567**, which is assigned exactly **100 Gloss Units (GU)** at all three angles.

$$GU = \frac{I_{sample}}{I_{standard}} \times 100$$

**The three standard geometries (ASTM D523):**

| Geometry | Use case | Surface gloss regime |
|---|---|---|
| **60°** | Universal / general-purpose | All gloss levels (the default angle) |
| **20°** | High-gloss surfaces | Coatings with 60° gloss > 70 GU |
| **85°** | Matte / low-gloss surfaces | Coatings with 60° gloss < 10 GU |

**Why multiple angles?**
A single 60° measurement cannot resolve the **angular distribution of reflected light** adequately. For example, two coatings may both read 50 GU at 60°, but one may be a near-mirror (high 20° reading) while the other is broadly scattering (low 20° reading). Multi-angle measurement produces a **"fingerprint"** that uniquely characterises surface microstructure. This is critical in industries like **automotive** where 20°-gloss is a key acceptance criterion (typically ≥ 90 GU for premium clear-coats).

#### Model Solution — Part (b)

**Step 1 — Component differences.**

$$\Delta L^* = 49.80 - 50.10 = -0.30$$
$$\Delta a^* = 47.00 - 45.20 = +1.80$$
$$\Delta b^* = 21.50 - 22.30 = -0.80$$

**Step 2 — Total colour difference.**

$$\Delta E^* = \sqrt{(-0.30)^2 + (1.80)^2 + (-0.80)^2}$$

$$= \sqrt{0.09 + 3.24 + 0.64} = \sqrt{3.97} = 1.992 \approx 1.99$$

**Step 3 — Perceptibility.**
$\Delta E^* \approx 1.99$ falls in the range **"perceptible on close inspection only"**. For a **high-end red automotive topcoat**, this is borderline — most OEM specifications require $\Delta E^* < 1.0$ for delivery acceptance. The batch is likely to be **rejected** by the customer's incoming-quality team.

**Step 4 — Dominant shift direction.**
The dominant change is $\Delta a^* = +1.80$ (production is **more red** and less green) and $\Delta b^* = -0.80$ (production is **less yellow**, slightly bluer). This is the classic signature of an **excess of a blue-shade red pigment** (e.g., too much quinacridone red, or insufficient yellow shading pigment such as isoindoline yellow).

**Step 5 — Corrective formulation change.**

- **Reduce the blue-shade red pigment** by approximately **2–4%** in the masterbatch, *or*
- **Add a small amount of a yellow-shade red pigment** (e.g., red iron oxide) at **0.3–0.6 wt%** to shift b* upward toward the target.
- Additionally, **verify pigment dispersion quality** — poor dispersion of the organic red can desaturate the colour and shift a* downward; over-dispersion (long bead-mill residence) can degrade the pigment crystals and shift hue.

> [!WARNING]
> **Common student mistakes in $\Delta E^*$ problems:**
> 1. **Forgetting to square** the components — leads to a wrong answer by a factor of ~3.
> 2. **Dropping the negative sign** on $\Delta L^*$ and squaring the raw value — accidentally correct here, but in derived problems can lead to nonsense "interpretation" of the shift direction.
> 3. **Confusing $\Delta E$ with $\Delta H$ (haze)** — completely different; examiners often insert one or two distractors to test this.
> 4. Stating "$\Delta E = 2$ is not perceptible" — **WRONG**. It IS perceptible on close inspection (the perceptibility table in §2.2.3 must be memorised).

---

### 5.3 TOPIC RECAP & IMPORTANT THINGS TO REMEMBER

> [!TIP]
> **High-Yield Bullet-Point Revision Checklist (for last-day exam revision):**

- **Adhesion** — governed by *wetting* (Young's equation, $\gamma_S > \gamma_L$) + *chemical bonding* + *mechanical interlocking*. Tests: **ASTM D3359** (cross-cut, 5B → 0B) and **ASTM D4541** (pull-off, MPa). Pull-off formula: $\sigma = F / (\pi r^2)$.
- **Hardness** — three flavours: **pencil (ASTM D3363)**, **pendulum (ISO 1522, König/Persoz)**, **indentation (Wolff-Wilborn/Barcol)**. Always state the **load, angle, and standard**.
- **Flexibility** — **ASTM D522 mandrel bend**; pass = no cracking at smallest mandrel. Related to **T$_g$**: coatings with T$_g$ well below service T are flexible.
- **Impact resistance** — **ASTM D2794** falling weight. Energy $E = mgh$. Report in **kg·m or J**; specify *direct* or *reverse*.
- **Abrasion** — **ASTM D4060 Taber Abraser** (mg loss / 1000 cycles). Wheels: CS-10 (mild) for decorative, CS-17 (severe) for industrial.
- **Gloss** — Specular reflectance, **ASTM D523** at 20°/60°/85°. Reported in **GU** with reference to black-glass standard ($n=1.567 \rightarrow 100$ GU). Higher $n$ of polymer ⇒ higher Fresnel $R$ ⇒ higher gloss.
- **Haze** — **ASTM D1003**, % diffuse transmittance within 2.5° of the specular beam. Critical for **displays and lens coatings** (< 1% required).
- **Colour** — **CIELAB L\*a\*b\*** space; $\Delta E^* = \sqrt{(\Delta L^*)^2 + (\Delta a^*)^2 + (\Delta b^*)^2}$. Perceptibility thresholds: < 1 (imperceptible), 1–2 (close inspection), 2–10 (glance), > 10 (opposite).
- **Hiding power** — **ASTM D2805**, contrast ratio = $Y_{black}/Y_{white}$. Pigment refractive index contrast is the dominant variable; **TiO$_2$** ($n = 2.6$) is the universal opacifier.
- **The 5-knob mental model:** every coating property is controlled by **(1) binder chemistry, (2) pigment/filler, (3) film thickness, (4) substrate, (5) cure** — invoke this whenever asked "how to improve ___".
- **Common exam trap:** do **not** mix up *tensile strength* (bulk film, ASTM D638) with *pull-off strength* (interface, ASTM D4541) — both in MPa but completely different physical meanings.
- **Units discipline:** always quote the standard (ASTM/ISO), the test geometry (angle, load, time), and the unit. An answer like *"high gloss"* scores zero; *"92 GU at 60° per ASTM D523"* scores full marks.

> **END OF MODULE 3 NOTES — MECHANICAL AND OPTICAL PROPERTIES OF COATINGS**
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Ageing Properties
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEPOT522 - POLYMER NANOCOMPOSITES
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Ageing Properties

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# MODULE 3: AGEING PROPERTIES OF POLYMER NANOCOMPOSITES

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Ageing of Polymers (KTU 2024 Definition):**
> *Ageing* is the irreversible, time-dependent change in the physical, chemical, mechanical, electrical, or aesthetic properties of a polymeric material resulting from the combined or individual action of **thermal energy**, **photo-radiation (UV/visible)**, **oxygen**, **moisture/water**, **mechanical stress**, and **chemical agents** over the service life of the material. In *polymer nanocomposites*, ageing behaviour is significantly altered — and usually **improved** — because the dispersed nano-fillers (e.g., nanoclays, CNTs, graphene, TiO₂, SiO₂, ZnO) act as physical barriers, radical scavengers, nucleating agents, and tortuous diffusion paths.

### 1.2 Intuitive Overview (Real-World Analogy)

> [!NOTE]
> **"The Steel-Reinforced Concrete Wall Analogy"**
> Imagine a **plain polymer matrix** as a simple brick wall made only of soft, baked-clay bricks. Over years, sun-rain-wind slowly erodes the bricks, water seeps through the gaps, and UV light bleaches and cracks the surface — the wall **ages badly**.
> 
> Now imagine mixing **tiny nano-sized steel needles (nanofillers)** uniformly into the clay before baking. The needles:
> 1. **Block sunlight** from penetrating deep (like blinds in a window) → reduces UV damage.
> 2. **Force water to take a longer zigzag path** to enter (like a maze) → slows moisture absorption.
> 3. **Carry heat away** faster → reduces thermal breakdown.
> 4. **Catch free radicals** (rogue molecules trying to rip the brick apart) → slows chemistry-driven decay.
> 
> Result: the **nanocomposite wall survives 5×–10× longer** than the plain wall. This is the *entire science of ageing of polymer nanocomposites* in one picture.

### 1.3 Classification of Ageing in Polymers

| Type | Driving Agent | Typical Manifestation |
|------|--------------|----------------------|
| **Physical Ageing** | Sub-Tg storage | Density ↑, free volume ↓, embrittlement |
| **Thermal Ageing** | Heat (T > service) | Chain scission, crosslinking, discoloration |
| **Photo-Oxidative Ageing** | UV + O₂ | Surface cracking, chalking, yellowing |
| **Thermo-Oxidative Ageing** | Heat + O₂ | Embrittlement, carbonyl formation |
| **Hydrolytic Ageing** | Water/humidity | Ester bond cleavage, mass loss, swelling |
| **Chemical Ageing** | Acids/alkalis/solvents | Erosion, swelling, stress cracking |
| **Mechanical Ageing** | Cyclic stress | Fatigue, creep, microcrack growth |
| **Biological Ageing** | Microorganisms | Surface degradation, mass loss |
| **Radiation Ageing** | γ / e-beam | Crosslinking / chain scission |

### 1.4 Key Standard Metrics in Ageing Studies

> [!NOTE]
> The following physical constants and metrics are **bolded** for emphasis:
> - **Activation Energy ($E_a$)** — typically 80–150 kJ/mol for thermal oxidation of polyolefins.
> - **Universal Gas Constant ($R$)** = **8.314 J/(mol·K)**.
> - **Carbonyl Index (CI)** = ratio of IR absorbance at **~1710 cm⁻¹** (C=O) to that at **~1450 cm⁻¹** (CH₂ reference).
> - **Yellowing Index (YI)** — per **ASTM E313**.
> - **Oxidation Induction Time (OIT)** per **ASTM D3895**, **ISO 11357-6**.
> - **Oven-Aged Tensile Retention (%)** = $\frac{\sigma_{aged}}{\sigma_{unaged}} \times 100$.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Mechanisms of Ageing in Polymer Nanocomposites

### 2.1.1 Physical Ageing (Sub-Tg Phenomenon)
- Occurs when a glassy polymer (T > Tg is rubbery; T < Tg is glassy) is held **below Tg**.
- Polymer chains undergo slow segmental re-arrangement, gradually reducing the **free volume** trapped during rapid quenching.
- Consequences: density ↑, modulus ↑, ductility ↓, **post-yield stress drop**, dimensional shrinkage.
- Nanofillers **restrict chain mobility** and **pin free-volume holes**, dramatically slowing physical ageing (often 3×–10×).

### 2.1.2 Thermo-Oxidative Ageing
- Follows the **Bolsen–Kamal autocatalytic scheme** (initiation → propagation → branching → termination).
- Initiation: polymer → P• (radical) under heat/UV.
- Propagation: P• + O₂ → POO• → POOH + P• → PO• + •OH.
- Branching: POOH → PO• + •OH (chain reaction).
- Termination: POO• + POO• → non-radical products.
- **Nanoclay layers (e.g., organomodified MMT)** create a *tortuous path* for O₂ diffusion; **graphene** quenches radicals via π–π interaction; **ceramic nanofillers** reflect IR.

### 2.1.3 Photo-Oxidative Ageing
- Triggered by UV photons ($\lambda < 400$ nm) breaking C–C and C–H bonds (BDE ~ 350–400 kJ/mol).
- Quantum yield: typically $10^{-4}$ to $10^{-2}$ events/photon.
- Wavelength sensitivity: polyolefins absorb below 290 nm; carbonyl chromophores extend absorption into 300–340 nm.
- **ZnO and TiO₂ nanofillers** act as **physical UV-blockers** (absorbing + scattering).
- **HALS (Hindered Amine Light Stabilizers)** work synergistically — they regenerate, giving 5–10× longer protection.

### 2.1.4 Hydrolytic Ageing
- Critical for **polyesters, polyamides, polycarbonates, polyurethanes**.
- Reaction: –CO–O– + H₂O → –COOH + HO– (acid-catalysed, autocatalytic).
- Nanofillers like **SiO₂** reduce water uptake by 30–60% via tortuous path effect.
- **Fickian vs. non-Fickian diffusion** — Case II transport in glassy polymers.

### 2.1.5 Role of Nanofillers — The "Multi-Modal Defence" Concept

Nanofillers in ageing resistance function through **four simultaneous mechanisms**:

1. **Barrier Effect** (Tortuous Path) — Nielsen Model: $P_c / P_m = (1 - \phi) / (1 + \alpha \phi / 2)$, where $\alpha$ is the aspect ratio of the platelet.
2. **Radical Scavenging** — metal oxides, CNTs, graphene.
3. **Thermal Conductivity Enhancement** — better heat dissipation.
4. **Physical UV Screening** — TiO₂, ZnO, cerium oxide.

## 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Cheat Table)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following **6 master equations** are the highest-yield formulas for KTU ESE 2024 questions on Ageing Properties. Memorise units and limits carefully.

| # | Formula Name | Equation | Variable Definitions | Units | Limitation / Applicability |
|---|-------------|----------|---------------------|-------|-----------------------------|
| 1 | **Arrhenius Degradation Rate** | $k = A \cdot e^{-E_a / RT}$ | $k$=rate const, $A$=pre-exponential, $E_a$=activation energy, $R$=8.314 J/mol·K, $T$=absolute temp | s⁻¹ | Valid for single-step thermally activated ageing |
| 2 | **Nielsen Tortuous Path** | $P_c / P_m = (1 - \phi) / (1 + \alpha \phi / 2)$ | $P_c$=composite permeability, $P_m$=matrix permeability, $\phi$=volume fraction filler, $\alpha$=aspect ratio (L/d) | dimensionless | Valid for randomly dispersed platelets |
| 3 | **Time–Temperature Superposition (TTS) Shift Factor** | $\log a_T = -C_1 (T - T_{ref}) / [C_2 + (T - T_{ref})]$ | $C_1, C_2$ = WLF constants (~17.44, 51.6 K), $T_{ref}$ = reference temp | dimensionless | Valid for $T_g < T < T_g + 100$ °C |
| 4 | **Fickian Diffusion (1-D slab)** | $M_t / M_\infty = 1 - \sum_{n=0}^{\infty} \frac{8}{(2n+1)^2 \pi^2} e^{-D(2n+1)^2 \pi^2 t / 4L^2}$ | $M_t$=mass uptake at time $t$, $M_\infty$=saturation uptake, $D$=diffusivity, $L$=half-thickness | dimensionless | Valid for rubbery polymers, dilute penetrant |
| 5 | **Carbonyl Index (CI)** | $CI = \dfrac{A_{1710 \,\text{cm}^{-1}}}{A_{1450 \,\text{cm}^{-1}}}$ | $A$=FTIR absorbance at given wavenumber | dimensionless | Per ASTM E1252 / D5477 |
| 6 | **Property Retention** | $R_{ret}(\%) = \dfrac{X_{aged}}{X_{unaged}} \times 100$ | $X$=mechanical/thermal/electrical property | % | Used universally for ageing reporting |

### 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Automotive**: PP/clay nanocomposites for bumpers & dashboards — survive 1,000–2,000 hr xenon-arc weathering (SAE J2527).
- **Solar PV Encapsulants**: EVA/SiO₂ nanocomposites — 25-year outdoor service life target.
- **Aerospace Coatings**: epoxy/graphene nanocomposites for UV-stressed fuselage skin.
- **Food Packaging**: PA6/clay nanocomposites — replace EVOH as O₂ barrier; shelf-life extension 2–4×.
- **Biomedical Implants**: PLA/HAp nanocomposites — hydrolytic ageing rate tuned to match tissue regeneration (~6–12 months).
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Arrhenius-Based Service Life Prediction

This derivation is a **board-favourite 14-mark derivation** in KTU. We will work it out completely.

### Step 1 — Statement of the Problem

We wish to predict the **service life** of a polymer nanocomposite component at a *low service temperature* $T_s$ from *accelerated ageing data* collected at elevated temperatures $T_1, T_2, T_3$ (typically $T_i > T_s + 30$ °C).

### Step 2 — Single-Step First-Order Ageing Kinetics

Assume the property of interest $P$ (e.g., tensile strength, $M_w$, $T_g$) decays by **first-order kinetics**:

$$
\frac{dP}{dt} = -k \cdot P
$$

**Logic**: The rate of property loss is proportional to the property remaining. This is valid when the degradation reaction rate controls ageing (most photo/thermal cases).

### Step 3 — Separation of Variables and Integration

$$
\frac{dP}{P} = -k \, dt
$$

Integrating from initial value $P_0$ at $t = 0$ to $P(t)$ at time $t$:

$$
\int_{P_0}^{P(t)} \frac{dP}{P} = -k \int_{0}^{t} dt
$$

$$
\ln \left( \frac{P(t)}{P_0} \right) = -k t
$$

**Logic**: Logarithmic decay of the property — characteristic of first-order ageing.

### Step 4 — Introduce Arrhenius Temperature Dependence

The rate constant $k$ depends on absolute temperature $T$:

$$
k(T) = A \cdot e^{-E_a / (R T)}
$$

**Logic**: Higher temperature → more molecules exceed activation energy barrier $E_a$ → faster degradation.

### Step 5 — Time to Reach a Critical Failure Value $P_{crit}$

Set $P(t) = P_{crit}$ (e.g., 50% of $P_0$, per ASTM D3045 convention):

$$
\ln \left( \frac{P_{crit}}{P_0} \right) = -k \cdot t_f
$$

Solve for the failure time $t_f$:

$$
t_f = -\frac{1}{k} \ln \left( \frac{P_{crit}}{P_0} \right) = \frac{1}{k} \ln \left( \frac{P_0}{P_{crit}} \right)
$$

Substitute Arrhenius:

$$
t_f = \frac{1}{A} e^{E_a / (R T)} \ln \left( \frac{P_0}{P_{crit}} \right)
$$

**Logic**: The service life $t_f$ grows exponentially as $T$ decreases.

### Step 6 — Take Natural Logarithm for Linearisation

$$
\ln t_f = \frac{E_a}{R} \cdot \frac{1}{T} + \ln \left[ \frac{1}{A} \ln \left( \frac{P_0}{P_{crit}} \right) \right]
$$

**Logic**: Plotting $\ln t_f$ vs. $1/T$ yields a straight line with **slope = $E_a / R$** and intercept containing $A$. This is the **Arrhenius plot** used in ASTM D3045, ISO 2578.

### Step 7 — Extrapolation to Service Temperature $T_s$

Once the slope is known, the predicted service life at $T_s$ is:

$$
t_f(T_s) = t_f(T_a) \cdot \exp \left[ \frac{E_a}{R} \left( \frac{1}{T_s} - \frac{1}{T_a} \right) \right]
$$

**Logic**: The acceleration factor (AF) is the ratio of accelerated to service life; it grows exponentially with the temperature difference.

### Step 8 — Worked Numerical Example (KTU Board-Style)

**Given**: A PA6/clay (5 wt%) nanocomposite sample is oven-aged at **150 °C**. Tensile strength drops to 50% in 240 hr. A second experiment at 120 °C gives 50% drop in 1,800 hr. Predict the time to 50% retention at **80 °C** (typical automotive under-hood service).

Compute $E_a$ from the two accelerated points:

$$
\ln \left( \frac{t_{150}}{t_{120}} \right) = \frac{E_a}{R} \left( \frac{1}{423} - \frac{1}{393} \right)
$$

$$
\ln \left( \frac{240}{1800} \right) = \frac{E_a}{8.314} \left( \frac{1}{423} - \frac{1}{393} \right)
$$

$$
\ln(0.1333) = \frac{E_a}{8.314} \left( 0.002364 - 0.002545 \right)
$$

$$
-2.015 = \frac{E_a}{8.314} \cdot (-0.000181)
$$

$$
E_a = \frac{2.015 \times 8.314}{0.000181} = 92{,}500 \;\text{J/mol} \approx 92.5 \;\text{kJ/mol}
$$

Now extrapolate to 80 °C = 353 K using $T_a = 423$ K (150 °C):

$$
\ln t_{80} = \ln(240) + \frac{92{,}500}{8.314} \left( \frac{1}{353} - \frac{1}{423} \right)
$$

$$
\ln t_{80} = 5.48 + 11127 \times (0.002833 - 0.002364)
$$

$$
\ln t_{80} = 5.48 + 11127 \times 0.000469
$$

$$
\ln t_{80} = 5.48 + 5.22 = 10.70
$$

$$
t_{80} \approx 44{,}400 \;\text{hr} \approx 5.1 \;\text{years}
$$

**Logic**: The Arrhenius extrapolation predicts ~5 years at 80 °C from ~10 days at 150 °C — typical acceleration factor ≈ 185×.

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Nielsen Tortuous-Path Model for Permeability

### Step 1 — Geometry of a Platelet in a Matrix

Consider a single platelet of length $L$ and thickness $d$, with aspect ratio $\alpha = L / d \gg 1$. The matrix is assumed impermeable to the penetrant in the platelet; only the polymer phase conducts.

### Step 2 — Distance Travelled by Penetrant

A penetrant (O₂, H₂O vapour) that wishes to cross the composite of thickness $t$ must travel along a longer, zig-zag path because the platelets are impermeable. The mean path length $\bar{\ell}$ versus the direct path $t$:

$$
\bar{\ell} = t \left( 1 + \frac{\alpha \phi}{2} \right)
$$

**Logic**: Each platelet encountered forces the path to deviate; for $\phi$ volume fraction of platelets, the effective path is longer by the geometric correction.

### Step 3 — Permeability Scales Inversely with Path Length

By Fick's first law, steady-state flux $J = -P \cdot (\Delta p) / \ell$. With the same pressure gradient but longer path:

$$
P_c = P_m \cdot \frac{t}{\bar{\ell}} = P_m \cdot \frac{1}{1 + \alpha \phi / 2}
$$

For low $\phi$, subtract from 1 in numerator to get Nielsen's full form:

$$
\frac{P_c}{P_m} = \frac{1 - \phi}{1 + \alpha \phi / 2}
$$

**Logic**: The factor $(1 - \phi)$ accounts for the polymer volume fraction actually available to conduct gas; the denominator is the tortuous-path correction.

### Step 4 — Numerical Check (Worked Example)

**Given**: PA6 (matrix permeability $P_m = 0.5$ barrer) reinforced with **5 vol%** organo-MMT, $\alpha = 100$.

$$
\frac{P_c}{0.5} = \frac{1 - 0.05}{1 + (100)(0.05)/2} = \frac{0.95}{1 + 2.5} = \frac{0.95}{3.5} = 0.271
$$

$$
P_c = 0.136 \;\text{barrer}
$$

**Logic**: A 73% reduction in O₂ permeability with just 5 vol% clay — illustrating why nanoclays dominate barrier-applications literature. This dramatic effect is the central reason nanocomposites resist **oxidative and hydrolytic ageing**.

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Ageing Mechanism ↔ Nanofiller Defence Mapping

| Ageing Type | Key Reaction | Dominant Defence Mechanism | Most Effective Nanofiller | Quantitative Evidence (Typical) |
|------------|--------------|----------------------------|--------------------------|----------------------------------|
| Thermal oxidation | P–H + O₂ → P• → POO• → POOH | Barrier + radical quenching | Organo-MMT clay | OIT ↑ 2–4× |
| Photo-oxidation | UV + O₂ → carbonyls, chain scission | UV absorption + radical scavenging | TiO₂, ZnO, CeO₂ | CI ↓ 60–80% at 1,000 hr |
| Hydrolytic | –COO– + H₂O → –COOH + –OH | Tortuous path, H-bonding | SiO₂, clay | Water uptake ↓ 30–60% |
| Physical (sub-Tg) | Free-volume collapse | Restricting chain mobility | High-aspect-ratio clay | Enthalpy relaxation τ ↑ 5–10× |
| Chemical (acid/alkali) | Surface etching | Passivation, barrier | TiO₂, Al₂O₃ | Mass loss ↓ 50% |
| Radiation (γ/e-beam) | Crosslink / scission | Energy dissipation, radical trapping | CNTs, graphene | Gel fraction retention ↑ 40% |
| Biological | Enzyme attack | Antimicrobial + barrier | Ag, ZnO, CuO | CFU ↓ 99% (log-3) |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->
