## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (2 × 3 Marks = 6 Marks)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model]**
*Distinguish between **adhesive failure** and **cohesive failure** in a polymer coating. Which type is generally more desirable in a well-designed coating system, and why?*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- **Adhesive failure (1 mark):** The fracture propagates along the *coating–substrate interface*. The detached coating surface appears clean, and the substrate is exposed.
- **Cohesive failure (1 mark):** The fracture propagates *through the bulk of the coating material itself*. Coating residue is visible on both the substrate and the detached film.
- **Desirability (1 mark):** Cohesive failure is generally preferred in a well-designed coating system because it indicates that the *adhesive bond at the interface is stronger than the bulk coating*. This means the limiting factor is now the coating's own strength, and the interface is "doing its job".

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model]**
*State the **Young–Dupré equation** and explain the physical significance of the contact angle θ in determining the wettability of a polymer coating on a substrate.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- **Equation (1.5 marks):**
$$W_a \;=\; \gamma_L(1 + \cos\theta)$$
- **Significance of θ (1.5 marks):** The contact angle θ, measured through the liquid (coating) at the three-phase contact line, is a direct measure of *wettability*:
  - θ < 30° → excellent wetting, high $W_a$
  - 30° ≤ θ ≤ 90° → moderate wetting
  - θ > 90° → poor wetting, low $W_a$, spontaneous dewetting risk
A lower contact angle indicates better spreading of the coating resin over the substrate, producing more intimate molecular contact and therefore a stronger adhesive bond.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Full-Answer Questions (Module Internal Choice: 1 × 14 Marks)

#### 🔹 Question A (14 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model — Module 4 Choice A]**

**(a) Explain in detail the different mechanisms of adhesion in polymer coatings. Describe how the addition of nano-fillers modifies these mechanisms. (7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

The five classical mechanisms of adhesion in polymer coatings are:

1. **Mechanical Interlocking (2 marks):** The coating resin flows into the micro-cavities, pores, and asperities of the substrate surface and hardens, forming physical "anchor points". This mechanism is dominant on roughened metallic substrates (e.g., sandblasted steel) and on porous substrates (e.g., wood, concrete). The strength contribution is approximately proportional to the *real* interfacial contact area, which is greater than the apparent area by a factor of (1 + r·R_a).

2. **Adsorption / Thermodynamic Theory (2 marks):** Once the coating has wetted the substrate, short-range van der Waals forces (London dispersion, Keesom dipole–dipole, Debye induction) and acid–base interactions act across the interface. These forces operate over 0.2–1.0 nm range. The work of adhesion is given by the Young–Dupré equation $W_a = \gamma_L(1 + \cos\theta)$.

3. **Chemical Bonding (1.5 marks):** Reactive functional groups in the coating resin (e.g., epoxide groups of epoxy, isocyanate of polyurethane, silanol of silicone) form primary chemical bonds (covalent, ionic, hydrogen) with active sites on the substrate (e.g., surface hydroxyls on metals, glass, ceramics). These bonds (50–1000 kJ/mol) are orders of magnitude stronger than van der Waals interactions.

4. **Diffusion Theory (1 mark):** Polymer chain segments from the coating inter-diffuse with segments of a polymeric substrate, forming an "interphase" of entangled chains. This mechanism is most relevant for polymer–polymer adhesion (e.g., a polyurethane topcoat on a polyurethane primer) and is not applicable to metal substrates.

5. **Electrostatic Theory (0.5 mark):** When the coating and substrate differ in electronic work function, charge transfer occurs across the interface, creating an electrical double layer that resists separation.

**Modification by Nano-fillers (1 mark):**
- SiO₂, TiO₂, and nanoclay create *nanoscale roughness*, increasing the real contact area and amplifying mechanical interlocking.
- Surface-functionalized nano-fillers (e.g., amino-silanized SiO₂) participate in *chemical bonding* with both the matrix and the substrate.
- Well-dispersed nano-fillers *increase cohesive strength* of the film, shifting the failure mode from cohesive to adhesive, and toughen the interface against crack propagation.

**(b) Discuss the various factors that affect the strength of the adhesive bond in a polymer nanocomposite coating. (7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

The major factors are organized into four groups:

**1. Substrate-Related Factors (2 marks):**
- **Surface roughness (R_a):** Optimum roughness (~ 50–75 μm for steel) maximizes mechanical interlocking. Over-rough surfaces trap air.
- **Surface cleanliness:** Oils, dust, and oxides reduce contact area and prevent molecular interaction; *solvent cleaning + grit blasting + chemical etching* can raise bond strength 2–5×.
- **Surface energy (γ_S):** High-energy surfaces (metals, ceramics) wet better than low-energy surfaces (PTFE, PE).

**2. Coating-Related Factors (2 marks):**
- **Surface tension (γ_L):** Must be ≤ γ_S for spontaneous spreading (Young's criterion).
- **Viscosity:** Low viscosity aids wetting; high viscosity may entrap air at the interface.
- **Molecular weight and crosslink density:** Higher crosslink density ⇒ higher cohesive strength but also higher internal stress on cure.
- **Nano-filler loading:** Optimum at 1–5 wt %; agglomeration above this reduces bond strength.

**3. Application and Curing Conditions (2 marks):**
- **Curing temperature and time:** Insufficient cure → weak cohesive strength; excessive T_cure → thermal-stress delamination.
- **Coating thickness:** Thin films (50–150 μm) generally give higher pull-off strengths; thick films build up residual stress.
- **Humidity during cure:** High RH can hydrolyze sensitive interfaces (polyesters, polyurethanes).

**4. Service and Environmental Factors (1 mark):**
- Temperature, UV, moisture, chemical exposure, and mechanical loading mode (peel is more damaging than tension).
- Thermal-expansion mismatch between coating and substrate generates cyclic interfacial stress.

**A representative KTU board "in 1 line" answer would list 4–6 factors with one-line explanations and one labelled diagram.**

#### 🔹 Question B (14 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model — Module 4 Choice B]**

**(a) Derive the Young–Dupré equation starting from Young's equation. Explain the importance of the contact angle measurement in evaluating the wettability and adhesive strength of a polymer coating on a solid substrate. (7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Derivation (4 marks):**
Starting from Young's equation at the three-phase contact line:

$$\gamma_{SV} \;=\; \gamma_{SL} \;+\; \gamma_{LV} \cos\theta \quad \text{...(1)}$$

Dupré's work of adhesion between the solid and liquid:

$$W_a \;=\; \gamma_{SV} \;+\; \gamma_{LV} \;-\; \gamma_{SL} \quad \text{...(2)}$$

Rearranging Young's equation: $\gamma_{SV} - \gamma_{SL} = \gamma_{LV}\cos\theta$.

Substituting into (2):

$$W_a \;=\; (\gamma_{SL} + \gamma_{LV}\cos\theta) + \gamma_{LV} - \gamma_{SL} = \gamma_{LV}(1 + \cos\theta)$$

This is the **Young–Dupré equation**. (Full step-by-step derivation shown in Section 3.1 of these notes.)

**Importance of contact angle (3 marks):**
- θ directly quantifies *wettability*: small θ = good wetting.
- Through Young–Dupré, θ gives the *thermodynamic work of adhesion* $W_a$.
- Using the OWRK method, contact angles measured with multiple probe liquids yield both the dispersive and polar components of substrate surface energy — a critical input for selecting compatible coating systems.
- In nanocomposite coatings, the contact angle is also used to verify the effectiveness of nano-filler surface modification (e.g., silane treatment).

**(b) Describe the methods used to evaluate the adhesion strength of coatings. Compare the pull-off adhesion test (ASTM D4541) and the cross-cut tape test (ASTM D3359), highlighting their relative advantages and limitations. (7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**1. Pull-Off Adhesion Test – ASTM D4541 (3.5 marks):**
- **Procedure:** A loading fixture (dolly) is glued perpendicular to the coating surface. A controlled tensile load is applied using a portable hydraulic or mechanical tester until the coating detaches.
- **Output:** Quantitative pull-off strength in MPa (or N/mm²). The failure mode (adhesive, cohesive, mixed) is also noted.
- **Advantages:** Quantitative; reproducible; applicable in field and laboratory.
- **Limitations:** Destructive; requires dolly bonding; time-consuming.

**2. Cross-Cut Tape Test – ASTM D3359 (2.5 marks):**
- **Procedure:** A lattice pattern is cut through the coating down to the substrate using a utility knife. Pressure-sensitive tape is applied and rapidly pulled off. The removed coating is rated on a 0B–5B scale.
- **Output:** Semi-quantitative rating (5B = no removal; 0B = > 65 % removal).
- **Advantages:** Quick, inexpensive, field-portable, non-instrumented.
- **Limitations:** Semi-quantitative; operator-dependent; not suitable for very thick or very thin coatings.

**3. Other Methods (1 mark):**
- **Scratch test** (ASTM D7027) — measures critical load for coating detachment.
- **Bend test** (ASTM D522) — for flexible substrates.
- **Indentation test** — measures interfacial fracture toughness.

**Comparison Summary (in 2 lines):**
- *Pull-off* gives a continuous quantitative value (MPa), whereas *tape test* gives a discrete rating (0B–5B).
- *Pull-off* is preferred for research and quality-critical applications; *tape test* is preferred for rapid in-process quality control.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **⚠️ KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout — Common Mark-Loss Traps:**
> 1. **Confusing adhesion with cohesion (–2 marks typical).** Always state the *interface* for adhesion and the *bulk* for cohesion in the first sentence.
> 2. **Wrong sign in Young's equation (–1 mark).** The correct form is $\gamma_{SV} = \gamma_{SL} + \gamma_{LV}\cos\theta$. Some students write $\gamma_{SL} = \gamma_{SV} + \gamma_{LV}\cos\theta$ — **wrong**.
> 3. **Forgetting units** in the final answer (–0.5 mark). Always state mN/m for surface tension, mJ/m² for work of adhesion, MPa for pull-off strength.
> 4. **Listing factors without grouping** (–1 mark). Examiners expect factors organized under substrate / coating / curing / service headings, not as a flat list.
> 5. **Omitting the diagram** when asked to "explain" a mechanism (–1 to –2 marks). A neat sketch of a sessile drop with labeled $\gamma_{SV}$, $\gamma_{SL}$, $\gamma_{LV}$, and θ is *expected* for full marks.
> 6. **Using "stickiness" as a synonym for adhesion** — this is a vague term and earns no credit; the correct term is *interfacial bond strength*.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- **Adhesion** = forces at the *coating–substrate interface*; **Cohesion** = forces *within the bulk coating*.
- **Five mechanisms:** mechanical interlocking, adsorption, chemical bonding, diffusion, electrostatic.
- **Key Equation #1 — Young:** $\gamma_{SV} = \gamma_{SL} + \gamma_{LV}\cos\theta$
- **Key Equation #2 — Young–Dupré:** $W_a = \gamma_L(1 + \cos\theta)$
- **Key Equation #3 — Fowkes / OWRK:** dispersive + polar decomposition of surface energy.
- **Lower contact angle** ⇒ better wetting ⇒ higher work of adhesion ⇒ stronger bond.
- **Optimum surface roughness (R_a) for steel:** ~ 50–75 μm (mechanical interlocking sweet spot).
- **Nano-filler sweet spot:** 1–5 wt %; agglomeration above this *reduces* bond strength.
- **Pull-off strength (ASTM D4541):** reported in **MPa**; typical "good" coating > 7 MPa.
- **Cross-cut tape test (ASTM D3359):** rated 0B to 5B.
- **Four factor groups:** Substrate factors, Coating factors, Curing factors, Service factors.
- **Failure modes:** Adhesive, Cohesive, Substrate, Mixed-mode (most common in real service).
- **Spreading condition:** $\gamma_{SV} > \gamma_{SL} + \gamma_{LV}$ ⇒ $S > 0$ ⇒ spontaneous spreading.
- **Work of cohesion:** $W_c = 2\gamma_L$.
- **Adhesion is a *system property*, not a material property** — always quote the substrate + coating pair.
- **Thermal expansion mismatch** is one of the *most under-appreciated* causes of adhesive failure in service.
- **For nanocomposite coatings**, nano-fillers improve both *adhesive* (via mechanical keying and chemical coupling) and *cohesive* (via matrix reinforcement) components of the bond.
- **A coating that fails adhesively** indicates a problem at the interface; **a coating that fails cohesively** indicates the interface is sound but the film is weak.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Thermodynamics of Adhesion
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEPOT522 - POLYMER NANOCOMPOSITES
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Thermodynamics of Adhesion

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Thermodynamics of Adhesion** is the branch of surface science that quantifies the energetics of intermolecular forces acting across an interface between a polymeric adhesive (or polymer nanocomposite matrix) and a solid substrate. It is governed by the principle that **adhesion is a surface phenomenon** driven by the reduction in total Gibbs free energy of the system when an interface is formed.

In polymer nanocomposites, the thermodynamic work of adhesion ($W_A$) is the reversible work required to separate a unit area of the adhesive-substrate interface, creating two new surfaces (solid-vapor and liquid-vapor). It is mathematically expressed as:

$$
W_A = \gamma_S + \gamma_L - \gamma_{SL}
$$

where $\gamma_S$ is the surface free energy of the solid, $\gamma_L$ is the surface free energy of the liquid (polymer melt), and $\gamma_{SL}$ is the interfacial free energy between them.

The fundamental governing equation of adhesion thermodynamics is the **Young-Dupré equation**:

$$
W_A = \gamma_L(1 + \cos\theta)
$$

where $\theta$ is the equilibrium contact angle of the liquid polymer droplet on the solid substrate.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy — "The Magnet and the Iron Filing"**
>
> Imagine you are trying to pull a sticky tape (the polymer) off a smooth table (the substrate). The force you need to apply is the **practical adhesion**. But the *thermodynamic work of adhesion* is the energy required to break every single microscopic "molecular handshake" between the tape and the table at the interface — one by one, in a perfectly reversible manner. It is the "ideal" energy cost of separation, measured in joules per square meter ($\text{J/m}^2$), not newtons.
>
> In polymer nanocomposites, the addition of nanofillers (clay, silica, CNTs, graphene) modifies the surface energy of the polymer, thereby *tuning* how strongly it adheres to a substrate or fiber.

## 1.3 Key Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

- **Surface free energy ($\gamma$)**: Measured in $\text{mJ/m}^2$ or equivalently $\text{ergs/cm}^2$. Typical values: water $\approx 72.2 \text{ mJ/m}^2$; polyethylene $\approx 31 \text{ mJ/m}^2$; PTFE $\approx 18 \text{ mJ/m}^2$.
- **Interfacial tension ($\gamma_{SL}$)**: Always a positive quantity in $\text{mJ/m}^2$.
- **Contact angle ($\theta$)**: Dimensionless, expressed in degrees ($0°\text{–}180°$).
- **Universal gas constant ($R$)**: $8.314 \text{ J/(mol·K)}$.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight (KTU 2024 — PEPOT522 Module 4)**
> Students are expected to derive the Young-Dupré equation, apply the Fowkes / Owens-Wendt / Wu approaches to compute $\gamma_{SL}$ from contact angle data, and interpret the thermodynamic vs. practical adhesion distinction as it applies to nanofiller-modified polymer systems.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Step-by-Step Logical Framework

### Step 1: The Three-Phase Equilibrium — Young's Equation
At the three-phase contact line (solid, liquid, vapor), mechanical equilibrium of surface tension vectors yields:

$$
\gamma_{SV} = \gamma_{SL} + \gamma_{LV}\cos\theta
$$

- $\gamma_{SV}$ = solid-vapor surface energy
- $\gamma_{SL}$ = solid-liquid interfacial energy
- $\gamma_{LV}$ = liquid-vapor surface energy (= $\gamma_L$ in simplified notation)
- $\theta$ = equilibrium contact angle

> **Why it matters:** This is the *mechanical* balance. The thermodynamic statement comes next.

### Step 2: The Dupré Equation — Pure Thermodynamic Work
Combining Young's equation with the definition of work of adhesion gives the **Young-Dupré equation**:

$$
W_A = \gamma_{LV}(1 + \cos\theta)
$$

> **Why it matters:** It directly converts a *measurable* quantity (the contact angle $\theta$) into the *thermodynamic* work of adhesion.

### Step 3: Decomposition of Surface Energy
Surface free energy is composed of multiple intermolecular contributions. The most general decomposition (van Oss) is:

$$
\gamma = \gamma^{LW} + \gamma^{AB} = \gamma^{LW} + 2\sqrt{\gamma^+\gamma^-}
$$

- $\gamma^{LW}$ — Lifshitz–van der Waals (dispersive) component
- $\gamma^{AB}$ — Acid–base (polar) component
- $\gamma^+$ — Lewis acid parameter
- $\gamma^-$ — Lewis base parameter

A more commonly used simplification (Owens–Wendt) is:

$$
\gamma = \gamma^d + \gamma^p
$$

### Step 4: Interfacial Energy from Component Theory
The interfacial energy between two condensed phases is calculated using geometric or harmonic mean combining rules.

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **The following table is HIGH-YIELD for the KTU board exam. Memorize the rightmost column verbatim.**

| # | Equation Name | Mathematical Form | Variables & Units | Physical Meaning |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Dupré Equation | $W_A = \gamma_S + \gamma_L - \gamma_{SL}$ | $W_A$ in $\text{mJ/m}^2$ | Reversible work of adhesion |
| 2 | Young Equation | $\gamma_{SV} = \gamma_{SL} + \gamma_{LV}\cos\theta$ | $\theta$ in degrees | Mechanical equilibrium at contact line |
| 3 | Young–Dupré | $W_A = \gamma_L(1 + \cos\theta)$ | $\gamma_L$ in $\text{mJ/m}^2$ | Work of adhesion from contact angle |
| 4 | Work of Cohesion | $W_C = 2\gamma_L$ | $\text{mJ/m}^2$ | Energy to separate two identical liquids |
| 5 | Spreading Coefficient | $S = \gamma_S - \gamma_{SL} - \gamma_L$ | $\text{mJ/m}^2$ | $S>0 \Rightarrow$ complete wetting |
| 6 | Fowkes Equation | $\gamma_{SL} = \gamma_S + \gamma_L - 2\sqrt{\gamma_S^d\gamma_L^d}$ | $\text{mJ/m}^2$ | Dispersive-only interfacial energy |
| 7 | Owens–Wendt (OWRK) | $\gamma_{SL} = \gamma_S + \gamma_L - 2\left(\sqrt{\gamma_S^d\gamma_L^d} + \sqrt{\gamma_S^p\gamma_L^p}\right)$ | $\text{mJ/m}^2$ | Dispersive + polar components |
| 8 | Wu Harmonic Mean | $\gamma_{SL} = \gamma_S + \gamma_L - 4\left(\dfrac{\gamma_S^d\gamma_L^d}{\gamma_S^d+\gamma_L^d} + \dfrac{\gamma_S^p\gamma_L^p}{\gamma_S^p+\gamma_L^p}\right)$ | $\text{mJ/m}^2$ | Better for polymer–polymer systems |
| 9 | Young–Dupré (OWRK form) | $\gamma_L(1+\cos\theta) = 2\left(\sqrt{\gamma_S^d\gamma_L^d} + \sqrt{\gamma_S^p\gamma_L^p}\right)$ | $\text{mJ/m}^2$ | Contact-angle data fit to OWRK |
| 10 | Acid–Base (van Oss) | $\gamma_{SL} = \gamma_S + \gamma_L - 2\left(\sqrt{\gamma_S^{LW}\gamma_L^{LW}} + \sqrt{\gamma_S^+\gamma_L^-} + \sqrt{\gamma_S^-\gamma_L^+}\right)$ | $\text{mJ/m}^2$ | Full Lifshitz + acid–base model |

> **CRITICAL NOTATION RULE (to prevent table break in markdown):** All absolute value bars or "such that" delimiters in equations above are rendered using LaTeX (e.g., `\vert`, `\mid`), never the raw pipe character `\vert`.

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Fiber–matrix adhesion in nanocomposites:** Predicting whether a polymer melt will wet a carbon fiber or a nanoclay surface.
- **Coating technology:** Selecting solvents and resins that spontaneously spread on substrates.
- **Adhesive formulation:** Designing epoxy or polyurethane adhesives with tuned surface chemistry.
- **Biomedical devices:** Polymer–tissue adhesion for implants, drug-delivery patches.
- **Microelectronics:** Underfill encapsulation in flip-chip packaging.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: From Young's Equation to Young–Dupré

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Goal:** Combine the mechanical force balance (Young) with the thermodynamic definition of $W_A$ (Dupré) to obtain a *directly measurable* formula for the work of adhesion.

**Step 1: Start with Young's equation (force balance at the contact line)**

$$
\gamma_{SV} = \gamma_{SL} + \gamma_{LV}\cos\theta
$$

**Step 2: Rearrange to isolate $\gamma_{SL}$**

$$
\gamma_{SL} = \gamma_{SV} - \gamma_{LV}\cos\theta
$$

> *Logic:* Subtract $\gamma_{LV}\cos\theta$ from both sides of the original balance. This is purely algebraic isolation.

**Step 3: Substitute into the Dupré definition of work of adhesion**

$$
W_A = \gamma_{SV} + \gamma_{LV} - \gamma_{SL}
$$

**Step 4: Replace $\gamma_{SL}$ using the result of Step 2**

$$
W_A = \gamma_{SV} + \gamma_{LV} - \left(\gamma_{SV} - \gamma_{LV}\cos\theta\right)
$$

> *Logic:* The substitution links the mechanical observable ($\theta$) with the thermodynamic quantity ($W_A$).

**Step 5: Simplify by cancelling $\gamma_{SV}$ terms**

$$
W_A = \cancel{\gamma_{SV}} + \gamma_{LV} - \cancel{\gamma_{SV}} + \gamma_{LV}\cos\theta
$$

**Step 6: Collect like terms**

$$
W_A = \gamma_{LV} + \gamma_{LV}\cos\theta = \gamma_{LV}(1 + \cos\theta)
$$

**Step 7: Final result — the Young–Dupré equation**

$$
\boxed{W_A = \gamma_{LV}(1 + \cos\theta)}
$$

> *Physical interpretation:* Measuring just the contact angle $\theta$ and the liquid surface tension $\gamma_{LV}$ gives the thermodynamic work of adhesion. No information about the solid's surface energy or the interfacial energy is required.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2: The Fowkes Geometric Mean Approximation

**Step 1: Assume the interfacial energy arises only from dispersive (London) van der Waals forces between phases $S$ and $L$.**

$$
\gamma_{SL} = \gamma_S + \gamma_L - 2\sqrt{\gamma_S^d \cdot \gamma_L^d}
$$

> *Logic:* Fowkes postulated that the work of adhesion across a dispersive–dispersive interface equals the geometric mean of the two dispersive surface energies. This comes from the London dispersion interaction across a planar interface, which scales as the geometric mean of the Hamaker constants, and surface energy is proportional to the Hamaker constant for a given material.

**Step 2: If phase $L$ is also purely dispersive** (e.g., a saturated hydrocarbon like hexane), then $\gamma_L = \gamma_L^d$, and we get:

$$
W_A = 2\sqrt{\gamma_S^d \cdot \gamma_L^d}
$$

**Step 3: Combining with Young–Dupré**

$$
\gamma_L(1 + \cos\theta) = 2\sqrt{\gamma_S^d \cdot \gamma_L^d}
$$

**Step 4: Solve for the *dispersive* component of the solid's surface energy**

$$
\gamma_S^d = \frac{\gamma_L(1 + \cos\theta)^2}{4}
$$

> *Logic:* Measuring $\theta$ with a purely dispersive test liquid (e.g., diiodomethane) yields the *dispersive* component of $\gamma_S$ directly.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3: The Owens–Wendt Extension

**Step 1: Extend Fowkes by adding a polar (Keeson + Debye + H-bond) component:**

$$
\gamma = \gamma^d + \gamma^p
$$

**Step 2: Assume polar–polar interactions also follow the geometric mean rule:**

$$
W_A^{polar} = 2\sqrt{\gamma_S^p \cdot \gamma_L^p}
$$

**Step 3: Total work of adhesion is the sum of dispersive + polar contributions:**

$$
W_A = 2\sqrt{\gamma_S^d \gamma_L^d} + 2\sqrt{\gamma_S^p \gamma_L^p}
$$

**Step 4: Equate with Young–Dupré:**

$$
\gamma_L(1 + \cos\theta) = 2\left(\sqrt{\gamma_S^d \gamma_L^d} + \sqrt{\gamma_S^p \gamma_L^p}\right)
$$

**Step 5: Divide by $2\sqrt{\gamma_L^d}$ and rearrange into the linear form $y = mx + c$:**

$$
\frac{\gamma_L(1+\cos\theta)}{2\sqrt{\gamma_L^d}} = \sqrt{\gamma_S^p} \cdot \sqrt{\frac{\gamma_L^p}{\gamma_L^d}} + \sqrt{\gamma_S^d}
$$

> *Logic:* This is the standard OWRK plot. Measuring $\theta$ for at least *two* probe liquids of known ($\gamma_L^d$, $\gamma_L^p$) gives a straight line whose slope and intercept yield $\gamma_S^p$ and $\gamma_S^d$ respectively. Total solid surface energy is then $\gamma_S = \gamma_S^d + \gamma_S^p$.

---

## 3.4 Numerical Example (Worked KTU-style)

> **Problem:** A polymer melt ($\gamma_L = 40 \text{ mJ/m}^2$, $\gamma_L^d = 30 \text{ mJ/m}^2$, $\gamma_L^p = 10 \text{ mJ/m}^2$) forms a contact angle of $\theta = 30°$ on a nanofiller-modified substrate. Compute: (a) $W_A$, (b) $\gamma_{SL}$ using Owens–Wendt if $\gamma_S^d = 28$ and $\gamma_S^p = 12 \text{ mJ/m}^2$.

**Solution:**

(a) Young–Dupré:

$$
W_A = 40 \times (1 + \cos 30°) = 40 \times (1 + 0.8660) = 40 \times 1.8660 = 74.64 \text{ mJ/m}^2
$$

(b) Owens–Wendt interfacial energy:

$$
\gamma_{SL} = 28 + 12 + 40 - 2\left(\sqrt{28 \times 30} + \sqrt{12 \times 10}\right)
$$

$$
\gamma_{SL} = 80 - 2\left(\sqrt{840} + \sqrt{120}\right) = 80 - 2(28.98 + 10.95) = 80 - 2(39.93)
$$

$$
\gamma_{SL} = 80 - 79.86 = 0.14 \text{ mJ/m}^2
$$

> **Physical interpretation:** An essentially zero interfacial energy indicates excellent thermodynamic compatibility between the polymer and the nanofiller-treated substrate — the system will spontaneously wet and form a strong interface.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture — The Adhesion Thermodynamics Workflow

The following diagram maps the complete analytical chain from raw experimental measurement to the final thermodynamic descriptors used to characterize polymer–nanofiller adhesion.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Probe Liquid Droplet] --> B[Measure Contact Angle theta]
    B --> C[Apply Young-Dupre Equation]
    C --> D[Compute Work of Adhesion W_A]
    B --> E[Apply Fowkes or OWRK Model]
    E --> F[Decompose Surface Energy]
    F --> G[Dispersive gamma_d]
    F --> H[Polar gamma_p]
    G --> I[Compute Interfacial Energy gamma_SL]
    H --> I
    I --> J[Compute Spreading Coefficient S]
    J --> K{Wetting Behaviour}
    K -->|S greater than 0| L[Complete Wetting Strong Adhesion]
    K -->|S less than 0| M[Partial Wetting Weak Adhesion]
    D --> N[Compare with W_C equals 2 gamma_L]
    N --> O{Adhesion vs Cohesion}
    O -->|W_A greater than W_C| P[Cohesive Failure in Adhesive]
    O -->|W_A less than W_C| Q[Adhesive Failure at Interface]
    L --> R[Optimize Nanofiller Surface Chemistry]
    M --> R
    P --> R
    Q --> R
    R --> S[Final Nanocomposite with Tuned Interfacial Energy]
```

## 4.2 Block Diagram — Surface Energy Decomposition Methods

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Total Surface Energy gamma] --> B[Method 1 Fowkes]
    A --> C[Method 2 Owens Wendt OWRK]
    A --> D[Method 3 Wu Harmonic Mean]
    A --> E[Method 4 van Oss Acid Base]
    B --> B1[gamma equals gamma_d only]
    C --> C1[gamma equals gamma_d plus gamma_p]
    D --> D1[gamma equals gamma_d plus gamma_p harmonic]
    E --> E1[gamma equals gamma_LW plus gamma_AB]
    B1 --> F[Choose Test Liquids]
    C1 --> F
    D1 --> F
    E1 --> F
    F --> G[At least Two Probe Liquids]
    G --> H[Compute Solid Surface Energy Components]
    H --> I[Compute gamma_SL]
    I --> J[Predict Wetting and Adhesion]
```

## 4.3 Block Diagram — Adhesion Failure Modes in Nanocomposites

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Applied Load on Adhered Joint] --> B{Type of Failure}
    B -->|Type 1| C[Adhesive Failure at Interface]
    B -->|Type 2| D[Cohesive Failure in Adhesive]
    B -->|Type 3| E[Cohesive Failure in Substrate]
    C --> F[Low W_A Indicator of Poor Wetting]
    D --> G[W_A greater than W_C Adhesive Stronger than Cohesion]
    E --> H[Substrate Weaker than Interface]
    F --> I[Modify Nanofiller Surface e.g. Silane Treatment]
    G --> J[Increase Adhesive Cohesion]
    H --> K[Strengthen Substrate]
    I --> L[Improved Thermodynamic Compatibility]
    J --> L
    K --> L
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Diagram Interpretation Tip (KTU Examiner's Hint):** When drawing or describing surface energy schematics in exams, ALWAYS label (i) the three interfacial tensions as arrows pointing away from the contact line, (ii) the contact angle $\theta$ *inside* the liquid droplet, and (iii) clearly distinguish $\gamma_{SV}$, $\gamma_{SL}$, and $\gamma_{LV}$. Examiners award 1–2 marks just for a correct, well-labeled diagram.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1
**[KTU University Exam — May 2024 Model]**
Define the **thermodynamic work of adhesion** and write the **Young–Dupré equation**. Explain the physical significance of each term.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- The thermodynamic work of adhesion $W_A$ is the reversible energy (per unit area) required to separate an adhesive–substrate interface into two free surfaces. It is a thermodynamic state function, not a force. (1 mark)
- The Young–Dupré equation is $W_A = \gamma_{LV}(1 + \cos\theta)$, where $\gamma_{LV}$ is the surface tension of the liquid adhesive and $\theta$ is the equilibrium contact angle. (1 mark)
- Physical significance: It links an *experimentally measurable* geometric quantity ($\theta$) to a *thermodynamic* quantity ($W_A$), enabling characterization of interfacial energetics without measuring $\gamma_{SV}$ directly. (1 mark)

---

### Question 2
**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Model]**
Differentiate between **work of adhesion** and **work of cohesion**. State the spreading criterion in terms of these quantities.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- *Work of adhesion* $W_A = \gamma_S + \gamma_L - \gamma_{SL}$: energy to separate a dissimilar interface. (1 mark)
- *Work of cohesion* $W_C = 2\gamma_L$: energy to separate two identical phases of the same liquid. (1 mark)
- Spreading criterion: a liquid spontaneously spreads on a solid if $W_A \geq W_C$, i.e., the spreading coefficient $S = W_A - W_C = \gamma_S - \gamma_{SL} - \gamma_L \geq 0$. (1 mark)

---

## Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Module Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) — Option 1

**[KTU University Exam — Sample Paper Style 2024]**
**(a)** Derive the **Young–Dupré equation** starting from Young's mechanical equilibrium condition. State the assumptions made. **(7 marks)**
**(b)** With a neat diagram, explain how **contact angle measurement** can be used to determine the surface free energy of a solid using the **Owens–Wendt (OWRK) method**. Show the linearised form of the OWRK equation. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution to (a):
1. State Young's equation: $\gamma_{SV} = \gamma_{SL} + \gamma_{LV}\cos\theta$. (1 mark)
2. Rearrange: $\gamma_{SL} = \gamma_{SV} - \gamma_{LV}\cos\theta$. (1 mark)
3. State Dupré's definition: $W_A = \gamma_{SV} + \gamma_{LV} - \gamma_{SL}$. (1 mark)
4. Substitute: $W_A = \gamma_{SV} + \gamma_{LV} - (\gamma_{SV} - \gamma_{LV}\cos\theta)$. (1 mark)
5. Simplify: $W_A = \gamma_{LV} + \gamma_{LV}\cos\theta$. (1 mark)
6. Final form: $W_A = \gamma_{LV}(1 + \cos\theta)$. (1 mark)
7. Assumptions: smooth, homogeneous, rigid, isotropic solid; no line tension; negligible gravitational distortion; thermodynamic equilibrium at the contact line. (1 mark)

#### Model Solution to (b):
1. Sketch: solid substrate, sessile liquid drop on top, vapor phase, with three interfacial tensions ($\gamma_{SV}$, $\gamma_{SL}$, $\gamma_{LV}$) shown as tangential vectors and $\theta$ marked inside the drop. (1 mark)
2. OWRK assumes surface energy decomposes as $\gamma = \gamma^d + \gamma^p$. (1 mark)
3. The OWRK equation: $\gamma_L(1+\cos\theta) = 2\left(\sqrt{\gamma_S^d\gamma_L^d} + \sqrt{\gamma_S^p\gamma_L^p}\right)$. (1 mark)
4. Divide both sides by $2\sqrt{\gamma_L^d}$: $\dfrac{\gamma_L(1+\cos\theta)}{2\sqrt{\gamma_L^d}} = \sqrt{\gamma_S^p}\sqrt{\dfrac{\gamma_L^p}{\gamma_L^d}} + \sqrt{\gamma_S^d}$. (1 mark)
5. This is $y = mx + c$ form, where $y = \dfrac{\gamma_L(1+\cos\theta)}{2\sqrt{\gamma_L^d}}$, $x = \sqrt{\dfrac{\gamma_L^p}{\gamma_L^d}}$, slope $= \sqrt{\gamma_S^p}$, intercept $= \sqrt{\gamma_S^d}$. (1 mark)
6. Procedure: measure $\theta$ for ≥ 2 probe liquids of known ($\gamma_L^d$, $\gamma_L^p$) — typically diiodomethane (dispersive) and water (dispersive + polar). (1 mark)
7. Plot, fit linear regression, extract $\gamma_S^d$ and $\gamma_S^p$, sum to get total solid surface energy $\gamma_S = \gamma_S^d + \gamma_S^p$. (1 mark)

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Option 2

**[KTU University Exam — Sample Paper Style 2024]**
**(a)** Describe the **Fowkes theory** for interfacial energy. Under what conditions is it strictly valid? **(7 marks)**
**(b)** A polymer nanocomposite matrix has $\gamma_L = 35 \text{ mJ/m}^2$ with $\gamma_L^d = 28 \text{ mJ/m}^2$ and $\gamma_L^p = 7 \text{ mJ/m}^2$. The contact angle of the polymer melt on a silane-treated nanofiller is $\theta = 45°$. Compute: (i) the work of adhesion $W_A$, (ii) the work of cohesion $W_C$, and (iii) the spreading coefficient $S$ given that $\gamma_S = 38 \text{ mJ/m}^2$. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution to (a):
1. Fowkes (1964) proposed that the interfacial free energy between two phases depends only on the **dispersive (London) component** of surface energy if both phases interact solely through dispersion forces. (2 marks)
2. The geometric mean form: $\gamma_{SL} = \gamma_S + \gamma_L - 2\sqrt{\gamma_S^d\gamma_L^d}$. (2 marks)
3. The factor of 2 and the geometric mean arise from London's pairwise dispersion interaction across a planar boundary. (1 mark)
4. Strict validity conditions: (a) both phases interact *only* via Lifshitz–van der Waals (dispersive) forces, (b) no polar, H-bonding, or acid–base interactions, (c) the phases are mutually insoluble and non-reactive. (1 mark)
5. Example systems: saturated hydrocarbons on low-energy solids (e.g., hexane on polyethylene or PTFE). (1 mark)

#### Model Solution to (b):
**(i) Work of adhesion:**

$$
W_A = \gamma_L(1 + \cos\theta) = 35(1 + \cos 45°) = 35(1 + 0.7071) = 35 \times 1.7071
$$

$$
W_A = 59.75 \text{ mJ/m}^2
$$

**(ii) Work of cohesion:**

$$
W_C = 2\gamma_L = 2 \times 35 = 70 \text{ mJ/m}^2
$$

**(iii) Spreading coefficient:**

$$
\gamma_{SL} = \gamma_S + \gamma_L - W_A = 38 + 35 - 59.75 = 13.25 \text{ mJ/m}^2
$$

$$
S = \gamma_S - \gamma_{SL} - \gamma_L = 38 - 13.25 - 35 = -10.25 \text{ mJ/m}^2
$$

> *Alternatively:* $S = W_A - W_C = 59.75 - 70 = -10.25 \text{ mJ/m}^2$ ✓ (both routes must agree)

**Interpretation:** $S < 0$ means the polymer melt will **not** spontaneously spread on this silane-treated nanofiller. To improve adhesion, the nanofiller surface should be modified (e.g., with aminosilanes) to raise $\gamma_S$ or reduce $\gamma_{SL}$. (1 mark for interpretation)

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfall Callout**
> 1. **Sign error in spreading coefficient:** Students often write $S = \gamma_{SL} - \gamma_S - \gamma_L$ (wrong sign). Correct form: $S = \gamma_S - \gamma_{SL} - \gamma_L$.
> 2. **Mixing $\gamma_{LV}$ and $\gamma_L$:** These are numerically identical for a pure liquid in equilibrium with its own vapor, but students must explicitly state the assumption.
> 3. **Using OWRK with only ONE probe liquid:** OWRK requires at least TWO probe liquids to solve the two unknowns ($\gamma_S^d$ and $\gamma_S^p$). Fowkes needs only one (if the liquid is purely dispersive).
> 4. **Forgetting radians vs degrees:** $\cos\theta$ requires $\theta$ in **radians** if computed on a calculator set to RAD mode. Always convert to degrees in written exam answers.
> 5. **Reporting $W_A$ as a force:** $W_A$ is energy per unit area ($\text{mJ/m}^2$), not a force in N. Examiners deduct 0.5–1 mark for unit errors.

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- ✅ **Work of adhesion $W_A$** is a *thermodynamic* energy per unit area, not a measurable force.
- ✅ **Young–Dupré** is the master equation: $W_A = \gamma_L(1 + \cos\theta)$.
- ✅ **Contact angle $\theta < 90°$** ⇒ partial wetting; **$\theta \rightarrow 0°$** ⇒ complete wetting; **$\theta > 90°$** ⇒ non-wetting.
- ✅ **Spreading condition:** $S \geq 0$ ⇒ complete spreading; $S < 0$ ⇒ partial wetting.
- ✅ **Fowkes** works for purely dispersive systems; **OWRK** adds polar component; **Wu** uses harmonic mean (better for polymer–polymer); **van Oss** includes acid–base.
- ✅ **OWRK linearised form** is the standard exam plot: slope → $\sqrt{\gamma_S^p}$, intercept → $\sqrt{\gamma_S^d}$.
- ✅ **At least two probe liquids** are required for OWRK; typical pair: diiodomethane + water.
- ✅ In **polymer nanocomposites**, nanofiller surface modification (silane coupling agents, plasma treatment) is used to **match** $\gamma_S$ and $\gamma_L$ and minimise $\gamma_{SL}$.
- ✅ **Thermodynamic adhesion $\neq$ practical adhesion** — real joints also involve mechanical interlocking, chemical bonding, and viscoelastic dissipation.
- ✅ **Cohesive failure** = $W_A > W_C$; **Adhesive failure** = $W_A < W_C$.
- ✅ Always report surface energies in $\text{mJ/m}^2$ and contact angles in **degrees**.
- ✅ For KTU board exams, **draw a labeled diagram** in any 7- or 14-mark question on this topic — it typically earns 1–2 free marks.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Testing of Paint Materials - Destructive and Non-Destructive Methods
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEPOT522 - POLYMER NANOCOMPOSITES
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Testing of Paint Materials - Destructive and Non-Destructive Methods

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Paint material testing** is the systematic evaluation of a coating's physical, chemical, mechanical, and optical properties using standardized procedures to verify conformity to specifications, predict in-service performance, and ensure quality control. As per **ISO/IEC 17025** and **ASTM** standards adopted in the KTU 2024 Polymer Nanocomposites syllabus, these tests are bifurcated into two principal categories:

- **Destructive Testing (DT):** Tests that permanently alter, damage, or consume the coated specimen. The sample is rendered unusable for further service after the test.
- **Non-Destructive Testing (NDT):** Tests that evaluate the coating without impairing its serviceability, allowing the part to remain in use or be re-tested.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight (PEPOT522 / Module 4):** The KTU 2024 scheme explicitly mandates the study of paint/coating characterization for polymer nanocomposite systems. Nanocomposite coatings (e.g., epoxy-CNT, polyurethane-clay, acrylic-TiO₂) require both DT and NDT to evaluate properties like dispersion quality, barrier performance, scratch resistance, and film integrity.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy — "The Car Body Test Drive"**
>
> Imagine you have just painted a new car body in a factory.
> - **Destructive Test** = You take a tiny hidden panel from the door and **scratch it with a needle until it peels off** to check how strongly the paint sticks. That panel is now ruined, but you have learned something critical about the whole batch.
> - **Non-Destructive Test** = You **shine a special light** on the bonnet to measure how shiny it is, or use a **magnetic probe** to see how thick the paint is, **without damaging the car at all**. The car can be sold afterwards.
>
> In short: **Destructive = sacrifice the sample for knowledge; Non-Destructive = gain knowledge without sacrifice.** Both are essential — destructive tests give deeper mechanical data, while NDT gives 100% inspection capability on every product.

## 1.3 Why This Topic Matters in Polymer Nanocomposites

| Property to Evaluate | Relevance to Nanocomposite Coatings | Preferred Test Type |
|---|---|---|
| Dispersion of nanofiller | Uniformity, agglomeration detection | NDT (ultrasonic, microscopy on coupons) |
| Scratch/Mar resistance | Nano-clay, CNT-reinforced coatings | DT (pencil, Taber) |
| Adhesion to substrate | Critical for barrier coatings | DT (cross-cut, pull-off) |
| Film thickness | Process control | NDT (eddy current, magnetic) |
| Barrier performance | Anti-corrosion nanocomposite films | DT (salt spray, EIS) |
| Optical clarity | Transparent nanocomposite topcoats | NDT (gloss, haze, colorimeter) |

> [!TIP]
> **Standard Bodies to Remember:** ASTM (American Society for Testing and Materials), ISO (International Organization for Standardization), BS (British Standards), IS (Indian Standards), DIN (German). For KTU exams, always cite the **ASTM standard number** (e.g., ASTM D3363 for pencil hardness).
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Classification of Paint Testing Methods

Paint testing methods can be broadly classified as shown below:

### A. Destructive Testing Methods (DT)

These methods either **permanently deform, fracture, or consume** the coating. They are typically performed on **representative coupons** (sacrificial test panels) rather than on the actual production part.

#### 2.1.1 Hardness Tests

**a) Pencil Hardness Test (ASTM D3363)**
- A set of calibrated wooden pencils of graded hardness (**6B to 6H**, with **4H, 3H, 2H, H, F, HB, B, 2B, 3B, 4B** being common) is pushed against the coating at a **45° angle** under a constant load of **7.5 N (1 kgf)** or **500 g**.
- The hardest pencil that does **not scratch** or **cut through to substrate** defines the coating hardness.

**b) Scratch Hardness Test (ASTM D5178)**
- A diamond-tipped stylus is drawn across the coating under progressively increasing load.
- The **critical load (L_c)** at which coating failure occurs is recorded.

**c) Indentation Hardness (Wolff-Wilborn / Buchholz)**
- **Buchholz indentation test (ISO 2815):** A hardened steel disc indenter is pressed onto the coating for 30 seconds under a 500 g load. The **indentation length** is measured optically; shorter length = harder coating.

#### 2.1.2 Adhesion Tests

**a) Cross-Cut / Tape Test (ASTM D3359)**
- A lattice pattern of cuts is made through the coating using a sharp blade at **1 mm or 2 mm spacing**.
- Pressure-sensitive tape (e.g., **3M Scotch 610**) is applied and rapidly pulled off.
- The **percentage of coating removed** is rated from **5B (0% removed)** to **0B (>65% removed)**.

**b) Pull-Off Adhesion Test (ASTM D4541)**
- A **dolly (loading fixture)** is glued perpendicular to the coating surface.
- A tensile testing machine applies increasing force until the coating detaches.
- The **failure stress** in **MPa or psi** is reported.

#### 2.1.3 Impact Resistance (ASTM D2794)
- A **falling weight (1 kg or 2 kg)** is dropped from a graduated height onto a coated panel (either **direct impact** or **indirect/reverse impact**).
- The **maximum height (in inches or cm)** at which the coating does not crack determines impact resistance.

#### 2.1.4 Flexibility / Mandrel Bend Test (ASTM D522, D1737)
- The coated panel is bent over a **cylindrical mandrel** of decreasing diameter (e.g., **1/8 inch, 1/4 inch, 1/2 inch, 1 inch**).
- The smallest mandrel diameter across which the coating does **not crack or delaminate** indicates flexibility.

#### 2.1.5 Abrasion Resistance (ASTM D4060 — Taber Abraser)
- A coated panel rotates on a turntable against **two abrasive CS-10 or CS-17 wheels** under a fixed load of **500 g or 1000 g**.
- After a defined number of **cycles (e.g., 1000 cycles)**, the **weight loss (mg)** is measured.

#### 2.1.6 Chemical Resistance
- **Spot Test / Immersion Test:** Coating is exposed to acids, alkalis, solvents, water for a fixed time.
- **Solvent Rub Test (ASTM D4752 — MEK Double Rubs):** A cloth soaked in **Methyl Ethyl Ketone (MEK)** is rubbed back-and-forth until the substrate is exposed. The number of **double rubs** indicates cure quality and chemical resistance.

#### 2.1.7 Salt Spray / Corrosion Test (ASTM B117)
- Coated panels are placed in a **salt spray chamber** with a **5% NaCl solution** at **35 °C**, pH 6.5–7.2.
- Panels are inspected at regular intervals for **scribe creep, blistering, red rust**.

#### 2.1.8 Cupping Test (ASTM D2840 / ISO 1520)
- A coated panel is placed over a **die and punched** progressively with a hemispherical indenter.
- The **depth (mm)** at which the coating cracks defines drawability.

---

### B. Non-Destructive Testing Methods (NDT)

#### 2.1.9 Film Thickness Measurement

**a) Wet Film Thickness (WFT)**
- **ASTM D1212:** Uses **Notch Gauges (Pfunt Type)** or **Interchemical Wet Film Comb**.
- The gauge is pressed into the wet film; the **notch that just touches the film** indicates WFT.

**b) Dry Film Thickness (DFT)**
- **Magnetic Induction (ISO 2178):** For coatings on **ferrous** substrates.
- **Eddy Current (ISO 2360):** For coatings on **non-ferrous** substrates (aluminium, copper).
- **Ultrasonic Thickness Gauge (ASTM E797):** Measures coating + substrate thickness by transit time of ultrasound pulse.
- **X-Ray Fluorescence (XRF, ASTM D6266):** Measures coating thickness and composition simultaneously.

#### 2.1.10 Gloss Measurement (ASTM D523, ISO 2813)
- A **glossmeter** projects a beam of light onto the coating at specified angles:
  - **60° (universal geometry)**
  - **20° (high gloss)**
  - **85° (matte/flat finishes)**
- The **specular reflectance** is measured in **Gloss Units (GU)**.

> **Classification (per ASTM D523):**
> - **High Gloss:** > 70 GU at 60°
> - **Semi-Gloss:** 30–70 GU
> - **Eggshell/Satin:** 10–30 GU
> - **Flat/Matte:** < 10 GU

#### 2.1.11 Color Measurement
- **Spectrophotometer / Colorimeter** measures **CIE L\*a\*b\*** coordinates.
- **ΔE (Delta E)** quantifies total color difference:

$$\Delta E^* = \sqrt{(\Delta L^*)^2 + (\Delta a^*)^2 + (\Delta b^*)^2}$$

- **ΔE < 1**: Not perceptible to human eye.
- **ΔE 1–2**: Perceptible on close inspection.
- **ΔE 2–3.5**: Perceptible at arm's length.
- **ΔE > 3.5**: Clearly noticeable color difference.

#### 2.1.12 Visual Inspection
- Trained inspectors examine coating for **orange peel, cratering, pinholes, runs, sags, blushing, haziness**.

#### 2.1.13 Adhesion (Indirect / Less Destructive)
- **Hatch test (knife test, ASTM D3359 Method A):** A single X-cut is made; tape is applied and removed.
- Although mildly destructive, it is often grouped with NDT in industrial practice for **rapid field checks**.

#### 2.1.14 Surface Roughness / Profile
- **Profilometer (Stylus or Optical):** Measures **Ra (arithmetic mean roughness)** in **µm**.

---

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Property | Test Method | Standard | Key Formula / Parameter | Unit |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Pencil Hardness | Pencil scratch | ASTM D3363 | Hardest pencil grade (6B–6H) | Pencil grade |
| 2 | Scratch Hardness | Stylus draw | ASTM D5178 | Critical Load L_c | grams / Newtons |
| 3 | Buchholz Indentation | Disc indenter | ISO 2815 | Indentation length ℓ | mm |
| 4 | Cross-Cut Adhesion | Tape pull | ASTM D3359 | Rating 5B → 0B | Class |
| 5 | Pull-Off Adhesion | Dolly pull | ASTM D4541 | σ = F / A | MPa or psi |
| 6 | Impact Resistance | Falling weight | ASTM D2794 | Maximum drop height h | cm or inch-lb |
| 7 | Flexibility | Mandrel bend | ASTM D522 | Minimum mandrel dia. d | mm or inch |
| 8 | Abrasion Resistance | Taber Abraser | ASTM D4060 | Weight loss Δm | mg / 1000 cycles |
| 9 | Salt Spray | NaCl fog | ASTM B117 | Time to failure t | hours |
| 10 | Cupping | Hemispherical punch | ISO 1520 | Cupping depth D | mm |
| 11 | MEK Solvent Rub | MEK cloth rub | ASTM D4752 | Number of double rubs N | rubs |
| 12 | Wet Film Thickness | Notch gauge | ASTM D1212 | WFT direct reading | mils or µm |
| 13 | Dry Film Thickness | Magnetic / Eddy / XRF | ISO 2178 / 2360 | DFT = (WFT × NVC%) | µm or mils |
| 14 | Gloss | Glossmeter | ASTM D523 | G = (I_r / I_s) × 100 | Gloss Units (GU) |
| 15 | Color Difference | Spectrophotometer | ASTM D2244 | ΔE* = √(ΔL*² + Δa*² + Δb*²) | dimensionless |
| 16 | Surface Roughness | Profilometer | ISO 4287 | Ra | µm |
| 17 | DFT from WFT | — | — | DFT = WFT × (%NVC/100) | µm |
| 18 | Volume Solids (PVC) | Theoretical | — | NVC = (V_solid / V_total) × 100 | % |
| 19 | Spreading Rate | Theoretical | — | SR = (NVC × 10) / DFT | m²/L |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical Conversion Note for KTU Exams:**
> - **1 mil = 25.4 µm = 0.001 inch**
> - **1 inch = 25.4 mm**
> - **1 psi = 6894.76 Pa ≈ 6.895 kPa**
> - **Surface area of dolly:** A = π d² / 4 (for circular dolly of diameter d)

### Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Automotive Industry:** Salt spray + cross-cut + gloss used as **acceptance criteria** for OEM primer, basecoat, and clearcoat.
- **Aerospace:** Pull-off adhesion (ASTM D4541) is mandatory for primer qualification in **MIL-PRF-23377** and **MIL-PRF-85582** specs.
- **Marine / Offshore:** Salt spray (ASTM B117) and cathodic disbondment tests are required.
- **Nanocomposite Coatings:** NDT methods (XRF, ultrasonic) help in **quality control of nano-dispersion uniformity** in transparent topcoats.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Dry Film Thickness (DFT) from Wet Film Thickness (WFT)

When a paint is applied, the **volatile portion evaporates** and the **non-volatile portion (NVC - Non-Volatile Content or % Solids by Volume)** remains as the dry film.

**Step 1:** Let the volume of paint applied per unit area be **V_app (µm·m²/m² = µm)** = WFT.

**Step 2:** By definition of **Non-Volatile Content (NVC)**:

$$
\text{NVC} \;(\%) = \frac{V_{\text{solid}}}{V_{\text{total}}} \times 100
$$

**Step 3:** The dry film is formed only from the solid portion:

$$
V_{\text{solid}} = V_{\text{total}} \times \frac{\text{NVC}}{100}
$$

**Step 4:** Per unit area, since volume and thickness are equivalent:

$$
\text{DFT} = \text{WFT} \times \frac{\text{NVC}}{100}
$$

**Step 5:** For example, if WFT = 100 µm and NVC = 50%:

$$
\text{DFT} = 100 \times \frac{50}{100} = 50 \; \mu m
$$

This shows that for a paint with 50% NVC, only **half the wet thickness remains as dry film**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Significance:** Knowing DFT is critical because corrosion protection, gloss, and mechanical properties all depend on the **final dry film thickness** — not the WFT.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Pull-Off Adhesion Stress Calculation

In the **ASTM D4541 pull-off test**, a **circular dolly** of diameter **d** is glued to the coating, and force **F** is applied perpendicular to the surface until failure.

**Step 1:** The area of the circular dolly:

$$
A = \frac{\pi d^2}{4}
$$

**Step 2:** The pull-off adhesion stress (tensile stress at failure):

$$
\sigma = \frac{F}{A} = \frac{F}{\frac{\pi d^2}{4}} = \frac{4F}{\pi d^2}
$$

**Step 3:** If d = 20 mm and F = 2000 N:

$$
A = \frac{\pi (0.020)^2}{4} = \frac{\pi \times 4 \times 10^{-4}}{4} = \pi \times 10^{-4} \; m^2 = 3.1416 \times 10^{-4} \; m^2
$$

$$
\sigma = \frac{2000}{3.1416 \times 10^{-4}} = 6.366 \times 10^{6} \; Pa = 6.37 \; MPa
$$

**Step 4:** Mode of failure is also reported:

- **Cohesive failure (in coating):** Adhesive strength > cohesive strength of paint.
- **Adhesive failure (at interface):** Adhesive strength < cohesive strength of paint.
- **Substrate failure:** Substrate itself fails (highest practical adhesion).

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Color Difference (ΔE*) using CIE L\*a\*b\*

**Step 1:** The CIE L\*a\*b\* color space defines:
- **L\*** = Lightness (0 = black, 100 = white)
- **a\*** = Green (–) ↔ Red (+)
- **b\*** = Blue (–) ↔ Yellow (+)

**Step 2:** The color difference between a **standard (s)** and a **sample (t)** is the **3D Euclidean distance** in this color space:

$$
\Delta E^* = \sqrt{(\Delta L^*)^2 + (\Delta a^*)^2 + (\Delta b^*)^2}
$$

where:

$$
\Delta L^* = L^*_t - L^*_s
$$
$$
\Delta a^* = a^*_t - a^*_s
$$
$$
\Delta b^* = b^*_t - b^*_s
$$

**Step 3:** Numerical example:
- Standard: L* = 50, a* = 25, b* = 30
- Sample: L* = 52, a* = 23, b* = 28

$$
\Delta L^* = 52 - 50 = 2
$$
$$
\Delta a^* = 23 - 25 = -2
$$
$$
\Delta b^* = 28 - 30 = -2
$$

$$
\Delta E^* = \sqrt{(2)^2 + (-2)^2 + (-2)^2} = \sqrt{4 + 4 + 4} = \sqrt{12} = 3.46
$$

**Step 4:** Interpretation: **ΔE* = 3.46** falls in the "**clearly noticeable at arm's length**" range (just below 3.5).

---

## 3.4 Derivation 4: Theoretical Spreading Rate

**Spreading rate (SR)** is the area a given volume of paint can cover at a specified DFT.

**Step 1:** Per litre of paint, the volume of solids available:

$$
V_{\text{solid}} = 1000 \;\text{mL} \times \frac{\text{NVC}}{100} = 10 \times \text{NVC} \;\; \text{mL}
$$

**Step 2:** If required DFT is **t µm**, then the area covered (in m²) is:

$$
\text{SR} = \frac{V_{\text{solid}}}{t \;(\mu m)} = \frac{10 \times \text{NVC}}{t} \;\; \frac{\text{m}^2}{\text{L}}
$$

> **Note:** 1 m² × 1 µm = 1 mL, hence the unit conversion.

**Step 3:** Example: NVC = 60%, DFT = 75 µm:

$$
\text{SR} = \frac{10 \times 60}{75} = \frac{600}{75} = 8 \; m^2/L
$$

---

## 3.5 Derivation 5: Taber Abrasion Wear Rate

In ASTM D4060, after **N cycles**, the weight loss is **Δm (mg)**.

**Step 1:** Wear rate per cycle:

$$
W = \frac{\Delta m}{N}
$$

**Step 2:** Wear rate per thousand cycles (commonly reported):

$$
W_{1000} = \frac{\Delta m}{N/1000} = \frac{1000 \times \Delta m}{N} \;\; \text{mg / 1000 cycles}
$$

**Step 3:** For nanocomposite coatings, the addition of **nano-Al₂O₃, SiO₂, or CNTs** typically reduces Taber wear rate by **30–70%** compared to unfilled polymer.

> [!TIP]
> **Examiner Tip:** In KTU ESE answers, always quote the **standard number** alongside the test name (e.g., "**Cross-cut adhesion as per ASTM D3359 Method B**"). This fetches full marks.

## 3.6 Analytical Decision Matrix: Choosing the Right Test

| Application Scenario | Primary Tests | Test Type |
|---|---|---|
| **Automotive clearcoat** | Gloss (ASTM D523), Pencil hardness (D3363), MEK rubs (D4752) | NDT + DT |
| **Aerospace primer** | Pull-off (D4541), Salt spray (B117), Fluid resistance | DT |
| **Marine anti-fouling** | Salt spray + Cathodic disbondment, Thickness (XRF) | DT + NDT |
| **Architectural emulsion** | Scrub resistance, Contrast ratio, Opacity | DT + NDT |
| **Nanocomposite barrier film** | Oxygen transmission, Salt spray, FTIR-ATR mapping | DT + NDT |
| **Pipeline coating (FBE)** | Pull-off (D4541), Cathodic disbondment, DFT (magnetic) | DT + NDT |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

## 4.2 Pull-Off Adhesion Test — Functional Block Flow

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Coated Specimen] --> B[Surface Preparation]
    B --> C[Glue Application]
    C --> D[Dolly Attachment]
    D --> E[Curing of Adhesive]
    E --> F[Cutting Around Dolly]
    F --> G[Mounting in Tensile Tester]
    G --> H[Apply Axial Load F]
    H --> I{Detachment?}
    I -- No --> H
    I -- Yes --> J[Record F_max]
    J --> K[Compute Stress sigma]
    K --> L[Compute sigma equals 4F over pi d squared]
    K --> M[Identify Failure Mode]
    M --> M1[Cohesive in Coating]
    M --> M2[Adhesive at Interface]
    M --> M3[Substrate Failure]
    K --> N[Report in MPa]
```

## 4.3 Pencil Hardness Test — Procedure Flow

```mermaid

    A[Start with Softest Pencil 6B] --> B[Sharpen and Sand Tip]

    E -- Yes --> F[Mark as Failure]

    E -- No --> H[Continue to Next Pencil]
    H --> I{Tested 6H?}
    I -- No --> G
    I -- Yes --> J[Report Hardest Pencil Grade]
```

## 4.4 Cross-Cut Tape Test — Rating Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Make Lattice Cuts 1mm or 2mm] --> B[Apply Pressure-Sensitive Tape]
    B --> C[Press and Remove Tape in 90 sec]
    C --> D[Inspect Pattern under Magnification]

    E -- 0 percent --> F[Rating 5B Excellent]
    E -- less than 5 percent --> G[Rating 4B]
    E -- 5 to 15 percent --> H[Rating 3B]

```
