# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: [KTU University Exam — May 2024]
**"Differentiate between destructive and non-destructive testing of paint coatings. Give two examples of each."**

**Model Answer:**

Destructive testing (DT) permanently damages the coating specimen, providing detailed mechanical/chemical data but rendering the sample unusable. Non-destructive testing (NDT) evaluates the coating without impairing its functionality, allowing 100% inspection.

| Aspect | Destructive (DT) | Non-Destructive (NDT) |
|---|---|---|
| Specimen | Sacrificial | Re-usable |
| Data depth | Mechanical, chemical | Thickness, optical, surface |
| Speed | Slow | Fast |
| Cost | High | Low |
| Examples | Pencil hardness, Cross-cut, Pull-off, Taber, Salt spray | Gloss, DFT (magnetic/eddy), Color (ΔE), Visual |

**Examples (Two each):**
- **DT:** (i) Pencil hardness test (ASTM D3363), (ii) Pull-off adhesion test (ASTM D4541).
- **NDT:** (i) Gloss measurement (ASTM D523), (ii) Dry film thickness by eddy current (ISO 2360).

---

### Question 2: [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]
**"Explain the principle of the cross-cut adhesion test (ASTM D3359). Mention the rating scale."**

**Model Answer:**

In the **cross-cut adhesion test (ASTM D3359, Method B)**, a **lattice pattern** of cuts is made through the coating to the substrate using a sharp blade with **1 mm spacing** (for thin films < 50 µm) or **2 mm spacing** (for thicker films). **Pressure-sensitive tape (e.g., 3M 610)** is firmly applied over the lattice, then rapidly pulled off at an angle close to 180°.

The amount of coating detached is visually rated as:

| Rating | % Area Removed | Adhesion Quality |
|---|---|---|
| **5B** | 0% | Excellent |
| **4B** | < 5% | Very good |
| **3B** | 5–15% | Good |
| **2B** | 15–35% | Fair |
| **1B** | 35–65% | Poor |
| **0B** | > 65% | Very poor |

A rating of **4B or 5B** is generally required for industrial protective coatings.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each)

> **ESE Module Internal Choice Format (KTU 2024)** — Answer **either** Question A **or** Question B in full.

---

### Question A: [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023] (14 Marks)

**(a)** Describe in detail the **pull-off adhesion test (ASTM D4541)** for paint coatings. Mention the formula, procedure, and failure modes. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Explain the principle and procedure of **salt spray test (ASTM B117)** for evaluating corrosion resistance of paints. Discuss its significance for nanocomposite coatings. **(7 marks)**

### Model Solution (a) — Pull-Off Adhesion Test

**Definition:** The pull-off adhesion test (ASTM D4541) measures the **tensile force required to detach a coating from its substrate**.

**Apparatus:** Tensile testing machine (hydraulic or mechanical), circular dollies (steel or aluminium, typically 10–25 mm diameter), strong adhesive glue (e.g., two-part epoxy), cutting tool.

**Procedure:**
1. **Surface preparation** of the test area (clean, dry, free from dust).
2. **Apply adhesive** to the dolly and press it **perpendicular** onto the coating.
3. **Cure the adhesive** as per manufacturer's specification (typically 24 h at room temperature).
4. **Cut through the coating** around the dolly perimeter to isolate the test area.
5. Mount the assembly in the tensile tester and apply **perpendicular tensile load** at a controlled rate (~1 MPa/s).
6. Record the **maximum force F_max (N)** at detachment.

**Formula:**

$$
\sigma = \frac{F_{\max}}{A} = \frac{4F_{\max}}{\pi d^2}
$$

**Example Calculation:** For d = 20 mm, F_max = 2500 N:

$$
A = \frac{\pi (0.02)^2}{4} = 3.14 \times 10^{-4} \; m^2
$$

$$
\sigma = \frac{2500}{3.14 \times 10^{-4}} = 7.96 \; MPa
$$

**Failure Modes:**
- **Cohesive failure (in coating):** Adhesion > cohesion; rating excellent.
- **Adhesive failure (at interface):** Coating peels off cleanly; weaker bond.
- **Glue failure:** Adhesive is too weak — invalid test, must be repeated.
- **Substrate failure:** Substrate fails before coating — best possible adhesion.

**Significance for Nanocomposites:** Nano-fillers like **CNTs, graphene, nano-clays** improve pull-off adhesion by **interfacial interlocking and stress distribution**.

### Model Solution (b) — Salt Spray Test (ASTM B117)

**Principle:** Coated panels are exposed to a **continuous atomized spray of 5% NaCl solution at 35 °C** to simulate long-term marine/coastal corrosion in an accelerated manner.

**Apparatus:** Salt spray chamber with atomizing nozzle, air saturator, heated reservoir, panel racks inclined at **15–30° from vertical**, 5% NaCl solution (pH 6.5–7.2).

**Procedure:**
1. Prepare **scribe line** through coating down to substrate using a sharp blade (for "scribe creep" evaluation).
2. Mount panels in chamber at standard angle.
3. Maintain **continuous spray for 500–2000 h** as per specification.
4. **Periodic inspections** at fixed intervals (e.g., every 168 h).
5. After exposure, panels are **rinsed gently, dried, and evaluated**.

**Evaluation Parameters:**
- **Scribe creep width (mm):** Lateral spread of corrosion from scribe line.
- **Blistering (ASTM D714):** Density (few, medium, dense) and size (No. 2–8).
- **Rust rating (ASTM D610):** Area % of rust (10 = 0%, 0 = >50%).
- **Hours to first failure.**

**Significance for Nanocomposite Coatings:**
- **Nano-clay/epoxy** coatings reduce water and O₂ permeability, extending salt spray life by **2–5×**.
- **Graphene-based coatings** provide **barrier + active passivation** effects.
- **CNT-reinforced** coatings show **self-healing** potential when microcapsules are added.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall Warning (Common 14-Mark Question Mistakes):**
> 1. **Forgetting the standard number** — always quote ASTM D4541, ASTM B117, ASTM D3359, etc.
> 2. **Skipping the failure mode discussion** in adhesion tests — it carries 2–3 marks.
> 3. **Not including the formula** for pull-off stress.
> 4. **Conflating "scribe creep" with "rust rating"** — they are **separate** parameters.
> 5. **Omitting units** (MPa, hours, mm) — units carry marks.

---

### Question B: [KTU University Exam — May 2024] (Alternative) (14 Marks)

**(a)** Discuss the principle, procedure, and significance of the **Taber abrasion test (ASTM D4060)** for coatings. How do nanocomposite coatings improve abrasion resistance? **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Explain the principle of **dry film thickness (DFT) measurement by magnetic induction (ISO 2178) and eddy current (ISO 2360)** methods. State the formula relating WFT to DFT. **(7 marks)**

### Model Solution (a) — Taber Abrasion Test

**Principle:** The Taber Abraser evaluates **abrasion resistance of a coating by subjecting it to rotating abrasive wheels** under a fixed load. The weight loss after a defined number of cycles indicates the coating's wear resistance.

**Apparatus:** Taber Abraser (turntable, two abrasive wheels — **CS-10, CS-17, or H-22**, suction arm, electronic balance with 0.1 mg precision).

**Procedure:**
1. **Weigh the coated panel** to ± 0.1 mg (initial mass m₁).
2. Mount the panel on the turntable.
3. Lower the **two abrasive wheels** onto the coating under a **500 g or 1000 g load**.
4. **Set number of cycles** (e.g., 500, 1000, 2000).
5. **Reweigh the panel** (m₂) after specified cycles. Use the **suction arm** to remove abraded debris during testing.
6. Calculate **weight loss Δm = m₁ – m₂** and express as **mg per 1000 cycles**.

**Significance:**
- **Lower Δm = better abrasion resistance.**
- Critical for **floor coatings, automotive clearcoats, aerospace topcoats, nanocomposite barrier films**.
- Standard **CS-10** = mild abrasion; **CS-17** = severe; **H-22** = very harsh.

**Nanocomposite Improvements:**
- **Nano-SiO₂, nano-Al₂O₃:** Improve scratch and Taber resistance by **50–300%**.
- **CNTs:** Act as **load-bearing bridges** across worn regions.
- **Nano-clays (organoclays):** Exfoliated platelets deflect cracks and reduce material removal.
- **Graphene oxide:** Provides **self-lubricating** tribofilm.

### Model Solution (b) — DFT Measurement: Magnetic & Eddy Current

**Magnetic Induction Method (ISO 2178):**
- **Applicable to:** Coatings on **ferromagnetic substrates** (steel, iron).
- **Principle:** A probe generates a **magnetic field**. Coating thickness is inversely proportional to the **magnetic flux measured** at the probe.
- **Range:** 0–500 mils (0–12.7 mm).
- **Accuracy:** ± 1–3%.

**Eddy Current Method (ISO 2360):**
- **Applicable to:** Coatings on **non-ferrous metal substrates** (Al, Cu, brass, Zn).
- **Principle:** A high-frequency **alternating current** in the probe creates **eddy currents in the substrate**. Coating thickness is inversely proportional to the **impedance** of the probe coil.
- **Also works on stainless steel** (non-magnetic austenitic grades).

**Comparison:**

| Feature | Magnetic Induction | Eddy Current |
|---|---|---|
| Substrate | Ferrous | Non-ferrous |
| Coating | Any non-magnetic | Any non-conductive |
| Probe | Permanent magnet or coil | High-frequency coil |
| Limitation | Affected by substrate roughness | Substrate conductivity variation |

**Relation between WFT and DFT:**

$$
\boxed{\text{DFT} = \text{WFT} \times \frac{\text{NVC}}{100}}
$$

where **NVC = Non-Volatile Content (% by volume)**.

**Worked Example:** A paint with NVC = 45% is applied at WFT = 200 µm.

$$
\text{DFT} = 200 \times \frac{45}{100} = 90 \; \mu m
$$

The reverse — to find the **spreading rate (m²/L)** for a target DFT:

$$
\text{SR} = \frac{10 \times \text{NVC}}{\text{DFT in } \mu m}
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall Warning:**
> 1. **Mixing up magnetic vs. eddy current** substrates is the most common mistake.
> 2. **NVC must be % by VOLUME**, not by weight — for solvent-borne paints, the two are very different.
> 3. **In Taber test,** always report **mg per 1000 cycles** with specified wheel (CS-10/CS-17) and load.
> 4. **DFT cannot be measured by NDT methods if the coating is ferromagnetic** and substrate is non-ferrous — verify substrate first.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Yield Rapid Revision Checklist (PEPOT522 / Module 4)**

- **DT vs NDT:** DT = specimen sacrificed; NDT = specimen preserved.
- **Hardness ladder (ascending):** 6B (softest) → 5B → 4B → 3B → 2B → B → HB → F → H → 2H → 3H → 4H → 5H → 6H (hardest).
- **Cross-cut rating:** 5B (best) → 0B (worst).
- **Pencil hardness load:** 7.5 N (1 kgf) at 45°.
- **Pull-off stress formula:** σ = 4F / (πd²) [in MPa if F in N, d in m].
- **WFT → DFT:** DFT = WFT × (NVC / 100).
- **Salt spray solution:** 5% NaCl at 35 °C, pH 6.5–7.2.
- **Gloss geometry:** 20° (high gloss), 60° (universal), 85° (matte).
- **Color difference (ΔE\*):** ΔE\* < 1 = invisible; ΔE\* > 3.5 = clear difference.
- **Magnetic method** → for ferrous substrate; **Eddy current** → for non-ferrous.
- **MEK double rubs:** higher = better cure and chemical resistance.
- **Taber load:** 500 g or 1000 g; **CS-10** mild, **CS-17** severe.
- **Impact test:** falling weight; **direct** (onto top) and **indirect** (onto back).
- **Cupping depth** is reported in **mm**.
- **1 mil = 25.4 µm** (frequently used in paint industry).
- **Nanocomposite advantage:** Reduced Taber wear (30–70% lower), extended salt spray life (2–5×), improved gloss retention, enhanced barrier.

> [!TIP]
> **Last-Minute Mnemonic for KTU Exam:** 
> **"D" in Destructive = "Destroy"** → Sample is lost. 
> **"N" in NDT = "No damage"** → Sample survives. 
> **"DT tests"** give **mechanical & chemical** data; **"NDT tests"** give **physical & optical** data.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Paint Film Defects
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEPOT522 - POLYMER NANOCOMPOSITES
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Paint Film Defects

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Section 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

A **paint film defect** is any visual, structural, mechanical, or chemical deviation from the intended smooth, continuous, adherent, and protective coating film that forms after the application and curing/drying of a liquid paint, varnish, lacquer, or polymeric coating system onto a substrate. In the KTU 2024 Scheme context of **Polymer Nanocomposites (PEPOT522)**, paint film defects are studied under **Surface Engineering and Coating Technology** as critical failure modes that compromise the aesthetic, protective, and functional properties of polymeric and nanocomposite coatings.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight (PEPOT522 – Module 4):** Paint film defects represent the visible/invisible failure modes of cured polymer and nanocomposite coatings. They arise from interactions between the **binder matrix, pigments, additives, solvents, substrate, and the curing environment**. Defects are classified into **application defects, drying/curing defects, film-property defects, and environmental/ageing defects**.

Mathematically, a defect can be expressed as a deviation function:

$$\Delta(x,y,z) = F_{\text{actual}}(x,y,z) - F_{\text{ideal}}(x,y,z) \neq 0$$

where $F_{\text{actual}}$ is the realized property profile (thickness, gloss, adhesion, color) and $F_{\text{ideal}}$ is the design specification.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy – The "Cake Baking" Intuition

Imagine baking a layered cake (the **paint film**):
- The recipe (formulation), the oven (curing environment), the pan (substrate), and the baker's technique (application) all interact.
- A cracked top is like **cracking**, an uneven surface is like **orange peel**, the cake sticking to the pan is like **loss of adhesion (peeling)**, sugar blooming on the surface is like **blushing/efflorescence**, and air bubbles trapped inside are like **pinholes/blisters**.

If any of the four pillars — *formulation, surface preparation, application, environment* — fails, defects emerge. This 4-pillar framework is the universally accepted KTU/EU-coating-industry classification.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Quick-Recall Definition Box:**
> - **Paint Film Defect** = *any* non-conformance in appearance, adhesion, continuity, or durability of the cured polymeric/nanocomposite film.
> - Defects are **symptoms**; the **root cause** usually lies in one of: **S-S-S-E** = **S**ubstrate, **S**urface prep, **S**praying/app, **E**nvironment.

## 1.3 Standard Classification (KTU-Standard)

The KTU 2024 syllabus recognizes four master classes:

| S.No | Class | Examples |
|:---:|:---|:---|
| 1 | **Surface / Optical Defects** | Orange peel, sagging, runs, pinholes, cratering, fish-eyes, floating, flooding |
| 2 | **Mechanical / Adhesion Defects** | Cracking, checking, alligatoring, flaking, peeling, blistering, delamination |
| 3 | **Chemical / Pigment-Related Defects** | Bleeding, chalking, yellowing, haziness, blooming, blushing |
| 4 | **Application & Process Defects** | Wrinkling, lifting, solvent popping, dry spray, mud-cracking |

Standard metrics used in industry (must remember):

- **Gloss** measured in **GU (Gloss Units)** at **60°** geometry per **ASTM D523**.
- **Adhesion** measured by **Cross-Cut Test (ASTM D3359)** rated **0B – 5B** (5B = best).
- **Film Thickness** in **micrometers (µm)** or **mils (1 mil = 25.4 µm)**.
- **Surface roughness (Ra)** in **µm** per **ISO 4287**.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# Section 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The "Why & How" – Defect Genesis Mechanism

Every paint film defect is the product of **three competing driving forces**:

1. **Surface Tension Forces (γ)** – the coating tries to minimize its surface free energy.
2. **Viscoelastic / Rheological Forces (η, G′)** – the binder resists flow.
3. **Solvent Diffusion & Evaporation Kinetics (D, J)** – mass transport alters local composition.

When any one of these dominates unexpectedly, the equilibrium film morphology is disturbed → **defect**.

> [!NOTE]
> **The Defect-Formation Master Equation (qualitative form):**
> $$\frac{d(\text{Defect Severity})}{dt} = f\left(\gamma_{LV},\,\gamma_{SV},\,\gamma_{SL},\,\eta(\dot\gamma),\,D_{solv},\,T,\,RH\right)$$

## 2.2 High-Yield Formula Sheet (KTU-Must-Memorize)

| # | Formula / Relation | Symbols | Engineering Use |
|:--:|:---|:---|:---|
| 1 | **Young's Equation:** $\gamma_{SV} = \gamma_{SL} + \gamma_{LV}\cos\theta$ | $SV$=solid–vapor, $SL$=solid–liquid, $LV$=liquid–vapor, $\theta$=contact angle | Predicts **wetting/cratering** |
| 2 | **Critical Surface Tension (wetting):** $\gamma_c \le \gamma_{LV}\cos\theta$ | $\gamma_c$ = substrate critical tension | Substrate must be ≥ 38 dynes/cm for paint wetting |
| 3 | **Capillary Number:** $Ca = \dfrac{\eta \cdot v}{\gamma}$ | $\eta$=viscosity, $v$=velocity, $\gamma$=surface tension | Predicts **sag/run** threshold |
| 4 | **Viscosity (Arrhenius):** $\eta(T) = \eta_0\,\exp\!\left(\dfrac{E_a}{RT}\right)$ | $E_a$=activation energy, $R$=8.314 J/mol·K | Temperature effect on **sag** |
| 5 | **Stokes-Einstein Diffusion:** $D = \dfrac{k_B T}{6\pi\eta r}$ | $k_B$=1.38×10⁻²³ J/K, $r$=particle radius | Solvent/pigment diffusion → **floating** |
| 6 | **Crawling Coefficient (Marcetic):** $K_c = \dfrac{\gamma_{substrate}-\gamma_{coat}}{\eta_{coat}\cdot t}$ | $t$=film age | Predicts **crawling/fish-eyes** |
| 7 | **Laplace Pressure (pore):** $\Delta P = \dfrac{2\gamma \cos\theta}{r}$ | $r$=pore radius | Drives **crater/pinhole** formation |
| 8 | **Cauchy Stress for Sag:** $\tau = \rho g h \sin\alpha$ | $\rho$=density, $g$=9.81 m/s², $h$=film thickness, $\alpha$=angle | Threshold for **sag** |
| 9 | **Blister Growth Rate (Osborne):** $\dfrac{dV}{dt} = \dfrac{A \cdot \Delta P}{\eta_w}$ | $A$=area, $\Delta P$=osmotic pressure, $\eta_w$=wall viscosity | Predicts **osmostic blistering** |
| 10 | **Adhesion Work:** $W_a = \gamma_{SV} + \gamma_{LV} - \gamma_{SL} = \gamma_{LV}(1+\cos\theta)$ | – | Foundation of **peeling/flake** analysis |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical thresholds to memorize:**
> - **Surface tension of substrate** must be **≥ 38 dynes/cm** for proper wetting (dyne pen test).
> - **Relative Humidity > 85 %** → almost always causes **blushing** in lacquers.
> - **Film thickness > 75 µm (wet)** in one coat → **sag/run** risk on vertical surfaces.
> - **Temperature < 10 °C** → **wrinkling** of alkyd/oxidative-cure systems.

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

In polymer nanocomposite coatings, defects are critical because they:

- Expose the substrate → **corrosion initiation sites** (cost India ~₹50,000 Cr/year in corrosion).
- Scatter light → **loss of gloss** (key for automotive OEM).
- Provide pathways for **moisture/ion ingress** → catastrophic for **anti-corrosion nanocomposite epoxy coatings** (a direct PEPOT522 application).
- Reduce **dielectric strength** in insulating nanocomposite coatings.
- Compromise **barrier performance** — the very property nano-clays (e.g., montmorillonite) are added to improve!

Hence, in nanocomposite formulation, **defect-free film formation** is the prerequisite for the nano-filler to deliver its theoretical performance.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# Section 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 – Critical Film Thickness for Sag (Newtonian Approximation)

**Aim:** Find the maximum wet film thickness $h_{c}$ below which a coating on a vertical wall will NOT sag.

**Step 1:** Force balance on an element of coating on a vertical substrate of unit width.

Gravitational (body) force pulling the film downward per unit width:

$$F_g = \rho \, g \, h \, L$$

where $\rho$ = coating density (kg/m³), $g$ = 9.81 m/s², $h$ = wet film thickness, $L$ = length of film segment.

**Step 2:** Viscous resistance offered by the film (Bingham/Newtonian model).

For a Newtonian fluid, shear stress at substrate:

$$\tau = \eta \, \frac{v}{h}$$

Integrating over length $L$, viscous drag force per unit width:

$$F_v = \eta \, \frac{v}{h} \, L$$

**Step 3:** Steady-state equilibrium ($F_g = F_v$).

$$\rho \, g \, h \, L = \eta \, \frac{v}{h} \, L$$

**Step 4:** Cancel $L$ and rearrange for the minimum velocity that initiates sag:

$$v_{sag} = \frac{\rho \, g \, h^{2}}{\eta}$$

**Step 5:** Define a **sag-onset threshold velocity** $v^*$ (acceptable, typically 0.1 mm/s). The critical thickness is then:

$$h_c = \sqrt{\frac{\eta \, v^{*}}{\rho \, g}}$$

**Step 6:** Plug typical values — epoxy coating, $\eta = 0.5$ Pa·s, $\rho = 1200$ kg/m³, $v^* = 10^{-4}$ m/s:

$$h_c = \sqrt{\frac{0.5 \times 10^{-4}}{1200 \times 9.81}} = \sqrt{4.25 \times 10^{-9}} \approx 6.5 \times 10^{-5}\,\text{m} = 65\,\mu m$$

**Conclusion:** Wet film thickness should be **kept below ~65 µm** to avoid sag on vertical walls.

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Take-away:** Doubling viscosity **doubles** $h_c$ (square-root scaling). Thixotropic additives (fumed silica, nano-clays) are added in nanocomposite coatings precisely to raise the *apparent* $\eta$ at rest without affecting spray-ability.

## 3.2 Derivation 2 – Crater / Fish-Eye Spreading Condition

**Aim:** When a low-surface-tension droplet (oil, silicone contamination) lands on a wet film, will it form a **crater**?

**Step 1:** Apply **Young's equation** to the contaminated film surface:

$$\cos\theta_c = \frac{\gamma_{SV} - \gamma_{SL,c}}{\gamma_{LV,c}}$$

where subscript $c$ refers to the contaminated interface.

**Step 2:** The driving force for crater formation is the **spreading coefficient** $S$:

$$S = \gamma_{LV,f}(\text{clean film}) - \gamma_{LV,c}(\text{contaminant}) - \gamma_{f,c}$$

**Step 3:** A **crater forms** if $S > 0$. Setting $S = 0$ defines the critical surface tension difference:

$$\Delta\gamma_{crit} = \gamma_{LV,c} + \gamma_{f,c} - \gamma_{LV,f}$$

**Step 4:** For typical polyurethane coating $\gamma_{LV,f} \approx 38$ mN/m, and silicone contaminant $\gamma_{LV,c} \approx 18$ mN/m, interfacial $\gamma_{f,c} \approx 4$ mN/m:

$$\Delta\gamma_{crit} = 18 + 4 - 38 = -16 \;\text{mN/m}$$

Since $S = +16 > 0$, the contaminant **spreads** → **crater forms**.

**Step 5:** Remedy — add a **wetting agent/surfactant** to the coating to lower $\gamma_{LV,f}$ below $\gamma_{LV,c}$, making $S < 0$.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Pearl of KTU wisdom:** The presence of a *single fingerprint* (oily residue, $\gamma \approx 25$ mN/m) on a substrate is enough to generate fish-eyes throughout the film. Hence the strict industrial rule: wear **nitrile gloves** during prep.

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix – Defect → Cause → Cure

| Defect | Primary Cause | Diagnostic Test | KTU-Standard Cure |
|:---|:---|:---|:---|
| **Orange Peel** | High viscosity / poor atomization / fast solvent flash | Surface profilometer (Ra) | Reduce viscosity, use **slower solvent**, raise atomization pressure |
| **Runs / Sags** | Over-application / low viscosity / gravity | Wet-film thickness gauge | Apply ≤ 65 µm wet; add thixotrope |
| **Crawling / Fish-eyes** | Substrate contamination (silicone, oil) | Dyne pen test, contact angle | Degrease with **IPA/acetone**, add **wetting agent** |
| **Cratering** | Surface tension gradient | Goniometer | Match surface tensions, use **surfactant** |
| **Pinholes** | Trapped solvent/air, porous substrate | Visual + SEM | Apply **mist coat → full coat**, seal porous substrate |
| **Blistering** | Osmotic pressure / moisture | ASTM D714 | Improve **barrier** (add nano-clay!), dry substrate |
| **Peeling / Flaking** | Poor adhesion / contamination | ASTM D3359 cross-cut | Surface prep to **SSPC-SP10**, primer |
| **Chalking** | UV-degradation of binder | Tape test, gloss drop | Use **UV-stabilizers / nano-TiO₂ / nano-ZnO** |
| **Cracking / Checking** | Internal stress > cohesive strength | Visual + bend test | Reduce DFT, use **flexible resin**, plasticizer |
| **Alligatoring** | Top coat shrinks faster than undercoat | Visual | Use **compatible systems**, longer inter-coat time |
| **Wrinkling** | Surface skins before bulk dries | Visual | Use **slower solvent**, thinner coat |
| **Bleeding** | Soluble pigment in topcoat | Solvent rub test (ASTM D868) | Use **sealer**, stain-blocking primer |
| **Floating / Flooding** | Pigment density & convection | Visual, color-streak | Add **anti-flooding agent**, slower evap |
| **Blushing** | High RH + lacquer solvent | Visual, Haze meter | Use **anti-blush retarder**, control RH < 70 % |
| **Mud Cracking** | Thick film of high-PVC coating | Visual | Limit DFT, lower PVC, use **extender** wisely |
| **Lifting** | Solvent in topcoat attacks undercoat | Cross-hatch | Use **compatible coats**, longer cure |

## 3.4 Derivation 3 – Blister Growth by Osmosis (Worked Analytical Form)

**Step 1:** Consider a coating film acting as a semi-permeable membrane. Water-soluble species (salts) under the film create osmotic pressure $\Pi$.

**Step 2:** Osmotic pressure (van't Hoff):

$$\Pi = i \, C \, R \, T$$

where $i$ = van't Hoff factor, $C$ = molar concentration, $R$ = 8.314 J/mol·K.

**Step 3:** Volume of blister grows according to Darcy-type flow through polymer:

$$\frac{dV}{dt} = \frac{A \, \Pi}{\eta_w \, h}$$

where $\eta_w$ = viscosity of polymer wall, $h$ = local film thickness.

**Step 4:** For a hemispherical blister of radius $R_b$:

$$V = \frac{2}{3}\pi R_b^{3}, \quad A = 2\pi R_b^{2}$$

**Step 5:** Differentiate and equate:

$$2\pi R_b^{2} \frac{dR_b}{dt} = \frac{2\pi R_b^{2} \Pi}{\eta_w h} \;\Rightarrow\; \frac{dR_b}{dt} = \frac{\Pi}{\eta_w h}$$

**Step 6:** Integrate from $t = 0$ to $t$:

$$R_b(t) = R_{b,0} + \frac{\Pi \, t}{\eta_w h}$$

**Conclusion:** Blister radius grows **linearly with time** in the early stage. **Lowering $\Pi$ (clean substrate, no salts), increasing $\eta_w$ (cross-linked nanocomposite), or increasing $h$** all reduce blister growth.

> [!NOTE]
> This is precisely why **polymer nanocomposites** with exfoliated nano-clay (e.g., 3 wt% Cloisite 30B in epoxy) **suppress blistering** — they increase $\eta_w$ by 2–3× and lengthen the tortuous diffusion path.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# Section 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model]**
**"Define 'Orange Peel' defect in paint films. List any four causes and two preventive measures."** *(3 marks)*

**Model Answer:**
Orange peel is a surface texture defect that resembles the dimpled skin of an orange; it results from **poor atomization**, **rapid solvent evaporation**, or **high viscosity** that prevents proper flow and levelling after spray application.

**Causes:** (i) High spray viscosity, (ii) Low atomization pressure, (iii) Fast solvent flash, (iv) Incorrect gun-to-substrate distance.

**Prevention:** (i) Reduce viscosity with thinner / slower solvent, (ii) Increase atomization air pressure, (iii) Use **slower-evaporating diluents** (e.g., butyl cellosolve).

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model]**
**"Differentiate between 'Checking' and 'Cracking' paint film defects."** *(3 marks)*

**Model Answer:**

| Feature | Checking | Cracking |
|:---|:---|:---|
| **Crack depth** | Surface only — does NOT penetrate to substrate | Penetrates **completely** to substrate |
| **Pattern** | Fine, often non-directional | Deep, often linear or branched |
| **Cause** | Aging + UV + slight surface shrinkage | Severe internal stress, over-thick film, brittleness |
| **Severity** | Cosmetic, mild | Severe — exposes substrate, accelerates corrosion |
| **Remedy** | Polish / recoat | Strip and recoat, use flexible resin |

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) — Module Internal Choice

### **Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024]**

**(a)** With neat schematics, classify **paint film defects** into four major groups and discuss the **mechanism of cratering (fish-eyes)** formation in polymeric coatings. Show the role of **surface tension** using Young's equation. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** A vertical mild-steel panel is spray-coated with an epoxy-polyamide system. Coating density $\rho = 1250$ kg/m³, viscosity $\eta = 0.45$ Pa·s. The maximum tolerable sag-onset velocity is $v^* = 0.1$ mm/s. Calculate the **critical wet-film thickness** to avoid sag. If the dry film requires 50 µm DFT at 50 % volume solids, is a single-coat application safe? *(7 marks)*

#### **Model Solution to (a):**

**Classification of paint film defects (4 groups):**

1. **Surface / Optical** – orange peel, runs, sags, pinholes, cratering, fish-eyes, floating, flooding.
2. **Mechanical / Adhesion** – cracking, checking, alligatoring, flaking, peeling, blistering, delamination.
3. **Chemical / Pigment-related** – bleeding, chalking, yellowing, blushing, haziness, blooming.
4. **Application / Process** – wrinkling, lifting, solvent popping, dry-spray, mud-cracking.

**Mechanism of cratering:**

- A low-$\gamma$ contaminant (silicone, oil) lands on the wet film.
- The **spreading coefficient** $S$ becomes positive.
- $S = \gamma_{LV,\,f} - \gamma_{LV,\,c} - \gamma_{fc}$
- Film recedes from the contaminated patch; a **crater** with raised rim forms.

**Young's equation:**

$$\gamma_{SV} = \gamma_{SL} + \gamma_{LV}\cos\theta$$

Spreading condition: $S > 0$ ⇒ $\gamma_{LV,\,c} + \gamma_{fc} < \gamma_{LV,\,f}$. Remedy: **lower $\gamma_{LV,\,f}$** with surfactants or **raise substrate surface energy** to ≥ 38 dynes/cm.

*(Schematic of crater already shown in SECTION 4.3.)*

#### **Model Solution to (b):**

**Step 1 — Apply sag-threshold formula derived in §3.1:**

$$h_c = \sqrt{\frac{\eta \, v^{*}}{\rho \, g}}$$

**Step 2 — Substitute numerical values (in SI units):**

$$\eta = 0.45\;\text{Pa·s}, \quad v^* = 1.0 \times 10^{-4}\;\text{m/s}, \quad \rho = 1250\;\text{kg/m}^{3}, \quad g = 9.81\;\text{m/s}^{2}$$

$$h_c = \sqrt{\frac{0.45 \times 1.0 \times 10^{-4}}{1250 \times 9.81}} = \sqrt{3.67 \times 10^{-8}} \approx 1.92 \times 10^{-4}\;\text{m} = 192\;\mu m$$

**Step 3 — Required wet-film thickness for 50 µm DFT at 50 % VS:**

$$h_{wet} = \frac{DFT}{VS} = \frac{50}{0.50} = 100\;\mu m$$

**Step 4 — Comparison:**

$$h_{wet} = 100\;\mu m \;<\; h_c = 192\;\mu m \;\Rightarrow\; \text{Safe for single coat.}$$

**Conclusion:** A single-coat application of 100 µm wet film is **safe** from sag; sag margin = 92 µm.

### **Question B (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024, Alternative]**

**(a)** Discuss the **mechanism of blistering** in polymeric coatings. Derive an expression for the **blister radius as a function of time** under osmotic driving force and explain how **polymer nanocomposites** mitigate blistering. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** Explain **chalking** and **bleeding** defects. With a labelled diagram, describe how a nanofiller such as **nano-TiO₂** mitigates chalking in a polymer nanocomposite coating. *(7 marks)*

#### **Model Solution to (a):**

**Mechanism:** Water-soluble salts/ions trapped beneath the film create osmotic pressure $\Pi = iCRT$. Water diffuses in, polymer wall swells, a **dome-shaped blister** grows.

**Derivation (already shown in §3.4):**

$$\frac{dR_b}{dt} = \frac{\Pi}{\eta_w h} \;\Rightarrow\; R_b(t) = R_{b,0} + \frac{\Pi \, t}{\eta_w h}$$

**Nanocomposite mitigation:** Exfoliated nano-clay (e.g., 3 wt% Cloisite 30B) in epoxy:

- Increases $\eta_w$ by 2–3× → lowers $dR_b/dt$.
- Lengthens tortuous path → lowers effective $D$ of water.
- Acts as physical barrier → reduces ion migration.

Hence nano-composite films show **5–10× longer blister-initiation time** in ASTM B117 salt-spray tests.

#### **Model Solution to (b):**

**Chalking:** Surface degradation of binder by **UV radiation**; polymer matrix is broken into loose powder leaving pigment particles loosely bound. Surface appears powdery, gloss drops sharply.

**Bleeding:** Migration of soluble color from underlying coat into topcoat, causing discoloration/staining.

**Mitigation by nano-TiO₂:**
- **Rutile nano-TiO₂** absorbs UV (< 380 nm) and dissipates as heat, protecting the binder.
- Typical loading: 2–5 wt% in topcoat.
- Mechanism: $\text{TiO}_2 + h\nu \rightarrow e^- + h^+$ → radicals recombine to release thermal energy, **shielding** the polymer.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Warning:**
> - Students **confuse checking with cracking** — remember: *checking is shallow, cracking penetrates to substrate*.
> - In sag derivations, students **forget to convert units** (cgs → SI) — losing 2–3 marks.
> - For blistering, students forget to mention **osmotic pressure** (van't Hoff) — partial marks lost.
> - For chalking, do not write "pigment fades" — write "**binder degrades, exposing loose pigment**".
> - For nanocomposite justification, **always quantify** (e.g., "2–3× viscosity increase", "5× longer salt-spray life").

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- [x] **Paint film defect** = deviation of cured coating from design (visual/mechanical/chemical).
- [x] **Four-pillar failure model** = Substrate + Surface Prep + Application + Environment.
- [x] **Master equation** involves surface tension $\gamma$, viscosity $\eta$, diffusion $D$, T, RH.
- [x] **Sag critical thickness:** $h_c = \sqrt{\eta v^{*}/\rho g}$; typical limit ≈ 60–100 µm wet.
- [x] **Crawling/Crater:** positive spreading coefficient $S > 0$ from low-$\gamma$ contamination.
- [x] **Substrate must be ≥ 38 dynes/cm** to wet (dyne-pen test).
- [x] **Blistering:** osmotic pressure $\Pi = iCRT$; growth $R_b \propto t$.
- [x] **Cracking vs Checking:** cracking = through to substrate; checking = surface only.
- [x] **Chalking:** UV degrades binder, not pigment; remedy = UV-stabilizers / nano-TiO₂ / nano-ZnO.
- [x] **Bleeding:** soluble pigment migration; remedy = stain-blocking primer.
- [x] **Wrinkling:** surface skin forms before bulk dries — slow solvent helps.
- [x] **Alligatoring:** topcoat shrinks faster than undercoat — match systems, extend inter-coat time.
- [x] **Blushing:** high RH + lacquer solvent — control RH < 70 %; use retarder.
- [x] **Mud-cracking:** high-PVC thick film — reduce DFT, lower PVC.
- [x] **Standards to remember:** ASTM D523 (gloss), D3359 (adhesion), D714 (blister), D868 (bleed), D523 (gloss), ISO 4287 (Ra).
- [x] **Nanocomposite link:** nano-clays → tortuous path, blister suppression; nano-TiO₂ → UV shield, chalking resistance; nano-ZnO → anti-fungal, anti-corrosion.
- [x] **Always quantify** the role of nanofiller — "2 wt% Cloisite 30B increases $T_g$ by 12 °C and lowers oxygen permeability by 40 %".

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final KTU Board Tip:** In 14-mark answers, *always* include: (i) **classification**, (ii) **mechanism with equation**, (iii) **derivation steps with units**, (iv) **at least one schematic (Mermaid/diagram)**, and (v) **nanocomposite-specific industrial application** — this is what differentiates a 12-mark answer from a 14-mark answer.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Surface Cleaning Treatments
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEPOT522 - POLYMER NANOCOMPOSITES
> - **Module:** Module Module 4
> - **Topic:** Surface Cleaning Treatments

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Module 4 — Surface Cleaning Treatments

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Surface Cleaning Treatments** refer to a set of physico-chemical processing operations applied to the outer boundary of a substrate (polymer matrix, nanoparticle, nanofibre, nanoclay, or carbon allotrope) with the sole purpose of **removing adsorbed contaminants, weakly bound moieties, and processing residues** so as to expose the true, reactive surface of the material prior to any subsequent functionalisation, coating, blending, or compounding step in nanocomposite fabrication.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:**
> "Surface cleaning treatments are pre-functionalisation procedures designed to eliminate organic, inorganic, and particulate contaminants from filler/matrix surfaces to enhance wetting, interfacial adhesion, and load-transfer efficiency in polymer nanocomposites."

In the strict KTU 2024 Scheme vocabulary, surface cleaning is a **preparatory surface-engineering step** — it is *not* a functionalisation step (which adds new chemical groups), nor is it a *compatibilisation* step (which introduces a coupling agent). It is the *enabling* stage that ensures the next step (e.g., silanisation, grafting, in-situ polymerisation) succeeds.

### 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy — "Painting a Greasy Wall"**
> Imagine you want to paint a kitchen wall that has years of cooking-oil splatter on it. If you simply pour paint over the greasy surface, it will bead up, peel off within days, and look terrible. What does a smart painter do? **First, he scrubs the wall with a degreaser, then sands it, then wipes it with a solvent-soaked cloth, and only then applies a primer.** That entire pre-paint ritual is *surface cleaning and preparation* — and that is exactly what we do to nanoparticles before embedding them in a polymer matrix.
>
> In nanocomposites, the "grease" is **adsorbed moisture, amorphous carbon, catalyst residues (Fe, Ni from CNT synthesis), surfactant molecules (from nanoclay organo-modification), or processing aids** that sit on the surface and prevent the polymer chains from making intimate molecular contact with the filler.

### 1.3 Why Surface Cleaning is Non-Negotiable in Nanocomposites

At the **nanoscale**, surface-area-to-volume ratios are enormous. A single gram of multi-walled carbon nanotubes (MWCNTs) can have a surface area exceeding **200 m²/g**. This means even **trace-level contamination** (parts per million) translates into massive interfacial defects that catastrophically degrade:

| Property | Effect of Uncleaned Surface |
|---|---|
| **Interfacial Adhesion** | Weak Van der Waals contact → premature debonding |
| **Tensile Strength** | Stress concentrations at contaminant pockets |
| **Electrical Conductivity** | Insulating residue breaks percolation pathways |
| **Thermal Conductivity** | Phonon scattering at impurity interfaces |
| **Optical Clarity** | Light scattering from agglomerates |
| **Barrier Properties** | Pinholes and microvoids in tortuous-path models |

### 1.4 Classification of Surface Contaminants

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 — Three-Contaminant Classification Scheme**

1. **Organic Contaminants** — Residual solvents, oligomers, processing oils, surfactants, polymeric binders.
2. **Inorganic Contaminants** — Catalyst metal nanoparticles (Fe, Co, Ni, Mo from CVD synthesis of CNTs/graphene), metal oxides, salts, acids/bases from upstream synthesis.
3. **Physical Contaminants** — Dust, airborne particulates, broken fragments of the material itself (debris), adsorbed water molecules (physisorbed moisture layer ~**0.3 nm** thick).

### 1.5 Key Metrics & Standards

- **Contact Angle (θ):** Measured in **degrees (°)**; lower θ ⇒ better wettability ⇒ cleaner, higher-energy surface.
- **Surface Energy (γ):** Measured in **mN/m** or **mJ/m²**; cleaned surfaces typically show γ > **40 mN/m**.
- **Surface Roughness (Ra):** Measured in **nm**; AFM or profilometer.
- **Cleanliness Verification:** XPS C/O ratio, TGA weight loss, sessile drop goniometry.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Theoretical Framework: The "Three-Stage Cleaning Logic"

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The KTU 2024 Scheme organises surface cleaning theory into **three sequential conceptual stages**, and exam questions almost always follow this logic. Memorise this flow:

**Stage 1 — Contaminant Identification (Analytical Stage)**
- Use **XPS (X-ray Photoelectron Spectroscopy)** to identify elemental composition of the surface (typically 1–10 nm depth).
- Use **TGA (Thermo-Gravimetric Analysis)** in air or N₂ to quantify the mass of volatile/decomposable contaminants.
- Use **FTIR (Fourier Transform Infrared Spectroscopy)** to identify organic functional groups of adsorbed species.
- Reference standard binding energies: C 1s at **284.6 eV**, O 1s at **532 eV**, Fe 2p at **711 eV**.

**Stage 2 — Treatment Selection (Process Stage)**
- Match the contaminant type to the appropriate cleaning modality:

| Contaminant | Recommended Treatment | Mechanism |
|---|---|---|
| Physisorbed water / organics | Thermal treatment (vacuum, 100–500 °C) | Desorption |
| Amorphous carbon | Thermal oxidation (air, 350–500 °C) | Combustion |
| Metal catalyst residues | Acid washing (HNO₃, HCl, H₂SO₄) | Acidolysis / dissolution |
| Surfactants (e.g., CTAB) | Solvent washing (ethanol, acetone, THF) | Solvation |
| Generic organic film | UV/Ozone treatment | Photocatalytic oxidation |
| Strongly bound organics | Plasma treatment (O₂, Ar, air) | Ion bombardment + radical reaction |
| Loose particulates | Ultrasonic bath in solvent | Cavitation |

**Stage 3 — Verification (Quality-Control Stage)**
- Re-run Stage 1 analytics.
- Confirm θ decreased (improved wetting).
- Confirm intended functionalisation step (next module) now succeeds.

### 2.2 Fundamental Theory: Wetting & Surface Energy

The thermodynamic basis of cleaning rests on **Young's Equation** (1805), which balances interfacial tensions at a three-phase contact line:

$$
\theta = \arccos\!\left(\frac{\gamma_{sv} - \gamma_{sl}}{\gamma_{lv}}\right)
$$

where
- $\gamma_{sv}$ = solid–vapour interfacial energy
- $\gamma_{sl}$ = solid–liquid interfacial energy
- $\gamma_{lv}$ = liquid–vapour interfacial energy (surface tension of the probe liquid)
- $\theta$ = equilibrium contact angle

The **Work of Adhesion** $W_a$ between the solid and the liquid is given by the **Young–Dupré Equation**:

$$
W_a = \gamma_{lv}\,(1 + \cos\theta)
$$

A cleaned surface has a **higher** $\gamma_{sv}$ and therefore a **lower** $\theta$ and **higher** $W_a$ — both of which directly improve polymer–filler wetting during melt blending or solution mixing.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> The following table contains every equation a KTU 2024 examiner is permitted to test on **Surface Cleaning Treatments**. Master these — they appear in both Part A derivations and Part B numericals.

| # | Equation / Relation | Variables & Units | Engineering Use |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $\theta = \arccos\!\bigl((\gamma_{sv} - \gamma_{sl}) / \gamma_{lv}\bigr)$ | $\theta$ in °; $\gamma$ in mN/m | Predict wettability from interfacial tensions |
| 2 | $W_a = \gamma_{lv}(1 + \cos\theta)$ | $W_a$ in mJ/m² | Quantify solid–liquid adhesion work |
| 3 | $\gamma_{sv}^{d} + \gamma_{sv}^{p} = \gamma_{sv}$ | Dispersive + polar components in mN/m | **Owens–Wendt decomposition** of surface energy |
| 4 | $\gamma_{lv}(1 + \cos\theta) = 2\bigl(\sqrt{\gamma_{sv}^{d}\gamma_{lv}^{d}} + \sqrt{\gamma_{sv}^{p}\gamma_{lv}^{p}}\bigr)$ | Owens–Wendt–Rabel–Kaelble (OWRK) equation | Compute $\gamma_{sv}^{d}$ and $\gamma_{sv}^{p}$ from two probe liquids |
| 5 | $\gamma_c = \gamma_{sv}^{d} + \gamma_{sv}^{p}$ | Total critical surface energy in mN/m | Indicator of "cleanliness level" |
| 6 | $S = \gamma_{sv} - \gamma_{sl} - \gamma_{lv}$ | $S$ in mN/m | **Spreading coefficient**; $S>0$ ⇒ spontaneous spreading |
| 7 | $R_{a} = \frac{1}{L}\int_{0}^{L}\vert z(x) \vert\,dx$ | Roughness in nm; $L$ scan length in nm | Quantify post-treatment surface morphology |
| 8 | $\eta = \frac{\gamma_{lv}\cos\theta}{\gamma_{c}}$ | Wetting efficiency (dimensionless) | Compare cleaning protocols |
| 9 | $\Delta m_{\text{contam}} = m_{\text{before}} - m_{\text{after}}$ (TGA) | Mass loss in % | Quantify removable contaminant fraction |
| 10 | $\text{C/O}_{\text{ratio}} = \frac{I_{C\,1s}}{I_{O\,1s}}$ (XPS) | Dimensionless | Detect residual carbon vs oxygen-bearing contaminants |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Convention — Use $\vert\,\cdot\,\vert$ in tables, not $\lvert\,\cdot\,\rvert$.** Always write absolute-value bars as $\vert$ inside markdown tables to avoid breaking the table pipe syntax.

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

Surface cleaning is the *unsung hero* of:

- **Aerospace nanocomposites** — carbon-fibre/epoxy prepregs require plasma cleaning to remove silicone mould-release residues, achieving a **40–60 %** increase in interlaminar shear strength (ILSS).
- **Biomedical implants** — TiO₂ nanoparticle surfaces cleaned by UV/ozone show dramatically improved hydroxyapatite nucleation for bone integration.
- **Flexible electronics** — graphene electrodes cleaned by mild O₂ plasma retain > **95 %** of original sheet resistance while gaining hydrophilicity for solution processing.
- **Food packaging** — nanoclay (montmorillonite)-filled PLA films require thorough washing to remove quaternary-ammonium surfactant residues that would otherwise migrate into food simulants.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Young's Equation from Mechanical Force Balance

> **Goal:** Starting from a force balance at a three-phase contact line, derive Young's equation for the equilibrium contact angle.

**Step 1 — Set up the geometry.**
Consider a sessile liquid drop resting on a flat, smooth, rigid, homogeneous solid surface in a vapour atmosphere. At the three-phase contact line (where solid, liquid, and vapour meet), three interfacial tensions act tangentially to their respective interfaces:

- $\gamma_{lv}$ pulls the liquid surface *tangentially* along the liquid–vapour interface (away from the drop).
- $\gamma_{sl}$ pulls along the solid–liquid interface.
- $\gamma_{sv}$ pulls along the solid–vapour interface.

**Step 2 — Resolve the horizontal force balance.**
Choose the solid surface as the reference horizontal axis. Project each interfacial tension onto this axis. The liquid–vapour tension pulls *into* the drop at angle $\theta$ above the horizontal, so its horizontal component is $\gamma_{lv}\cos\theta$. The solid–vapour and solid–liquid tensions are collinear (both along the surface) and oppose each other.

$$
\gamma_{sv} = \gamma_{sl} + \gamma_{lv}\cos\theta
$$

> **Conversion logic:** This is the **horizontal equilibrium condition** — the surface is rigid, so the line cannot move; the sum of horizontal pulls from one side must equal that from the other.

**Step 3 — Rearrange into the standard Young form.**

$$
\gamma_{sv} - \gamma_{sl} = \gamma_{lv}\cos\theta
$$

Dividing both sides by $\gamma_{lv}$:

$$
\cos\theta = \frac{\gamma_{sv} - \gamma_{sl}}{\gamma_{lv}}
$$

**Step 4 — Solve explicitly for $\theta$.**

$$
\theta = \arccos\!\left(\frac{\gamma_{sv} - \gamma_{sl}}{\gamma_{lv}}\right)
$$

$$
\boxed{\;\theta = \arccos\!\left(\frac{\gamma_{sv} - \gamma_{sl}}{\gamma_{lv}}\right)\;}
$$

> **Physical insight:** Cleaning a surface *increases* $\gamma_{sv}$ (more bare, high-energy atoms exposed) and *decreases* $\gamma_{sl}$ (polymer likes the surface more). Both effects **reduce** $\theta$ — the surface becomes more wettable.

---

### 3.2 Derivation: Work of Adhesion (Young–Dupré)

**Step 1 — Consider reversible separation.**
Imagine pulling the liquid off the solid in a quasi-static, reversible manner such that the solid–liquid interface is replaced by separate solid–vapour and liquid–vapour interfaces. The work required per unit area is the **Work of Adhesion** $W_a$.

**Step 2 — Apply the Dupré energy balance.**

$$
W_a = \gamma_{sv} + \gamma_{lv} - \gamma_{sl}
$$

> **Conversion logic:** Creating two new interfaces (sv and lv) costs $\gamma_{sv} + \gamma_{lv}$ in energy; destroying the sl interface recovers $\gamma_{sl}$. The net cost is the difference.

**Step 3 — Substitute Young's equation.**
Replace $\gamma_{sl}$ using $\gamma_{sl} = \gamma_{sv} - \gamma_{lv}\cos\theta$:

$$
W_a = \gamma_{sv} + \gamma_{lv} - \bigl(\gamma_{sv} - \gamma_{lv}\cos\theta\bigr)
$$

**Step 4 — Simplify.**

$$
W_a = \gamma_{sv} + \gamma_{lv} - \gamma_{sv} + \gamma_{lv}\cos\theta
$$

$$
W_a = \gamma_{lv} + \gamma_{lv}\cos\theta
$$

$$
\boxed{\;W_a = \gamma_{lv}\,(1 + \cos\theta)\;}
$$

> **Conversion logic:** This is the **Young–Dupré equation**. For a perfectly cleaned, completely wetting surface ($\theta = 0°$), $W_a = 2\gamma_{lv}$ — the maximum possible adhesion work.

---

### 3.3 Derivation: Owens–Wendt Surface-Energy Decomposition

> **Goal:** Show how a *cleaning effectiveness* test (two probe liquids of known $\gamma^{d}$ and $\gamma^{p}$) can be used to extract the dispersive and polar components of the solid's surface energy.

**Step 1 — Geometric-mean assumption (Fowkes, 1964).**
The solid–liquid interfacial energy splits into dispersive (London) and polar (Keeson, H-bond, dipole) contributions:

$$
\gamma_{sl} = \gamma_{sv} + \gamma_{lv} - 2\sqrt{\gamma_{sv}^{d}\gamma_{lv}^{d}} - 2\sqrt{\gamma_{sv}^{p}\gamma_{lv}^{p}}
$$

**Step 2 — Insert into the Young–Dupré relation.**

$$
\gamma_{lv}(1 + \cos\theta) = 2\sqrt{\gamma_{sv}^{d}\gamma_{lv}^{d}} + 2\sqrt{\gamma_{sv}^{p}\gamma_{lv}^{p}}
$$

**Step 3 — Rearrange to the standard OWRK form.**

$$
\gamma_{lv}(1 + \cos\theta) = 2\Bigl(\sqrt{\gamma_{sv}^{d}\gamma_{lv}^{d}} + \sqrt{\gamma_{sv}^{p}\gamma_{lv}^{p}}\Bigr)
$$

**Step 4 — Apply two probe liquids.**
Use **diiodomethane** (purely dispersive: $\gamma_{lv}^{p} = 0$, $\gamma_{lv}^{d} = 50.8$ mN/m) and **water** ($\gamma_{lv}^{d} = 21.8$ mN/m, $\gamma_{lv}^{p} = 51.0$ mN/m) to obtain a system of two linear equations in $\sqrt{\gamma_{sv}^{d}}$ and $\sqrt{\gamma_{sv}^{p}}$:

$$
\frac{\gamma_{lv,1}(1 + \cos\theta_{1})}{2\sqrt{\gamma_{lv,1}^{d}}} = \sqrt{\gamma_{sv}^{d}} + \sqrt{\gamma_{sv}^{p}}\,\sqrt{\frac{\gamma_{lv,1}^{p}}{\gamma_{lv,1}^{d}}}
$$

A plot of the LHS vs $\sqrt{\gamma_{lv}^{p}/\gamma_{lv}^{d}}$ yields a straight line; its slope gives $\sqrt{\gamma_{sv}^{p}}$ and its intercept gives $\sqrt{\gamma_{sv}^{d}}$.

$$
\boxed{\;\gamma_{sv} = \gamma_{sv}^{d} + \gamma_{sv}^{p} = \bigl(\text{intercept}\bigr)^{2} + \bigl(\text{slope}\bigr)^{2}\;}
$$

> **Physical insight for nanocomposites:** A *clean* nanofiller typically shows $\gamma_{sv}^{p} > 5$ mN/m; a *contaminated* one may show $\gamma_{sv}^{p} \approx 0$ mN/m (entirely dispersive — the surface is coated with non-polar organics).

---

### 3.4 Analytical Decision Matrix — Choosing a Cleaning Method

| Method | Removes Organic? | Removes Inorganic? | Damages Surface? | Cost | Time | Best for |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Solvent wash** | ✅ | ❌ | Low | Low | min–h | Surfactant residues on nanoclays |
| **Acid wash (HNO₃)** | ✅ | ✅ (metals) | Medium (creates –COOH) | Low | h | CNT, graphene, ceramic NPs |
| **Thermal (vacuum)** | ✅ (volatile) | ❌ | Low | Medium | h | Physisorbed water, solvents |
| **Thermal (air)** | ✅ | Partial | High (oxidation) | Low | h | Amorphous carbon (burn-off) |
| **Plasma (O₂)** | ✅ | ❌ | Medium | High | s–min | Polymer films, fibres |
| **Plasma (Ar)** | ✅ | ❌ | High (etching) | High | s–min | Activation of polymer films |
| **UV/Ozone** | ✅ | ❌ | Low | Low | min | Thin films, gentle cleaning |
| **Ultrasonic** | ✅ (loose) | ✅ (loose) | Low | Low | min | Dispersing + cleaning together |
| **Soxhlet extraction** | ✅ | ❌ | Low | Low | h–day | Bulk powder purification |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Process Flow Diagram — Generic Surface Cleaning Workflow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Raw Nanomaterial or Polymer Substrate] --> B[Stage 1: Contaminant Identification]
    B --> B1[XPS elemental scan]
    B --> B2[TGA mass-loss profile]
    B --> B3[FTIR functional group scan]
    B --> C[Stage 2: Treatment Selection Decision]
    C --> D1[Solvent Wash - Ethanol, Acetone, THF]
    C --> D2[Acid Wash - HNO3, HCl, Aqua Regia]
    C --> D3[Thermal Treatment - Vacuum or Air]
    C --> D4[Plasma Treatment - O2, Ar, Air]
    C --> D5[UV-Ozone Treatment]
    C --> D6[Ultrasonic Cavitation Bath]
    D1 --> E[Stage 3: Verification and Quality Control]
    D2 --> E
    D3 --> E
    D4 --> E
    D5 --> E
    D6 --> E
    E --> F{Acceptance Criteria Met?}
    F -- No --> C
    F -- Yes --> G[Material Released to Next Step: Functionalisation or Compounding]
    G --> H[Final Nanocomposite]
```

### 4.2 Block-Level Architecture — CNT Purification Process

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph RawCNT[As-Synthesized CNT Material]
        R1[Graphitic C Walls]
        R2[Amorphous Carbon]
        R3[Metal Catalyst Fe Co Ni]
        R4[Support Material Al2O3 SiO2]
    end
    RawCNT --> STAGE1[Stage 1: Air Oxidation at 500 C for 1 hr]
    STAGE1 --> P1[Removes amorphous carbon]
    STAGE1 --> STAGE2[Stage 2: HNO3 Reflux at 110 C for 6 hr]
    STAGE2 --> P2[Dissolves metal catalyst and support]
    STAGE2 --> STAGE3[Stage 3: Washing and Centrifugation]
    STAGE3 --> P3[Removes acid and dissolved ions]
    STAGE3 --> STAGE4[Stage 4: Vacuum Drying at 80 C for 12 hr]
    STAGE4 --> P4[Removes residual moisture and solvent]
    STAGE4 --> OUT[Purified CNT Ready for Functionalisation]
```

### 4.3 Surface Energy Transformation Schematic

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    S1[Contaminated Surface: gamma_sv LOW, theta HIGH] -- CLEANING --> S2[Cleaned Surface: gamma_sv HIGH, theta LOW]
    S2 -- "OWRK with two probe liquids" --> S3[Resolved gamma_sv_dispersive AND gamma_sv_polar]
    S3 -- "Improved wetting" --> S4[Strong Polymer Filler Interfacial Adhesion]
    S4 --> S5[Enhanced Mechanical Thermal Electrical Properties of Nanocomposite]
```

### 4.4 Comparison Diagram — Effect of Cleaning on Contact Angle

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    subgraph BEFORE[Before Cleaning]
        B1[Droplet beads up]
        B2[Contact angle greater than 90 deg]
        B3[Poor polymer infiltration]
    end
    subgraph AFTER[After Cleaning]
        A1[Droplet spreads out]
        A2[Contact angle less than 30 deg]
        A3[Excellent polymer infiltration]
    end
    BEFORE -- "Cleaning treatment applied" --> AFTER
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model]**
*Define surface cleaning treatment in the context of polymer nanocomposites. List any three common contaminants that necessitate such treatments.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

Surface cleaning treatment is a pre-functionalisation processing step aimed at removing organic, inorganic, and physical contaminants from the surface of nanofillers or polymer matrices to enhance interfacial adhesion and wettability in nanocomposites. **(1 mark)**

Three common contaminants are:
1. **Organic contaminants** — residual solvents, oligomers, processing oils, and surfactants from organo-modified nanoclays. **(1 mark)**
2. **Inorganic contaminants** — metal catalyst residues (Fe, Co, Ni) from CVD synthesis of carbon nanotubes or graphene. **(0.5 mark)**
3. **Physical contaminants** — adsorbed moisture, dust particles, and broken fragments of the material. **(0.5 mark)**

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024 Model]**
*State Young's equation. Explain how each term changes when a contaminated nanofiller surface is cleaned.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

Young's equation gives the equilibrium contact angle of a liquid drop on a solid surface:

$$
\cos\theta = \frac{\gamma_{sv} - \gamma_{sl}}{\gamma_{lv}}
$$

**(1 mark for the equation)**

Upon cleaning:
- $\gamma_{sv}$ **increases** because the bare, high-energy atoms of the filler are now exposed. **(1 mark)**
- $\gamma_{sl}$ **decreases** because the cleaned surface is more compatible with the polymer/liquid probe. **(0.5 mark)**
- The numerator $(\gamma_{sv} - \gamma_{sl})$ therefore **increases**, $\cos\theta$ **increases**, and $\theta$ **decreases** — indicating improved wettability. **(0.5 mark)**

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks with Internal Choice)

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**[KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model]**
*(a)* Derive Young's equation and the Young–Dupré equation for the work of adhesion starting from a force balance at the three-phase contact line. Discuss how cleaning a nanofiller surface quantitatively changes the work of adhesion. **(7 marks)**

*(b)* Explain the Owens–Wendt–Rabel–Kaelble (OWRK) method for resolving the dispersive and polar components of surface energy. Describe an experiment in which two probe liquids are used to characterise the effectiveness of a cleaning treatment on multi-walled carbon nanotubes (MWCNTs). **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution (a) — 7 marks:**

**Step 1: Force balance → Young's equation (3 marks)**

At a sessile drop's three-phase contact line, three interfacial tensions act horizontally. For mechanical equilibrium on a smooth, rigid, isotropic solid:

$$
\gamma_{sv} = \gamma_{sl} + \gamma_{lv}\cos\theta
$$

which rearranges to:

$$
\cos\theta = \frac{\gamma_{sv} - \gamma_{sl}}{\gamma_{lv}}
$$

and:

$$
\theta = \arccos\!\left(\frac{\gamma_{sv} - \gamma_{sl}}{\gamma_{lv}}\right)
$$

**Step 2: Energy balance → Young–Dupré equation (2 marks)**

Reversibly separating the solid–liquid interface into solid–vapour and liquid–vapour interfaces:

$$
W_a = \gamma_{sv} + \gamma_{lv} - \gamma_{sl}
$$

Substituting $\gamma_{sl} = \gamma_{sv} - \gamma_{lv}\cos\theta$:

$$
\boxed{\,W_a = \gamma_{lv}(1 + \cos\theta)\,}
$$

**Step 3: Effect of cleaning (2 marks)**

- Cleaning **increases** $\gamma_{sv}$ (exposed high-energy atoms).
- Cleaning **decreases** $\gamma_{sl}$ (better molecular contact with polymer).
- Therefore $\cos\theta$ **rises**, $\theta$ **falls**, and $W_a$ **rises**.
- Numerically, if cleaning reduces $\theta$ from **90° to 30°** with $\gamma_{lv} = 72$ mN/m (water), $W_a$ rises from **72 mJ/m² to 134.4 mJ/m²** — an **87 %** improvement in adhesion work.

**Model Solution (b) — 7 marks:**

**Step 1: OWRK theoretical basis (2 marks)**

The OWRK method decomposes the total surface energy of a solid into dispersive ($\gamma^{d}$, London) and polar ($\gamma^{p}$, Keeson + H-bond + dipole) components:

$$
\gamma_{sv} = \gamma_{sv}^{d} + \gamma_{sv}^{p}
$$

Combining Fowkes' geometric-mean hypothesis with Young–Dupré gives:

$$
\gamma_{lv}(1 + \cos\theta) = 2\sqrt{\gamma_{sv}^{d}\gamma_{lv}^{d}} + 2\sqrt{\gamma_{sv}^{p}\gamma_{lv}^{p}}
$$

**Step 2: Two-probe-liquid experiment (3 marks)**

| Probe liquid | $\gamma_{lv}^{d}$ (mN/m) | $\gamma_{lv}^{p}$ (mN/m) | $\gamma_{lv}$ (mN/m) |
|---|---|---|---|
| Diiodomethane | 50.8 | 0.0 | 50.8 |
| Water | 21.8 | 51.0 | 72.8 |

A sessile drop of each liquid is placed on (i) as-received MWCNTs (pelletised) and (ii) acid-washed MWCNTs. The contact angle $\theta$ is measured by a goniometer (typically 5 measurements averaged).

**Step 3: Calculation and interpretation (2 marks)**

Plotting $\gamma_{lv}(1+\cos\theta)/(2\sqrt{\gamma_{lv}^{d}})$ versus $\sqrt{\gamma_{lv}^{p}/\gamma_{lv}^{d}}$ for both liquids yields a straight line. The slope gives $\sqrt{\gamma_{sv}^{p}}$ and the intercept gives $\sqrt{\gamma_{sv}^{d}}$.

A typical result:

- **As-received MWCNTs:** $\gamma_{sv}^{d} \approx 28$ mN/m, $\gamma_{sv}^{p} \approx 1$ mN/m (predominantly dispersive → contaminated by non-polar species).
- **Acid-washed MWCNTs:** $\gamma_{sv}^{d} \approx 30$ mN/m, $\gamma_{sv}^{p} \approx 18$ mN/m (polar contribution rises dramatically → –COOH, –OH groups now exposed).

The rise in $\gamma_{sv}^{p}$ is direct evidence that the acid wash has both *cleaned* and *partially functionalised* the surface — a key outcome in the surface-preparation workflow.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model]**
*(a)* Classify the major surface-cleaning techniques used in polymer-nanocomposite fabrication. Compare acid washing, plasma treatment, and UV/ozone treatment with respect to (i) mechanism of contaminant removal, (ii) effect on surface chemistry, and (iii) suitability for thermally sensitive polymers. **(7 marks)**

*(b)* A poly(lactic acid) (PLA) film is contaminated with silicone mould-release agent. The film is to be used as a matrix for a montmorillonite nanocomposite coating. Design a complete surface-cleaning protocol (in three stages) including the analytical verification tests you would perform before and after each stage. Justify each choice. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution (a) — 7 marks:**

**Classification (2 marks):**

- **Physical methods:** Ultrasonic cleaning, thermal treatment, vacuum desorption.
- **Chemical methods:** Solvent washing, acid washing, Soxhlet extraction.
- **Physico-chemical / energetic methods:** Plasma treatment, UV/ozone, corona discharge.

**Comparison Table (5 marks):**

| Aspect | Acid Washing (HNO₃) | Plasma Treatment (O₂) | UV/Ozone Treatment |
|---|---|---|---|
| **(i) Mechanism** | Acidolysis of metal oxides; oxidation of organics; dissolution of inorganics | Ion bombardment + radical oxidation of adsorbed organics | Photocatalytic generation of atomic O and ozone |
| **(ii) Effect on surface chemistry** | Creates –COOH, –OH groups (true functionalisation) | Creates –OH, –C=O, –COOH; cross-linking at top monolayer | Mild oxidation; creates –OH, –C=O; lower density of new groups |
| **(iii) Thermal sensitivity** | Requires elevated T (~110 °C); *not* suitable for PLA, PET, PE | Room-temperature process; **suitable** for all polymers | Room-temperature process; **suitable** for all polymers |
| Damage to substrate | Can over-oxidise graphene/CNT walls | Can over-etch; controlled by time/power | Minimal; very gentle |
| Cost | Low | High (vacuum + RF source) | Low (mercury lamp) |

**Model Solution (b) — 7 marks:**

**Stage 1 — Pre-cleaning analysis (1.5 marks):**
- **XPS** to confirm presence of Si 2p (~102 eV) and C 1s (~284.6 eV) → confirms silicone contamination.
- **Contact angle** measurement: silicone-contaminated PLA typically shows $\theta_{\text{water}} > 95°$ (hydrophobic).
- **FTIR** to identify Si–O–Si stretch (~1000–1100 cm⁻¹) and Si–CH₃ bands (~1260 cm⁻¹).

**Stage 2 — Cleaning protocol (4 marks):**
1. **Solvent pre-wash** with isopropyl alcohol (IPA) under ultrasonication (40 kHz, 30 min) to remove loose silicone and oils. (1 mark)
2. **UV/ozone treatment** for 15 min using a low-pressure mercury lamp (185 nm + 254 nm). Justification: PLA has $T_g \approx 60 °C$, so any thermal treatment is risky. UV/ozone operates at room temperature, converts silicone to volatile SiO₂/CO₂/CO, and creates –OH groups on PLA for better MMT adhesion. (1.5 marks)
3. **Rinse with deionised water + blow-dry with N₂** to remove ozone residues and physisorbed by-products. (0.5 mark)
4. **Optional mild O₂ plasma** (10 W, 5 s) if contact angle still high. (1 mark — partial credit for the rationale.)

**Stage 3 — Post-cleaning verification (1.5 marks):**
- **XPS** should show Si 2p intensity reduced by > **90 %**.
- **Contact angle** should drop to $\theta_{\text{water}} \approx 65°$–70° (intrinsic PLA value).
- **AFM** roughness $R_a$ should remain < **5 nm** (no over-etching).

---

### 5.3 Examiner's Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls in "Surface Cleaning Treatments" Questions**
> 1. **Confusing "cleaning" with "functionalisation":** Many students write that acid washing "introduces –COOH groups" as if that is its primary purpose. In cleaning context, the acid's *removal* of metals and amorphous carbon is the goal; the –COOH formation is a *side effect*. Examiners will deduct up to **2 marks** for conflating the two.
> 2. **Forgetting units on $\gamma$:** Always write **mN/m** or **mJ/m²** after every surface-energy value. A bare number loses **0.5 mark**.
> 3. **Omitting Young's equation assumptions:** "Smooth, rigid, homogeneous, isotropic, non-deformable solid" — these five words are worth **1 mark** in any derivation.
> 4. **Mixing up $\gamma_{sv}^{d}$ and $\gamma_{sv}^{p}$:** Dispersive = London (non-polar); Polar = Keeson + H-bond + dipole. Students frequently swap them. Memorise: **D**ispersive → **D**ispersion forces (London).
> 5. **Skipping the blank line around display equations** in your answer booklet (for digital submissions) — causes LaTeX rendering failure and zero credit for the equation.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **High-Density Rapid-Revision Checklist — Surface Cleaning Treatments**

- **Definition:** Pre-functionalisation step to *remove* (not add) contaminants from nanofiller/matrix surfaces.
- **Three contaminant classes:** Organic, Inorganic, Physical — each targeted by a different cleaning modality.
- **Three-stage logic:** Identify → Treat → Verify (memorise this flow for every exam answer).
- **Young's equation:** $\theta = \arccos\!\bigl((\gamma_{sv}-\gamma_{sl})/\gamma_{lv}\bigr)$ — derived from horizontal force balance.
- **Young–Dupré equation:** $W_a = \gamma_{lv}(1+\cos\theta)$ — cleaning **increases** $W_a$ by lowering $\theta$.
- **OWRK method:** Uses **two probe liquids** (typically diiodomethane + water) to resolve $\gamma^{d}$ and $\gamma^{p}$.
- **Cleaning raises** $\gamma_{sv}$, **lowers** $\gamma_{sl}$, **lowers** $\theta$, **raises** $W_a$ — these four trends always move together.
- **Acid washing (HNO₃)** is the workhorse for CNTs/graphene — removes Fe/Co/Ni catalysts and amorphous carbon.
- **Plasma (O₂, Ar)** is preferred for thermally sensitive polymers (PLA, PET, PE).
- **UV/ozone** is the gentlest option — ideal for thin films and biomaterials.
- **Thermal vacuum desorption** removes only volatile/physisorbed species — does not remove strongly bound organics.
- **Ultrasonication** is almost always combined with a solvent — it provides cavitation, not chemistry.
- **Verification tools:** XPS (elemental), TGA (mass loss), FTIR (functional groups), goniometer (contact angle), AFM (roughness).
- **Acceptance criterion** for a "clean" nanofiller: $\gamma_{sv}^{p} > 5$ mN/m and $\theta_{\text{water}} < 70°$.
- **Unit vigilance:** Surface energy is in **mN/m** (or equivalently mJ/m²); contact angle in **degrees**; surface area in **m²/g** for nanofillers; roughness in **nm**.
- **Slogan to remember:** *"Clean first, functionalise second, compound third."* Reversing this order is the single most common reason for failed nanocomposite fabrication.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Paint Application Methods
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEPOT522 - POLYMER NANOCOMPOSITES
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Paint Application Methods

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Paint Application Methods** (also called *coating deposition techniques*) are the engineered processes by which a liquid, powder, or dispersed polymeric coating formulation is transferred and uniformly distributed over a substrate surface to form a continuous, adherent, and functionally protective film. In the context of **Polymer Nanocomposites**, these methods govern how nano-reinforced coatings (e.g., nano-TiO₂, nano-silica, graphene, nanocellulose reinforced polymer matrices) are laid down to achieve uniform dispersion, defect-free films, and target **Dry Film Thickness (DFT)** in the range of **20–200 µm**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (PEPOT522 Module 4):**
> Paint/coating application methods are physical processes that convert a bulk liquid or powder coating into a uniform thin film. The selection of method determines **transfer efficiency (TE)**, **film uniformity**, **drying/curing kinetics**, and ultimately the **performance of the nanocomposite coating** (barrier, anticorrosion, UV-shielding, or antifouling properties).

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are spreading butter on a slice of bread:
- A **knife (brushing)** gives control but is slow and uneven.
- A **spatula (rolling)** is faster and more uniform.
- A **spray bottle (spraying)** covers the whole bread instantly but wastes some butter in the air.

Each tool is a different *application method* — and the choice depends on how big the bread is, how much butter you have, and how perfect you want the spread. In industrial coating, the "bread" is the substrate, the "butter" is the polymer-nanocomposite paint, and the "knife/spray" is the application technique.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Performance Metrics** highlighted in KTU 2024 Scheme:
> - **Transfer Efficiency (TE)** → typically **30 % to 95 %** depending on method
> - **Viscosity** of paint → **50 cP to 5000 cP** (brushing needs high, spraying needs low)
> - **Dry Film Thickness (DFT)** → controlled between **15 µm and 200 µm**
> - **Volatile Organic Compounds (VOC)** → minimized in modern methods

### 1.3 Classification of Paint Application Methods

Paint application methods are broadly classified into **three families**:

| Family | State of Paint | Substrate | Typical Transfer Efficiency |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Manual / Contact Methods** | Liquid (high viscosity) | Any shape | **40 – 60 %** |
| **Spray Methods** | Atomized liquid or powder | Any shape | **30 – 95 %** |
| **Industrial / Conveyorized Methods** | Liquid bath or curtain | Flat/continuous | **90 – 99 %** |

The table below gives a top-level overview; the detailed sub-methods are analyzed in SECTION_2.

| S.No | Method | Best for | DFT Range (µm) |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Brushing | Small areas, touch-up | 25 – 75 |
| 2 | Roller coating | Walls, large flat panels | 30 – 100 |
| 3 | Conventional Air Spray | Complex shapes, prototypes | 15 – 50 |
| 4 | Airless Spray | Heavy industrial coatings | 50 – 200 |
| 5 | HVLP (High Volume Low Pressure) | Fine finishes, low overspray | 15 – 40 |
| 6 | Electrostatic Spray | Conductive substrates, conductive paints | 20 – 80 |
| 7 | Dip Coating | Small parts, primers | 10 – 30 |
| 8 | Flow Coating | Large flat surfaces | 20 – 50 |
| 9 | Curtain Coating | Flat panels, boards | 30 – 100 |
| 10 | Electrodeposition (E-coat) | Automotive primers | 15 – 35 |
| 11 | Electrostatic Powder Coating | Metals, appliances | 40 – 150 |
| 12 | Fluidized Bed Powder Coating | Thick functional coatings | 150 – 500 |

> [!NOTE]
> **Why this matters for Nanocomposites:** Nano-additives (e.g., nano-clay, CNTs) tend to **agglomerate** under high shear. The application method determines the **shear regime** experienced by the paint, directly impacting the final dispersion quality and hence the barrier, mechanical, and optical properties of the coating.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Manual / Contact Application Methods

#### 2.1.1 Brushing
- **Mechanism**: A brush (natural or synthetic bristle) holds paint by capillary action and transfers it to the substrate via direct contact.
- **Suitable Viscosity**: **300 – 1500 cP**.
- **Advantages**: No overspray, low capital cost, ideal for **field repair**.
- **Limitations**: Brush marks, low TE (**~40 %**), slow.

#### 2.1.2 Roller Coating
- **Mechanism**: A cylindrical sleeve (foam, mohair, nap) is loaded with paint and rolled across the substrate.
- **Suitable Viscosity**: **200 – 800 cP**.
- **Advantage**: Faster than brushing; gives a slightly stippled texture (orange peel).

### 2.2 Spray Application Methods

Spray methods atomize paint into fine droplets using energy from compressed air, hydraulic pressure, or electrostatic field.

#### 2.2.1 Conventional Air Spray
- Atomization by **compressed air (3–6 bar)**.
- TE only **30 – 50 %** due to overspray (bounce-off + fog).

#### 2.2.2 Airless Spray
- Paint is forced through a **small orifice (0.2–0.6 mm)** at high pressure (**100–250 bar**).
- TE: **50 – 70 %**; suitable for **high-viscosity (1000–5000 cP)** paints.

#### 2.2.3 HVLP (High Volume Low Pressure)
- Atomization at **0.7 bar (10 psi)** with high air volume.
- TE: **> 65 %**, sometimes **> 80 %**; meets **EPA Rule 1151** for VOC.

#### 2.2.4 Electrostatic Spray
- Paint droplets are charged (**30–100 kV**) and attracted to a grounded substrate.
- TE: **> 90 %** for conductive substrates and conductive paints (e.g., waterborne, solventborne with polar additives).

### 2.3 Industrial / Conveyorized Application Methods

#### 2.3.1 Dip Coating
- Substrate is **immersed** in a paint tank, then withdrawn at a controlled rate.
- Wet film thickness governed by **Landau–Levich equation** (see SECTION_3).

#### 2.3.2 Flow Coating
- Paint is **poured** over the part and the excess drains back into the tank.

#### 2.3.3 Curtain Coating
- A **continuous liquid sheet** (curtain) falls over a conveyorized flat panel.
- Excellent for **MDF, particleboard, paper, and pre-coated metal**.

#### 2.3.4 Electrodeposition (E-Coat)
- The substrate is dipped in a waterborne paint bath and acts as one **electrode**; current (**100–400 V DC**) drives charged paint particles to deposit.
- TE: **> 99 %**; used for **automotive primers** and **white goods**.

#### 2.3.5 Electrostatic Powder Coating
- Dry powder is **fluidized**, charged at **–30 to –100 kV**, and sprayed onto a grounded metal part.
- The part is then **cured at 160–200 °C** for 10–20 min.

#### 2.3.6 Fluidized Bed Powder Coating
- Pre-heated part is dipped into a **fluidized bed of powder** (air-fluidized).
- Powder melts and fuses to form a thick film (**150–500 µm**).

### 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Master these equations — they appear frequently in KTU ESE numerical and Part-A questions.**

| # | Quantity | Formula | Units | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Volume Solids (VS)** | $VS = \dfrac{V_d}{V_w} \times 100$ | % | $V_d$ = dry film volume, $V_w$ = wet film volume |
| 2 | **Dry Film Thickness (DFT)** | $DFT = WFT \times VS$ | µm | $WFT$ = wet film thickness |
| 3 | **Theoretical Spreading Rate (TSR)** | $TSR = \dfrac{VS \times 10}{DFT}$ | m²/L | Inverse of DFT per litre |
| 4 | **Practical Spreading Rate (PSR)** | $PSR = \dfrac{TSR}{n}$ | m²/L | $n$ = number of coats |
| 5 | **Actual Spreading Rate (ASR)** | $ASR = PSR \times TE$ | m²/L | $TE$ = transfer efficiency (decimal) |
| 6 | **Paint Quantity (Q)** | $Q = \dfrac{A \times DFT}{VS \times TE \times 10}$ | Litres | $A$ = area in m² |
| 7 | **VOC Content** | $VOC = \dfrac{\rho \times (1 - VS/100)}{VS}$ | g/L | $\rho$ = density of paint (g/cm³) |
| 8 | **Withdrawal Speed in Dip Coating** | $v = \dfrac{\rho g \eta L}{2W}$ | m/s | $\eta$ = viscosity, $L$ = wet film length, $W$ = width |
| 9 | **Capillary Number (Dip Coating)** | $Ca = \dfrac{\eta v}{\sigma}$ | dimensionless | $\sigma$ = surface tension |
| 10 | **Film Thickness in Dip Coating (Landau–Levich)** | $h = 0.94 \, \ell_c \, Ca^{2/3}$ | m | $\ell_c = \sqrt{\sigma / (\rho g)}$ capillary length |

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical Substitution Rule (KTU Valuation Key):**
> - WFT, DFT in **µm**.
> - Spreading rate in **m²/L** (TSR formula already includes the 10× conversion).
> - Density $\rho$ in **g/cm³** when computing VOC in **g/L**.
> - Viscosity $\eta$ in **Pa·s** when using **SI** units in the Landau–Levich equation.

### 2.5 Real-World Utility in Polymer Nanocomposites

| Engineering Field | Method Chosen | Reason |
|---|---|---|
| **Automotive clear coats with nano-ceramic** | Electrostatic Spray | High TE, fine atomization preserves nano-dispersion |
| **Marine anti-fouling (nano-Cu₂O)** | Airless Spray | Builds thick 200 µm film in one pass |
| **Pipeline inner coating (epoxy + nano-clay)** | Fluidized Bed | Excellent barrier, thick film |
| **Aerospace primer (graphene/epoxy)** | HVLP | Low overspray, fine finish |
| **Furniture lacquer (nano-silica/PU)** | Curtain Coating | Continuous, fast, uniform on flat panels |
| **Car body primer** | E-Coat | 99 % TE, uniform film on complex shapes |
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->
