## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of the Spreading Rate and Paint Quantity

Starting from the definitions:

**Step 1 — Relate WFT to DFT.**
By definition of volume solids (VS, expressed as a decimal):

$$VS = \frac{V_d}{V_w} = \frac{A \cdot DFT}{A \cdot WFT} = \frac{DFT}{WFT}$$

Rearranging to solve for DFT:

$$DFT = VS \times WFT \quad \text{...(1)}$$

**Step 2 — Compute the volume of paint required for an area $A$.**
Volume of wet paint:

$$V_w = A \times WFT = \frac{A \times DFT}{VS} \quad \text{...(2)}$$

**Step 3 — Convert volume to spreading rate (area per litre).**
One litre = **10⁶ µm·m² / 1 m² = 1000 cm³**, and $1 \,\mu m = 10^{-6} \,m$. The spreading rate in **m²/L** for a single coat of thickness $DFT$ (in µm) is:

$$TSR = \frac{10^{6} \,\mu m \cdot m^{2} / L}{DFT \,\mu m} \times VS = \frac{10 \times VS}{DFT} \quad \text{...(3)}$$

**Step 4 — Account for number of coats and transfer efficiency.**

$$ASR = \frac{10 \times VS \times TE}{n \times DFT} \quad \text{...(4)}$$

**Step 5 — Compute the paint quantity for a known area $A$.**

$$Q = \frac{A \times n \times DFT}{10 \times VS \times TE} \quad \text{...(5)}$$

---

### 3.2 Worked Example 1 — KTU Typical 7-Mark Numerical

> **Question (KTU 2019 ESE Style):**
> A polymer nanocomposite paint contains **50 % volume solids** (VS = 0.50). The required **dry film thickness is 75 µm per coat** and **2 coats** are to be applied on a wall of area **100 m²**. The transfer efficiency of the chosen **airless spray** is **65 %**. Calculate:
> (a) The theoretical spreading rate (TSR).
> (b) The actual spreading rate (ASR).
> (c) The total volume of paint required in litres.

**Given:**

$$VS = 0.50, \quad DFT = 75 \,\mu m, \quad n = 2, \quad A = 100 \,m^{2}, \quad TE = 0.65$$

**Step (a) — TSR:**

$$TSR = \frac{10 \times VS}{DFT} = \frac{10 \times 0.50}{75} = \frac{5.00}{75} = 0.0667 \, m^{2}/L$$

**Step (b) — ASR (with $n$ coats and TE):**

$$ASR = \frac{10 \times VS \times TE}{n \times DFT} = \frac{10 \times 0.50 \times 0.65}{2 \times 75} = \frac{3.25}{150} = 0.02167 \, m^{2}/L$$

**Step (c) — Total paint volume:**

$$Q = \frac{A}{ASR} = \frac{100}{0.02167} \approx 4615 \, L \quad \text{(unreasonable; see note)}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Correction — Unit-Mistake Trap (Common KTU Pitfall):**
> The ASR we obtained is per litre. To avoid absurd values, use the *consolidated* formula directly:

$$Q = \frac{A \times n \times DFT}{10 \times VS \times TE} = \frac{100 \times 2 \times 75}{10 \times 0.50 \times 0.65}$$

$$Q = \frac{15000}{3.25} = 4615.4 \, L$$

This large value is **physically correct** for a 100 m² wall at 150 µm total DFT — students often miss the factor of $n$ (number of coats) and the DFT unit (µm) and end up reporting **46 L** (a 100× error) which loses full marks.

**Realistic verification:**
- Total DFT = 2 × 75 = **150 µm** = 1.5 × 10⁻⁴ m
- Volume of dry film = 100 × 1.5 × 10⁻⁴ = 1.5 × 10⁻² m³ = **15 L**
- Volume of wet paint = 15 / 0.50 = **30 L**
- With TE = 0.65 → 30 / 0.65 ≈ **46.2 L** ✓ (this is the *actual* paint you must buy)

> [!WARNING]
> The 4615 L is wrong because the TSR formula is per coat. KTU 2019 actually expected students to compute **per coat** and then scale. Always state which coat count you are using.

---

### 3.3 Worked Example 2 — KTU 7-Mark Numerical on VOC

> **Question:**
> A nanocomposite paint has a density of **1.25 g/cm³** and volume solids of **45 %**. Compute the **VOC in g/L** of the *applied* coating.

**Step 1 — Mass of 1 L of paint:**

$$m_{paint} = \rho \times 1000 = 1.25 \times 1000 = 1250 \, g/L$$

**Step 2 — Mass of solids in 1 L:**

$$m_{solids} = m_{paint} \times VS = 1250 \times 0.45 = 562.5 \, g/L$$

**Step 3 — Mass of volatiles (VOC) in 1 L:**

$$m_{VOC} = 1250 - 562.5 = 687.5 \, g/L$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Alternative (compact) form:** $VOC = \rho \times 1000 \times (1 - VS)$
> $VOC = 1.25 \times 1000 \times 0.55 = 687.5$ g/L ✓

This is a regulatory metric — KTU 2024 emphasizes this in the context of **green nanocomposite coatings**.

---

### 3.4 Derivation of Film Thickness in Dip Coating (Landau–Levich)

For a Newtonian fluid of viscosity $\eta$, density $\rho$, and surface tension $\sigma$ being withdrawn at velocity $v$ from a bath, the steady-state film thickness (on a flat plate) is governed by a balance between **viscous drag**, **gravity**, and **capillary forces**.

**Step 1 — Define the capillary length:**

$$\ell_c = \sqrt{\frac{\sigma}{\rho g}} \quad \text{...(L1)}$$

**Step 2 — Define the Capillary number:**

$$Ca = \frac{\eta v}{\sigma} \quad \text{...(L2)}$$

**Step 3 — Apply the Landau–Levich result** (derived from lubrication theory with matching to the static meniscus):

$$h_0 = 0.94 \, \ell_c \, Ca^{2/3} \quad \text{...(L3)}$$

Substituting (L1) and (L2):

$$h_0 = 0.94 \sqrt{\frac{\sigma}{\rho g}} \left( \frac{\eta v}{\sigma} \right)^{2/3}$$

$$h_0 = 0.94 \, \eta^{2/3} \, v^{2/3} \, \rho^{-1/3} \, g^{-1/2} \, \sigma^{-1/6} \quad \text{...(L4)}$$

**Interpretation (KTU Viva style):**
- Higher withdrawal speed $v$ → thicker film (exponent 2/3).
- Higher viscosity $\eta$ → thicker film.
- Higher surface tension $\sigma$ → **thinner** film (the meniscus pulls paint back).
- Higher density $\rho$ → thinner film (gravity drains the film).

---

### 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Application Methods vs. Nanocomposite Property

| Method | Shear Regime | Risk to Nano-Dispersion | Best Nano-Filler Type |
|---|---|---|---|
| Brushing | High, oscillatory | High (re-agglomeration) | Micro-scale pigments |
| Roller | Moderate | Moderate | Nano-silica, nano-TiO₂ |
| Air Spray | Very high | Low–moderate (one-pass) | CNTs, nano-clay |
| Airless Spray | High | Low | Nano-Al₂O₃, graphene |
| HVLP | Low–moderate | Low | Nano-ceramics, nano-ZnO |
| Electrostatic Spray | Moderate | Low | Conductive nano-fillers (Ag, CNTs) |
| Dip Coating | Very low | **Lowest** (ideal) | Graphene oxide, MXenes |
| Curtain Coating | Low | Low | Pre-dispersed nano-slurries |
| E-Coat | Low | Low | Water-dispersible nano-latex |
| Powder Coating | High (in melt) | Moderate | Nano-clay, nano-silica |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 High-Yield Insight:** For shear-sensitive nano-fillers (e.g., **MXene**, **graphene oxide**, **nano-cellulose**), use **dip coating** or **curtain coating** to preserve the 2D/1D morphology that gives the nanocomposite its barrier property.

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
> *Define transfer efficiency in paint application and list the typical range for (a) brushing, (b) airless spray, and (c) electrostatic spray.*

**Model Answer:**

Transfer efficiency (TE) is the ratio of the mass of paint **deposited on the substrate** to the mass of paint **used (dispensed) by the application system**, expressed as a percentage:

$$TE = \frac{m_{deposited}}{m_{used}} \times 100 \,\%$$

Typical ranges:
- (a) **Brushing**: **40 – 60 %**
- (b) **Airless spray**: **50 – 70 %**
- (c) **Electrostatic spray**: **80 – 95 %**

> **KTU Mark Split:** Definition (1.5 marks) + 3 ranges × 0.5 mark = 1.5 marks.

---

> **Q2. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
> *What is the difference between conventional air spray and HVLP spray? Mention the role of HVLP in VOC reduction.*

**Model Answer:**

| Parameter | Conventional Air Spray | HVLP Spray |
|---|---|---|
| Atomization pressure | 3 – 6 bar | ≤ **0.7 bar (10 psi)** |
| Air consumption | Low | High (4–8× more) |
| Droplet velocity | High (overspray) | Low (gentle deposition) |
| Transfer efficiency | 30 – 50 % | **65 – 85 %** |
| Finish quality | Good | Excellent |

**Role in VOC reduction:** Because HVLP deposits more paint and produces less overspray, the **total paint consumption per unit area drops**, which proportionally reduces the **volatile organic compound (VOC) emissions**. It complies with **US EPA NESHAP Rule 1151** and EU Directive **2004/42/EC**.

> **KTU Mark Split:** Tabular comparison (2 marks) + VOC role (1 mark).

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Internal Choice)

> **Q3 (A). [KTU University Exam – Model Paper, Module 4]**
> **(a)** Classify the different paint application methods. Describe the working principle, advantages, and limitations of **electrodeposition (e-coat)** process. **(7 marks)**
> **(b)** A waterborne polymer nanocomposite paint has **volume solids = 40 %**, **density = 1.20 g/cm³**, and is applied by **e-coat** with a transfer efficiency of **95 %**. Required **DFT = 25 µm per coat**, **2 coats**, area = **250 m²**. Calculate (i) the paint quantity required in litres, and (ii) the **VOC content in g/L** of the *applied coating*. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution — Part (a):**

**Classification** (3 marks):
- **Manual / Contact:** Brushing, rolling, padding.
- **Spray:** Conventional air, airless, HVLP, electrostatic, plasma.
- **Industrial / Bath:** Dip, flow, curtain, roller (conveyorized).
- **Powder:** Electrostatic powder, fluidized bed.
- **Electrochemical:** Electrodeposition (e-coat).

**E-Coat Working Principle** (2 marks):
The part is dipped in a waterborne paint bath containing charged polymer particles. The part acts as the **anode (anaphoretic)** or **cathode (cataphoretic)**. A DC voltage (**100 – 400 V**) is applied; ions/micelles migrate to the part and **coagulate** on the surface to form a uniform film. The film grows until it becomes insulating, at which point deposition self-limits — this is the **Throwing Power** phenomenon.

**Advantages & Limitations** (2 marks):

| Advantages | Limitations |
|---|---|
| TE > 99 % | Limited to waterborne / ionic paints |
| Uniform film on complex geometry | High capital cost (bath + rectifier) |
| Self-limiting thickness | Substrate must be **electrically conductive** |
| Excellent corrosion resistance | Slow line speed |
| Low VOC, eco-friendly | Colour-change requires bath dump |

---

**Model Solution — Part (b):**

**Given:**
$$VS = 0.40, \quad \rho = 1.20 \,g/cm^{3}, \quad TE = 0.95, \quad DFT = 25 \,\mu m, \quad n = 2, \quad A = 250 \,m^{2}$$

**Step (i) — Paint quantity:**

Using the consolidated formula:

$$Q = \frac{A \times n \times DFT}{10 \times VS \times TE}$$

$$Q = \frac{250 \times 2 \times 25}{10 \times 0.40 \times 0.95} = \frac{12500}{3.80} = 3289.5 \, L$$

**Step (ii) — VOC content:**

$$VOC = \rho \times 1000 \times (1 - VS) = 1.20 \times 1000 \times 0.60 = 720 \, g/L$$

> **KTU Mark Split:** (i) 3.5 marks (with full substitution) + (ii) 3.5 marks (with explanation).

---

> **Q3 (B). [Alternative Internal Choice, KTU 2024 Pattern]**
> **(a)** Explain with neat sketches the **conventional air spray** and **airless spray** processes. Compare their atomization mechanism, transfer efficiency, and typical applications. **(7 marks)**
> **(b)** Derive the **Landau–Levich equation** for the wet film thickness in **dip coating**. A nanocomposite dispersion is withdrawn at **0.05 m/s** from a dip tank. The viscosity is **0.5 Pa·s**, density **1100 kg/m³**, and surface tension **0.04 N/m**. Calculate the wet film thickness. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution — Part (a):**

**Conventional Air Spray (3 marks):**
- Compressed air at **3–6 bar** enters a nozzle (air cap) surrounding a fluid tip.
- The high-velocity air jet creates a **low-pressure region** (Bernoulli effect) that siphons paint from the cup and **atomizes** it into 10–100 µm droplets.
- The droplets are shaped into a fan by the air cap.
- **TE = 30 – 50 %**, high overspray.

**Airless Spray (2 marks):**
- Paint is pressurized to **100 – 250 bar** by a hydraulic pump and forced through a **small orifice (0.2 – 0.6 mm)**.
- The pressure drop atomizes the paint **without compressed air**.
- **TE = 50 – 70 %**; can spray highly viscous paints.
- Typical uses: marine, structural steel, tank exteriors.

**Comparison (2 marks):** (see Section 2.2 — same tabular format).

---

**Model Solution — Part (b):**

**Derivation (4 marks):** as in SECTION 3.4, leading to:

$$h_0 = 0.94 \, \eta^{2/3} \, v^{2/3} \, \rho^{-1/3} \, g^{-1/2} \, \sigma^{-1/6}$$

**Numerical substitution (3 marks):**
- $\eta = 0.5$ Pa·s, $v = 0.05$ m/s, $\rho = 1100$ kg/m³, $g = 9.81$ m/s², $\sigma = 0.04$ N/m.

**Step 1 — Capillary number:**

$$Ca = \frac{\eta v}{\sigma} = \frac{0.5 \times 0.05}{0.04} = 0.625$$

**Step 2 — Capillary length:**

$$\ell_c = \sqrt{\frac{\sigma}{\rho g}} = \sqrt{\frac{0.04}{1100 \times 9.81}} = \sqrt{3.708 \times 10^{-6}} = 1.926 \times 10^{-3} \, m$$

**Step 3 — Wet film thickness:**

$$h_0 = 0.94 \times \ell_c \times Ca^{2/3}$$

$$Ca^{2/3} = (0.625)^{2/3} = 0.730$$

$$h_0 = 0.94 \times 1.926 \times 10^{-3} \times 0.730 = 1.32 \times 10^{-3} \, m = 1320 \, \mu m$$

> [!NOTE]
> This is a **very thick** wet film — appropriate for a highly viscous nanocomposite dip. For thin films (< 50 µm), withdraw at much lower speed (< 0.001 m/s).

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — Lose Marks Here:**
> 1. **Unit mismatch in WFT/DFT formula:** Students write $DFT = WFT \times VS$ in cm or mm and forget to convert. Always use **µm** for the 10× conversion to m²/L to work.
> 2. **Forgetting the factor $n$ (number of coats):** A 7-mark problem expects 3–4 marks for the correct setup; missing $n$ = **–3 marks**.
> 3. **Mixing transfer efficiency with %:** TE must be a **decimal** (0.65, not 65) inside the formula.
> 4. **Sign in VOC:** VOC is always **positive** and reported in **g/L** of *liquid paint* (not dry film).
> 5. **Dip-coating numerics:** Forgetting the capillary length $\ell_c$ and writing $h_0 \propto v$ (linear) instead of $v^{2/3}$ — **–2 marks**.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Paint Application Methods (Module 4)**

- **Five families** of paint application: **Manual, Spray, Industrial, Powder, Electrochemical (E-coat)**.
- **Transfer Efficiency (TE)** is the single most important metric for cost and VOC.
  - Lowest TE: **Conventional air spray (~30 %)**.
  - Highest TE: **E-coat (>99 %)** and **Electrostatic spray (80–95 %)**.
- **Volume Solids (VS)** is the bridge between **WFT** and **DFT**: $DFT = WFT \times VS$.
- **Spreading Rate (TSR)** in m²/L = $10 \times VS / DFT$ (per coat, theoretical).
- **Paint quantity** $Q = (A \times n \times DFT) / (10 \times VS \times TE)$ — keep units consistent.
- **VOC** in g/L = $\rho \times 1000 \times (1 - VS)$ — regulatory metric for green coatings.
- **Dip coating** film thickness follows the **Landau–Levich** law: $h_0 = 0.94 \, \ell_c \, Ca^{2/3}$.
  - $h_0 \propto v^{2/3}$ (withdrawal speed), $h_0 \propto \eta^{2/3}$ (viscosity), $h_0 \propto \sigma^{-1/6}$ (surface tension).
- **For polymer nanocomposites**: prefer **low-shear** methods (dip, curtain, e-coat) to preserve nano-dispersion of **2D materials** (MXene, GO, nanoclay).
- **Conductive nano-fillers** (CNT, Ag, graphene) → **Electrostatic spray** is ideal.
- **High-viscosity or high-DFT** coatings → **Airless spray** or **Fluidized bed**.
- **VOC regulations** drive industry to **HVLP**, **e-coat**, and **powder coating**.
- **Curing** of powder coatings occurs at **160 – 200 °C** for **10 – 20 minutes**.
- **E-coat** uses **100 – 400 V DC**; throws power enables uniform coating on cavities.
- **Curtain coating** is restricted to **flat or near-flat panels** moving on a conveyor.
